AS

Amarendra Sharma, PhD

Economist

Dr. Amarendra Sharma is a seasoned economist with over two decades of teaching experience spanning Principles of Microeconomics, Principles of Macroeconomics, Intermediate Macroeconomics, Intermediate Microeconomics, International Trade, International Economics, Money & Finance, Finance, Statistics, Financial Econometrics, Econometrics, Game Theory, Labor Economics, Urban Economics, Industrial Organization, and Economics in Films. He is currently at Binghamton University (SUNY), and has previously held academic positions at the University of Maryland Global Campus, American University, Ithaca College, the University of Tampa, Elmira College, and Miami University.

Dr. Sharma earned his PhD in Economics from the University of Cincinnati, where his doctoral dissertation explored Internet economics. He holds two Master's degrees — one from the University of Cincinnati and one from the Delhi School of Economics, one of India's most prestigious graduate programs in economics. His research spans Development Economics, Public Economics, International Economics, Industrial Organization, Labor Economics, Sports Economics, and Education Economics. He has published in peer-reviewed journals and presented at numerous conferences. Dr. Sharma has been listed in Who's Who in the World and Who's Who in America.

This textbook grew out of his work developing MacroNations, a competitive macroeconomic policy simulation platform that challenges students to govern a nation across ten rounds of fiscal, monetary, and structural policy decisions. The book is designed to be used both as a standalone text for principles or intermediate macroeconomics courses and as the intellectual foundation for competing in the MacroNations tournament.

Table of Contents

HOW TO USE THIS TEXTBOOK

Use the Level toggle in the header to switch between Principles and Intermediate content. Principles sections provide intuitive, example-rich explanations. Intermediate sections add formal models, algebra, and graphical analysis. Each chapter ends with multiple-choice, short-answer, and policy scenario questions. Live charts pull real-time data from the Federal Reserve Economic Database (FRED).

Chapter 1 · Part I: Introduction

What Economics Actually Studies

Six recurring themes that organize the discipline — scarcity and choice, incentives and unintended consequences, trade and specialization, markets and their failures, aggregation and emergence, and the role of institutions — and why each is more contested in practice than introductory textbooks usually admit

If you ask ten economists to define economics, you will get ten different answers, which is itself a clue about the discipline. Lionel Robbins's classic 1932 definition — "the science of human behavior in the face of scarce means with alternative uses" — captures the standard view but is narrow enough that working economists exceed it constantly. The 2024 Nobel Prize, awarded to Acemoglu, Johnson, and Robinson for studying how political institutions shape long-run prosperity, was given for work that fits awkwardly into Robbins's frame. The 2019 Nobel for Banerjee, Duflo, and Kremer for the randomized-controlled-trial approach to development was given for methodology Robbins could not have imagined. The 2017 Nobel for Richard Thaler was given for showing that the rational-choice model on which Robbins's definition rests is empirically wrong in identifiable, predictable ways.

Economics is, in practice, the study of how people, organizations, and societies allocate scarce resources, respond to incentives, organize cooperation, and build the institutions that govern all of these. It overlaps heavily with political science, sociology, psychology, history, and increasingly biology and computer science. What distinguishes the economic approach is not a fixed subject matter but a particular set of analytical tools: the assumption (often relaxed) that agents respond to incentives, the use of equilibrium reasoning, the disciplined comparison of policies via their welfare effects, and increasingly the empirical credibility revolution of the past 30 years. This chapter introduces six recurring themes that organize how economists think — themes you'll see again and again throughout this book, applied to consumer behavior, firm decisions, market structures, and ultimately to whole-economy outcomes.

✦ Sharma's Take — Why I don't teach "the ten principles of economics"

Most introductory textbooks open with a list of ten or so "principles" — like "people respond to incentives," "trade can make everyone better off," and "markets are usually a good way to organize economic activity." These principles are not wrong, exactly, but they're misleading as a framework. They present a stylized version of economics where the rough edges have been smoothed off and the central debates have been resolved in favor of one particular vision (broadly: markets work, government usually doesn't, prices are good signals). The actual discipline is more contested than the principles framework suggests, the historical record is messier than the textbook stories admit, and the empirical evidence is more nuanced than the bullet-point principles imply.

The framework I'll use instead is six recurring themes rather than principles. Themes are questions and patterns the discipline keeps returning to, not settled conclusions. Each theme has a stylized story you'll find in most textbooks, and each has a more complicated empirical reality that working economists actually engage with. If you finish this book understanding both the stylized version and why it's a simplification, you'll have done what an introductory course can deliver. Pretending the simplifications are the truth would be teaching you something we know is wrong.

— A.S.

1.1 Theme One: Scarcity Forces Choice — Including Choices About Choice

📘 Principles Level — Scarcity as the starting point

The classical starting point of economics is scarcity: human wants exceed available resources, so choices must be made about what to produce, who gets to consume, when to consume vs. save, what to invest in. Every economic system — capitalist, socialist, hunter-gatherer, feudal — has to answer these questions. The differences across systems are largely about how they answer them: through markets, through central planning, through tradition, through political negotiation, through some mix.

Scarcity is real. In any given afternoon you cannot both go to the gym and finish reading a book. Two minutes spent here cannot also be spent there. Money spent on rent cannot also be spent on food. Hours of public sector spending on K-12 education cannot also be spent on Medicare. The recognition that all choices involve foregone alternatives — the opportunity cost of the path taken — is one of the truly portable concepts in economics. It applies to your decision about which course to take this semester, to a firm's decision about which project to fund, to a country's decision about its defense vs. social spending mix.

⚠ Common Pitfall — "We don't have the resources for that"

You will hear politicians, commentators, and ordinary people say "we can't afford that" about specific government programs. This is sometimes correct as a budget statement, but it's almost never correct as an economic claim. The U.S. produces approximately $28 trillion of goods and services per year. The question is not whether resources exist; the question is whether using resources for purpose X means giving up purpose Y. "We can't afford universal pre-K" usually means "we choose not to reallocate from other uses." That's a legitimate choice, but it's a choice, not a constraint.

The economic question — and the more interesting one — is the trade-off, not the affordability. If universal pre-K requires raising taxes by 0.5% of GDP and produces a benefit of (let's say) 1.5% of GDP in higher long-run productivity, then it passes a cost-benefit test even though it requires reallocating resources. The "can't afford it" framing obscures the actual decision being made. Whenever you hear "we can't afford it," substitute "we choose other priorities" and see if the argument still feels the same. Often it doesn't.

1939-1945 United States Wartime Reallocation

How the U.S. Found 40% of GDP for Defense

In 1939, U.S. defense spending was about 1% of GDP. By 1944 it was 38% of GDP — a 37-percentage-point shift in a five-year period. To accomplish this, the U.S. instituted rationing of consumer goods (sugar, gasoline, tires, butter), price controls, capacity reallocation from automobiles to aircraft, civilian draft of millions of workers into military service, and massive borrowing financed by patriotic war bonds. Real consumer spending per capita actually fell during the war — Americans had higher nominal income but were not allowed to spend it on the goods they wanted, because those goods were not being produced.

The interesting question for our purposes is this: in 1939, could a U.S. politician have said "we can't afford a war that takes 38% of GDP"? They might have, but they would have been wrong. The resources existed; what was required was reallocation. Once the political consensus changed in late 1941, the reallocation happened with extraordinary speed. This case illustrates the difference between budget arithmetic ("we don't have the money allocated") and economic possibility ("the resources don't exist"). The latter is much rarer than the former. When you next hear about a hypothetical government program, ask: is this really an affordability problem, or is it a priorities problem?

1.2 Theme Two: Incentives Matter — And Often Backfire

📘 Principles Level — The textbook story

People — individuals, firms, governments — respond to incentives. Raise the price of gasoline through a tax, and people drive less, buy more efficient cars, or move closer to work. Subsidize solar panels, and more get installed. Increase fines for speeding, and more people slow down. The mechanism is straightforward: incentives change the relative attractiveness of different choices, and people shift toward more attractive options.

This is one of the most empirically supported propositions in economics. Hundreds of studies confirm that demand curves slope downward (people buy less of a good when its price rises) and supply curves slope upward (firms produce more when they're paid more). When economists predict the consequences of policy, they're applying this framework: a higher minimum wage will tend to reduce employment (firms substitute away from labor); a tax credit for solar installation will tend to increase solar installation; a tax on cigarettes will tend to reduce smoking. The empirical regularity is robust.

📗 Intermediate Level — Why incentives produce unintended consequences

The complication is that the incentive working as intended is only one of many incentives a policy creates. Policies create direct incentives (the intended response) and indirect incentives (responses you didn't anticipate). The history of economic policy is largely the history of indirect incentives turning out to matter more than direct ones. Consider some examples that you will see again throughout this book:

  • Rent control reduces rents for current tenants (intended) but reduces the supply of rental housing over time as landlords convert to other uses (unintended), reducing total housing availability.
  • Cobra bounties in colonial India were intended to reduce cobra populations by paying for dead cobras. People started breeding cobras to collect the bounty. When the program ended, the breeders released their cobras, increasing the population. This is now called the "cobra effect."
  • Pay-for-performance in schools intended to raise math scores often does — by inducing teachers to teach to the test, reducing time on subjects not on the test, and sometimes leading to outright cheating on standardized tests.
  • Stricter mortgage underwriting rules intended to prevent another 2008 crisis make it harder for first-time buyers to get mortgages, reducing homeownership among the young.

The principles framework would say "people respond to incentives." The fuller principle is: people respond to all the incentives a policy creates, and the unintended responses often turn out to be larger or more important than the intended ones. Designing policies that work well requires anticipating these indirect responses, not just the direct ones.

✦ Sharma's Take — Why economic policymaking is hard

The principle "people respond to incentives" combined with the corollary "responses go in many directions, including ones you didn't anticipate" is most of why economic policy is genuinely difficult. Almost any policy that produces a desired direct effect also produces indirect effects that may partially or fully offset it. This isn't an argument for never doing anything — that conclusion ignores the direct benefits. It's an argument for taking the indirect responses seriously when designing policy and not being surprised when they show up.

The economists who do the best policy analysis are not the ones who can identify the intended effect (anyone can do that). They're the ones who can identify the full set of incentive responses and estimate their relative magnitudes. This is also why economists often disagree about policy effects: they can have similar models of incentives but different estimates of which incentive responses will dominate empirically. When you read economic policy commentary, ask: what are the direct effects the policy intends? What indirect effects might it also produce? Which is likely to matter more? Most policy debates can be productively reframed in those terms.

— A.S.

1.3 Theme Three: Trade and Specialization Create Wealth — But Not for Everyone

📘 Principles Level — The standard story

When two parties trade voluntarily, both expect to gain — otherwise they wouldn't trade. This basic insight, applied at scale, explains why economic specialization and trade increase aggregate wealth. A farmer who's good at growing wheat can specialize in wheat and trade with a baker who's good at baking bread; both end up with more wheat and more bread than if each tried to do both. Apply this to nations: countries with different endowments (Saudi Arabia's oil, Germany's manufacturing capacity, Bangladesh's labor force) can specialize and trade and end up with more of everything than if each tried to be self-sufficient. David Ricardo showed in 1817 that this works even when one party is absolutely better at everything — comparative advantage means trade benefits both sides as long as relative costs differ.

This is one of the most robust empirical findings in economics. Countries that opened to international trade in the second half of the 20th century — South Korea, Taiwan, Singapore, China after 1978, Vietnam after 1986, India after 1991 — generally experienced large gains in measured GDP per capita. Cross-country regressions consistently find a positive correlation between trade openness and income levels.

📗 Intermediate Level — The distribution of gains

The complication: trade increases aggregate wealth, but the gains and losses are distributed unevenly. When a country imports manufactured goods more cheaply from abroad, consumers gain (lower prices) but domestic manufacturing workers lose (lower wages, fewer jobs, sometimes closed factories). The aggregate gain is positive, but the gains and losses go to different people.

Economists have known this in theory for a century — the Stolper-Samuelson theorem from 1941 formalized when trade hurts which groups. The empirical evidence on the magnitude of the distributional effects has grown dramatically since the early 2000s. The work of Autor, Dorn, and Hanson on the "China Shock" showed that U.S. manufacturing communities exposed to Chinese import competition experienced large and lasting employment declines, reduced earnings, and increased participation in disability insurance programs. The aggregate U.S. welfare gain from trade with China is meaningful — Caliendo and Parro (2015) estimate roughly 3.4% over the 1995-2011 period — while the local losses in specific manufacturing communities were also large and concentrated. The textbook claim "trade benefits both sides" is true at the country level and false at the community level.

2001-2019 United States The China Shock

The Empirical Case That Reshaped How Economists Think About Trade

From 2001 (China's WTO accession) to roughly 2007, the U.S. experienced a unique import surge from China. Manufacturing employment in the U.S. fell from approximately 17 million workers in 2000 to a low of about 11.5 million in early 2010 — roughly a one-third reduction. Autor, Dorn, and Hanson's series of papers (2013, 2016, 2021) used cross-county variation in exposure to Chinese imports to identify the employment, wage, and social effects. Their findings transformed trade economics.

The numbers: in counties heavily exposed to Chinese import competition, manufacturing employment fell roughly twice as fast as in less-exposed counties. Median earnings in exposed communities fell about 5-10% over a decade. Participation in federal disability programs rose meaningfully. Marriage rates fell. Mortality rates rose (the "deaths of despair" literature finds connections with trade exposure). Net new manufacturing job creation outside the affected sectors took roughly 10 years to materialize, and the new jobs were rarely in the same communities.

What this evidence did to economic thinking: most economists in 2001 expected trade with China to produce textbook gains (lower consumer prices, expansion of comparative advantage sectors, modest adjustment costs). The aggregate price benefits did materialize — consumer prices for goods affected by Chinese competition fell substantially. But the adjustment costs in affected communities were larger and more persistent than the textbook framework predicted, and the political consequences (rising opposition to trade liberalization across both parties) followed. The China Shock didn't refute the "gains from trade" framework — it showed that distribution matters more than aggregate measures suggested. Modern trade policy debates take this much more seriously than they did 25 years ago.

✦ Sharma's Take — How to think about trade after the China Shock

The pre-2001 textbook treatment of trade focused almost entirely on aggregate efficiency gains and treated distributional effects as a footnote. The post-2013 treatment takes distribution as a central consideration. Both extremes can be wrong. "Trade is good" is too simple — it ignores serious adjustment costs that fall heavily on specific people. "Trade is bad" is also too simple — it ignores real and substantial benefits to consumers and to comparative-advantage industries. The honest answer: trade produces aggregate gains that are real but often modest in magnitude, and concentrated losses that are real and often large for affected groups. The interesting policy questions are about how to capture the gains while compensating the losers — questions that political systems have proven bad at answering.

The U.S. has a Trade Adjustment Assistance program that nominally helps workers displaced by trade. Empirical evaluation finds it has weak effects on long-run outcomes. European countries with stronger labor protections and more aggressive active labor market policies (training, relocation assistance, job placement) have handled trade adjustment somewhat better, though not perfectly. The key insight: the political backlash against trade liberalization that we've seen across developed democracies since 2016 isn't irrational ignorance of the gains from trade. It's a reasonable response by people whose communities bore concentrated costs while the gains went to others. Understanding this is part of understanding modern trade policy.

— A.S.

1.4 Theme Four: Markets Are Powerful — And Have Predictable Failure Modes

📘 Principles Level — What markets do well

Markets — decentralized systems where buyers and sellers transact at prices that emerge from their interactions — accomplish something genuinely remarkable. They aggregate information from millions of individual decisions into prices that signal scarcity and value. They coordinate production across geographically dispersed actors who don't know each other. They incentivize innovation. They allow specialization to flourish. Hayek's classic 1945 essay "The Use of Knowledge in Society" articulated this as the central insight of market economics: no central planner can possibly have the dispersed local information that prices aggregate automatically. The implication is that for many problems — what shoes to make, what crops to plant, what restaurants to open — market processes produce better outcomes than top-down planning.

The empirical evidence supports this for a wide class of problems. The Soviet Union's central planning of consumer goods produced chronic shortages and surpluses that markets handled effortlessly. China's reforms after 1978, which gradually allowed markets to operate in agriculture and manufacturing, produced the largest poverty reduction in human history. Countries with stronger property rights and more functional markets are systematically richer than countries without these institutions.

📗 Intermediate Level — When markets fail

The complication: there's a recognized class of problems where markets predictably fail to produce socially optimal outcomes. Economists call these market failures, and the catalog of them — refined over a century of theoretical and empirical work — is one of the discipline's most important contributions. The major categories:

  • Externalities: when a transaction affects parties who aren't involved in it. Pollution is the canonical example: a factory imposes costs on people downwind who aren't part of the buying-and-selling. The market fails because the factory doesn't pay these costs.
  • Public goods: goods that, once provided, can be used by anyone without diminishing availability (national defense, basic research, GPS). Markets undersupply these because the producer can't charge users.
  • Information asymmetries: when one party knows more than the other. The used-car market is the classic example (sellers know defects buyers don't see); insurance is another (buyers know health information that affects their costs to the insurer).
  • Monopoly and concentrated market power: when one firm dominates a market, it can charge prices above efficient levels. Competition fails to constrain pricing.
  • Coordination failures: when multiple equilibria exist and the economy gets stuck in a bad one. Standards adoption (QWERTY keyboard, gas vs electric vehicles for a century) often involves this.
  • Behavioral failures: when people don't behave rationally enough to make markets work as theorized. Addiction, present bias, loss aversion, framing effects — Kahneman, Thaler, and others document systematic deviations from rational choice that affect market outcomes.
⚠ Common Pitfall — "Markets work" vs "Markets fail"

Don't reason from "markets fail for problem X" to "government should do X" without checking whether government can do better. Markets fail in identifiable ways, but governments also have their own characteristic failures: bureaucratic inertia, regulatory capture, electoral short-termism, principal-agent problems between politicians and voters, lobbying influence, knowledge limitations. The right question is rarely "does the market fail?" — for many problems the answer is yes. The right question is "does the market fail badly enough that the available government interventions improve outcomes?" Sometimes yes (national defense, basic research, environmental regulation, infrastructure), sometimes no (most consumer goods, food production), and sometimes the answer depends heavily on the specifics of how government intervention is designed (housing markets are a great example where bad government intervention has made things worse and good intervention could help).

Both extremes get this wrong. The libertarian extreme says markets always work and government intervention always makes things worse. The opposite extreme says markets always fail and government should run everything. The empirical evidence supports neither extreme. Markets work superbly for some problems and badly for others. Government works well for some problems and badly for others. Most interesting policy debates are about specific problems where the answer is genuinely contested, not about which side of an ideological binary you're on.

1.5 Theme Five: Macro Is Not Just Aggregated Micro

📗 Intermediate Level — Emergence and aggregation

One of the deepest themes in economics, and one of the most counterintuitive, is that the behavior of the whole economy is not simply the sum of the behavior of individual agents. Things happen at the aggregate level that don't happen at the individual level. This has its own name in economics: the fallacy of composition.

The classic example is saving. For an individual household, saving more is generally good — it builds wealth, provides for emergencies, funds retirement. From the individual perspective, more saving is unambiguously a virtue. But if all households suddenly decide to save more simultaneously, the resulting fall in aggregate consumption can trigger a recession, reducing everyone's income and therefore reducing total saving. This is the "paradox of thrift" — what's wise for one household becomes destructive when adopted by all. Keynes's General Theory built much of its argument on insights of this kind.

Other examples: an individual firm cutting costs is good for that firm; all firms cutting costs simultaneously reduces aggregate demand and can deepen a recession. An individual exporter benefits from a weaker currency; all countries simultaneously trying to depreciate their currencies (competitive devaluation) makes everyone worse off. An individual investor selling stocks during a downturn limits their losses; all investors selling simultaneously turns a price decline into a crash.

✦ Sharma's Take — Why this theme is so hard to grasp

The fallacy of composition is conceptually difficult because the human brain is wired for thinking about individual cases and not for thinking about aggregation. We learn lessons from our own experience and apply them to general claims. This works fine for almost everything in life except macroeconomics. The reasoning processes that serve you well in personal finance, in firm management, in community organizing — they often produce the wrong answers when applied to whole-economy questions.

This is why macroeconomic policy debate is so contested. The intuitive arguments — "the government should balance its budget like a household," "if we just cut taxes everyone will work harder," "if we just raised the minimum wage poverty would end" — apply individual-level reasoning to aggregate-level questions. Sometimes the answers are similar, but often they're not. One of the most important things you'll learn in macroeconomics (later in this book) is to recognize when individual-level intuition is misleading you about aggregate outcomes. The theme of aggregation and emergence runs through this whole textbook.

— A.S.

1.6 Theme Six: Institutions Are Where Economics and Politics Meet

📗 Intermediate Level — Why institutions matter

The sixth theme is the one most underemphasized in standard introductory textbooks: that economic outcomes depend heavily on the institutions within which economic activity takes place. Institutions are the formal and informal rules that structure interactions — property rights, contract enforcement, judicial systems, regulatory frameworks, central banks, voting systems, social norms.

Why this matters: two countries with similar resources, similar populations, similar geography can have very different economic outcomes if their institutions differ. North Korea and South Korea share a peninsula, a history, an ethnic background, a climate. South Korea is 25-30x wealthier than North Korea. The difference is institutional: South Korea has property rights, functional courts, competitive elections, regulatory frameworks that allow markets to operate. North Korea has none of these. The institutional difference explains nearly all of the income gap.

Acemoglu, Johnson, and Robinson's research program — for which they shared the 2024 Nobel Prize — argues that institutional quality is the deepest determinant of long-run prosperity. Their key claim: "inclusive" institutions that distribute power broadly and allow innovation produce sustained growth; "extractive" institutions that concentrate power among elites and discourage broad participation produce stagnation. The argument has been refined and debated for two decades, but the central insight — institutions matter more than resources — has become mainstream.

1989-1995 Eastern Europe Institutional Change

How Quickly Can Institutions Change? The Post-Soviet Experience

When the Soviet Union collapsed in 1991, Eastern European countries faced a near-simultaneous transition from central planning to market institutions. The outcomes varied dramatically depending on institutional choices. Poland, the Czech Republic, Hungary, and Slovenia chose rapid liberalization with strong rule-of-law reforms; they recovered from initial transition recessions and joined the European Union by 2004. By 2024 their GDP per capita had converged substantially with Western European levels. Russia and Ukraine pursued slower and less complete reforms, with weaker rule-of-law institutions; their growth has been much more volatile and less sustained.

The lesson for the institutional theme: institutions can change, but the rate of change and the quality of new institutions matter enormously for outcomes. Poland's institutions didn't become functional in a year — they were built over a decade through deliberate construction of judicial systems, regulatory bodies, property rights frameworks. The countries that moved faster on institutional construction (despite often slower on price liberalization) generally fared better than those that liberalized prices without building the supporting institutions. Markets need institutions; without them, "market reform" can just be a license for connected elites to capture assets, as happened in much of post-Soviet Russia in the 1990s. Institutions are not just background conditions — they're the operating system on which economic activity runs.

1.7 Interactive: Six Themes Cross-Reference

🎛 Interactive — Which themes apply to your policy question?

Select a policy or economic question; see which of the six themes are most relevant

Most economic questions involve multiple themes. Real policy debates usually become productive when participants identify which themes are doing the most work in their disagreement.

How to read this: the chart shows which of the six themes apply most strongly to each policy question. Notice that most questions involve multiple themes — that's why economic policy is hard. The themes that most apply usually identify where the most genuine disagreement among economists lies.

1.8 Worked Examples

📐 Worked Example 1.1 — Opportunity Cost in Personal and Public Decisions
A college student is considering whether to spend an additional semester at college earning a master's degree. Tuition is $20,000. The student would also forgo $40,000 in income from working full-time during that semester. (a) What is the opportunity cost of the master's? (b) The student's lifetime earnings would rise by an estimated $200,000 (present value) with the master's. Is the decision economically defensible? (c) How does this analysis change if the student already has a job that requires a master's degree to keep?
1
(a) Opportunity cost: the full economic cost of the decision is tuition ($20,000) plus foregone income ($40,000) = $60,000. The "out of pocket" cost is $20,000, but ignoring foregone earnings is a common analytical mistake.
2
(b) Cost-benefit: expected lifetime gain ($200,000) far exceeds total opportunity cost ($60,000). Net expected value of about +$140,000. The decision passes the cost-benefit test. Even discounting the $200,000 for risk and uncertainty about the actual earnings premium, the decision looks defensible.
3
(c) Changed circumstances: if the master's is required to keep the existing job, then the foregone option is much worse — losing the entire career path, not just a year of income. The opportunity cost framing now includes the entire foregone career, not just one year. The decision becomes even more defensible, as the alternative (not getting the master's) involves much larger losses.
The portable insight: opportunity cost includes everything you give up — direct costs (tuition) plus foregone alternatives (lost wages, career path, time). Most "expensive" decisions in life are not expensive because of the dollars out of pocket; they're expensive because of what you don't do. This same framework applies to government decisions: spending money on Program X means not spending it on Program Y. The opportunity cost is whatever Y would have produced, not the dollar figure of X alone.
📐 Worked Example 1.2 — Unintended Consequences of a Subsidy
A government wants to encourage homeownership. It introduces a $10,000 first-time homebuyer tax credit. (a) What is the intended direct effect? (b) Identify at least three indirect or unintended consequences. (c) Predict what happens to median home prices in the short run.
1
(a) Direct effect: the credit reduces the effective cost of homeownership for eligible buyers, increasing the demand for owner-occupied housing among first-time buyers.
2
(b) Indirect consequences include: (i) Some of the credit gets capitalized into home prices, raising prices for buyers who don't receive the credit. (ii) Renters delay buying decisions to qualify as "first-time" buyers, distorting the timing of purchases. (iii) Sellers of homes for under (some threshold) tighten their negotiations, knowing buyers have additional resources. (iv) The credit may pull forward demand from future years, reducing demand later when the program ends.
3
(c) Short-run price effect: the increased demand pushes prices up. Empirical evidence from past first-time homebuyer credits (e.g., U.S. 2008-2010) suggests roughly 30-60% of the credit gets captured by sellers in higher home prices. The actual benefit to buyers is therefore much smaller than the nominal $10,000.
The deeper lesson: a policy intended to help first-time buyers can largely benefit sellers if the supply of housing is inelastic (which it typically is in the short run). This is one of the most important results in housing economics: housing subsidies tend to get capitalized into prices, with much of the benefit accruing to existing homeowners and sellers rather than to the intended beneficiaries. The general lesson: when designing a policy intended to help one group, ask who else is affected by the resulting price changes, and check whether the indirect effects might dominate the direct ones.
🎮 MacroNations Connection — The Six Themes in the Engine

Each of the six themes shows up directly in the MacroNations simulation engine. Theme 1 (Scarcity): every round you have one budget — increasing welfare_spending and infrastructure together means raising tax_rate or debt. Theme 2 (Incentives): the engine implements the Laffer curve (tax_rate > 40% triggers efficiency drag), the underfunding drag at tax_rate < 12%, and inflation expectations that respond to your past monetary credibility. Theme 3 (Trade and specialization): trade_openness directly affects productivity (+0.6 per pp above 65) but rapid swings invite currency volatility — the distribution-of-gains problem in microcosm. Theme 4 (Markets and their failures): regulation has an optimum at 50 (both excessive and insufficient hurt fin_health) — the engine's recognition that pure laissez-faire and pure heavy regulation both fail. Theme 5 (Aggregation): the engine's output-gap formula synthesizes individual lever decisions into aggregate GDP — small changes to multiple levers compound into large GDP swings that individual-lever intuition can't predict. Theme 6 (Institutions): there's no "institutional quality" lever, but every state variable (potential, productivity, fin_health) evolves slowly and embodies the institutional patience of your strategy. Tournaments are won by integrating all six themes — exactly what this textbook teaches.

Chapter 1 Quiz

Scarcity, incentives, trade and distribution, market failures, aggregation, institutions
Part A — Multiple Choice (Principles)
1. The opportunity cost of attending a year of college is best described as:
A The tuition cost alone
B Zero, since education is an investment
C Tuition plus foregone wages plus the value of anything else you could have done with the time
D The lifetime earnings premium from college
2. The "China Shock" research (Autor, Dorn, Hanson) found that:
A Trade with China produced no aggregate gains for the U.S.
B Trade with China produced modest aggregate gains but large and persistent local losses in specific manufacturing communities
C Trade benefited the U.S. but harmed China
D The China Shock had no measurable economic effects
3. The "cobra effect" refers to:
A The role of snake oil in colonial economies
B A specific market failure in pharmaceuticals
C A policy producing the opposite of its intended effect because of unanticipated incentive responses
D A type of investment strategy
4. The "paradox of thrift" describes:
A Why saving is always bad for the economy
B Why individual prudence (saving) can produce collective harm (reduced demand and recession) — an example of the fallacy of composition
C Why interest rates and inflation move together
D The relationship between thrift and unemployment
Part B — Multiple Choice (Intermediate)
5. Which of the following is NOT a recognized type of market failure?
A Externalities
B Information asymmetries
C Public goods
D Voluntary exchange between informed adults
6. The Acemoglu-Johnson-Robinson institutional thesis argues that long-run prosperity depends primarily on:
A Natural resource endowments
B Geographic location
C Institutional quality — particularly whether institutions distribute power broadly and protect property rights
D Population size
7. A first-time homebuyer tax credit of $10,000 in a market with inelastic short-run housing supply will primarily benefit:
A First-time buyers (the intended beneficiaries)
B The government through tax revenue
C Home sellers, through higher prices (much of the credit gets capitalized into home prices)
D Real estate agents
Part C — Short Answer
8. Identify a current policy debate (in any country) and explain which of the six themes from this chapter are doing the most analytical work in that debate. Be specific about the empirical mechanisms.
Model answer (example): The U.S. minimum wage debate involves Theme 1 (scarcity — trade-off between higher wages for employed workers vs reduced employment), Theme 2 (incentives — firms substitute toward labor-saving technology), Theme 5 (aggregation — what's true for individual firms isn't always true for the whole labor market, since demand effects of higher wages partially offset substitution), and Theme 6 (institutions — minimum wage interacts with state right-to-work laws, union density, immigration policy). The empirical work on minimum wages over the past 30 years (Card-Krueger, Cengiz et al.) finds that modest minimum wage increases produce small employment effects but real wage gains for low-wage workers — supporting both Theme 2 (some effect exists) and complicating naive applications of it.
9. Explain why "we can't afford universal pre-K" is an economically incomplete statement, and what a more economically meaningful framing would be.
Model answer: "We can't afford it" suggests that the resources don't exist. They do — the U.S. economy produces $28 trillion per year. The question is reallocation, not affordability. A more meaningful framing: "We currently choose other priorities for the resources that would be required" or "We don't think the benefits exceed the trade-offs with alternative uses." Then the debate becomes: what are the alternative uses, what are the actual benefits of universal pre-K (Heckman's work suggests 7-13x return), what would have to be given up, and is that trade-off acceptable? This is a richer and more honest debate than "can we afford it." It reveals where the actual disagreement lies — usually in the valuation of long-run vs short-run outcomes, in trust in government to deliver the program well, in disagreement about which alternative uses would be sacrificed.
10. A friend says: "Trade is great. Both sides benefit. The U.S. should never have any tariffs." Using the China Shock case, explain why this is too simple a view — without arguing that "trade is bad."
Model answer: The claim that "both sides benefit" is true at the country-aggregate level but can be false at the individual or community level. The China Shock evidence shows that while U.S. consumers benefited from lower prices (an aggregate welfare gain estimated at ~3.4% by Caliendo and Parro 2015) and Chinese workers benefited from manufacturing jobs and rising wages, U.S. manufacturing workers in import-competing communities experienced substantial and persistent losses: ~30% manufacturing employment declines, 5-10% earnings reductions, increased disability program participation, and connections to rising mortality rates. The aggregate gain doesn't automatically compensate the specific losers. The honest response: trade produces real gains, but distribution matters. Policy should consider whether the gains can be captured while compensating the losers (Trade Adjustment Assistance, retraining, community investment). The empirical question is whether this can be done well, and the U.S. historically has not done it very well — which is part of why political support for trade liberalization has eroded.
The live textbook auto-grades MCQs and stores progress.

References

Acemoglu, D., Johnson, S., & Robinson, J. A. (2001). The colonial origins of comparative development: An empirical investigation. American Economic Review, 91(5), 1369–1401.

Acemoglu, D., & Robinson, J. A. (2012). Why Nations Fail: The Origins of Power, Prosperity, and Poverty. Crown Business.

Autor, D. H., Dorn, D., & Hanson, G. H. (2013). The China syndrome: Local labor market effects of import competition in the United States. American Economic Review, 103(6), 2121–2168.

Autor, D. H., Dorn, D., & Hanson, G. H. (2021). On the persistence of the China shock. Brookings Papers on Economic Activity, Fall 2021.

Banerjee, A. V., & Duflo, E. (2019). Good Economics for Hard Times. PublicAffairs.

Hayek, F. A. (1945). The use of knowledge in society. American Economic Review, 35(4), 519–530.

Keynes, J. M. (1936). The General Theory of Employment, Interest and Money. Macmillan.

Robbins, L. (1932). An Essay on the Nature and Significance of Economic Science. Macmillan.

Stolper, W. F., & Samuelson, P. A. (1941). Protection and real wages. Review of Economic Studies, 9(1), 58–73.

Thaler, R. H. (2015). Misbehaving: The Making of Behavioral Economics. W.W. Norton.

Chapter 2 · Part I: Introduction

How Economists Argue

From thought experiments to natural experiments to randomized trials — what counts as evidence in modern economics, why the discipline transformed during the "credibility revolution" of the past 30 years, and how to read empirical claims with appropriate skepticism

In October 2021, the Nobel Prize in Economic Sciences was awarded to David Card, Joshua Angrist, and Guido Imbens for "methodological contributions to the analysis of causal relationships." This was unusual. Most Nobel Prizes go to economists for theoretical insights or substantive findings about how the economy works. The 2021 prize went to three economists for refining the methods by which we figure out what works — specifically, for showing how to extract causal conclusions from observational data using natural experiments. The committee was acknowledging something that had transformed empirical economics: over the past 30 years, the discipline has become dramatically better at distinguishing correlation from causation, and that methodological improvement has reshaped what economists believe about consequential policy questions.

This chapter is about how economists argue — what counts as evidence, what methods produce credible conclusions, why economists sometimes disagree even when they share data, and what changes when the empirical methods improve. By the end you should be able to read economic claims (including the ones in this textbook) with appropriate calibration: confident in well-supported findings, skeptical of poorly identified ones, and aware that the empirical state of knowledge is itself a moving target.

✦ Sharma's Take — What changed in the 1990s and why it matters

If you took an undergraduate economics course in 1985 and another in 2025, you would notice a substantial shift in how empirical claims are made and defended. The 1985 course would feature theoretical models with parameters often pulled from "calibration" exercises rather than statistical estimation; cross-country regressions that we now recognize as poorly identified; and empirical claims that were really more like illustrative exercises. The 2025 course would feature randomized controlled trials, difference-in-differences designs from natural experiments, regression discontinuity designs exploiting threshold effects in policies, and instrumental variables with explicit defenses of identification assumptions. This isn't a stylistic difference — it represents a genuine improvement in what economists know and how confidently we can claim it.

Why does this matter for an introductory course? Two reasons. First, the empirical foundation for what we teach has changed. Many "facts" that introductory textbooks reported confidently 30 years ago are now contested or known to be wrong, and many "controversial" claims have moved toward consensus. Second, knowing how the methods work helps you read economic claims critically. When someone says "study X shows that policy Y has effect Z," you should know how to ask: how did the study identify the causal effect? What's the counterfactual? Are there reasons to think the result generalizes? These questions are not optional — they're the difference between economic reasoning and just-so storytelling.

— A.S.

2.1 What Economists Actually Do

📘 Principles Level — Two complementary modes of reasoning

Economic reasoning takes two complementary forms: theoretical reasoning (constructing models that predict what should happen in stylized situations) and empirical reasoning (testing what actually happens in real-world data). Both modes have evolved enormously since the discipline became modern in the late 19th century, but the basic distinction has been stable: theories generate predictions, empirics test them.

Theoretical economics has its own internal disagreements — about which assumptions are reasonable, which models are most useful, which mathematical techniques are appropriate. But the major theoretical frameworks (consumer choice theory, firm production theory, general equilibrium, game theory, growth theory, business cycle theory) are reasonably well-established. They produce predictions that can be tested.

Empirical economics is where most of the real action has been in the past 30 years. The challenge is fundamental: most economic phenomena cannot be directly experimented on. We can't randomly assign half the country to receive a tax cut and the other half not. We can't randomly give one set of workers a higher minimum wage and another set the same wage. So how do we figure out causal effects? This is the central methodological problem of empirical economics, and the answer has evolved substantially.

2.2 The Correlation-Causation Problem and Why It Matters

📘 Principles Level — Why correlation isn't causation

The single most important distinction in empirical work is between correlation (two variables move together) and causation (one variable produces changes in another). They are not the same thing. Two variables can be correlated without one causing the other for at least three reasons:

  • Reverse causation: what looks like X causing Y might actually be Y causing X. The classic example: higher police presence is correlated with higher crime rates, but it's not the police causing the crime; cities deploy more police where there's more crime.
  • Confounding (omitted variable bias): a third variable Z affects both X and Y. Higher SAT scores are correlated with higher lifetime earnings, but this could be because both SAT scores and earnings are caused by family income, talent, or schooling quality — variables that affect both.
  • Selection effects: the people who choose to do X are not random, so the difference between X-doers and non-X-doers reflects the choice as much as the effect of X. Students who attend Ivy League schools earn more than students who don't — but they also had different characteristics (motivation, family resources, ability) when they applied.

Naive empirical work that ignores these problems produces correlations that look like causal effects but aren't. Most pre-1990 empirical economics, and most popular discussions of economic data today, fail to take these problems seriously enough.

⚠ Common Pitfall — "Studies show that..."

You'll see news articles that say "studies show that X causes Y." Treat this with skepticism. Most studies establish correlations, not causal effects. Before believing a causal claim from a study, ask: (1) Could the relationship be reversed? Is it possible Y is causing X, or that there's mutual causation? (2) What's the counterfactual? If we hadn't observed X, what would have happened? (3) Could there be a third variable? What unmeasured factor might be producing both X and Y? (4) Is the sample representative? Does this generalize beyond the specific group studied? (5) Is the effect size meaningful? A statistically significant effect can still be too small to matter practically.

The credibility revolution in economics was, in essence, about taking these questions seriously. The pre-1990 norm was "we ran some regressions and found that X correlates with Y, here are the coefficients." The post-1990 norm is "we found an identification strategy that lets us isolate causal effects of X on Y, and here's why we believe the strategy works." This is a much higher methodological bar. The good news is that empirical economics has gotten much more credible. The bad news is that many old "established findings" haven't survived the higher bar — including some that introductory textbooks still report confidently.

2.3 The Credibility Revolution: Four Methods That Changed Empirical Economics

The four canonical methods of the credibility revolution
MethodHow it worksExample application
1. Randomized Controlled Trials (RCTs) Randomly assign participants to treatment vs control group. Compare outcomes. The gold standard because random assignment eliminates selection bias. Banerjee-Duflo-Kremer: hundreds of RCTs of development interventions (mosquito nets, deworming, microfinance, conditional cash transfers).
2. Natural Experiments Exploit situations where something close to random assignment happened naturally — a policy was introduced in one place but not another, a draft lottery, a sudden rule change, a geographic boundary. Card-Krueger 1994: New Jersey raised minimum wage in 1992, Pennsylvania didn't. Compared fast-food employment before/after in both states.
3. Regression Discontinuity Compare outcomes just above and just below an arbitrary threshold (a test score cutoff, an income limit, a geographic line). Units just above and below are similar except for treatment status. Angrist-Lavy 1999: Maimonides' rule limits class size at 40 in Israel. Compares classes just above (small) vs just below (large) the cutoff.
4. Instrumental Variables Use a variable (the "instrument") that affects the treatment X but doesn't directly affect outcome Y. The instrument's effect on Y must work entirely through X. Angrist 1990: Vietnam draft lottery as instrument for military service. Random lottery number → forced military service → effects on lifetime earnings.
1992-1994 New Jersey vs Pennsylvania Natural Experiment

The Card-Krueger Minimum Wage Study That Changed a Field

In April 1992, New Jersey raised its minimum wage from $4.25 to $5.05 — an 18.8% increase. Pennsylvania, just across the river, did not. David Card and Alan Krueger had begun surveying fast-food restaurants in both states the previous February. They re-surveyed the same restaurants after the minimum wage increase. This was a textbook natural experiment: a policy change in one place but not in a nearly identical neighboring place.

The economics profession's predicted result, based on textbook supply-and-demand reasoning, was that fast-food employment in New Jersey should have fallen relative to Pennsylvania. The actual result: New Jersey employment rose by 13% while Pennsylvania employment fell by 1%. The minimum wage increase had not destroyed jobs as theory predicted; if anything, it had created them.

The reaction was intense. Some economists argued the data was flawed (different measurement methods produced different results). Some argued that this specific case was anomalous. Some argued that the standard textbook model was missing something important — perhaps fast-food labor markets weren't competitive, perhaps firms had wage-setting power (monopsony), perhaps the higher wage attracted more workers and reduced turnover. The debate ran for years. But the methodological contribution was lasting: Card and Krueger had shown what serious empirical economics could look like — exploiting a natural experiment to identify a causal effect that contradicted standard theory.

Subsequent decades of careful minimum-wage research, using similar designs across many states and time periods, has produced a much more nuanced picture than either the original textbook story or the Card-Krueger result alone. The current consensus: modest minimum wage increases (10-20%) typically produce small employment effects (perhaps -1% to +1%), with real wage gains for low-wage workers offsetting the small employment cost. Very large minimum wage increases (50%+) can produce larger employment losses. The Card-Krueger study earned David Card the 2021 Nobel Prize.

2.4 Positive and Normative — But Less Cleanly Separated Than You Think

📘 Principles Level — The standard distinction

Economics textbooks have traditionally distinguished between positive economics (descriptive — what is, or what would happen if X) and normative economics (prescriptive — what should be done). The distinction is supposed to be clean: positive claims are scientific, testable, and the proper domain of economic expertise; normative claims involve value judgments that economics has no special authority over. The textbook example: positive — "a $15 minimum wage will reduce employment in certain sectors by 2%"; normative — "we should raise the minimum wage to $15."

📗 Intermediate Level — Why the distinction is messier

The clean distinction is mostly a useful fiction. In practice, positive and normative reasoning bleed into each other constantly:

  • Choosing what to study is normative. Why do economists study minimum wages so much and rent control somewhat less? Because we collectively think the questions matter. The research agenda reflects values about what's important.
  • Defining variables involves normative choice. What counts as "unemployment"? The official U.S. rate excludes "discouraged workers" who've stopped looking. The choice of what to include reflects a view about what kind of joblessness "counts."
  • Welfare measurements embed assumptions. When economists evaluate policies by their effect on "consumer welfare," they're making assumptions about how to aggregate gains and losses across people, how much to discount future generations, what kind of inequality matters. These are normative choices dressed in positive language.
  • Trade-off recommendations cross the line. When economists say "the optimal policy is X," they're combining positive analysis with implicit normative weights on competing objectives.

This doesn't make economics useless or "just opinion." Empirical work that establishes causal effects of policies is still meaningful and important. But it means that economists' policy recommendations are not the pure result of scientific analysis. They incorporate value choices, often unstated. The honest economist makes those choices explicit; the less-honest economist pretends they don't exist.

✦ Sharma's Take — When economists pretend to be neutral

One pattern you'll see throughout your encounters with economic policy debate: economists sometimes claim to be making purely scientific recommendations when they're actually making value-laden ones. "The optimal policy is X" almost always combines empirical claims (here are the effects of X) with normative weights (how to value those effects against alternatives). When you read a confident policy recommendation from an economist, ask: what value choices are implicit in this analysis? What objective is being maximized? Whose welfare is being weighted, and how?

The best economists are transparent about their value choices. They say "if you value equality more, this policy is better; if you value growth more, that policy is better." They distinguish between empirical disagreement (about what would happen) and normative disagreement (about which outcome is preferred). The economists who pretend their recommendations are purely positive are either fooling themselves or trying to fool you. Take their empirical work seriously, but treat their recommendations as containing both science and values — usually with the values understated.

— A.S.

2.5 Why Economists Disagree

Public commentary often portrays economists as in constant disagreement about everything, which is wrong. Economists agree about more than the public realizes — surveys of professional economists show consensus on many issues that are politically contested (e.g., that carbon taxes effectively reduce emissions, that free trade has aggregate benefits, that rent control reduces housing supply over time). But there is genuine disagreement, and understanding what generates it is important. Four main sources:

Sources of economic disagreement
SourceHow it generates disagreement
Empirical uncertaintyThe effect of any policy depends on parameters we can't measure precisely. Different economists make different point estimates within reasonable uncertainty ranges. Example: the elasticity of taxable income (relevant for tax policy) ranges from 0.1 to 0.4 in the literature.
Model selectionDifferent models emphasize different mechanisms. Saltwater and freshwater macroeconomists model business cycles differently and reach different policy conclusions. Neither is "wrong"; they're focusing on different aspects of the same reality.
Time horizonsShort-run and long-run effects of policies are often different, sometimes opposite. Economists who weight short-run more reach different conclusions than those who weight long-run more.
Normative valuesHow to weight competing objectives (growth vs equality, present vs future, individual freedom vs collective welfare) is genuinely a value choice. Economists with different value weights reach different policy conclusions even when they agree on the empirical facts.

2.6 Interactive: Evaluating Empirical Claims

🎛 Interactive — Reading Empirical Claims Critically

Each tab presents an empirical claim. Identify the credibility issues.

Each tab below shows an empirical claim you might see in popular discussion. The hidden analysis identifies the credibility issues — correlation vs causation, sample selection, omitted variables, generalizability. Try to identify the issues yourself before reading.

The pattern: almost all real-world empirical claims face one or more credibility issues. The question is not whether the claim is "true" in some absolute sense — it's whether the available evidence supports a causal interpretation. Good empirical research takes these issues seriously; popular reporting usually doesn't.

2.7 Worked Examples

📐 Worked Example 2.1 — Identifying the Counterfactual
A study finds that students who attend an after-school tutoring program score 0.3 standard deviations higher on math tests than students who don't attend. The study concludes the tutoring program raises math achievement. (a) What's the implicit counterfactual? (b) What selection effects might be at work? (c) How would you design a study to identify the causal effect of tutoring more credibly?
1
(a) Implicit counterfactual: the study compares actual tutored students to actual non-tutored students. The implicit counterfactual is "what would the tutored students have scored without tutoring." Without random assignment, we don't observe this directly.
2
(b) Selection effects: students (and parents) who choose tutoring are different from those who don't in many ways: parental engagement, baseline ability, motivation, family resources, awareness of programs. These factors independently affect math scores. Some of the 0.3 SD difference is the tutoring effect; some is the selection effect of who chose to enroll.
3
(c) Better designs: (i) RCT: randomly assign students to tutoring vs control. The gold standard. (ii) Lottery: if the program is oversubscribed, compare students randomly selected via lottery vs not selected. (iii) Regression discontinuity: if eligibility is based on a test cutoff, compare students just below vs just above the cutoff. (iv) Difference-in-differences: compare tutored students' before-vs-after improvements to non-tutored students' improvements over the same period.
The general principle: for any causal claim about the effect of a policy or treatment, identify the counterfactual and ask how the data lets you observe it. If the answer is "we can't directly observe the counterfactual, but we compare similar groups," ask how the groups were selected. The more selection bias there is, the less the comparison reveals about the causal effect. This single question — "what's the counterfactual?" — has more analytical power than almost any other in empirical economics.
📐 Worked Example 2.2 — Designing a Natural Experiment
You want to estimate the effect of increased state spending on K-12 education on student achievement. Cross-state correlations are confounded (states with more spending also have higher incomes, different demographics, etc.). (a) What kind of natural experiment would help identify the causal effect? (b) Give two real examples of natural experiments in education economics. (c) What identification assumptions does your design require?
1
(a) Useful natural experiments: (i) State supreme court rulings forcing school finance reform that affected districts differently based on pre-existing wealth gaps. (ii) Sudden changes in formula-based funding that shifted resources unpredictably. (iii) Policy changes affecting specific cohorts but not others, allowing comparisons of "treated" and "untreated" cohorts.
2
(b) Real examples: (i) Jackson, Johnson, and Persico (2016) used the timing of court-mandated school finance reforms across states. They found 25% increases in per-pupil spending raised wages of affected cohorts by ~10% in adulthood. (ii) Lafortune, Rothstein, Schanzenbach (2018) used variation in state finance reforms to estimate the effects of $1,000 in additional per-pupil spending — found 0.1 SD improvement in test scores.
3
(c) Identification assumptions: (i) Parallel trends: in the absence of treatment, treated and untreated areas would have evolved similarly. Hard to test directly; usually justified by showing they were tracking similarly before treatment. (ii) No anticipation: districts didn't change behavior before the policy was implemented in anticipation. (iii) No spillovers: untreated districts weren't indirectly affected by the policy.
Why this matters: education spending is one of the most-studied causal questions in economics. The early literature (pre-1990) reached pessimistic conclusions because it relied on naive cross-state correlations and got confounded estimates. The post-2000 literature using natural experiments has found that money matters — but the design is everything. If you read "studies show that spending doesn't affect outcomes," that's largely from the pre-credibility-revolution literature. The credibly-identified post-2000 literature finds positive effects of well-spent money. This is exactly the kind of issue where knowing the methodology matters for knowing what to believe.
⚙ Conceptual Walkthrough — Difference-in-Differences
DiD estimate = (Y_treated_after − Y_treated_before) − (Y_control_after − Y_control_before)
Y_treated_after − Y_treated_before
How much the treated group's outcome changed after the policy.
Y_control_after − Y_control_before
How much the control group's outcome changed over the same time. Captures everything else happening in the world that's affecting both groups.
The difference
Subtracting the second from the first removes everything that affected both groups identically. What's left is (hopefully) the causal effect of the treatment on the treated group.
Key assumption
Parallel trends: in the absence of treatment, the two groups would have evolved similarly over time. Defended by showing they were on parallel pre-treatment paths.

Why DiD is so widely used: it doesn't require random assignment, but uses comparison across two dimensions (time and group) to subtract out confounding factors. Card-Krueger 1994 was a DiD design: difference in (New Jersey - Pennsylvania) employment, before vs after minimum wage increase. Difference-in-differences is now the workhorse method of policy evaluation in economics — used for evaluating everything from healthcare reform to minimum wage to trade liberalization.

🎮 MacroNations Connection — Empirical Reasoning in the Engine

The MacroNations engine is deterministic — given the same inputs, you get the same outputs. This is unlike real economies but useful pedagogically because it lets you isolate causal effects of lever choices. In one tournament round, change only tax_rate by 5pp and observe the effects: GDP gap shifts via the engine's policy-gap multiplier (1.4 for spending, −1.1 for taxes); debt accumulates differently; Gini moves through the tax_rate_gini coefficient. The engine effectively performs a "perfect natural experiment" for each lever change: holding everything else constant, what does this change cause? Real-world empirical economics never has this luxury. The credibility revolution has been about getting closer to this kind of "ceteris paribus" identification in real-world settings. One useful exercise: run two rounds with identical settings except for one lever, and compute the difference. You're doing a difference-in-differences with perfect control, which is the methodological ideal of empirical economics.

Chapter 2 Quiz

Empirical methods, credibility revolution, positive vs normative, sources of disagreement
Part A — Multiple Choice (Principles)
1. The "credibility revolution" in empirical economics refers to:
A The introduction of macroeconomics to undergraduate curricula
B The use of larger datasets
C The shift toward methods (RCTs, natural experiments, regression discontinuity, instrumental variables) that credibly identify causal effects
D The greater use of cross-country regressions
2. The 2021 Nobel Prize in Economics was awarded to David Card, Joshua Angrist, and Guido Imbens for:
A Discovering the Phillips curve
B Methodological contributions to causal analysis — natural experiments and related techniques
C Theoretical contributions to growth theory
D Discovery of behavioral biases
3. "Higher police presence is correlated with higher crime rates" is a classic example of:
A Confirmation bias
B Sample selection
C Reverse causation — cities deploy more police where crime is higher, so the police didn't cause the crime
D Statistical significance
4. The Card-Krueger 1994 minimum wage study used:
A A randomized controlled trial
B A natural experiment — comparing New Jersey (which raised its minimum wage) to Pennsylvania (which did not) using difference-in-differences
C A regression discontinuity
D A theoretical model only
Part B — Multiple Choice (Intermediate)
5. The key identification assumption for difference-in-differences (DiD) is:
A Random assignment of treatment
B Parallel trends — in the absence of treatment, treated and control groups would have evolved similarly
C Large sample size
D Linear functional form
6. Which of the following is the LEAST credible source of evidence for a causal effect?
A Well-designed RCT with random assignment
B Natural experiment with strong identification
C Regression discontinuity at a clear threshold
D Simple cross-country correlation
7. Why do economists sometimes disagree about policy even when they share the same data?
A Empirical uncertainty about parameter estimates
B Different models that emphasize different mechanisms
C Different normative values for weighting competing objectives
D All of the above
Part C — Short Answer
8. A friend says: "Studies show that countries with more economic freedom have faster growth." Explain why you'd want to know more before believing this is a causal claim.
Model answer: Several questions need answering. (1) Reverse causation: maybe faster growth causes economic freedom (richer countries can afford more freedom) rather than the other way. (2) Confounding: countries with more "economic freedom" tend to differ from those without in many ways — geography, history, education levels, institutional quality. Any of these could cause both freedom and growth, making the correlation spurious. (3) Measurement: how is "economic freedom" measured? Most indices include institutions that themselves correlate with growth for unrelated reasons. (4) Selection: the sample of countries available isn't random; the relationship in available data may not generalize. To make a causal claim, we'd need an identification strategy — a natural experiment, regression discontinuity, or strong instrumental variable. Cross-country correlations alone are highly suggestive but not causal.
9. A study claims that students who attend private schools score higher on college entrance exams than students at public schools, and concludes that private schools are better. Identify three credibility issues with this claim.
Model answer: (1) Selection: families who choose private school are different from those who don't — typically higher income, more education, more parental engagement. These factors independently affect exam scores. (2) Confounding: private school students also typically have access to more resources outside school (tutoring, study materials, college counselors). The exam difference may reflect these resources rather than the school itself. (3) Generalizability: the private school sample isn't random; the students who could afford private school are not representative. Even if the comparison shows private schools "cause" higher scores within this sample, the effect may not generalize. A more credible design: voucher lottery studies that randomly assign students to private vs public school. Existing voucher RCTs find more modest effects than naive comparisons suggest.
10. Write a 250-word reflection on the question: how would the way economists discuss policy be different if the public understood the methodology better? Be specific.
Model answer: Several things would change. First, ideological claims dressed in scientific language would have less rhetorical power. "Studies show X causes Y" is a frequent move in policy debate, but most studies establish correlations rather than causes. A methodologically informed public would ask "what's the identification strategy?" and "what's the counterfactual?" — questions that filter the credible from the cherry-picked. Second, the credibility revolution has updated many empirical findings since 1990. Some "settled" textbook claims (about minimum wages, about education spending, about deficit effects) have been revised by better methods. A public aware of this would treat older empirical claims with appropriate skepticism. Third, the public would distinguish empirical disagreement (about what would happen) from normative disagreement (about which outcome is better). Most policy debates conflate these. If we knew that economists across the political spectrum largely agree that carbon taxes work, that rent control reduces housing supply, that trade has aggregate benefits, the political debate would shift from "is this true?" to "given that this is true, what should we do?" — which is the more productive debate. Better methodology has produced better economics, but the gains haven't fully transmitted to public discourse. Bridging that gap is part of what economic education should do.
The live textbook auto-grades MCQs and stores progress.

References

Angrist, J. D. (1990). Lifetime earnings and the Vietnam era draft lottery. American Economic Review, 80(3), 313–336.

Angrist, J. D., & Lavy, V. (1999). Using Maimonides' rule to estimate the effect of class size on scholastic achievement. Quarterly Journal of Economics, 114(2), 533–575.

Angrist, J. D., & Pischke, J.-S. (2010). The credibility revolution in empirical economics. Journal of Economic Perspectives, 24(2), 3–30.

Banerjee, A. V., & Duflo, E. (2011). Poor Economics: A Radical Rethinking of the Way to Fight Global Poverty. PublicAffairs.

Card, D., & Krueger, A. B. (1994). Minimum wages and employment: A case study of the fast-food industry. American Economic Review, 84(4), 772–793.

Cengiz, D., Dube, A., Lindner, A., & Zipperer, B. (2019). The effect of minimum wages on low-wage jobs. Quarterly Journal of Economics, 134(3), 1405–1454.

Friedman, M. (1953). The methodology of positive economics. In Essays in Positive Economics. University of Chicago Press.

Imbens, G. W., & Rubin, D. B. (2015). Causal Inference for Statistics, Social, and Biomedical Sciences. Cambridge University Press.

Jackson, C. K., Johnson, R. C., & Persico, C. (2016). The effects of school spending on educational and economic outcomes. Quarterly Journal of Economics, 131(1), 157–218.

Lafortune, J., Rothstein, J., & Schanzenbach, D. W. (2018). School finance reform and the distribution of student achievement. American Economic Journal: Applied Economics, 10(2), 1–26.

Manski, C. F. (1995). Identification Problems in the Social Sciences. Harvard University Press.

Chapter 3 · Part I: Introduction

Trade, Comparative Advantage, and the Distributional Question

Why specialization produces gains, how Ricardo's comparative advantage works mathematically, why those gains aren't distributed evenly, what trade policy instruments actually do, and what the empirical evidence of the past 30 years has taught us

In 1817, David Ricardo published an example that has dominated economic thinking about trade ever since. Portugal could produce both wine and cloth more efficiently than England — fewer hours of labor for each unit of either good. By the logic of common sense, Portugal should produce both and have no need to trade. Ricardo's insight: even when one country is better at everything, both countries gain from trade if their relative productivity differs. Portugal should specialize in wine (where its advantage is largest) and import cloth from England, while England should specialize in cloth (where its disadvantage is smallest). Both countries end up with more wine and more cloth than if each tried to produce both. This is comparative advantage, and it remains one of the most powerful arguments in economics: trade creates value through specialization, even when one party is more productive at everything.

This chapter develops the comparative advantage argument carefully, then complicates it with what 30 years of empirical work has taught us: that the gains from trade are real but unevenly distributed, that specific groups consistently bear concentrated costs from trade exposure, that trade policy is therefore more politically contested than the aggregate efficiency argument alone suggests. By the end, you should understand both why trade has been one of the most consistent engines of prosperity in modern history and why political opposition to trade liberalization has grown across the developed world.

✦ Sharma's Take — Why I teach trade this way

Most introductory textbooks present comparative advantage as a clean and complete argument for free trade. The presentation is technically correct but pedagogically misleading because it omits the most contested part of the contemporary debate: how the gains and losses are distributed. An economics student who learns Ricardo's argument and stops has the analytical equipment of 1817 — they can derive the aggregate gains from trade, but they cannot explain why so many people in 2026 oppose trade liberalization despite the aggregate gains being real.

The fuller story includes both the aggregate gains (Ricardo's contribution) and the distributional consequences (the Stolper-Samuelson theorem of 1941, the China Shock literature of 2013-2021, and the political-economy work showing that compensation rarely actually flows from winners to losers). If you can hold both parts of the story in mind simultaneously — the aggregate gains are real AND the distributional losses are real AND the losers don't typically get compensated — you understand modern trade economics. If you can only hold one part, you'll find yourself surprised by political developments you should have predicted.

— A.S.

3.1 Absolute vs Comparative Advantage

📘 Principles Level — Building intuition

The simplest way to see comparative advantage is to start with a stylized two-country, two-good example. Imagine two countries: Avianna and Bovinia. Each can produce wheat or cars. Workers in each country have different productivity:

CountryWheat (bushels/worker)Cars (cars/worker)
Avianna10020
Bovinia6030

Avianna has absolute advantage in wheat (100 > 60). Bovinia has absolute advantage in cars (30 > 20). For each country, the opportunity cost of one good in terms of the other is the key:

  • In Avianna, producing 1 car requires foregoing 100/20 = 5 bushels of wheat. So 1 Avianna car = 5 Avianna wheat bushels.
  • In Bovinia, producing 1 car requires foregoing 60/30 = 2 bushels of wheat. So 1 Bovinia car = 2 Bovinia wheat bushels.

Bovinia has comparative advantage in cars (its opportunity cost is lower — 2 instead of 5). Avianna has comparative advantage in wheat (its opportunity cost is lower — 1/5 of a car per wheat bushel, vs Bovinia's 1/2 a car per wheat bushel). Both countries can benefit from specializing in their comparative-advantage good and trading.

⚙ Equation Walkthrough — Computing the Gains from Trade
Country A's gain = (post-trade consumption) − (no-trade consumption)
Total world gain = sum of country gains = expansion of production possibilities
No-trade equilibrium
