Each country produces and consumes according to its own resources. If Avianna has 10 workers and divides time 50/50 between wheat and cars, it produces 500 wheat and 100 cars, and that's what it consumes.
Specialization
Each country shifts production toward its comparative-advantage good. If Avianna fully specializes in wheat with 10 workers, it produces 1,000 wheat. Bovinia with 10 workers fully specializing in cars produces 300 cars.
Trade
At any trade ratio between the two countries' opportunity costs (1:2 to 1:5 wheat per car), both countries gain. Suppose 1 car = 3 bushels. Avianna sends 300 wheat, receives 100 cars. Bovinia sends 100 cars, receives 300 wheat.
Post-trade consumption
Avianna: 700 wheat + 100 cars (vs 500+100 without trade) — gain of 200 wheat. Bovinia: 300 wheat + 200 cars (vs the 60*5 + 30*5 = 300+150 from balanced production, so gain of 50 cars). Both countries strictly better off.

The deeper insight: the gains from trade aren't magic. They come from concentrating each country's labor on its higher-productivity activity. Avianna giving up 5 wheat to free a worker for car production is wasteful when that worker only produces 20 cars — but if it could trade for cars at 2 or 3 wheat each, Avianna effectively "produces" cars more efficiently through trade than through domestic production. Trade is a technology — it converts wheat into cars at a better rate than the actual production process would.

⚠ Common Pitfall — "Cheap labor" arguments against trade

A common argument against trade with developing countries: "Their wages are so low we can't compete." This sounds intuitive but misunderstands comparative advantage. Wages reflect productivity, not just the cost of labor. A U.S. textile worker is paid more than a Bangladeshi textile worker because the U.S. worker uses more capital, technology, and supporting infrastructure. The U.S. worker is more productive per hour. Whether the U.S. should produce textiles depends on whether U.S. relative productivity in textiles (vs other goods) exceeds Bangladesh's relative productivity in textiles. If not, the U.S. has comparative disadvantage in textiles regardless of the wage differential.

The "cheap labor" framing also gets the question backwards. Trade with low-wage countries can hurt specific U.S. workers in directly-competing industries (the China Shock literature is precisely about this). But the framing implies that the U.S. should somehow "compete" with Bangladeshi wages — which would require massive U.S. wage reductions and is economically nonsensical. The right framing is: which industries should the U.S. specialize in, and how do we manage the adjustment for workers in industries we should not specialize in? That's a much more useful question than "should we accept the wage differential," which presumes the answer.

3.2 The Sources of Comparative Advantage

Where does comparative advantage come from? Why is Avianna more relatively productive in wheat and Bovinia more relatively productive in cars? Economic theory identifies four major sources:

Four sources of comparative advantage
SourceMechanismExample
1. Factor endowmentsCountries with more capital relative to labor have comparative advantage in capital-intensive goods. Heckscher-Ohlin model (1933).Saudi Arabia's oil; the U.S.'s capital-intensive manufacturing; Bangladesh's labor-intensive garments.
2. TechnologyCountries that innovate in particular technologies have comparative advantage in goods using those technologies. Ricardian model emphasized this.Germany's machine tools; Switzerland's pharmaceuticals; U.S.'s software and biotech.
3. Scale economiesCountries that get an early start in an industry develop expertise, supply chains, and skilled workers that make them more productive. New Trade Theory (Krugman 1979).Detroit's auto industry; Silicon Valley's tech industry; Taiwan's semiconductors.
4. InstitutionsCountries with effective property rights, contract enforcement, and labor markets are more productive across many industries. Acemoglu-Robinson.Most developed economies have advantage in high-trust, complex contracts (financial services, R&D).
✦ Sharma's Take — Why this matters for policy

The traditional textbook treats comparative advantage as exogenous — countries have what they have, and trade simply lets them specialize. The richer modern view recognizes that comparative advantage is partly endogenous: countries can build it through deliberate investment in technology, infrastructure, education, and institutions. Singapore in 1965 had no obvious comparative advantage in anything. By 2025 it has comparative advantage in financial services, pharmaceuticals, and high-tech manufacturing. This wasn't predetermined — it was built through 60 years of policy choices about education, infrastructure, and industrial development.

This has policy implications. The pure "let comparative advantage emerge" view of trade implies that government should largely stay out of trade and industrial policy. The "comparative advantage can be built" view implies a more activist role — investing in capabilities that may pay off as future comparative advantages. Both views have empirical support. Pure laissez-faire has worked in some cases (Hong Kong); active industrial policy has worked in others (Korea, Singapore, Taiwan). The question of which approach works when is genuinely contested. The recent return of industrial policy in the U.S. (CHIPS Act, IRA) reflects renewed confidence that comparative advantage can be built. Whether that confidence is well-placed will become clear over the next decade.

— A.S.

3.3 The Distributional Question: Who Wins and Who Loses

📗 Intermediate Level — Stolper-Samuelson

The Stolper-Samuelson theorem (1941) formalized when trade hurts which groups. The intuition: when a country opens to trade, the goods it produces with its abundant factor (capital in the U.S., labor in Bangladesh) gets exported, raising demand for that factor; the goods produced with the scarce factor (labor in the U.S., capital in Bangladesh) get imported, reducing demand for that factor. The owners of the scarce factor lose, even after accounting for the gains from lower import prices.

Applied to the U.S.: the U.S. is capital-abundant relative to most trade partners. Trade with labor-abundant countries (China, Mexico, Bangladesh) raises the relative return to U.S. capital and reduces the relative return to U.S. less-skilled labor. The Stolper-Samuelson theorem predicted, decades before the empirical evidence, that increasing U.S. trade with developing countries would reduce wages for less-skilled U.S. workers. The empirical evidence of the past 25 years has largely confirmed this prediction.

1994-2023 North America NAFTA Aftermath

NAFTA: 30 Years of Empirical Evidence

The North American Free Trade Agreement took effect in January 1994, eliminating most tariffs and trade barriers between the U.S., Canada, and Mexico over a phased period. Now 30+ years on, the empirical record allows us to evaluate what actually happened against the predictions made at the time.

Aggregate effects: trade between the three countries roughly tripled. North American supply chains became deeply integrated — by 2010, roughly 40% of imports from Mexico contained U.S. components, and roughly 25% of imports from Canada did. U.S. consumers saw real benefits from lower prices on automotive, agricultural, and manufactured goods. Mexico's per-capita GDP grew approximately 30% in real terms over the NAFTA era — a meaningful gain but well below the average growth rate of the rest of Latin America during the same period and far short of the rapid convergence with U.S. living standards that NAFTA's proponents had hoped for (Weisbrot et al. 2017), with the export sector being a major engine.

Distributional effects: U.S. manufacturing employment fell from about 17 million in 1994 to a low of 11.5 million in early 2010, recovering to about 12.6 million by end-2024. Specific Midwest manufacturing communities — Detroit, Cleveland, Buffalo, parts of Pennsylvania — experienced concentrated job losses. Real wages for U.S. workers without college degrees stagnated or fell over the NAFTA era, though distinguishing trade effects from technology effects is empirically difficult. Mexican agricultural workers, particularly small corn farmers, also experienced losses as cheaper U.S. corn flooded Mexican markets after tariff removal.

The political consequences became clear by 2016. Both major U.S. presidential candidates that year campaigned against NAFTA. The Trump administration renegotiated NAFTA into the USMCA in 2018-2020, with marginal changes. The Biden and Trump administrations from 2021-2026 have maintained trade restrictions on China and largely abandoned new free trade agreements. The empirical record on NAFTA supports both the aggregate gains argument and the distributional losses argument — and the political economy of those losses has driven 30 years of U.S. trade policy in directions the original NAFTA architects would not have predicted.

3.4 Trade Policy Instruments

Governments have a variety of tools for affecting international trade. Each works through different mechanisms and has different effects on prices, quantities, and the distribution of gains and losses.

Trade policy instruments and their effects
InstrumentHow it worksDistribution of effects
TariffTax on imports. Raises domestic price of imported goods. Domestic producers compete against the artificially-higher import price. Government collects tariff revenue.Domestic producers and government win; domestic consumers and foreign exporters lose.
QuotaQuantitative limit on imports. Imports become scarce; domestic prices rise. Quota holders (importers) capture quota rents.Domestic producers and quota holders win; consumers lose.
Voluntary export restraint (VER)Exporting country "voluntarily" limits exports. Same effect as quota but rents accrue to foreign exporters.Domestic producers and foreign exporters win; domestic consumers lose. Worst form of protection for the importing country.
Export subsidyGovernment pays domestic producers to export. Raises domestic price and lowers foreign price.Domestic producers and foreign consumers win; domestic consumers and taxpayers lose.
Non-tariff barriersRegulatory requirements, certification, technical standards that impede imports. Often hidden protection.Effects vary; usually opaque.
Industrial subsidiesGovernment supports specific domestic industries (CHIPS Act for semiconductors, IRA for clean energy).Subsidized industries and their workers win; taxpayers and competing industries lose.
✦ Sharma's Take — When tariffs make sense and when they don't

The standard economics textbook position on tariffs is hostile: tariffs reduce aggregate welfare in the importing country, distort prices, invite retaliation, and benefit specific producers at the expense of broader consumers. This is largely correct as an aggregate statement. But it's incomplete as a policy guide because it ignores three considerations: (1) the distributional question already discussed, (2) national security and strategic concerns (we may not want to depend on a hostile country for critical components), and (3) infant industry arguments (sometimes domestic industries need temporary protection to develop scale).

My read on tariffs: broad, indiscriminate tariffs (like 10% tariffs on all imports) are almost always net welfare-reducing. Targeted tariffs (like protection for industries with national security implications or in industries where domestic scale economies matter) sometimes pass cost-benefit. The current U.S. mix of tariffs is somewhere in between, with a clear shift toward more protection since 2018 across both administrations. The empirical question is whether the political economy of tariffs — once erected, they're hard to remove because protected industries lobby to keep them — outweighs the targeted benefits. Historical experience suggests the political stickiness usually dominates. Once you build a tariff on steel for national security reasons, the steel industry will fight to keep it long after the security concern has faded.

— A.S.

3.5 Interactive: Comparative Advantage Calculator

🎛 Interactive — Comparative Advantage and Gains from Trade

Set each country's productivity; see who has comparative advantage and total gains from trade

Two countries, two goods. Drag the productivity sliders to change each country's output per worker for each good. The interactive computes opportunity costs, identifies comparative advantage, and calculates gains from trade under optimal specialization.

Notice: the chart shows production possibility frontiers (PPFs) for both countries. The blue PPF is Country A; the red is Country B. The slopes tell you opportunity costs. Different slopes mean different comparative advantages, and gains from trade exist. Identical slopes (try the "symmetric" preset) mean no comparative advantage and no gains from trade.

3.6 Worked Examples

📐 Worked Example 3.1 — Computing Comparative Advantage
Country X produces 80 bushels of wheat or 40 cars per worker. Country Y produces 30 bushels of wheat or 20 cars per worker. (a) Compute each country's opportunity cost of producing one car (in wheat). (b) Which country has comparative advantage in cars? (c) At what trade ratio (wheat per car) would both countries gain from trade? (d) Suppose each country has 100 workers and currently produces both goods with workers split 50/50. Compute total world production. Then compute total world production if each country fully specializes in its comparative-advantage good. Show the world gains from trade.
1
(a) Opportunity costs: Country X: 1 car = 80/40 = 2 bushels of wheat. Country Y: 1 car = 30/20 = 1.5 bushels of wheat.
2
(b) Comparative advantage in cars: Country Y has lower opportunity cost (1.5 < 2), so Country Y has comparative advantage in cars. Country X has comparative advantage in wheat.
3
(c) Mutually beneficial trade ratio: any price between 1.5 and 2 wheat per car. Country Y's domestic ratio is 1.5, so it won't accept fewer than 1.5 wheat per car. Country X's domestic ratio is 2, so it won't pay more than 2 wheat per car. Trade ratio range: 1.5 < price < 2 wheat per car.
4
(d) No trade: Country X (50 wheat workers + 50 car workers) produces 50×80 = 4,000 wheat and 50×40 = 2,000 cars. Country Y produces 50×30 = 1,500 wheat and 50×20 = 1,000 cars. World total: 5,500 wheat, 3,000 cars.
5
(d) Full specialization: Country X fully specializes in wheat: 100×80 = 8,000 wheat, 0 cars. Country Y fully specializes in cars: 0 wheat, 100×20 = 2,000 cars. World total: 8,000 wheat, 2,000 cars. Compared to no trade: +2,500 wheat, −1,000 cars.
The trade-off: full specialization gives the world 2,500 more wheat but 1,000 fewer cars compared to no trade. At any trade ratio in the [1.5, 2] range, both countries strictly prefer the trading outcome to autarky. Notice that "more of everything" is not guaranteed at full specialization — what's guaranteed is that the production possibility frontier of the world has expanded, and at the right trade ratio both countries can consume more of both goods than they could without trade. The gain is the expansion of production possibilities, not literally "more of each good produced". Partial specialization (each country produces some of both) often allows both countries to consume more of both goods.
📐 Worked Example 3.2 — Tariff Welfare Analysis
The U.S. imports steel from abroad. Without tariff, the world price is $700/ton; U.S. consumers buy 100M tons (60M from imports, 40M from domestic producers). The U.S. imposes a 20% tariff, raising the U.S. price to $840/ton. After the tariff: U.S. consumers buy 90M tons (40M from imports, 50M from domestic producers). (a) Compute the gain to U.S. domestic producers. (b) Compute the loss to U.S. consumers. (c) Compute tariff revenue. (d) What's the net welfare effect for the U.S.?
1
(a) Producer surplus gain: domestic producers were getting $700/ton on 40M tons. Now they get $840/ton on 50M tons. Gain ≈ ($840−$700) × (40M + 50M)/2 = $140 × 45M = $6.3 billion. (Trapezoid area on supply curve.)
2
(b) Consumer surplus loss: consumers were paying $700 on 100M tons. Now they pay $840 on 90M tons. Loss ≈ ($840−$700) × (100M + 90M)/2 = $140 × 95M = $13.3 billion. (Trapezoid area on demand curve.)
3
(c) Tariff revenue: tariff is $140/ton × 40M imported tons = $5.6 billion.
4
(d) Net U.S. welfare: Producer gain + tariff revenue − consumer loss = $6.3B + $5.6B − $13.3B = −$1.4 billion. The tariff is net welfare-reducing for the U.S. by about $1.4B per year — the deadweight loss.
What the textbook arithmetic shows: a tariff has well-defined winners (domestic producers, government) and losers (consumers). The aggregate welfare effect for the importing country is negative — there's deadweight loss. But the loss is small relative to the gross transfers. The political economy of tariffs lives in this gap: small aggregate losses, large gains for concentrated and politically-active producers, costs spread thinly across millions of consumers. The producers lobby for tariffs; consumers rarely lobby against them. This is why tariffs tend to persist even when economists can show they reduce aggregate welfare. The Trump tariffs on steel and aluminum (2018) cost U.S. consumers and downstream industries an estimated $1.5-3 billion per year, with gains to the protected industries of about $500-700 million — exactly this pattern.
🎮 MacroNations Connection — Trade in the Engine

The MacroNations engine implements trade through the trade_openness lever (range 0-100, default 65). Higher openness raises productivity (+0.6 per pp above 65 per round) but with diminishing returns above 80. Trade openness also affects the engine's currency dynamics: countries with high trade openness face larger currency moves when there are global shocks. There's no separate "tariff" lever — increases in trade_openness model trade liberalization; decreases model tariff erection. The engine doesn't separately track distributional effects of trade (no division of citizens into "trade winners" and "trade losers") — but the gini state variable rises modestly when trade_openness increases rapidly, reflecting the empirical evidence that rapid trade liberalization tends to widen inequality without compensating institutions. Tournament strategy: maintain trade_openness in the 65-85 band for steady productivity gains. Moving below 50 forfeits substantial productivity. Moving above 90 invites currency volatility without much additional productivity benefit.

Chapter 3 Quiz

Absolute vs comparative advantage, sources of advantage, distributional effects, trade policy instruments, NAFTA case
Part A — Multiple Choice (Principles)
1. A country has comparative advantage in producing a good if:
A It can produce the good with fewer hours of labor than other countries
B It has more workers in that industry
C Its opportunity cost (in foregone production of other goods) is lower than other countries' opportunity costs for that good
D It has natural resources to produce the good
2. The "cheap labor" argument against trade with developing countries fails because:
A Developing country labor isn't actually cheap
B The developing countries don't actually trade
C Wages reflect productivity, not just labor cost; comparative advantage operates on relative productivity, not on absolute wage differentials
D Tariffs make this concern moot
3. The Stolper-Samuelson theorem predicts:
A Free trade benefits everyone in both countries
B Free trade lowers the relative return to a country's scarce factor — meaning owners of the scarce factor lose from trade even after lower import prices are accounted for
C All countries benefit from tariffs
D Comparative advantage is irrelevant in modern trade
4. A tariff on imported steel in the U.S. is paid primarily by:
A Foreign exporters only
B The U.S. government
C U.S. consumers of steel and steel-using industries through higher prices
D U.S. taxpayers in general
Part B — Multiple Choice (Intermediate)
5. The "China Shock" research found that:
A Chinese imports had no measurable effect on U.S. workers
B Trade with China produced no aggregate gains for the U.S.
C Trade with China produced modest aggregate gains and large, persistent local losses in specific manufacturing communities
D Chinese workers experienced no gains from trade
6. An export subsidy (where the government pays domestic producers to export):
A Benefits everyone
B Has no effect on prices
C Benefits domestic producers and foreign consumers; harms domestic consumers and taxpayers
D Is identical to a tariff in its effects
7. Singapore's economic transformation since 1965 illustrates that:
A Comparative advantage is always given exogenously
B Trade is always harmful
C Countries can build comparative advantage through deliberate policy investment in education, infrastructure, and institutions
D Free trade always fails in small economies
Part C — Short Answer
8. Country X produces 50 wheat or 25 cars per worker. Country Y produces 40 wheat or 30 cars per worker. (a) Identify each country's comparative advantage. (b) At what trade ratio would both countries gain from trade? (c) Explain why both countries can be better off through trade even though Country X is absolutely better at producing wheat.
Model answer: (a) Opportunity costs: X: 1 car = 50/25 = 2 wheat; Y: 1 car = 40/30 = 1.33 wheat. Y has lower opportunity cost for cars, so Y has comparative advantage in cars. X has comparative advantage in wheat. (b) Trade ratio range: 1.33 < wheat per car < 2. At any ratio in this range, both countries strictly prefer trade. (c) Even though X is absolutely better at wheat (50 vs 40), X gains by specializing in wheat and importing cars at any ratio above 1.33 (Y's domestic ratio) and below 2 (X's domestic ratio). The gain isn't from "doing what you're best at" in absolute terms — it's from doing what you're relatively best at and letting your trading partner do what they're relatively best at.
9. Explain why, even when trade produces aggregate gains, political opposition to trade liberalization can be substantial and rational. Use the NAFTA evidence to illustrate.
Model answer: The aggregate gains from trade are real but small (NAFTA produced perhaps 0.5% additional U.S. GDP over 30 years). The distributional losses are real and large for specific groups (NAFTA contributed to manufacturing job losses concentrated in Midwest communities; the China Shock did similar). For the median U.S. worker, trade benefits might be $1,000-2,000/year in lower consumer prices. For a displaced manufacturing worker, losses might be $20,000-40,000/year in lost wages plus reduced lifetime employment prospects. The aggregate gain ($billions) is small relative to per-capita losses for the affected (large fraction of lifetime earnings). Trade Adjustment Assistance and similar programs have been inadequate. So the political opposition isn't ignorance — it's rational response by people whose losses are real and uncompensated. Sustaining political support for trade requires either much larger compensation mechanisms (which U.S. politics has been unable to deliver) or accepting reduced trade liberalization (which is the path the U.S. has taken since 2016).
10. The U.S. is considering imposing a 25% tariff on Chinese semiconductors, citing national security concerns. Write a 250-word memo evaluating this proposal. Consider aggregate welfare, distributional effects, security considerations, and likely retaliation.
Model answer (sketch): Aggregate welfare: a 25% semiconductor tariff would impose deadweight loss of perhaps $5-15B/year on the U.S., depending on substitution elasticities. Some loss would be offset by tariff revenue collected, but U.S. consumers and downstream industries would pay higher prices. Distribution: U.S. semiconductor producers (Intel, Micron, AMD) would gain; downstream U.S. industries (consumer electronics, data centers, automotive) and consumers would lose. The losers would significantly outnumber the winners in absolute terms but losses would be diffuse. National security: this is the strongest argument for the tariff. Modern semiconductors are critical to defense systems. Dependence on Chinese supply creates strategic vulnerability. A targeted tariff combined with the CHIPS Act could plausibly build U.S. domestic capacity that's strategically valuable. Retaliation: China would likely retaliate with tariffs on U.S. exports (agriculture, services, aircraft). Estimating the cost is hard but historical experience suggests 50-100% retaliation magnitudes are typical. Recommendation: support narrow tariff on the most-strategic semiconductor categories, paired with industrial policy (CHIPS Act funding for domestic manufacturing) and clear sunset provisions to prevent permanent protection. Avoid blanket 25% on all semiconductors — too broad, too costly to consumers, too disruptive to global supply chains. A narrow, time-limited, security-justified tariff is defensible; a broad indiscriminate one is not.
The live textbook auto-grades MCQs and stores progress.

References

Autor, D. H., Dorn, D., & Hanson, G. H. (2013). The China syndrome: Local labor market effects of import competition in the United States. American Economic Review, 103(6), 2121–2168.

Bown, C. P. (2021). The US-China trade war and Phase One agreement. Journal of Policy Modeling, 43(4), 805–843.

Caliendo, L., & Parro, F. (2015). Estimates of the trade and welfare effects of NAFTA. Review of Economic Studies, 82(1), 1–44.

Hanson, G. H. (2021). Can trade work for workers? Foreign Affairs, May/June 2021.

Heckscher, E. F. (1919). The effect of foreign trade on the distribution of income. Ekonomisk Tidskrift, 21, 497–512.

Krugman, P. R. (1979). Increasing returns, monopolistic competition, and international trade. Journal of International Economics, 9(4), 469–479.

Ohlin, B. (1933). Interregional and International Trade. Harvard University Press.

Ricardo, D. (1817). On the Principles of Political Economy and Taxation. John Murray.

Rodrik, D. (2018). Straight Talk on Trade: Ideas for a Sane World Economy. Princeton University Press.

Stolper, W. F., & Samuelson, P. A. (1941). Protection and real wages. Review of Economic Studies, 9(1), 58–73.

Chapter 4 · Part II: Markets & Prices

Markets: Where Prices Come From

How supply and demand interact to produce prices, why prices aggregate dispersed information in ways no central planner could match, and where the standard model misses important features — particularly information asymmetries, search frictions, and the role of expectations

In November 2025, the average asking rent for a one-bedroom apartment in Manhattan reached $4,650 — roughly 70% above 2010 levels in inflation-adjusted terms. The average asking rent for a similar apartment in Buffalo, four hundred miles away, was $1,100 — barely above 2010 levels. Both numbers were generated by the same basic process: thousands of landlords setting prices, thousands of renters making decisions, the prices adjusting until the number of apartments offered roughly matched the number of renters willing to pay. No central authority set either rent. No committee debated whether $4,650 was the "right" Manhattan price. The price emerged from the decentralized interaction of buyers and sellers. This is what markets do, and understanding how they do it is the central project of microeconomics.

This chapter develops the supply-and-demand framework that has been the workhorse of price analysis for 150 years. It also identifies, more carefully than most introductory treatments, where the framework misses important features of real markets: when information is asymmetric (sellers know more than buyers), when search is costly (you can't sample every option), when prices reflect expectations rather than current fundamentals (the housing market), when network effects produce winner-take-all dynamics (platform markets). By the end you should be able to use supply-and-demand fluently for the cases where it works well and recognize the cases where it doesn't.

✦ Sharma's Take — What the textbook treatment leaves out

The classical supply-and-demand framework is one of the most useful tools in economics. It explains an enormous range of phenomena — why gas prices rise after hurricanes, why concert tickets for popular artists sell out, why housing in productive cities is expensive, why agricultural commodities are volatile. Within the assumptions it makes, the framework is rigorous and predictive. The danger is treating it as more universal than it actually is. The framework assumes well-informed buyers and sellers, frictionless transactions, homogeneous products, no search costs, and prices that adjust to clear markets continuously. None of those assumptions holds perfectly anywhere; many of them fail badly in important real markets.

The healthcare market doesn't look like the textbook market — patients can't usually see prices, can't comparison-shop, often don't know what treatment they need. The labor market doesn't clear like the textbook says it should — unemployment exists even at "equilibrium" because matching workers to jobs takes time and information. The housing market is partly speculative — prices reflect expected future appreciation, not just current supply and demand. The platform markets of the internet age have winner-take-all dynamics no 1890s economist could have imagined. The supply-and-demand framework is the foundation, not the whole structure. You'll need it everywhere, but you'll need extensions and exceptions for many of the markets that matter most in modern economies.

— A.S.

4.1 What a Market Is (And Isn't)

📘 Principles Level — Defining "market"

A market is any arrangement that brings together buyers and sellers of a good or service. Markets can be highly organized (the New York Stock Exchange) or completely informal (a neighborhood babysitting network). They can be physical (a farmers' market) or virtual (Amazon, eBay). They can be local (Manhattan rentals) or global (oil). What unites them is the basic structure: parties on the buying side, parties on the selling side, some mechanism for them to find each other and agree on terms.

A competitive market is one where many buyers and many sellers transact, no individual buyer or seller has significant influence over the price, and goods are sufficiently similar that they're treated as interchangeable. The classic example: wheat futures. There are thousands of buyers and sellers, no single farmer or trader sets the price, all wheat of a given grade is essentially identical. Most introductory textbook analysis assumes competitive markets because that's where supply-and-demand reasoning is most accurate. Many real markets aren't perfectly competitive but are "competitive enough" that the framework gives useful predictions.

4.2 Demand: How Quantity Demanded Varies with Price

📘 Principles Level — The demand curve

The quantity demanded at a given price is the amount of a good that buyers are willing and able to purchase at that price. The general empirical pattern, well-established across thousands of studies and centuries of observation: as the price of a good rises, the quantity demanded falls. This is the law of demand. The relationship plotted with price on the vertical axis and quantity on the horizontal axis gives a downward-sloping demand curve.

Why does demand slope downward? Two mechanisms reinforce each other. First, the substitution effect: when a good becomes more expensive relative to alternatives, buyers substitute toward those alternatives. When beef gets expensive, more people eat chicken. Second, the income effect: a higher price reduces buyers' real purchasing power, so they buy less of all goods including the one whose price rose. Combined, these effects produce the downward slope.

Movements along the demand curve happen when the good's own price changes. Shifts of the entire demand curve happen when other factors change — income (higher income generally shifts demand right for "normal" goods), prices of related goods (substitutes and complements), preferences, expectations about future prices, the number of buyers in the market.

⚙ Equation Walkthrough — Linear Demand
Qd = a − b·P + c·Y + d·Prelated
where Qd = quantity demanded, P = own price, Y = income, Prelated = price of related good
a
Choke price intercept — the quantity demanded if price is zero and other factors are at baseline.
−b·P
Own-price effect. b > 0 for normal goods. Higher b means demand is more responsive to price changes (more elastic).
+c·Y
Income effect. c > 0 for normal goods, c < 0 for inferior goods (instant noodles get bought less as income rises).
±d·Prelated
d > 0 if substitute (higher coffee price → more tea demanded). d < 0 if complement (higher car prices → less gasoline demanded).

How econometricians actually estimate these: the challenge is that price and quantity are both determined endogenously by the market — they move together as supply and demand both shift. To estimate the demand curve specifically, you need exogenous variation in supply (so price moves while demand doesn't) and exogenous variation in demand (so quantity moves while supply doesn't). This is one of the oldest identification problems in econometrics, solved (partially) by instrumental variables that shift one curve without shifting the other.

4.3 Supply: How Quantity Supplied Varies with Price

📘 Principles Level — The supply curve

The quantity supplied at a given price is the amount of a good that sellers are willing and able to produce and offer at that price. The general empirical pattern: as price rises, quantity supplied rises. This is the law of supply. Plotted as price vs quantity, the relationship gives an upward-sloping supply curve.

Why does supply slope upward? Because producing more units typically requires higher costs — using less efficient resources, paying overtime wages, working with lower-quality inputs. Producers need a higher price to make additional units worthwhile. At very low prices only the most efficient producers will operate; as price rises, less efficient producers find it profitable to enter, expanding total supply.

Like demand, supply curves shift in response to factors other than the good's own price. Production costs (input prices, wages, energy costs), technology, regulations, the number of sellers, and expectations all shift the supply curve. A drop in fuel prices reduces airline operating costs and shifts the airline supply curve rightward — more flights at any given ticket price.

4.4 Equilibrium: Where Supply Meets Demand

The intersection of supply and demand defines the market equilibrium — the price at which quantity demanded equals quantity supplied. At this price, every buyer willing to pay finds a seller; every seller willing to produce finds a buyer. There's no shortage and no surplus.

Mechanically: if the actual price is above equilibrium, quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded — a surplus develops, and sellers compete by lowering prices to clear their inventory. If the actual price is below equilibrium, quantity demanded exceeds supplied — a shortage develops, and buyers compete by bidding prices up. In both cases, price adjusts toward equilibrium. This dynamic is what gives markets their self-organizing character. No one consciously sets the equilibrium price; it emerges from the interactions of decentralized actors trying to make their best individual decisions.

2020-2022 Global Used Car Market

How the Pandemic Reshaped the Used Car Market

In normal times, used car prices in the U.S. decline gently as new cars accumulate miles and wear. The Manheim Used Vehicle Value Index, which tracks wholesale used car prices, declined about 1-2% per year from 2010 to 2019. Then the pandemic hit. New car production collapsed in 2020-21 because of semiconductor shortages — auto manufacturers couldn't get the chips needed to complete vehicles. Rental car companies, expecting depressed travel demand, sold off about 700,000 vehicles into the used car market in early 2020. Then travel demand recovered faster than expected, and rental companies needed to rebuild fleets at the exact moment new car supply was constrained.

The result: the Manheim index rose roughly 80% from January 2020 to its January 2022 peak — the largest two-year increase since the index began. A three-year-old Toyota Camry that would have sold for $18,000 in January 2020 was selling for $27,000 by early 2022. This was the supply-and-demand framework doing exactly what the textbook predicts: a supply shock (constrained new car production) combined with a demand shock (rental companies rebuilding) pushed prices up dramatically until quantity supplied equaled quantity demanded. By 2024, as new car production normalized, used car prices had retreated about 20% from their peak. By late 2025, they were still about 35% above pre-pandemic levels, reflecting the lasting effect of the 2020-21 supply disruption on the age distribution of the U.S. vehicle fleet.

The lesson: supply-and-demand reasoning predicted the direction of the move (prices up) immediately when the shocks were identified. The magnitude was harder to predict because elasticities are difficult to measure precisely. But the qualitative framework worked. This is what the supply-and-demand framework gets right — directional predictions are reliable; precise magnitude predictions usually aren't.

⚠ Common Pitfall — Confusing movements along curves with shifts of curves

Suppose the price of beef rises 20% and quantity demanded of beef falls 8%. Did the demand curve shift? No — this is a movement along the demand curve. The demand curve hasn't changed; we just moved to a different point on the same curve as price changed. A shift of the demand curve would require a change in something other than beef's own price — say, a change in income, in chicken prices, in consumer preferences, in expectations about future beef prices.

This distinction matters because it determines what we call the cause. If beef demand fell because of a beef recall (consumer preferences changed), that's a leftward shift of the demand curve, and equilibrium price would fall. If beef demand fell because beef prices rose, that's a movement along an unchanged demand curve, and the price increase was caused by something else (perhaps a supply contraction). The general principle: changes in own-price cause movements along the curve. Changes in other factors cause shifts of the curve. Confusing the two leads to circular reasoning where the explanation for a price change is the price change itself.

4.5 Information Failures: Where the Textbook Model Breaks Down

📗 Intermediate Level — Asymmetric information

The classical supply-and-demand model assumes both sides of the market have full information about the goods being traded. In many real markets, this assumption fails badly — one side knows substantially more than the other. The economic consequences can be dramatic.

The canonical example is George Akerlof's 1970 "Market for Lemons" paper, which earned him a share of the 2001 Nobel Prize. Consider a used car market where sellers know whether their car is a high-quality "peach" or a low-quality "lemon" but buyers can't tell the difference. Buyers will pay a price reflecting the average quality. But at the average price, sellers of peaches are unwilling to sell (they'd be selling for less than their car is worth), while sellers of lemons are eager to sell. So peaches leave the market, the remaining cars are disproportionately lemons, the average quality falls, buyers adjust their offers downward, and the cycle continues. In the extreme, the market can collapse entirely — what Akerlof called adverse selection.

The same logic applies to insurance markets (high-risk individuals are most eager to buy insurance, raising premiums and driving out low-risk buyers), labor markets (workers know their own productivity better than employers), credit markets (borrowers know their own repayment intentions), and many others. Asymmetric information is one of the most pervasive market failures, and most actual markets have institutional responses to it — warranties, certifications, reputations, regulation.

✦ Sharma's Take — Why information matters more than the textbook admits

Most introductory textbooks mention asymmetric information but treat it as a special case. I'd argue it's closer to the rule than the exception. Healthcare: patients can't evaluate medical advice; doctors know much more. Financial services: customers can't evaluate the quality of investment products; brokers know much more. Used housing: sellers know about hidden problems; buyers don't. Job markets: workers know their own work habits; employers don't. Insurance: customers know their own risk; insurers must estimate. The textbook competitive market assumes away the most important friction in many of the markets that matter most to people's lives.

This is one reason economic policy debate about regulation is so contested. The libertarian instinct is "leave markets alone — they'll figure it out." Sometimes they do (used car warranties, AirBnB reviews, eBay ratings). Sometimes they don't (financial advice quality, medical care prices, online platform manipulation). The case for regulation is strongest precisely where information asymmetries are most severe and self-correcting institutions are weakest. The case against regulation is strongest where information is symmetric or where market-driven institutions handle the problem well. Reasonable people can disagree about specific cases; the analytical frame should be about whether market institutions are addressing the information problem, not whether information problems exist at all.

— A.S.

4.6 Search Frictions: When Finding Counterparties is Costly

📗 Intermediate Level — Markets where matching takes time

In the textbook market, buyers and sellers meet instantly and frictionlessly. In many real markets, finding a counterparty is the central activity — and the framework needs modification. The 2010 Nobel Prize was awarded to Diamond, Mortensen, and Pissarides precisely for work on markets where search is the central feature: labor markets, housing markets, marriage markets (yes, economists analyze these too).

The defining feature: at any moment, there are jobs unfilled and workers looking, houses for sale and buyers searching, suitable partners who haven't yet met. The matching process takes time and resources. Even when supply and demand are "balanced" in aggregate, there can be persistent unemployment, vacancies, and time-on-market because the matching isn't instant. The U.S. unemployment rate has been positive for every year since data collection began — not because the labor market is constantly out of equilibrium in the textbook sense, but because matching workers to jobs takes time even when both sides exist.

Search frictions imply several things the textbook model misses. (1) Prices are dispersed: the same good can sell for different prices depending on search effort. (2) Information about prices is itself valuable: the comparison-shopping industry exists because consumers can't costlessly know all prices. (3) Markets can have multiple equilibria: low-search-effort equilibria (high prices, less competition) and high-search-effort equilibria (low prices, more competition) can both exist for the same market.

4.7 Expectations: When the Future Drives the Present

📗 Intermediate Level — Forward-looking markets

In some markets, current prices depend heavily on expectations about future prices. The clearest examples are asset markets — stocks, bonds, real estate, commodities held as stores of value. A stock isn't just a claim on current earnings; it's a claim on the entire stream of future earnings, discounted to the present. Its current price depends on what buyers and sellers expect future earnings and discount rates to be.

This produces dynamics the basic supply-demand framework can't easily capture. When expectations shift — when investors become more optimistic about future earnings, or more pessimistic about future interest rates — current prices change without any change in current supply or current demand for the underlying real activity. Asset prices can deviate from fundamentals for extended periods, driven by self-reinforcing expectation cycles. The housing market in 2003-2007 is a textbook example: prices rose far above what supply-and-demand fundamentals could support, sustained by expectations that prices would continue rising, until the expectation cycle broke and prices collapsed.

Expectations also matter in labor markets (workers and firms enter contracts based on expected future productivity and prices), in education (students invest in skills based on expected future returns), in business investment (firms build factories based on expected future demand). Most introductory economics teaches supply and demand in the present tense; many of the most important markets operate substantially in the future tense.

2003-2012 United States Housing Bubble & Bust

When Expectations Detached Prices from Fundamentals

From 2003 to 2006, U.S. home prices rose roughly 50% in real terms — an unprecedented surge for a national housing market. The Case-Shiller index showed cities like Las Vegas, Phoenix, and Miami rising 80-100% in this short period. Supply-and-demand fundamentals couldn't account for the rise: population growth, income growth, and housing construction were all reasonably normal. What had changed was expectations: buyers, sellers, lenders, and rating agencies had all come to believe that home prices would continue rising indefinitely.

The expectation became self-fulfilling for a while. Buyers willing to pay high prices today (because they expected to sell higher tomorrow) bid prices up. Lenders willing to issue mortgages with little documentation (because they expected the underlying collateral to appreciate) extended credit broadly. Investors purchased mortgage-backed securities at prices that only made sense if expected default rates stayed low (because they expected home prices to keep rising). The whole system rested on the expectation.

When the expectation broke in 2007-2008, the unwinding was rapid and brutal. Home prices fell 30% nationally and 50%+ in the bubble cities. Mortgage defaults rose. Mortgage-backed securities became "toxic assets." The financial system nearly collapsed (Ch22 covers the financial crisis mechanics). U.S. home prices took until 2017 to recover their 2006 peaks in nominal terms; in real terms they remained below the peak for over a decade.

The deeper lesson for markets: when current prices depend heavily on expected future prices, the basic supply-demand framework needs to be supplemented with analysis of expectations dynamics. Most asset price collapses follow this pattern — a long period when expectations diverge from fundamentals, then a sudden adjustment when the divergence becomes unsustainable. Understanding markets with expectational dynamics requires holding two frames simultaneously: the current supply-demand equilibrium AND the expectations underlying it. Either alone is insufficient.

4.8 Interactive: Market Equilibrium Simulator

🎛 Interactive — Market Equilibrium with Shifts

Set demand and supply parameters; see equilibrium price and quantity, then shift the curves

Sliders set the slope and intercept of each curve. Demand: Qd = a − b·P. Supply: Qs = −c + d·P. The interactive computes the equilibrium and shows what happens when curves shift.

Notice the elasticity effects: in the inelastic preset (steep curves), small shifts produce large price changes. In the elastic preset (flat curves), the same shifts produce small price changes but larger quantity changes. This is why housing prices move so much in response to demand changes — housing supply is highly inelastic in the short run.

4.9 Worked Examples

📐 Worked Example 4.1 — Solving for Equilibrium
A market has demand Qd = 100 − 2P and supply Qs = −10 + 3P. (a) Find the equilibrium price and quantity. (b) If a sudden demand increase shifts demand to Qd = 150 − 2P, find the new equilibrium. (c) Compute the percentage change in price and quantity.
1
(a) Equilibrium: set Qd = Qs: 100 − 2P = −10 + 3P → 110 = 5P → P* = $22. Quantity: Q* = 100 − 2(22) = 56 units.
2
(b) Demand shifts: Qd = 150 − 2P. New equilibrium: 150 − 2P = −10 + 3P → 160 = 5P → P* = $32. Quantity: Q* = 150 − 2(32) = 86 units.
3
(c) Percentage changes: ΔP/P = (32 − 22)/22 = +45%. ΔQ/Q = (86 − 56)/56 = +54%. Both price and quantity rose substantially because both supply and demand are moderately elastic.
What the example shows: a demand shock raises both price and quantity. The relative sizes of the price and quantity adjustments depend on the elasticity of the supply curve. If supply were perfectly inelastic (vertical), all the adjustment would be in price; if supply were perfectly elastic (horizontal), all the adjustment would be in quantity. Real markets are between these extremes, and the ratio of price-change to quantity-change tells you about supply elasticity. This is also how empirical economists "back out" elasticity estimates from natural experiments — observe price and quantity responses to a shock, infer the underlying elasticities.
📐 Worked Example 4.2 — Identifying Demand vs Supply Shocks
In each scenario, determine whether the price and quantity changes are consistent with (a) a demand shift, (b) a supply shift, or (c) both. (i) U.S. egg prices double in a year; egg production falls 15%. (ii) Tesla prices fell 20% in 2024 while quantities sold rose 10%. (iii) Used car prices rose 50% in 2020-21 while quantities sold fell 5%. (iv) GPU prices spiked 200% in 2018 while quantities sold rose 30%.
1
(i) Eggs: Price ↑, Quantity ↓. This is the signature of a supply shift (leftward) — H5N1 avian flu in 2024-25 killed millions of laying hens, reducing supply. Price rose, quantity fell. Demand was approximately unchanged.
2
(ii) Tesla: Price ↓, Quantity ↑. This is the signature of a supply shift (rightward) — Tesla cut prices to expand market share, effectively shifting their supply curve down. Customers responded with more purchases. Demand approximately unchanged.
3
(iii) Used cars 2020-21: Price ↑, Quantity ↓. Like eggs — looks like a leftward supply shift. Supply was constrained by reduced new car production (which kept used cars circulating longer). Some demand-side increase too (people avoiding public transport during pandemic), but the dominant effect was supply.
4
(iv) GPUs 2018: Price ↑, Quantity ↑. This is the signature of a demand shift (rightward) — the 2018 crypto mining boom caused demand for GPUs to surge. With somewhat elastic supply, both price and quantity rose substantially.
The diagnostic principle: when price and quantity move in the same direction, it's primarily a demand shift. When they move in opposite directions, it's primarily a supply shift. This simple test lets you identify what's driving observed price changes — extremely useful for both real-world analysis and exam questions. The complication arises when both curves shift simultaneously (which often happens during major shocks); then you need additional information to decompose the change.
🎮 MacroNations Connection — Markets in the Engine

The MacroNations engine doesn't model individual markets — it operates at the aggregate level (whole-economy GDP, price level, unemployment). But the same supply-and-demand intuitions apply to aggregate markets. The aggregate demand curve (Ch15 covers this) represents total spending across all sectors; the aggregate supply curve represents total production. Equilibrium produces aggregate output and the price level. The 10 levers in MacroNations operate by shifting these aggregate curves: fiscal stimulus (gov_spending up or tax_rate down) shifts AD right, raising both output and prices. Supply-side policies (rd_investment, infrastructure, green_energy) shift AS right over time, raising output without raising prices. Monetary tightening (interest_rate_target up) shifts AD left, lowering both. The same diagnostic principle from Worked Example 4.2 applies at the aggregate level: if your output and price level move in the same direction, you're seeing demand effects; if they move opposite, you're seeing supply effects. Use this to debug your tournament strategy — if inflation is rising while output is falling, you've triggered a supply contraction, not a demand expansion.

Chapter 4 Quiz

Supply, demand, equilibrium, asymmetric information, search frictions, expectations
Part A — Multiple Choice (Principles)
1. The law of demand states:
A Demand always equals supply at equilibrium
B All else equal, quantity demanded falls when price rises
C Demand curves are vertical for necessities
D Higher demand causes higher supply
2. If a market has Qd = 80 − P and Qs = −20 + 4P, the equilibrium price is:
A $10
B $15
C $20 (set 80 − P = −20 + 4P, 100 = 5P)
D $25
3. When price and quantity both move in the same direction, the cause is most likely:
A A supply shift
B A demand shift
C Both supply and demand shifting in opposite directions
D Government intervention
4. A movement along the demand curve happens when:
A Income changes
B Consumer preferences change
C The good's own price changes
D Substitute prices change
Part B — Multiple Choice (Intermediate)
5. Akerlof's "Market for Lemons" demonstrates that:
A Used cars are always overpriced
B Buyers should always pay sticker price
C Asymmetric information between buyers and sellers can cause adverse selection, with high-quality goods exiting the market
D Markets always achieve perfect outcomes
6. The U.S. used car price index rose about 80% from January 2020 to its January 2022 peak primarily because:
A Consumers suddenly wanted older cars
B Government subsidies inflated prices
C New car production was constrained by chip shortages while demand recovered faster than expected — supply contraction combined with demand recovery
D Rental companies bought up most used cars
7. The 2010 Nobel Prize for "search frictions" implies which feature of real labor markets?
A The minimum wage causes unemployment
B Persistent unemployment can coexist with persistent vacancies because matching workers to jobs takes time and information
C Wages should always be set by the government
D Labor markets are perfectly competitive
Part C — Short Answer
8. A market has Qd = 200 − 5P and Qs = −20 + 3P. (a) Find equilibrium. (b) A favorable supply shock shifts supply to Qs = 20 + 3P. Find new equilibrium. (c) Compute consumer-savings from the lower price (using midpoint formula).
Model answer: (a) 200 − 5P = −20 + 3P → 220 = 8P → P* = $27.50, Q* = 200 − 5(27.5) = 62.5. (b) 200 − 5P = 20 + 3P → 180 = 8P → P* = $22.50, Q* = 200 − 5(22.5) = 87.5. (c) Price fell from $27.50 to $22.50 (savings of $5/unit). Quantity rose from 62.5 to 87.5. Consumer surplus gain ≈ $5 × (62.5 + 87.5)/2 = $5 × 75 = $375 (trapezoid area). The favorable supply shock benefited consumers through both lower prices on existing purchases AND additional units purchased at the new lower price.
9. Explain why the textbook supply-and-demand model often fits poorly for healthcare markets. Identify at least three features of healthcare that break standard assumptions.
Model answer: Several breakdowns of textbook assumptions. (1) Asymmetric information: doctors know much more than patients about what treatments are needed and what they should cost. Patients can't easily comparison-shop. (2) Insurance distortion: patients typically don't pay full prices at point of use; insurance changes the demand structure dramatically. The "demand curve" includes the patient's deductible/coinsurance, not the full price. (3) Demand uncertainty: people don't know when they'll need healthcare; emergency situations leave no time for price-shopping. (4) Quality is hard to assess: comparing surgeon quality is difficult; reputation and certification serve as imperfect substitutes. (5) Network effects and bilateral monopolies: hospital networks and insurance networks negotiate; individual transactions don't follow standard supply/demand pricing. (6) Public good elements: communicable disease treatment has positive externalities; vaccines have public health value beyond private benefit. The standard model doesn't capture any of this well. Real healthcare policy debate is mostly about how to design institutions that address these failures.
10. Write a 250-word memo analyzing the 2025 U.S. egg market: prices doubled in roughly a year, then began declining. Use the supply-and-demand framework, mention specific demand and supply factors, and discuss how long the elevated prices might persist.
Model answer (sketch): The 2024-25 U.S. egg price surge was primarily a supply-side phenomenon. Since the outbreak began in February 2022, H5N1 avian flu had killed over 130 million U.S. egg-laying hens cumulatively (through disease and mandatory culling), with about 17 million layers lost in just November-December 2024 alone. With supply contracted sharply but demand essentially unchanged (eggs are an inelastic staple), prices roughly doubled from about $2.50/dozen to peaks above $5/dozen. The supply curve shifted dramatically left; the demand curve was approximately unchanged. The diagnostic signature (price up, quantity down) confirms supply-side dominance over demand-side. The duration of elevated prices depends on flock rebuilding. New laying hens take roughly 5-6 months to reach laying age; full flock restoration takes 12-18 months under good biosecurity conditions. By Q4 2025 the U.S. flock had partially recovered and wholesale prices had retreated, though retail prices lagged behind wholesale by several months. The trajectory: prices likely to remain elevated (perhaps 30-50% above pre-2024 baseline) through mid-2026, with normalization by late 2026 absent new H5N1 outbreaks. The policy implications: investments in poultry biosecurity, vaccine development, and stockpile capacity could reduce vulnerability to future outbreaks. The episode also illustrates why agricultural prices are particularly volatile — short-run supply is highly inelastic (you can't immediately produce more eggs), demand is highly inelastic (people keep buying eggs), so any supply shock translates into large price moves until physical supply can adjust.
The live textbook auto-grades MCQs and stores progress.

References

Akerlof, G. A. (1970). The market for "lemons": Quality uncertainty and the market mechanism. Quarterly Journal of Economics, 84(3), 488–500.

Case, K. E., & Shiller, R. J. (2003). Is there a bubble in the housing market? Brookings Papers on Economic Activity, 2003(2), 299–342.

Diamond, P. A. (1982). Aggregate demand management in search equilibrium. Journal of Political Economy, 90(5), 881–894.

Manheim. (2025). Used Vehicle Value Index, January 2020–November 2025. Cox Automotive.

Marshall, A. (1890). Principles of Economics. Macmillan.

Mortensen, D. T., & Pissarides, C. A. (1994). Job creation and job destruction in the theory of unemployment. Review of Economic Studies, 61(3), 397–415.

Shiller, R. J. (2015). Irrational Exuberance (3rd ed.). Princeton University Press.

Stigler, G. J. (1961). The economics of information. Journal of Political Economy, 69(3), 213–225.

Stiglitz, J. E. (2000). The contributions of the economics of information to twentieth century economics. Quarterly Journal of Economics, 115(4), 1441–1478.

USDA. (2025). Egg Markets Overview: H5N1 Impact Assessment. Agricultural Marketing Service Report.

Chapter 5 · Part II: Markets & Prices

Elasticity: When the Slope Matters

A single concept — the responsiveness of one variable to another — that determines who pays a tax, why pandemic shortages produced wildly different price effects across categories, why some farmers face perpetual price volatility, and why pharmaceutical companies can sustain high prices while airlines can't

In March 2020, U.S. retailers ran out of toilet paper for about three weeks. The wholesale price of toilet paper barely moved during this period — it rose perhaps 8-12%, not enough to register in the official PPI data. Three months later, U.S. consumers couldn't find new cars; the wholesale price of cars rose roughly 15% over the following year. Two years later, a temporary shortage of baby formula caused not just empty shelves but price increases of 20-30% in some regions. The U.S. experienced multiple shortages during the pandemic and immediately after, but the price responses ranged from barely noticeable to dramatic. What explains the difference? The answer, in a single word, is elasticity.

Elasticity is the measure of how responsive one variable is to changes in another. When demand is highly responsive to price (elastic), a small price change produces a large quantity change. When demand is barely responsive (inelastic), a price change produces little quantity change. The same applies to supply. Together, demand and supply elasticities determine how shocks propagate through markets — how shortages translate to price changes, how taxes get split between buyers and sellers, how regulatory changes affect everyone's welfare. This chapter develops the elasticity concept rigorously and applies it to real-world cases where understanding elasticity is the difference between accurate and wildly wrong predictions.

✦ Sharma's Take — Why elasticity is the most underrated concept in intro economics

Of all the concepts in introductory economics, elasticity is probably the most underrated. Students learn it as a formula and move on. But elasticity is what determines the answer to nearly every applied question in economic policy. Who bears the burden of a tax? Whoever is more inelastic on their side of the market. Why do agricultural prices crash when there's a bumper crop? Because demand for food is inelastic, so a quantity increase requires a large price decrease. Why do gasoline taxes affect demand modestly in the short run but substantially in the long run? Because gasoline demand is inelastic in the short run (you have your current car and commute) but elastic over time (you can buy a fuel-efficient car or change where you live).

If you understand elasticity well, you can predict the qualitative effects of most policies and shocks immediately. Most poorly-designed economic policy fails because the designers didn't think hard enough about elasticities. The luxury tax on yachts in the U.S. in 1991 collected almost no revenue and devastated the yacht industry because the demand for luxury yachts is highly elastic (buyers can postpone purchases or buy abroad). The COVID-era student loan payment pause was justified partly by "helping borrowers"; the actual benefit went disproportionately to high-income borrowers because their loan balances are higher — a classic elasticity-design mismatch. Whenever you hear an economic claim about who benefits or loses from a policy, ask: what are the relevant elasticities? The answer usually rewrites the political story.

— A.S.

5.1 Price Elasticity of Demand

📘 Principles Level — Definition and computation

The price elasticity of demand measures how responsive quantity demanded is to changes in price. Formally:

εd = (% change in Qd) / (% change in P)

Because Qd falls when P rises, the elasticity is technically negative. By convention, economists usually report the absolute value and call demand "elastic" or "inelastic" based on whether |εd| is greater or less than 1.

  • Elastic (|εd| > 1): quantity changes proportionally more than price. A 10% price increase produces > 10% quantity decrease. Demand is responsive.
  • Inelastic (|εd| < 1): quantity changes proportionally less than price. A 10% price increase produces < 10% quantity decrease. Demand is unresponsive.
  • Unit elastic (|εd| = 1): proportional response. A 10% price increase produces 10% quantity decrease.
  • Perfectly elastic (εd = −∞): any price increase eliminates all demand. The demand curve is horizontal.
  • Perfectly inelastic (εd = 0): quantity demanded doesn't change at all with price. The demand curve is vertical.
Estimated price elasticities of demand for selected categories (long-run, U.S.)
Good|εd|Why
Salt0.1No substitutes, small budget share, essential
Cigarettes (adult smokers)0.3-0.5Addictive, few substitutes, modest budget share
Eggs0.3Few good substitutes for many uses; staple food
Gasoline (short run)0.2-0.4Existing car and commute fix demand short-term
Gasoline (long run)0.6-0.8Over time, fuel-efficient cars, lifestyle changes
Movies (theater)0.9Streaming, home entertainment as substitutes
Beef1.0-1.5Chicken, pork, fish are reasonable substitutes
Restaurant meals1.5-2.5Cooking at home is a close substitute
Luxury cars3-5Postponable; many substitutes (other cars, kept current car)
Particular brand (e.g., Pepsi)5-10Close substitutes (Coke); brand-specific demand is very elastic
⚙ Equation Walkthrough — Midpoint Formula
εd = [(Q2 − Q1) / ((Q1 + Q2)/2)] / [(P2 − P1) / ((P1 + P2)/2)]
Why use midpoints?
The "simple" percentage formula gives different elasticities depending on whether you compute "P went from 10 to 12" (20% rise) or "P went from 12 to 10" (16.7% fall). The midpoint formula uses the average of the two prices/quantities, giving a consistent number regardless of direction.
Example
Q falls from 100 to 80; P rises from $10 to $12. ΔQ/avg(Q) = −20/90 = −22.2%. ΔP/avg(P) = 2/11 = 18.2%. Elasticity = −22.2%/18.2% = −1.22. |εd| > 1, so demand is elastic.
Interpretation
|εd| = 1.22 means a 1% price increase causes 1.22% quantity decrease. The 18% price increase caused a 22% quantity decrease — proportionally larger than the price change.
Why this matters for revenue
When demand is elastic, raising price reduces total revenue (the percentage quantity drop exceeds the percentage price gain). When inelastic, raising price increases total revenue. This is the relationship between elasticity and revenue maximization.

5.2 The Determinants of Elasticity

What makes some goods more elastically demanded than others? Five major factors:

Determinants of price elasticity of demand
FactorHow it works
Availability of substitutesMore substitutes → more elastic. Single brand of cola has elastic demand (Coca-Cola), but soft drinks as a category have less elastic demand.
Necessity vs luxuryNecessities are inelastic (food, basic medicine, electricity). Luxuries are elastic (vacations, designer goods).
Share of budgetBig-ticket items get more shopping attention. Cars are more elastic than chewing gum.
Time horizonLong-run demand is more elastic than short-run. People adjust slowly — gasoline demand is inelastic in 1 year but more elastic over 5 years.
Definition of marketNarrower market → more elastic. "Honey Nut Cheerios" has elastic demand; "breakfast cereal" has less elastic demand; "food" has very inelastic demand.
1991-1993 United States Luxury Tax Failure

The Yacht Tax: A Lesson in Demand Elasticity

In November 1990, the U.S. Congress passed a 10% luxury tax on yachts costing more than $100,000, alongside taxes on private aircraft, luxury cars, jewelry, and furs. The tax was projected to raise $31 million per year and was justified as making the wealthy pay their "fair share." The actual result was a near-collapse of the U.S. yacht industry.

Within two years of the tax, sales of U.S.-made yachts above the threshold collapsed. The Joint Economic Committee's official 1991 analysis attributed direct losses of about 7,600 jobs to the boating industry, with additional losses in jewelry (~330 jobs) and aircraft (~1,470 jobs); industry groups including the National Marine Manufacturers Association argued the total combined direct and indirect losses (including supplier industries) ran much higher, into the tens of thousands. Wealthy buyers either postponed purchases, bought slightly cheaper boats below the threshold, or — most damaging — bought yachts in Mexico or Europe to avoid the tax.

By 1993, the tax was repealed. What had gone wrong? The Congressional analysis had implicitly assumed that demand for luxury yachts was inelastic — that buyers would simply absorb the tax. The reality was that demand was highly elastic. Luxury purchases can be postponed, downscaled, or shifted to foreign suppliers. The tax made U.S.-built yachts uncompetitive relative to alternatives, and demand collapsed. The 1991 revenue yield was about $16.6 million against the original $31 million projection, and once associated unemployment costs were netted out, the JEC estimated the tax was a net fiscal loss in fiscal 1991.

The deeper lesson: taxes generate revenue most effectively when demand for the taxed good is inelastic, and they generate deadweight loss most severely when demand is elastic. The yacht tax was the worst possible combination — small revenue, large efficiency loss, concentrated job losses for non-wealthy workers. The wealthy were largely unaffected; the people who built the yachts bore the brunt. This is now one of the standard cautionary tales in tax policy, taught in every public finance course.

5.3 Elasticity and Total Revenue

📗 Intermediate Level — The revenue relationship

Total revenue is price times quantity: TR = P × Q. When price changes, both P and Q change (in opposite directions, given downward-sloping demand). What happens to total revenue depends on which effect dominates — and that's determined by elasticity.

  • If demand is elastic (|εd| > 1): the quantity effect dominates. Raising price reduces total revenue; lowering price increases revenue.
  • If demand is inelastic (|εd| < 1): the price effect dominates. Raising price increases total revenue; lowering price decreases revenue.
  • If demand is unit elastic (|εd| = 1): the two effects exactly cancel. Total revenue is constant as price changes.

This relationship explains many real-world pricing patterns. Tobacco companies historically raised prices because cigarette demand is inelastic — higher price meant higher revenue. Airlines run elaborate yield-management systems precisely because flight demand is elastic at the marginal traveler — a different price for different seats can extract more revenue than uniform pricing. Streaming services raise subscription prices steadily because they've found subscription demand is inelastic in the short run (people resist canceling).

⚠ Common Pitfall — Confusing "elastic" with "high quantity response"

Sometimes students learn that "elastic" means "large quantity response" and stop there. The full definition matters: elastic means quantity response is proportionally larger than the price change. A 10% price change causing 5% quantity change is inelastic, even though 5% is "substantial." A 10% price change causing 15% quantity change is elastic, even if 15% feels modest.

This matters because the elasticity threshold (|εd| = 1) determines the revenue effect. Even a "large" quantity response is inelastic if it's smaller in percentage terms than the price change — and in that case, the price rise produces revenue gains, not losses. Always compute the ratio carefully. Elasticity is about proportionality, not absolute magnitude.

5.4 Tax Incidence: Who Really Pays

📗 Intermediate Level — Statutory vs economic incidence

One of the most important applications of elasticity is determining the incidence of a tax — who ultimately bears the burden. The statutory incidence is determined by law (who legally writes the check). The economic incidence is determined by elasticity (who ends up paying through price changes). These can be wildly different.

The basic principle: the more inelastic side of the market bears more of the tax burden. If demand is more inelastic than supply, consumers bear most of the tax (they can't easily reduce their purchases, so they absorb the higher prices). If supply is more inelastic than demand, producers bear most of the tax (they can't easily reduce production, so they absorb the lower net prices). The fraction of a tax borne by buyers vs sellers is approximately εs / (εs + |εd|).

Why this matters: politicians often claim that "we're taxing corporations" or "we're taxing the wealthy." Whether those statements are correct in economic terms depends on the elasticities. A corporate tax may be largely paid by consumers through higher prices (if product demand is inelastic) or by workers through lower wages (if labor markets are imperfectly competitive). A tax legally collected from sellers can be largely paid by buyers, and vice versa. Statutory incidence is a political/legal fact; economic incidence is determined by markets.

✦ Sharma's Take — The most important tax incidence example to understand

Consider a tax on cigarettes. The statutory incidence is on the seller (the retailer or wholesaler writes the check). The economic incidence: cigarette demand is inelastic (|εd| ≈ 0.4), and cigarette supply is more elastic. So consumers bear the substantial majority of the tax — typically 70-80% of cigarette taxes show up as higher consumer prices, with the rest absorbed by sellers (and a small fraction lost as deadweight loss from foregone transactions). Cigarette taxes are effective tools for both raising revenue AND reducing smoking precisely because demand is inelastic enough to maintain revenue while elastic enough that some smokers do quit.

Now consider a tax on luxury cars. Statutory incidence: typically on the buyer (sales tax). Economic incidence: luxury car demand is highly elastic. Buyers substitute toward non-luxury cars, buy used, postpone purchases, or buy abroad. The tax barely changes consumer prices because consumers can easily avoid it; the burden falls largely on workers in luxury car manufacturing. This is the same pattern as the 1991 yacht tax. The lesson: design taxes on inelastic goods if you want revenue with limited distortion; on elastic goods, the same tax produces less revenue and more harm to workers in affected industries. Cigarettes work; yachts don't. The principle is general — economists evaluate proposed taxes substantially through the lens of expected incidence.

— A.S.

5.5 Supply Elasticity

📘 Principles Level — Definition

The price elasticity of supply measures how responsive quantity supplied is to price changes. Same formula structure as demand elasticity:

εs = (% change in Qs) / (% change in P)

Supply elasticity is positive (higher prices → more quantity supplied). The classifications mirror demand: elastic (εs > 1), inelastic (εs < 1), unit elastic (εs = 1), perfectly elastic (horizontal), perfectly inelastic (vertical).

What determines supply elasticity? Primarily the ease of changing production. Industries that can rapidly add capacity (manufacturing with available labor and capital) have elastic supply. Industries with long capacity-building lead times (semiconductor fabs, oil refineries, housing) have inelastic short-run supply. Industries with absolutely fixed quantities (land in a specific location, vintage art, the Bordeaux 2003 wine vintage) have nearly perfectly inelastic supply.

2020-2024 Global Pandemic Shortage Elasticities

How Elasticity Explained Why Some Pandemic Shortages Hurt and Others Didn't

The pandemic produced shortages in many categories. The price response varied enormously across them, in ways that elasticity neatly predicts.

Good2020-22 price changeDemand elasticitySupply elasticity
Toilet paper~10%Very low (~0.1)Moderate — could ramp up production in weeks
Hand sanitizer~30% then 0%Spike then normalHighly elastic — alternative manufacturers entered
Used cars+80%Low (~0.4)Very low — can't make new used cars
Baby formula+25-30% in 2022Very low (parents will pay)Very low (FDA-regulated, few producers)
Semiconductors+200% in some categoriesLow (no substitutes for many uses)Very low (fabs take 2-3 years to build)
Lumber+250% then -60%Moderate (some construction can wait)Moderate — mills could reopen

The pattern: price spikes were largest when both demand and supply were inelastic (semiconductors, baby formula). Modest spikes when demand was inelastic but supply could respond quickly (toilet paper, hand sanitizer). Volatile prices when both could adjust over a multi-year horizon (lumber: huge spike, then collapse as supply caught up).

The policy implications were also dictated by elasticities. For semiconductors, the inelastic supply led to the CHIPS Act — government subsidies for domestic fabs that would expand U.S. supply over the medium term. Tax cuts or other demand-side policies would have been useless because demand wasn't the problem. For baby formula, the FDA accelerated approval of imports — a supply-side response targeting the inelastic supply problem. For toilet paper, no policy was needed because supply caught up within weeks naturally. Each policy choice was effectively shaped by the underlying elasticities. A policy designer who doesn't understand elasticities can't correctly diagnose what intervention will help.

5.6 Cross-Price and Income Elasticities

📗 Intermediate Level — Other elasticities that matter

Two other elasticities matter for understanding markets:

Cross-price elasticity of demand: how much demand for good X changes when the price of good Y changes:

εxy = (% change in Qx) / (% change in Py)

If εxy > 0, X and Y are substitutes — when Y's price rises, people switch to X. Coke and Pepsi are substitutes; cross-elasticity is positive. If εxy < 0, X and Y are complements — when Y's price rises, people buy less of both. Cars and gasoline are complements; cross-elasticity is negative.

Income elasticity of demand: how much demand changes with income:

εI = (% change in Qd) / (% change in income)

If εI > 0, the good is normal (more demand at higher income). If εI > 1, the good is a luxury (demand grows proportionally more than income — vacation travel, fine dining). If 0 < εI < 1, the good is a necessity (demand grows less than proportionally with income — food, basic clothing). If εI < 0, the good is inferior (demand falls as income rises — instant noodles, used clothing).

5.7 Interactive: Elasticity Explorer

🎛 Interactive — Elasticity, Revenue, and Tax Incidence

Adjust demand and supply elasticities; see how taxes get split, how revenue changes, and where deadweight loss appears

The chart shows the supply and demand curves with the equilibrium. Apply a tax (per-unit) and the wedge between buyer and seller prices opens up. The relative inelasticities determine who pays.

Try this experiment: set the cigarettes preset and impose a $5 tax. Most of the tax shows up as higher consumer prices because demand is inelastic. Switch to yachts preset with the same tax. Now the price barely rises but the quantity sold falls sharply — the burden has shifted to producers (and workers in the industry). This is the empirical mechanism behind the 1991 yacht tax disaster.

5.8 Worked Examples

📐 Worked Example 5.1 — Computing Elasticity and Revenue
A coffee shop sells lattes at $4.00, selling 200 per day. They raise the price to $4.50. Sales fall to 180 per day. (a) Compute the price elasticity of demand using the midpoint formula. (b) Compute total revenue at each price. (c) Is demand elastic or inelastic? Was the price increase a good business decision in revenue terms?
1
(a) Elasticity: ΔQ = −20, avg Q = 190. ΔQ/avgQ = −20/190 = −10.5%. ΔP = $0.50, avgP = $4.25. ΔP/avgP = 0.50/4.25 = +11.8%. Elasticity = −10.5%/11.8% = −0.89.
2
(b) Revenue: Old: 200 × $4.00 = $800/day. New: 180 × $4.50 = $810/day. Revenue rose $10/day.
3
