Economy is booming above potential. Tight labor markets push up wages. Firms raise prices. Workers and firms start expecting higher inflation (Pe rises). SRAS shifts left. Eventually Y returns to Y*, but at a permanently higher price level.
Y < Y*
Economy in recession below potential. High unemployment pushes down wage demands. Firms cut prices. Expectations of lower inflation set in. SRAS shifts right. Eventually Y returns to Y*. But this self-correction can be very slow if wages are sticky downward (Chapter 14), which is why recessions can persist for years.

The asymmetry matters: the upward adjustment (Y above Y* → SRAS left) tends to happen reasonably fast because wages and prices adjust upward easily. The downward adjustment (Y below Y* → SRAS right) is slow because wages are sticky downward. This asymmetry is one major reason Keynesian economists argue for active policy intervention during recessions: the natural correction mechanism works much faster going down (preventing booms) than going up (escaping recessions).

15.4 Case Studies: Two Defining Supply Shocks

1973–1975 United States OPEC Oil Embargo

The Classic Negative Supply Shock

On October 17, 1973, the Organization of Arab Petroleum Exporting Countries (OAPEC) announced an oil embargo against the United States and several allies in response to U.S. support for Israel in the Yom Kippur War. Over the following five months, crude oil prices quadrupled — from about $3 per barrel in September 1973 to roughly $12 per barrel by March 1974. The shock was both sudden and economy-wide. Energy is an input to virtually every good and service produced in a modern economy. When energy prices spike, the cost of producing everything else rises in proportion.

In the AD-AS framework, this is a textbook negative supply shock: SRAS shifts left. The predicted result: higher prices, lower output, higher unemployment. The actual result fit the prediction almost perfectly:

  • Inflation jumped from 3.4% in 1972 to 12.3% in 1974 — the highest peacetime rate since 1947.
  • Real GDP contracted in five of the six quarters from late 1973 through early 1975. Output fell about 3% peak-to-trough.
  • Unemployment rose from 4.6% in October 1973 to 9.0% in May 1975, the highest since World War II.

The Fed faced a genuine policy dilemma. Inflation called for tightening. The recession called for easing. The Fed at first tried to do both — alternately tightening and loosening — and accomplished neither goal. The episode left inflation embedded in expectations for the rest of the 1970s, requiring the brutal Volcker disinflation of 1980-83 to root out. The 1973-75 episode is the canonical case study taught in nearly every macro textbook because it fits the AD-AS framework so cleanly. It is also why every modern central bank treats supply shocks with extreme caution.

2020–2024 United States The combined-shock episode

The Pandemic: A Triple Shock That Broke Standard Models

The COVID-19 pandemic and its policy response was the most complex AD-AS episode in modern U.S. history. Unlike 1973, the 2020-2022 period involved multiple simultaneous shocks of different signs and types:

Phase 1 — Spring 2020: a simultaneous demand AND supply collapse. Lockdowns shut down restaurants, gyms, travel, and in-person services — that's a demand collapse. Lockdowns also closed factories, broke supply chains, and removed workers from production — that's a supply collapse. AD shifted hard left, and SRAS shifted hard left at the same time. The result: GDP fell 9.1% in a single quarter (Q2 2020) — the largest quarterly drop ever recorded. But surprisingly, inflation initially fell. CPI rose only 0.6% in 2020. The demand collapse dominated, and the price level effects of the two shocks partially offset.

Phase 2 — 2021: massive positive demand shock collides with continuing supply problems. Federal stimulus payments, expanded unemployment insurance, and zero interest rates pushed AD sharply right. Supply chains were still snarled, semiconductor shortages persisted, and millions of workers had retired or dropped out (Chapter 14's outward Beveridge curve shift). AD up, SRAS still partly suppressed. Result: prices started rising fast, but the economy didn't fully recover potential output until 2022. CPI inflation jumped from 1.4% in January 2021 to 7.0% by December 2021.

Phase 3 — 2022: Russia invades Ukraine, oil and food prices spike. A pure negative supply shock layered on top of the existing demand-supply imbalance. SRAS shifted further left. Oil rose from $76 to $120 per barrel in three months. Wheat prices doubled. CPI inflation peaked at 9.1% in June 2022 — the highest reading in 40 years. Unemployment, somehow, stayed at 3.6%. The Phillips curve relationship between unemployment and inflation appeared to vanish.

Phase 4 — 2023-2024: the "immaculate disinflation". The Fed raised rates from near zero to 5.25-5.50% over 18 months — the fastest tightening cycle in 40 years. Inflation fell sharply: from 9.1% (June 2022) to 3.1% (June 2023) to 2.4% (September 2024). But output kept growing. Unemployment barely budged from 3.5-4.0%. The standard textbook prediction was that this kind of disinflation should require a recession. It didn't.

What this case teaches: the AD-AS framework correctly predicted the directions of most movements — demand-driven inflation in 2021-22, supply-side amplification in 2022. But the framework's standard treatment of the supply curve, and its prediction that disinflation requires recession, failed substantially in 2023-24. The most plausible explanation: the 2023 inflation decline came largely from the supply side normalizing (supply chains repaired, workers returning, energy markets stabilizing) — i.e., SRAS shifting right — combined with credible Fed commitment that kept inflation expectations from drifting up. Standard AD-AS gives you most of the story; the unusual nature of the supply curve's behavior gives you the rest.

15.5 Interactive: The AD-AS Diagram

🎛 Interactive — AD-AS Equilibrium

Shift AD and SRAS; see prices, output, and unemployment respond

What you're seeing: the blue AD curve slopes down (price level on Y, output on X); the red SRAS curve slopes up; the gray LRAS is vertical at potential output Y*. The green dot is the short-run equilibrium. The dashed lines show the original (pre-shock) curves. Drag the sliders or click a preset to see each case.

✦ Sharma's Take — When the standard diagram misleads

The standard AD-AS diagram assumes that aggregate demand and aggregate supply move independently. In 2020 they didn't. Lockdowns simultaneously suppressed AD (people couldn't go out) and SRAS (factories couldn't operate). The standard "shift one curve at a time" exercise doesn't capture this kind of correlated shock. Real policymakers had to think about the two shocks together and decide which was dominant for inflation (in 2020: the demand collapse) and for output (in 2020: roughly equal).

The standard diagram also assumes a stable mapping from output to unemployment via Okun's Law. In 2020-2022 that relationship broke down. Unemployment fell to 3.4% in April 2023, the lowest since 1969, while output grew at a moderate pace and inflation cooled rapidly. The labor market and product market decoupled in ways the standard models would not have predicted.

I am not asking you to throw out the AD-AS model. The model is the most useful single framework in macroeconomics. But every model is a simplification. When the simplification fails, you need to know which assumption broke and reason directly from first principles. The 2020-2024 episode was the most consequential test of macroeconomic models in our lifetimes, and the lesson is that frameworks must be applied with judgment, not memorized as catechism.

— A.S.

15.6 Worked Examples: Reading the AD-AS Diagram

📐 Worked Example 15.1 — Tracing a demand-driven recession
An economy starts in long-run equilibrium at Y = 100 and P = 100. The 2008 financial crisis hits: household wealth falls, consumer confidence collapses, and credit becomes scarce. Trace the short-run and long-run effects on Y, P, and unemployment, using the AD-AS framework.
1
Identify the shock: the wealth-and-confidence shock reduces consumption and investment. AD shifts left. No direct effect on production technology, so SRAS and LRAS are unchanged.
2
Short-run effects: at the new (lower) AD curve, the intersection with the unchanged SRAS occurs at lower Y and lower P. So Y < Y* and P < original P. Output falls, prices fall (or rise more slowly), and by Okun's Law, unemployment rises. This matches the 2008-09 data: GDP fell 4.3% peak-to-trough; CPI inflation collapsed from 5.6% (July 2008) to −2.1% (July 2009); unemployment rose from 5.0% to 10.0%.
3
Long-run effects (if no policy response): the recession lowers wage demands, which gradually shifts SRAS rightward. Lower nominal wages reduce production costs at every price level. Over time, the SRAS shift restores Y to Y*, but at an even lower price level. Self-correction works, but slowly — wages are sticky downward (Chapter 14).
4
With monetary policy response: the Fed cuts rates to zero (Dec 2008) and undertakes quantitative easing. This shifts AD back rightward toward its original position. The recovery is faster than self-correction would have been, though still slow because of the depth of the shock and zero-lower-bound constraints.
The 2008-09 episode is the cleanest demand-shock case study in modern U.S. history. AD-AS gets the directions and rough magnitudes right. The persistence of the recovery (it took until 2014 for unemployment to fall below 6%) reflects the depth of the shock and the slow nature of self-correction — features the static diagram captures only in spirit, not in timing.
📐 Worked Example 15.2 — A negative supply shock with fiscal response
An economy in long-run equilibrium suddenly faces a 100% increase in global oil prices (a la 1973 or 2022). The government considers fiscal stimulus to offset the recession. Trace the consequences and explain why the policy response is fraught.
1
The shock: oil is an input to nearly all production. Production costs rise at every price level. SRAS shifts left.
2
Short-run effects of the shock alone: with AD unchanged and SRAS shifted left, the new equilibrium has lower Y and higher P. Stagflation: recession plus inflation. This is the worst possible combination for policy.
3
Fiscal stimulus response: the government increases spending or cuts taxes to combat the recession. This shifts AD right. Result: Y rises back toward Y*, but P rises further. The recession is partially offset, but inflation gets worse.
4
The trade-off cannot be escaped. Fighting the recession (rightward AD shift) makes inflation worse. Fighting inflation (leftward AD shift, or central bank tightening) makes the recession worse. With pure supply shocks, monetary and fiscal policy face a no-win choice — they can pick between recession with stable prices, or growth with high inflation, but not both at once.
This is why supply shocks are uniquely difficult for policy. The 1973-1975 episode produced both a recession and double-digit inflation simultaneously, and U.S. policy lost credibility for nearly a decade trying to find a stable response. The 2022 episode could have repeated this disaster — but the Fed's quick and aggressive tightening, combined with the fact that the supply-chain disruption normalized in 2023, allowed for a much better outcome. The 1973-75 mistake was treating the supply shock with policies designed for demand shocks. The 2022-24 response, by contrast, was tightening even at the cost of growth — and it paid off.
🎮 MacroNations Connection — AD-AS in the Engine

The MacroNations engine implements AD-AS implicitly through its GDP and inflation equations. Aggregate demand shifts come from gov_spending_pct (1.4× multiplier), tax_rate (−1.1× multiplier), and interest_rate_target (−0.5/pp monetary impulse). Aggregate supply shifts come from rd_investment, infrastructure, and green_energy — these raise potential over 3-5 rounds, shifting LRAS rightward. Supply shocks are implemented as crisis shocks: a pandemic crisis subtracts 4pp from gdp_growth and adds 1.5pp to inflation simultaneously — exactly an SRAS leftward shift. The four-shock typology shows up in pillar scoring: demand booms raise GDP score short-term but cost Stability via inflation; supply shocks cost both pillars simultaneously; positive supply shocks (sustained R&D investment) raise GDP and Stability together by lifting potential without raising inflation. This is why patient R&D investment is uniquely valuable — it's the only lever that improves all five pillars simultaneously over time.

U.S. Real GDP vs. Potential GDP — Last 25 Years
Sources: BEA real GDP via FRED (Series: GDPC1); CBO potential GDP via FRED (Series: GDPPOT). When the blue line (actual real GDP) is below the gray line (potential GDP), the economy has an output gap — operating below potential, indicating a demand-driven recession or undercapacity. When actual exceeds potential, the economy is overheating (which contributed to the 2022 inflation). The 2008-09 gap was the largest in modern U.S. history; the 2020 collapse was sharper but recovered faster.
U.S. CPI Inflation YoY — Last 25 Years
Source: BLS via FRED (Series: CPIAUCSL, YoY change). Read this alongside the GDP chart above. The 2008-09 demand collapse pulled inflation briefly negative. The 2021-22 surge — peaking at 9.1% in June 2022 — combined demand and supply pressures. The post-2022 disinflation back to ~2.5% by 2025 happened without a recession, a result that surprised many economists and reflects the unusual nature of the underlying shocks.

Chapter 15 Quiz

AD, SRAS, LRAS, shocks, equilibrium adjustment
Part A — Multiple Choice (Principles)
1. The aggregate demand curve slopes downward primarily because:
A Higher prices cause consumers to substitute toward other goods
B The wealth, interest rate, and exchange rate effects together reduce real spending as the price level rises
C Government always cuts spending when prices rise
D The Fed always raises rates when prices rise
2. The long-run aggregate supply (LRAS) curve is:
A Upward-sloping
B Downward-sloping
C Vertical at potential output
D Horizontal at the natural rate of inflation
3. A negative supply shock (e.g., an oil price spike) causes which combination in the short run?
A Higher output, higher prices
B Lower output, lower prices
C Lower output, higher prices (stagflation)
D Higher output, lower prices
4. A positive shift in aggregate demand (e.g., fiscal stimulus) causes in the short run:
A Lower output and lower prices
B Higher output and higher prices
C No change in output but higher prices
D Higher output and lower prices
Part B — Multiple Choice (Intermediate)
5. Long-run self-correction after a negative demand shock works through:
A Falling wage demands shifting SRAS rightward, restoring Y to Y*
B The Fed cutting interest rates automatically
C Government deficits naturally rising
D Capital depreciation reducing potential output
6. Why is the policy response to supply shocks fundamentally more difficult than to demand shocks?
A Because supply shocks always involve the financial sector
B Because supply shocks push prices and output in opposite directions, so monetary policy cannot stabilize both at once
C Because supply shocks last longer than demand shocks
D Because the Fed has no tools to fight supply shocks
7. The 2023-2024 U.S. disinflation (inflation falling from 9% to under 3% without a major recession) is best explained by:
A A massive cut in government spending
B A combination of Fed tightening and a rightward shift of SRAS as supply chains normalized
C A trade war reducing import prices
D Population decline reducing demand
Part C — Numerical Problems
8. An economy is in long-run equilibrium at Y* = $20 trillion and P = 100. A confidence shock reduces consumer spending; AD shifts left by 10%. SRAS is initially unchanged. (a) Approximate the new short-run Y and P, given that the SRAS slope is such that a 10% leftward AD shift causes Y to fall by 7% and P to fall by 3%. (b) Compute the output gap. (c) Using Okun's Law (each 1% gap in output corresponds to about 0.5 percentage points of unemployment above the natural rate), if u* = 4%, what is the new unemployment rate? (d) Compute the new outcomes if SRAS then shifts right by 5% due to wage adjustment, holding AD fixed at its new lower position.
Model answer: (a) New Y ≈ $20T × (1 − 0.07) = $18.6T. New P ≈ 100 × (1 − 0.03) = 97. (b) Output gap = (Y − Y*) / Y* = (18.6 − 20) / 20 = −7%. (c) Okun's Law: unemployment above natural rate = 0.5 × 7% = 3.5 percentage points. New u = 4% + 3.5% = 7.5%. (d) If SRAS shifts right by 5%, Y rises further (closer to Y*), and P falls further. Approximate: Y ≈ $18.6T × 1.05 = $19.5T (gap narrows to −2.5%); P falls another 2-3 points to ≈ 94-95. This shows the slow self-correction: it takes time for sticky wages to ease, but as they do, the economy regains output at the cost of a lower price level.
9. Country Z's GDP deflator is 105 (base year = 100) and real GDP is $15 trillion. Potential GDP is $15.5 trillion. (a) Compute nominal GDP. (b) Compute the output gap. (c) The Fed wants to close the gap with fiscal stimulus. If the marginal propensity to consume is 0.7, what increase in autonomous government spending would, in principle, close the gap? (Assume no crowding out for simplicity.) (d) Discuss two reasons why this calculation is likely to overstate the needed stimulus.
Model answer: (a) Nominal GDP = Real GDP × deflator/100 = $15T × 1.05 = $15.75T. (b) Output gap = ($15T − $15.5T) / $15.5T = −3.2%. (c) Gap to close = $0.5T. With MPC = 0.7, simple multiplier = 1 / (1 − MPC) = 1 / 0.3 ≈ 3.33. Required ΔG = $0.5T / 3.33 ≈ $150B. (d) Reasons this overstates: (i) crowding out — the stimulus raises interest rates, reducing investment, partially offsetting the boost; (ii) higher prices — as Y approaches Y*, prices rise and the multiplier shrinks; (iii) Ricardian equivalence (partial) — households may save some of the stimulus expecting future tax increases; (iv) leakages — some spending goes to imports.
Part D — Policy Scenario
Scenario: You are advising a central bank that faces the following situation in early 2027: CPI inflation is running at 5.5%, well above the 2% target. Real GDP is growing at 3%, slightly above estimated potential. Unemployment is 3.8%, below the natural rate estimate of 4.2%. A new wave of supply-chain disruptions from a geopolitical crisis has just begun, with energy prices up 25% in two months. Three policy options: (1) Raise rates aggressively (+150 bp over the next two meetings) to fight inflation. (2) Hold rates steady, treating the new supply shock as "transitory" and waiting for it to dissipate. (3) Cut rates modestly to support output, accepting higher inflation in the short run.
10. Write a 250-word memo to the Governor recommending one option. Reference: the AD-AS framework, the lessons of 1973-75 vs. 2022, the role of inflation expectations, and the distinction between demand-side and supply-side inflation. Take a clear position.
Model answer (sketch): The economy faces both demand-side overheating (Y above Y*, u below u*) and a new supply shock (energy +25%). This is the most difficult scenario in macro because demand and supply both push prices up but in opposite directions for output.

Option 3 (cut rates): rejected. Cutting rates pushes AD further right when AD is already too high; aggravates inflation; risks unanchoring expectations.

Option 2 (hold rates, treat as transitory): rejected. The 2022 experience showed that the "transitory" framing can be a costly delay; the 1973-75 experience showed that letting supply shocks pass through to expectations causes lasting damage. Inflation is already 5.5%, not 3%; expectations are vulnerable.

Option 1 (raise rates +150 bp): recommended. (i) The economy is already overheating from the demand side, so tightening is appropriate even ignoring the supply shock. (ii) Aggressive action communicates credibility — central to keeping inflation expectations anchored. (iii) The 2022-24 episode showed that aggressive Fed tightening combined with eventual supply normalization can deliver "immaculate disinflation" without a deep recession, but only if the central bank moves early enough to anchor expectations. Modest tightening (50 bp) would risk repeating the 2021 mistake of being behind the curve.

Risk: if the supply shock proves long-lasting, aggressive tightening will eventually require accepting a recession. Be prepared to ease if Y falls below Y* by a clear margin. Position: raise rates aggressively. Communicate clearly that the priority is inflation expectations. Reassess at next meeting based on incoming data.
This is a preview — the live textbook auto-grades MCQs and stores progress.

References

Blanchard, O. (2017). Macroeconomics (7th ed.). Pearson.

Blanchard, O., & Bernanke, B. S. (2023). What caused the U.S. pandemic-era inflation? NBER Working Paper No. 31417.

Friedman, M. (1968). The role of monetary policy. American Economic Review, 58(1), 1–17.

Goodfriend, M., & King, R. G. (1997). The new neoclassical synthesis and the role of monetary policy. NBER Macroeconomics Annual, 12, 231–283.

Lucas, R. E. (1973). Some international evidence on output-inflation tradeoffs. American Economic Review, 63(3), 326–334.

Pigou, A. C. (1943). The classical stationary state. Economic Journal, 53(212), 343–351.

Powell, J. (2022, August 26). Monetary policy and price stability. Remarks at the Jackson Hole Economic Policy Symposium.

Rudd, J. B. (2022). Why do we think that inflation expectations matter for inflation? (And should we?). Review of Keynesian Economics, 10(1), 25–45.

Chapter 16 · Part VII: Short-Run Fluctuations

The IS-LM Model

A two-curve framework that has been at the center of macroeconomic policy debates for nearly ninety years — what it gets right, where it broke in 2008, and how modern central banks have quietly replaced it without telling anyone

On December 16, 2008, the Federal Reserve cut its policy interest rate to a range of 0 to 0.25%. The financial crisis had been unfolding for fourteen months; Lehman Brothers had collapsed in September; unemployment was at 7.3% and rising fast. The Fed had used essentially every conventional weapon it had — a 525-basis-point easing cycle from September 2007 to December 2008, the most aggressive rate-cutting campaign in the institution's history. And it had now arrived at zero. For the next seven years, U.S. interest rates would remain at the zero lower bound — the practical floor on how low a central bank can push interest rates, because once rates approach zero, people simply hold paper cash rather than accept negative returns on bonds. Conventional monetary policy was finished.

According to the IS-LM model — the workhorse framework you'll learn in this chapter, taught in nearly every introductory macroeconomics textbook for the past eighty years — what happened next should have been straightforward. The Fed had run out of room to lower rates, so monetary policy could do nothing more; only fiscal policy could pull the economy out of recession. And initially, that's exactly what President Obama and Congress tried, with the $787 billion American Recovery and Reinvestment Act of February 2009. But IS-LM also makes a strong prediction about what happens after monetary policy hits its lower bound: any further increase in the money supply has zero effect on output. This prediction was tested at unprecedented scale between 2008 and 2014 when the Fed expanded its balance sheet from $900 billion to $4.5 trillion through three rounds of quantitative easing. The standard IS-LM model said it shouldn't have worked. It... mostly didn't. And that mostly-didn't-work outcome reshaped how modern macroeconomists think about IS-LM.

This chapter teaches you the IS-LM model carefully because it remains the standard framework taught in every macroeconomics course and used in every policy discussion. It also teaches you why the model broke down after 2008, why post-crisis macroeconomics has quietly replaced the LM curve with something called the monetary policy reaction function — essentially a formula describing how the central bank chooses interest rates in response to inflation and output, instead of choosing a money supply — packaged together as the modern IS-MP framework, and why the 2022-2024 episode revealed yet another wrinkle in how the model maps onto reality. By the end of the chapter, you should be able to draw IS-LM, manipulate it competently, and know exactly where it's misleading.

✦ Sharma's Take

IS-LM was developed in 1937 by John Hicks as a graphical interpretation of John Maynard Keynes's General Theory. For most of the next sixty years, it was THE framework macroeconomists used to think about short-run policy. Every macroeconomics PhD student learned it. Every Fed staff economist used it. Every undergrad textbook from Samuelson through the 1990s built on it. And then it stopped working. The 2008-2014 zero-lower-bound period exposed structural assumptions in the model that had been invisible during normal times. Modern central banks have largely moved on — they think in terms of interest rate rules and inflation targets, not money supplies and LM curves.

But IS-LM still has enormous pedagogical value. It teaches the crucial insight that monetary and fiscal policy interact — that the effects of one depend on what the other is doing — in a way no other simple model captures. It teaches the concept of crowding out. It teaches why central bank independence matters. It teaches the liquidity trap — the situation where conventional monetary policy stops working because interest rates have hit their lower bound. I want you to leave this chapter knowing the standard model AND knowing exactly when not to trust it. That dual literacy is what separates someone who has taken intro macro from someone who actually understands modern monetary policy.

— A.S.

16.1 The Goods Market and the IS Curve

📘 Principles Level — Where output is determined when prices are fixed

The IS curve describes equilibrium in the goods market. In the short run, the framework assumes the overall price level is sticky (this is the same sticky-price assumption we used to derive the upward-sloping SRAS curve in Chapter 15). With prices fixed, output adjusts to clear the market: whatever quantity people want to buy at the current price level, firms will produce. The IS curve traces out the combinations of interest rate (r) and output (Y) that keep this goods-market equilibrium intact.

Why does it slope downward? Because lower interest rates encourage investment — businesses borrow more to build factories, households borrow more to buy cars and houses — which raises total spending and therefore output. Higher rates do the opposite. So lower r ↔ higher Y, which is a downward-sloping curve when r is on the vertical axis and Y is on the horizontal axis.

⚙ Equation Walkthrough — Deriving the IS Curve
Y = C(Y − T) + I(r) + G + NX
Y
Real output (the variable on the horizontal axis of the IS-LM diagram).
C(Y − T)
Consumption as a function of disposable income. As Y rises and T (taxes) is fixed, consumption rises by the marginal propensity to consume (MPC). This builds in the Keynesian spending multiplier.
I(r)
Investment as a function of the interest rate. Crucial: this is where r enters the IS curve. As r rises, I falls. As r falls, I rises. This is the linchpin of the entire IS-LM story.
G
Government spending. Exogenous — chosen by Congress, not by the model. Shifts the IS curve when changed.
NX
Net exports. Treated as exogenous in the simple closed-economy version of IS-LM. Generalizations (the Mundell-Fleming model) make NX depend on the exchange rate.

Reading the IS curve: as r falls, I rises, which raises Y through both the direct increase in I and the multiplier effect on C. So lower r → higher equilibrium Y. The IS curve slopes downward in (r, Y) space.

What shifts IS right: higher G (fiscal stimulus), lower T (tax cuts), surge in business confidence (autonomous I rises), surge in consumer confidence (autonomous C rises), favorable exchange rate movements (NX rises). What shifts IS left: the opposite of each.

⚙ Equation Walkthrough — Why "IS"? The Investment-Saving Identity
I(r) = S(Y) + (T − G) + (−NX)
"Investment = Saving"
I(r)
Desired investment at interest rate r.
S(Y)
Private saving as a function of output.
T − G
Public saving (government surplus). Negative if there's a deficit.
−NX
Foreign saving inflow (capital imports from the rest of the world).

The original Hicksian intuition for the "IS" label: at every point on the IS curve, planned investment equals planned saving. Below the curve, planned I exceeds planned S, causing output to expand. Above the curve, planned S exceeds planned I, causing output to contract. Either way, the economy gravitates toward the IS line. This is the same accounting identity from Chapter 13 (S − I = NX), rearranged for the closed-economy and short-run context.

⚠ Common Pitfall — Confusing the IS curve with a demand curve

The IS curve looks like a downward-sloping demand curve, and students often confuse the two. They are entirely different objects.

A demand curve fixes income and traces out how quantity responds to price. The IS curve fixes the goods-market equilibrium condition (S = I) and traces out how the equilibrium quantity Y responds to the interest rate r. Income is not held constant on the IS curve — it varies along the curve, and the variation matters for the multiplier effect. The IS curve is an equilibrium locus, not a demand schedule. If you remember nothing else from this section, remember: the IS curve answers "what combinations of r and Y are consistent with goods-market equilibrium?", not "how much do people want to buy at each price?"

16.2 The Money Market and the LM Curve

📘 Principles Level — Where the interest rate is determined when money supply is fixed

The LM curve describes equilibrium in the money market. The central bank chooses the money supply M (or so the model assumes). The demand for money depends on two things: it rises with income Y (more transactions need more cash) and falls with the interest rate r (higher r is the opportunity cost of holding non-interest-bearing money instead of bonds). The LM curve traces out the combinations of r and Y at which the demand for real money balances equals the supply.

Why does it slope upward? Because higher Y raises money demand. To keep the money market in equilibrium with a fixed supply, something has to fall — and that something is the willingness to hold money, which falls when r rises. So higher Y must be matched by higher r along the LM curve.

⚙ Equation Walkthrough — Deriving the LM Curve
M / P = L(Y, r)
M / P
Real money supply. M is the nominal money stock (controlled by the central bank). P is the price level (assumed fixed in the short run). Their ratio is the real value of money in the economy.
L(Y, r)
Real money demand. Rises with Y (more income → more transactions → need more money). Falls with r (higher r → bigger opportunity cost of holding money).

Reading the LM curve: if Y rises, money demand rises, and r must rise to bring demand back down to the fixed supply. So higher Y ↔ higher r along the curve. LM slopes upward.

What shifts LM right (downward): the central bank raises the money supply (M ↑) — historically done through open market operations, in which the central bank buys bonds from private banks and pays for them with newly-created reserves, putting more money into the banking system. This means at any Y, equilibrium r is lower. What shifts LM left (upward): central bank cuts M, or P rises (which lowers M/P even with fixed M). The COVID-era M2 expansion (Chapter 12) was an enormous rightward LM shift.

⚙ Equation Walkthrough — Why "LM"? Liquidity Preference and Money
L(Y, r) = M / P
"Liquidity preference = Money supply"
L
Liquidity preference — Keynes's terminology, introduced in his 1936 General Theory. It just means "the demand for money." Keynes called it "liquidity preference" because it captures the public's preference for holding their wealth in liquid form (cash, which is immediately spendable) rather than in less liquid forms (bonds, which earn interest but must be sold first to spend). The function L expresses how strong this preference is at any given combination of income and interest rate.
M
The money supply (controlled by the central bank).

The original Hicksian intuition: at every point on the LM curve, the public's desired holdings of money equal the actual supply. Off the curve, money markets clear by interest-rate adjustment — bond prices rise or fall until people willingly hold the supply of money the central bank has chosen.

16.3 IS-LM Equilibrium: Where the Two Markets Clear Simultaneously

📘 Principles Level — Combining the two curves

The two curves give us two relationships between r and Y: the IS curve (goods-market equilibrium, downward-sloping) and the LM curve (money-market equilibrium, upward-sloping). Both must hold at the same time for the economy to be in short-run equilibrium. Graphically, that means the equilibrium point is the intersection of the two curves. Algebraically, it's the unique pair (r*, Y*) that satisfies both equations simultaneously.

The power of the framework is that it lets you ask: what happens to r and Y when something changes? The answer always involves shifting one or both curves and finding the new intersection.

16.4 Fiscal and Monetary Policy in IS-LM

📘 Principles Level — Two levers, two effects

The IS-LM framework lets us see clearly what each kind of macroeconomic policy does. Fiscal policy (changes in G or T) shifts the IS curve. Monetary policy (changes in M) shifts the LM curve. Each shift has a predictable effect on equilibrium r and Y.

The four basic IS-LM policy experiments
Policy actionCurve that shiftsr changes toY changes to
Fiscal expansion
(higher G or lower T)		 IS shifts right Higher (rises along LM) Higher (but less than the rightward IS shift due to crowding out)
Fiscal contraction
(lower G or higher T)		 IS shifts left Lower Lower
Monetary expansion
(higher M)		 LM shifts right/down Lower (rises along IS) Higher (driven by investment response to lower r)
Monetary contraction
(lower M)		 LM shifts left/up Higher Lower
⚙ Crowding Out — Why Fiscal Stimulus is Smaller Than the Multiplier Suggests
ΔG → ΔY (direct) → ΔY (multiplier) → r ↑ → I(r) ↓ → ΔY shrinks
Step 1
Government spends an additional dollar. Total demand rises by $1 directly.
Step 2
Households earn that dollar, spend MPC of it on consumption. Other households earn that, and so on. Direct multiplier kicks in. Naive prediction: ΔY = ΔG × 1/(1−MPC).
Step 3
Higher Y raises money demand. With the money supply fixed, the interest rate rises (we move along the LM curve).
Step 4
Higher interest rates reduce private investment I(r). Some of the demand created by government spending is offset by reduced private investment. This is crowding out.

The bottom line: the actual change in Y is smaller than the naive multiplier predicts. How much smaller depends on the slope of the LM curve (how interest-rate-sensitive money demand is) and the slope of the IS curve (how interest-rate-sensitive investment is). This is one of the most important practical insights from IS-LM: fiscal stimulus is real, but it doesn't deliver the full multiplier — some of it leaks into higher rates instead of higher output.

Exception: when monetary policy "accommodates" fiscal expansion by also expanding M (shifting LM right alongside IS), crowding out can be avoided. This is what happened during World War II: the Fed pegged interest rates and let the money supply expand to finance war spending without raising r. Output rose dramatically without crowding out.

⚠ Common Pitfall — Believing the IS-LM model gives THE answer about crowding out

Whether crowding out is large or small in any specific situation depends entirely on parameters the model doesn't pin down. The slope of LM depends on how sensitive money demand is to interest rates. The slope of IS depends on how sensitive investment is to interest rates. In severe recessions with high unemployment, real-world estimates suggest crowding out is small and the multiplier is large (fiscal stimulus works well). In booms with full employment, crowding out is large and the multiplier is small. The model frames the question correctly but doesn't answer it. Quantitative estimates require empirical work that goes far beyond the diagram.

16.5 The Liquidity Trap and the Zero Lower Bound

📗 Intermediate Level — When the LM curve goes flat

Suppose the central bank lowers its policy interest rate — the very short-term overnight rate the central bank directly controls (in the U.S. this is the federal funds rate; in the U.K. the Bank Rate; in the eurozone the deposit facility rate) — to zero. Can monetary policy push it lower? In principle, slightly — a few major economies (the European Central Bank, the Bank of Japan, Switzerland) have experimented with mildly negative policy rates. But there's a hard practical limit: if interest rates become sufficiently negative, people just hold paper cash, which yields exactly zero. So rates can't go far below zero. This is the zero lower bound (ZLB) — sometimes called the effective lower bound (ELB) because the true floor is slightly below zero, not exactly at zero.

At the ZLB, monetary policy as conventionally understood is stuck. The central bank can flood the economy with money, but if rates can't fall further, money demand is infinitely elastic at the ZLB — any extra money just sits as bank reserves earning the floor rate. The LM curve becomes horizontal at r = 0. In IS-LM terms, the economy is in a liquidity trap: shifts in the LM curve do nothing to r, and therefore nothing to Y.

This is exactly the prediction that Keynes had warned about in 1936, and what Hicks's IS-LM model formalized in 1937 — though for much of the postwar era many macroeconomists treated the zero-lower-bound case as a theoretical edge case that was unlikely to bind in practice. Then it happened, twice.

2008–2015 United States Zero Lower Bound

Seven Years at the Zero Lower Bound — IS-LM's Most Famous Test

In December 2008, the Federal Reserve cut the federal funds rate to a target range of 0 to 0.25%, where it remained until December 2015. Seven years. During that period, the Fed expanded its balance sheet through three rounds of quantitative easing (QE1 in 2008-10, QE2 in 2010-11, QE3 in 2012-14), growing from $900 billion in 2008 to $4.5 trillion by 2015. In standard IS-LM terms, these were enormous rightward shifts of the LM curve. The standard model's prediction: with rates already at zero, these shifts should do nothing to output. The economy was in a liquidity trap.

The actual outcome was more nuanced. The QE rounds appear to have had some effect on long-term interest rates (which were not at zero) by changing the composition of the central bank's balance sheet, and may have affected inflation expectations. Most empirical studies find QE was modestly stimulative — far less powerful than conventional rate cuts would have been, but not literally zero as standard IS-LM predicted.

Where IS-LM was clearly right: conventional monetary policy hit a wall. The Fed could not stimulate further by cutting rates; it had to invent new tools. And the recovery was painfully slow. Real GDP didn't return to its pre-crisis trend until 2014. Unemployment took until late 2015 to fall below 5%. The 2008-09 episode is the strongest evidence in modern macroeconomic history that the liquidity trap is real, and that policymakers should be very cautious about letting nominal rates get close to zero.

This was also the period that effectively killed the conventional money-supply-based view of monetary policy embedded in LM. The Fed couldn't even define a single useful "money supply" anymore — broader aggregates like M2 grew dramatically, but most of the growth sat as bank reserves rather than circulating in the economy. Trying to track monetary policy through M became hopeless. Central banks shifted decisively to interest-rate-based frameworks.

2020–2022 United States Pandemic-era ZLB

The COVID Lower-Bound Episode — Faster, Sharper, Different

The U.S. hit the ZLB a second time in March 2020. The Fed cut rates from 1.50-1.75% to 0-0.25% in two emergency moves over twelve days. This time, the ZLB period lasted only twenty-two months (until March 2022) rather than seven years. The Fed combined zero rates with another aggressive QE program, expanding its balance sheet from $4.3 trillion to $9 trillion. The recovery was also dramatically faster — unemployment fell from 14.8% (April 2020) to 6.7% (December 2020) to 3.9% (December 2021).

But the COVID ZLB period was different in one critical respect: it was paired with enormous fiscal stimulus. The CARES Act (March 2020), the December 2020 supplemental, and the American Rescue Plan (March 2021) together amounted to nearly $5 trillion of direct fiscal injection — roughly 25% of GDP across three packages. In IS-LM terms, this was an unprecedented rightward IS shift, made possible by the LM curve being flat at zero (no crowding out via interest rates since rates couldn't rise).

This is exactly the IS-LM prescription for a liquidity trap: monetary policy is impotent, so fiscal policy must do the heavy lifting, and the flat LM means no crowding out. The outcome — rapid output recovery, eventually accompanied by inflation — is precisely what the model would predict for that combination of policies. The 2020-2022 episode is in some ways the cleanest large-scale validation of the IS-LM logic about fiscal-monetary interaction at the ZLB that we have.

16.6 The Modern Reformulation: From LM to MP

📗 Intermediate Level — Why central banks no longer think in terms of LM

If you walk into any major central bank today — the Federal Reserve, the European Central Bank, the Bank of England, the Bank of Japan — and ask the staff economists how they think about monetary policy, you will not hear them talk about LM curves or money supplies. You will hear them talk about interest rate rules: the central bank sets the short-term policy rate based on a reaction function that responds to inflation and output. The money supply is whatever it has to be to implement the chosen rate; it is not itself a policy instrument.

This shift in thinking — from "central bank chooses M, the LM curve determines r" to "central bank chooses r directly, M adjusts passively" — has been formalized in what's sometimes called the IS-MP model. The MP curve replaces LM. It traces out the interest rate the central bank will set as a function of output (and inflation, though that's typically suppressed in the simple version). It is usually drawn as a flat or slightly upward-sloping horizontal line at the central bank's chosen rate.

In the IS-MP version, the policy story becomes simpler: fiscal expansion shifts IS right, and unless the central bank "accommodates" by holding rates fixed (flat MP) or raises rates (upward MP shift), output rises until inflation pressure builds, at which point the central bank tightens. There is no LM curve to worry about, no money supply to track, no shift in M that might or might not transmit to rates.

⚙ A Taylor-Style MP Rule

The most influential reaction function in modern monetary policy is the Taylor rule, proposed by Stanford economist John B. Taylor in 1993. The rule says the central bank should respond to two things: how far inflation is from its target, and how far output is from potential. Taylor showed that a simple formula along these lines actually fit how the Fed had behaved from 1987 to 1992 — making it both a recommendation for what central banks should do and a reasonable description of what they actually were doing.

r = r* + φπ(π − π*) + φY(Y − Y*) / Y*
r*
The "neutral" or "equilibrium" real interest rate the central bank would set when inflation is at target and output equals potential.
π − π*
Inflation gap. Positive when inflation exceeds target.
Y − Y*
Output gap. Positive when output exceeds potential.
φ values
Reaction coefficients. Taylor (1993) suggested φπ = 1.5 and φY = 0.5 as reasonable values matching Fed behavior 1987-1992. The Taylor principle — a foundational requirement for stable monetary policy — says that φπ must be greater than 1. Translation: when inflation rises by 1 percentage point, the central bank must raise nominal rates by more than 1 percentage point, so that the real rate (nominal minus inflation) actually rises. If the central bank raises nominal rates by less than the inflation increase, real rates fall, which stimulates demand and pushes inflation higher — a self-reinforcing spiral. The 1970s great inflation is often blamed partly on Fed violations of this principle.

This is the modern monetary policy framework. It quietly replaces LM. The "money market" in LM is gone — replaced by an explicit policy reaction function. The 2022-2024 Fed tightening cycle was a textbook implementation: inflation rose from 2% to 9%, so the Fed raised rates by 525 basis points to bring real rates from deeply negative to positive. That's the φπ term doing its job, exactly as Taylor's rule prescribes.

✦ Sharma's Take — On teaching a model that central banks no longer use

You might reasonably ask: if real central banks have abandoned LM and use Taylor-style rules instead, why are we still teaching IS-LM at all? Three reasons:

First, pedagogical foundations matter. IS-LM teaches you the connection between the goods market and the money market, the concept of crowding out, and the central insight that fiscal and monetary policy interact. These ideas survive in IS-MP — you can't really understand the modern framework without first understanding the older one.

Second, IS-LM at the zero lower bound is exactly the right framework. When the central bank cannot adjust rates because they're stuck at zero, the LM curve is horizontal — and the IS-LM story for fiscal policy is much sharper than the IS-MP story (which assumes the central bank is choosing rates actively). The 2008-2015 and 2020-2022 episodes are best understood through IS-LM.

There is also a closely-related tool worth naming here: forward guidance — the practice of central banks telling markets what they plan to do in the future, in order to influence current behavior. After 2008, when the policy rate could not be cut further, the Fed leaned heavily on forward guidance ("we expect to keep rates exceptionally low at least through mid-2013," for example, in its August 2011 statement). The idea: even if today's short rate is stuck at zero, a credible commitment to keep rates low for years lowers long-term rates immediately, because long rates are essentially averages of expected future short rates. Forward guidance is now a standard part of every major central bank's toolkit.

Third — and this is the most important point — every Fed staff memo, every Brookings paper, every Congressional Budget Office study still cites IS-LM-style reasoning when discussing fiscal-monetary interaction. It is the lingua franca of policy discussion. You need to know it to read what economists are actually writing about. Even economists who don't believe in LM as a literal description of the money market still use IS-LM diagrams to communicate.

So: learn IS-LM. Use it well. Know its limits. And remember that when you read modern central bank speeches, the framework is closer to IS-MP than IS-LM — but the basic intuitions about fiscal-monetary interaction are unchanged.

— A.S.

16.7 Interactive: The IS-LM Diagram

🎛 Interactive — IS-LM Equilibrium

Shift IS (fiscal policy) and LM (monetary policy); see how r and Y respond

What you're seeing: the blue IS curve slopes down (lower r → higher Y via investment); the red LM curve slopes up (higher Y → higher r to keep money market clearing). The green dot is the equilibrium. Dashed lines show the original (pre-shock) curves. Notice in the 2008 preset that even with a huge LM rightward shift, equilibrium r doesn't fall much because we hit the zero lower bound — represented by the LM curve flattening at low r values.

16.8 Worked Examples

📐 Worked Example 16.1 — Tracing a fiscal expansion
An economy is in IS-LM equilibrium at Y = $20 trillion and r = 3%. Congress passes a $500 billion increase in government spending. The marginal propensity to consume is 0.7. The interest sensitivity of investment is such that each 1 percentage point rise in r reduces investment by $80 billion. Trace the effects on r and Y, and compute the crowding-out effect.
1
Naive multiplier effect (ignoring r): multiplier = 1 / (1 − MPC) = 1 / 0.3 ≈ 3.33. So ΔY without crowding out would be 3.33 × $500B = $1.67 trillion.
2
IS curve shifts right. At unchanged r = 3%, equilibrium Y would rise by $1.67T. But this raises money demand, pulling us up the LM curve.
3
New equilibrium: the actual change in (r, Y) depends on both curves' slopes. Suppose LM is moderately steep — let r rise by 0.8 percentage points to 3.8%. Crowding-out check: the higher r reduces I by 0.8 × $80B = $64B.
4
Actual ΔY: the $64B reduction in I, multiplied through, gives $64B × 3.33 ≈ $213B reduction from the naive effect. So actual ΔY ≈ $1.67T − $213B ≈ $1.46 trillion. Effective multiplier ≈ 1.46 / 0.5 ≈ 2.9, lower than the naive 3.33.
The fiscal expansion did most of what was intended — but about 13% of the effect was crowded out by higher interest rates reducing private investment. The size of the crowding-out effect depends critically on the LM slope. In a deep recession with low interest sensitivity of money demand (LM nearly flat), crowding out is minimal. In a hot economy with high sensitivity (LM steep), crowding out dominates. This is why the same dollar of fiscal stimulus has wildly different effects in different macroeconomic environments — a fact often missed in political debates about stimulus.
📐 Worked Example 16.2 — Monetary policy at and away from the zero lower bound
Compare two cases. Case A: the economy is in a normal equilibrium at Y* and r = 4%. The Fed undertakes a monetary expansion that shifts LM rightward by an amount that would lower r by 1 percentage point. Case B: the economy is in a deep recession with Y well below Y* and r already at 0%. The Fed undertakes the same-sized LM shift. Trace and compare the output effects.
1
Case A — normal times: LM shift lowers r from 4% to 3%. This stimulates investment by I'(r) × ΔR per the IS curve. Y rises along the unshifted IS curve to a new higher equilibrium. Standard monetary stimulus works through the interest rate channel.
2
Case B — at the ZLB: the LM shift cannot push r below 0% because banks would prefer to hold paper cash. So r remains at 0%. Without a change in r, investment doesn't change. Without a change in investment, Y doesn't change. The monetary expansion has zero effect on output.
3
The economic intuition: in Case A, banks hold reserves only reluctantly because they earn nothing while loans earn 4%. Extra reserves get lent out, money multiplies through the banking system, interest rates fall. In Case B, banks already earn 0% on loans — extra reserves just sit on the central bank's books. The transmission mechanism is broken.
This is the formal IS-LM prediction of monetary impotence at the ZLB. Empirically the 2008-2015 episode produced a more nuanced result — quantitative easing affected long-term rates and asset prices even with short rates pinned at zero, so the effect was not literally zero but was much smaller than equivalent rate cuts in normal times would have been. The textbook IS-LM prediction was directionally correct: at the ZLB, monetary policy is much weaker. This is why economists pivoted so strongly to fiscal policy as the primary tool after 2008.
🎮 MacroNations Connection — IS-LM in the Engine

The MacroNations engine uses the modern IS-MP convention rather than classical IS-LM: you set the interest_rate_target directly (not the money supply), and the engine handles monetary mechanics. The IS-curve logic still applies: gov_spending_pct and tax_rate shift the IS curve via the 1.4× and −1.1× multipliers respectively. Crowding-out is partial in this engine: when you raise gov_spending_pct, the immediate GDP boost arrives via the multiplier, but inflation rises via the Phillips curve, which forces real interest rates higher and dampens future investment. Unlike textbook IS-LM, the engine doesn't have a separate money-market equilibrium — the LM curve is effectively flat at whatever rate you set. The IS-MP framework from §16.7 maps better to the engine than classical IS-LM does.

U.S. Private Fixed Investment — Quarterly, Last 25 Years
Source: BEA via FRED (Series: FPIC1). Investment is the key channel through which interest rate changes affect output in IS-LM. Notice the 2008-09 collapse (investment fell about 25% peak-to-trough), the slow recovery during the ZLB period, and the renewed weakness during 2022-23 as the Fed raised rates rapidly. Investment is far more cyclical than consumption — the textbook IS-LM prediction that the interest rate channel works mainly through investment is well-supported by the data.
U.S. 10-Year Treasury Rate — Daily, Last 25 Years
Source: Board of Governors via FRED (Series: GS10). The 10-year rate captures longer-term interest rate expectations that affect investment more directly than the overnight federal funds rate. Note the historic lows during 2020 (under 1%), reflecting both the Fed's ZLB stance and broader pessimism about long-run growth — and the dramatic spike in 2022-23 as inflation surged and the Fed signaled aggressive tightening.

Chapter 16 Quiz

IS curve, LM curve, equilibrium, crowding out, ZLB, modern reformulation
Part A — Multiple Choice (Principles)
1. The IS curve slopes downward because:
A Higher prices reduce real wealth
B Lower interest rates stimulate investment, raising equilibrium output
C Higher output raises money demand
D The central bank raises rates when output rises
2. The LM curve slopes upward because:
A Higher Y raises money demand, which requires higher r to clear the money market at fixed M
B Lower r stimulates investment
C The central bank cuts rates when Y rises
D Higher prices reduce real money balances
3. An increase in government spending in the IS-LM model causes:
A Higher Y and lower r
B Higher Y and higher r
C Lower Y and lower r
D No change in either
4. "Crowding out" refers to:
A Government spending displacing private consumption
B Higher interest rates from fiscal expansion reducing private investment
C The Fed selling bonds in open market operations
D Foreign imports replacing domestic production
Part B — Multiple Choice (Intermediate)
5. At the zero lower bound, the IS-LM model predicts that an increase in the money supply will:
A Sharply raise output
B Have essentially zero effect on output
C Sharply lower output
D Lower interest rates to negative territory
6. The modern IS-MP reformulation replaces the LM curve with:
A A Phillips curve
B An aggregate supply curve
C A monetary policy reaction function (e.g., a Taylor rule)
D A money multiplier function
7. The "Taylor principle" requires that the central bank's response coefficient on inflation, φπ, be greater than 1. The reason is:
A To match historical Fed behavior since 1900
B So that the real interest rate rises when inflation rises — making monetary policy stabilizing rather than destabilizing
C To ensure that fiscal policy is always crowded out completely
D To prevent currency depreciation
Part C — Numerical Problems
8. An economy starts at IS-LM equilibrium with Y = $25 trillion and r = 2%. The simple multiplier (no crowding out) is 2.5. Congress passes a $400 billion fiscal stimulus. After the stimulus, equilibrium r rises to 3% and Y rises to $25.7 trillion. (a) What would Y have been if there were zero crowding out? (b) Compute the actual ΔY observed. (c) How much output was "lost" to crowding out? (d) Compute the effective multiplier (actual ΔY ÷ ΔG). (e) Comment on what conditions would have made the crowding-out effect larger or smaller.
Model answer: (a) Naive ΔY = 2.5 × $400B = $1.0T, so Y* = $25T + $1.0T = $26.0T. (b) Actual ΔY = $25.7T − $25T = $0.7T. (c) Crowding-out loss = $1.0T − $0.7T = $300B. (d) Effective multiplier = $0.7T / $0.4T = 1.75, lower than the naive 2.5. (e) Larger crowding out if: economy near full employment (steep LM), high interest sensitivity of investment, central bank refuses to accommodate. Smaller crowding out if: economy in deep recession with slack capacity (flat LM at low rates), low interest sensitivity, central bank accommodates by holding rates steady. The 2009 ARRA stimulus operated under near-zero crowding out because of the ZLB; the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act faced significant crowding out because the Fed was simultaneously tightening.
9. The Fed faces inflation at 5% (target 2%) and output 2% above potential. Using a Taylor rule with r* = 1%, φπ = 1.5, φY = 0.5: (a) Compute the rule-prescribed real policy rate. (b) Suppose the rule-prescribed rate is the Fed's target, and current expected inflation is 5%. What is the implied nominal policy rate? (c) Compare to what would happen if φπ = 0.5 (violating the Taylor principle): would real rates rise or fall when inflation rises? Why is this destabilizing? (d) Use this to explain a key reason the 1970s "great inflation" persisted.
Model answer: (a) r = 1% + 1.5(5−2) + 0.5(2) = 1% + 4.5% + 1% = 6.5% real rate. (b) Nominal = real + expected inflation = 6.5% + 5% = 11.5%. (c) With φπ = 0.5: r = 1% + 0.5(3) + 0.5(2) = 3.5% real rate. Now check: as inflation rises 1 pp, nominal rises only 0.5 pp, so REAL rates FALL by 0.5 pp. Lower real rates stimulate spending, which pushes inflation higher, which lowers real rates further. Self-reinforcing spiral. (d) In the late 1960s and early 1970s, the Fed under Arthur Burns systematically failed the Taylor principle — raising nominal rates by less than inflation rose, allowing real rates to fall as inflation accelerated. This destabilizing behavior is widely seen as a key cause of the 1970s great inflation. Volcker's tightening in 1980-82 finally implemented something like the Taylor principle, brutally — pushing real rates to near 10% before inflation broke.
Part D — Policy Scenario
Scenario: You advise a central bank during a deep financial crisis. Output has fallen 6% below potential. Unemployment has risen from 4% to 9%. Inflation has fallen from 2% to −0.5% (mild deflation). The policy rate is already at 0.25% — essentially at the lower bound. The legislature has just passed a $2 trillion fiscal stimulus package representing about 8% of GDP. The Treasury asks you whether the central bank should: (1) Commit to keeping rates at zero for at least three years to maximize the fiscal effect. (2) Undertake additional quantitative easing alongside the fiscal package. (3) Stay neutral and let the fiscal package work on its own.
10. Write a 250-word memo to the central bank Governor. Reference: the IS-LM framework, the liquidity-trap analysis, the 2008-2015 empirical evidence, and the interaction between fiscal and monetary policy at the ZLB. Take a clear position.
Model answer (sketch): The economy is in a clear liquidity trap. Standard IS-LM analysis predicts that monetary expansion alone has little effect when r is at the ZLB — the 2008-2015 experience confirmed this directionally. Fiscal policy is the dominant tool in this regime; the standard fiscal multiplier is amplified because there is no crowding out via higher rates (the LM curve is flat at zero).

Option 3 (stay neutral): rejected. Doing nothing risks the recovery losing momentum if inflation expectations fall further toward deflation. Active monetary commitment matters even when conventional policy is constrained.

Option 2 (additional QE): helpful but secondary. The 2008-2015 QE rounds appear to have modestly compressed long-term rates and risk premia, with measurable but smaller effects than equivalent conventional rate cuts. QE can be part of the toolkit but should not be the centerpiece.

Option 1 (three-year zero-rate commitment): recommended. The IS-LM logic at the ZLB strongly supports complementing fiscal stimulus with credible forward guidance. Committing to keep rates at zero throughout the recovery: (i) maximizes the IS-curve effect of the fiscal package, since the flat LM ensures no crowding out throughout the multi-year transmission of stimulus; (ii) raises expected inflation modestly, which lowers real rates further and stimulates investment; (iii) anchors long-term rates lower by signaling. Risk: if inflation surges later, the commitment becomes embarrassing. But that's a future problem; today's risk is deflation. Pair Option 1 with Option 2 (QE) as a secondary support. Position: commit to zero rates for three years; supplement with QE as needed. The IS-LM-at-the-ZLB framework recommends precisely this fiscal-monetary configuration, and the 2008-2015 experience supports it.
This is a preview — the live textbook auto-grades MCQs and stores progress.

References

Bernanke, B. S., Reinhart, V. R., & Sack, B. P. (2004). Monetary policy alternatives at the zero bound. Brookings Papers on Economic Activity, 2004(2), 1–100.

Eggertsson, G. B., & Krugman, P. (2012). Debt, deleveraging, and the liquidity trap. Quarterly Journal of Economics, 127(3), 1469–1513.

Hicks, J. R. (1937). Mr. Keynes and the "classics": A suggested interpretation. Econometrica, 5(2), 147–159.

Keynes, J. M. (1936). The general theory of employment, interest and money. Macmillan.

Krugman, P. (1998). It's baaack: Japan's slump and the return of the liquidity trap. Brookings Papers on Economic Activity, 1998(2), 137–205.

Romer, D. (2000). Keynesian macroeconomics without the LM curve. Journal of Economic Perspectives, 14(2), 149–169.

Taylor, J. B. (1993). Discretion versus policy rules in practice. Carnegie-Rochester Conference Series on Public Policy, 39, 195–214.

Woodford, M. (2003). Interest and prices: Foundations of a theory of monetary policy. Princeton University Press.

Chapter 17 · Part VII: Short-Run Fluctuations

Monetary Policy

How central banks actually conduct monetary policy in the real world — the institutional structure, the toolkit beyond the textbook diagram, the unprecedented 2022-2024 tightening cycle, and the dramatic August 2025 framework shift no other textbook has yet covered

On March 16, 2022, the Federal Open Market Committee raised the federal funds rate target by 25 basis points — its first increase since 2018, and the start of what would become the most aggressive monetary tightening cycle in 40 years. Inflation that month was 8.5% year-over-year, the highest reading since 1981. Over the next 18 months, the Federal Reserve would raise rates 11 times, lifting the target range from 0–0.25% all the way to 5.25–5.50% — a total of 525 basis points, the fastest increase since Paul Volcker's brutal disinflation of 1980-82. And by mid-2024, inflation had fallen to under 3%, output was still growing, and unemployment was still below 4%. The textbook prediction — that this kind of tightening should cause a deep recession — had been violated. Whether this outcome reflected exceptional Fed skill, exceptional luck, or both is a question economists will debate for years.

This chapter is about how central banks actually do monetary policy. Chapter 16 gave us the IS-LM and IS-MP frameworks. Those are the models economists use to think about monetary policy. This chapter is about the institutions — the Federal Reserve, the European Central Bank, the Bank of England — and the actual mechanics of how they translate a policy decision into changes in interest rates, asset prices, and ultimately the broader economy. It's also about the most consequential monetary policy episode of our lifetime, the 2022-2024 tightening cycle, and the framework shift at Jackson Hole in August 2025 that ended the Fed's controversial five-year experiment with "Flexible Average Inflation Targeting."

✦ Sharma's Take

Introductory treatments of monetary policy often present it as something close to a frictionless exercise: the central bank picks a money supply or an interest rate, the IS-LM diagram tells you what happens to output and prices. End of story. Real monetary policy looks nothing like this. Central banks operate in a fog of uncertainty about where the economy actually is, debate fiercely among themselves about what to do, communicate carefully to avoid market disruption, weigh political pressures, and frequently get things wrong — sometimes catastrophically. Understanding monetary policy without understanding the institutional reality is like understanding war by reading the rulebook of chess.

The 2022-2024 episode is the central case study of this chapter because it tested almost every theoretical proposition about how monetary policy works. The Fed entered with a relatively new framework (FAIT, adopted August 2020) that explicitly committed to running inflation hot. Then inflation surged, the framework became an obstacle to fighting it, and the Fed had to reverse course aggressively. The success of the 2023-24 disinflation — without a recession — was unexpected by most economists. Understanding why it worked, and whether it can be repeated, is the central question of monetary policy today.

— A.S.

17.1 The Central Bank's Mandate

📘 Principles Level — What is the central bank actually trying to achieve?

Every central bank operates under a mandate — a legal and political statement of what its monetary policy is supposed to achieve. The mandate determines what the central bank prioritizes when choices conflict — for example, when fighting inflation might cause unemployment to rise.

Central bank mandates vary across countries in ways that matter. The Federal Reserve has a dual mandate — established by the Federal Reserve Reform Act of 1977 — which requires the Fed to promote both "maximum employment" and "stable prices." The European Central Bank, by contrast, has a hierarchical mandate: price stability is the primary objective; supporting general economic policies is secondary, and only allowed when it doesn't conflict with price stability. The Bank of England, the Bank of Canada, and the Reserve Bank of New Zealand all have hierarchical mandates focused primarily on inflation. Most modern central banks have settled on some version of these two models.

When economists talk about the Fed having a "dual mandate," they mean the 1977 statutory requirement to pursue both stable prices and maximum employment simultaneously. This sounds simple but creates genuine policy dilemmas. If unemployment is high and inflation is also high (as in 1979 or, briefly, in 2022), which problem do you fight? The Fed's answer has evolved over decades: in the late 1970s it tried to fight unemployment first and inflation took off; under Volcker (1979 onward) it fought inflation aggressively and accepted high unemployment; under Greenspan, Bernanke, Yellen, and the early Powell years it generally treated the two as complementary because inflation stayed low. The 2022-2024 cycle was the first major test of the dual mandate under genuinely conflicting conditions in 40 years.

⚙ A Numerical Inflation Target
π* = 2% (for nearly every major central bank)
π*
The central bank's inflation target. In the U.S., this was made explicit by the Fed only in January 2012, when it announced a 2% target measured by the Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) price index. The ECB, Bank of England, Bank of Canada, and Bank of Japan all have similar 2% targets. The Reserve Bank of Australia targets 2–3%. Why 2% and not 0%? Because measured inflation slightly overstates true inflation (quality improvements are hard to measure), and because a small positive target gives the central bank room to cut real rates in a recession without hitting the zero lower bound. The exact number is somewhat arbitrary, but the principle — anchored expectations around a publicly-known target — has been widely accepted since the 1990s.

17.2 Who Decides? The FOMC and Its Counterparts

📘 Principles Level — Decision-making bodies

Monetary policy decisions are not made by one person. In the U.S., they are made by the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) — the Fed's policy-setting body. The FOMC has twelve voting members: the seven Governors of the Federal Reserve Board (including the Chair), the President of the Federal Reserve Bank of New York (permanent vote), and four of the other eleven regional Fed Bank Presidents (rotating votes). The Chair (currently Jerome Powell, whose term runs until May 2026) sets the agenda and speaks for the committee, but does not unilaterally decide.

The FOMC meets eight times per year on a published schedule. Each meeting produces a statement, a Summary of Economic Projections (SEP) four times a year including the famous "dot plot" showing each member's expected rate path, and a press conference by the Chair. Six weeks later, the meeting minutes are released. This is one of the most carefully orchestrated communication exercises in any government — every word is scrutinized by markets for shifts in tone.

The European Central Bank's Governing Council operates similarly, with 25 voting members (six members of the Executive Board plus 19 national central bank governors, with rotating voting rights for some). The Bank of England's Monetary Policy Committee has nine voting members. The committee structure matters because it institutionalizes deliberation and slows down decision-making — a feature, not a bug, in environments where rash decisions can be very costly.

17.3 The Conventional Toolkit

📘 Principles Level — Three conventional tools

Before the 2008 crisis, central banks operated mostly with three conventional tools. After 2008, they had to invent several new ones (covered in §17.4). The three traditional tools are: open market operations, the discount rate, and reserve requirements.

The three conventional monetary policy tools
ToolWhat it isFrequency of use
Open market operations The central bank buys or sells government bonds in the open market. Buying bonds injects reserves into the banking system and lowers the policy rate; selling bonds drains reserves and raises the rate. This is the primary tool for steering the federal funds rate day-to-day. Continuous (daily, sometimes multiple times per day)
The discount rate The interest rate the Fed charges banks that borrow directly from it (through the "discount window"). Banks turn to the discount window when they can't borrow in the federal funds market. The Fed sets this rate slightly above the policy rate, so borrowing from the discount window carries a stigma — banks only use it when they have to. Rare for most banks; spikes during crises (March 2020, March 2023 banking turmoil)
Reserve requirements The fraction of deposits banks must hold as reserves rather than lending out. Setting higher requirements forces banks to keep more cash on hand and reduces lending; lower requirements free banks to lend more. Historically a major tool in the 1950s-60s; gradually deemphasized as financial markets evolved. Effectively zero. The Fed reduced reserve requirements to 0% in March 2020 and has not changed them since.
⚠ Common Pitfall — "The Fed sets interest rates"

You'll often hear journalists say the Fed "raised interest rates" by 25 basis points. Strictly speaking, the Fed doesn't directly set any interest rate. What it does is set a target range for the federal funds rate — the overnight rate at which banks lend to each other — and then use open market operations to keep the actual federal funds rate within that target range. All other interest rates in the economy (mortgages, business loans, Treasury yields, credit card rates, etc.) are set by private actors in private markets. They respond to the federal funds rate, but they're not set by the Fed.

This distinction matters because the Fed's control over the broader economy is indirect. It can't make a 30-year mortgage rate fall by 25 basis points just by deciding so; it can only adjust the very-short-term rate and hope that the rest of the yield curve adjusts in roughly the way it wants. Sometimes the rest of the curve doesn't cooperate — like in 2022, when the Fed raised the federal funds rate aggressively but long-term Treasury yields lagged behind for months, frustrating the tightening campaign. The Fed sets a price for overnight reserves. Everything else is the market's response.

⚙ How a Rate Decision Reaches the Real Economy: Five Transmission Channels
1. Interest rate
The most direct channel. A rate cut reduces borrowing costs across the term structure. Households spend more on cars and houses; firms invest more in capital. The standard IS-LM transmission. Effective in normal times; impotent at the zero lower bound.
2. Exchange rate
Lower rates make domestic-currency assets less attractive to foreign investors, weakening the currency. A weaker currency raises exports and reduces imports, boosting net exports. Important for small open economies; modest for the U.S.
3. Asset prices
Lower rates raise asset prices: lower discount rates raise present values of future earnings, so stocks rise; mortgage rates fall, so house prices rise. Higher asset prices raise household wealth, which raises consumption. This channel became especially important in the QE era as long-rate manipulation became a primary Fed tool.
4. Credit
Lower rates improve banks' net interest margins and balance sheets, making them more willing to lend. Borrowers in turn find credit easier to obtain. This channel was crucial in the 2008 crisis when the credit channel collapsed even though the Fed had cut rates to zero.
5. Expectations
Perhaps the most important channel in modern monetary policy. The Fed's commitment to a future path of rates (forward guidance) and its credibility on inflation anchor expectations. If markets expect rates to stay low for years, long rates fall today. If markets expect the Fed will fight inflation aggressively, wage and price-setters moderate their expectations of future inflation today. Communication is policy.

17.4 The Unconventional Toolkit: What 2008 Forced the Fed to Invent

📘 Principles Level — Beyond the policy rate

When the federal funds rate hit zero in December 2008 and stayed there for seven years, the Fed couldn't use its primary conventional tool any further. So it invented new ones. These unconventional tools — sometimes lumped together as "non-traditional monetary policy" — are now permanent features of the modern Fed toolkit. Three deserve careful attention.

⚙ Three Unconventional Tools
QE
Quantitative Easing (introduced in Chapter 12). The central bank buys long-term assets (Treasury bonds, mortgage-backed securities) directly from the market, expanding its balance sheet and pushing down long-term interest rates. Used in three rounds 2008-2014, growing the Fed balance sheet from $900B to $4.5T; then again in 2020-2022 expanding it from $4.3T to $9T. The effects are debated empirically: most studies find QE compresses long-term rates by 50-100 basis points and supports asset prices, but the boost to real output is smaller than conventional rate cuts of equivalent size.
FG
Forward Guidance (introduced in Chapter 16). The central bank communicates its intentions for future policy in order to influence today's long-term rates and expectations. Two forms: Delphic guidance describes the central bank's economic outlook ("we expect inflation to return to 2%"); Odyssean guidance commits the central bank to a specific future action ("we will not raise rates before 2024 regardless of conditions"). Odyssean guidance is more powerful but harder to credibly commit to.
QT
Quantitative Tightening — the reverse of QE. The central bank lets its bond holdings run off (mature without reinvestment) or actively sells them, shrinking its balance sheet. Used by the Fed from 2017-2019 (modest) and again from June 2022 onward (more aggressive, alongside rate hikes). Reduces the supply of reserves in the banking system, pushing up long-term rates and tightening financial conditions.

A fourth tool deserves a mention: yield curve control (YCC). Under YCC, the central bank doesn't just buy bonds; it commits to buy whatever quantity is needed to keep a specific long-term yield at a target level. The Bank of Japan adopted YCC in 2016 and used it until 2024. The Federal Reserve has discussed YCC in internal papers but has never implemented it; the Fed's preference for flexibility over hard commitments has kept it off the U.S. table. YCC is the most aggressive forward-guidance commitment available — it backs the promise with whatever balance-sheet expansion is required to enforce it.

17.5 Central Bank Independence

📗 Intermediate Level — Why the Fed isn't run by the President

One of the most consequential design choices in any country's monetary policy framework is the degree of central bank independence — how insulated the central bank is from short-term political pressure. The Federal Reserve was created in 1913 with substantial protections: the seven Governors serve fourteen-year staggered terms (so no single President can replace the entire board), the Chair serves a four-year renewable term (so the President can replace one person every four years), and the Fed's operating budget comes from its own portfolio earnings rather than congressional appropriations (so Congress can't defund or starve it).

Why bother with independence? Because the political incentive is almost always to keep interest rates too low. Low rates make voters feel richer, raise asset prices, lower the cost of financing government debt, and boost short-term employment. Low rates also tend to generate inflation eventually — but the inflation comes after the next election. An elected official's incentive is to push rates down before each election, accept the inflation later, and let someone else clean up. The economic literature calls this the time-inconsistency problem in monetary policy: what's optimal in the long run (a credible commitment to low inflation) conflicts with what's optimal in the short run (looser policy that boosts employment temporarily). Independent central banks exist primarily to solve this problem.

✦ Sharma's Take — Independence under pressure

The empirical evidence on central bank independence is robust: countries with more independent central banks have lower inflation on average, and don't have systematically worse unemployment outcomes. This finding is one of the cleanest results in applied macroeconomics, replicated across dozens of studies and decades of data. It's why nearly every country has moved toward greater central bank independence since the 1980s — Germany's Bundesbank was the historical model, the Bank of England was made independent in 1997, the ECB was created with strong independence baked into the Maastricht Treaty.

That said, independence is never absolute, and political pressure on central banks has become a more visible issue in recent years. During his first term, President Trump publicly criticized Fed Chair Jerome Powell repeatedly for not cutting rates fast enough; in his second term, that pressure has intensified, with several public threats to fire Powell (the legal authority to do so is contested and untested).
