Other countries — Turkey under Erdoğan, Argentina under various administrations — have demonstrated what happens when central bank independence is genuinely undermined: inflation rises, the currency collapses, and economic credibility evaporates.

Whether the long-running consensus on central bank independence holds in the next decade is one of the most important open questions in macroeconomics. The Fed's success in the 2022-2024 disinflation — which required maintaining tight policy despite political pressure — was a strong vindication of the independence model. Whether that success can be repeated under more sustained political pressure remains to be seen.

— A.S.

17.6 The Fed's Framework: FAIT and Its 2025 Reversal

📗 Intermediate Level — A framework, an experiment, and a reversal

A monetary policy framework is the central bank's written statement of how it interprets its mandate and how it intends to respond to economic conditions. The Fed announced its first formal numerical inflation target (2%) in January 2012, after decades without a public target. It published a "Statement on Longer-Run Goals and Monetary Policy Strategy" that has been reviewed and revised twice — in 2020 and again in 2025.

The 2020 review concluded in August 2020 with one of the most consequential framework changes in Fed history: the adoption of Flexible Average Inflation Targeting (FAIT). Under FAIT, the Fed committed to allowing inflation to run modestly above 2% for some time following periods when inflation had run below 2%, so that average inflation over time would equal 2%. The motivation was honest: from 2012 to 2020, inflation had run persistently below 2% (averaging about 1.5%), the Fed had repeatedly failed to lift it to target, and Fed officials worried that low expected inflation was constraining policy effectiveness near the zero lower bound. FAIT was meant to anchor expectations higher by signaling that the Fed would not preemptively tighten as soon as inflation reached 2%.

The timing was unfortunate. The framework was adopted in August 2020 — six months into a pandemic that would unleash the largest fiscal stimulus in modern history and trigger an inflation surge unlike anything since the early 1980s. From August 2020 forward, every time inflation rose above 2%, the FAIT framework gave the Fed a reason to wait — inflation had been below target for years; some "make-up" was appropriate. Inflation rose to 7% by the end of 2021. The Fed continued purchasing assets (QE) until March 2022 and didn't begin raising rates until March 2022, when inflation was already at 8.5%. The framework that was meant to anchor expectations higher when inflation was too low became an obstacle to fighting inflation when inflation was too high.

August 2025 Jackson Hole Framework reversal

The End of FAIT — Powell's Jackson Hole Reversal

At the annual Jackson Hole Economic Policy Symposium in August 2025, Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell announced that the Fed was abandoning Flexible Average Inflation Targeting and returning to plain Flexible Inflation Targeting (FIT) — the framework the Fed had used from 2012 to 2020. The reversal was substantively significant. Under FIT, the Fed treats inflation deviations symmetrically: 1% above target is just as bad as 1% below target. There is no "make-up" requirement, and no commitment to let inflation run hot following periods of undershooting.

The motivation, as Fed staff papers have documented, was clear: FAIT had probably contributed to the post-2020 inflation surge by delaying the Fed's tightening response. A 2025 Dallas Fed working paper estimated that FAIT raised CPI inflation by about 1 percentage point over 2020-2022 compared to a counterfactual without FAIT. Other Fed officials concluded — somewhat more critically — that FAIT was simply too complicated to communicate effectively: markets and the public could never quite figure out how the Fed would respond to varying conditions under an "average" framework, which is the opposite of what a framework is supposed to do. Brookings papers in 2024 (e.g., Eric Swanson) had concluded that FAIT was "a giant step backward in clarity and communication and accountability."

The 2025 framework change matters for two reasons. First, it explicitly acknowledges that the 2020 framework contributed to the 2021-22 inflation problem — a meaningful institutional learning. Second, it signals that the Fed expects to operate in an environment where inflation surprises in both directions are now plausible, not just persistently-too-low inflation. The post-2008 world of permanent disinflation worries has, at least for now, ended.

17.7 The 2022-2024 Tightening Cycle: The Defining Episode

2022–2024 United States The Powell tightening

Fastest Tightening in 40 Years — And the Soft Landing That Wasn't Supposed to Happen

By the end of 2021, U.S. inflation had reached 7% and was still accelerating. The federal funds rate was at 0–0.25%. The Fed had been buying $120 billion of assets per month for nearly two years. Real rates were deeply negative. By any standard measure, monetary policy was wildly too loose for the inflation environment. The Fed faced a genuine policy challenge — but it also faced its own credibility being at risk if it didn't act decisively.

March 2022: First rate hike, 25 basis points. The Fed began tapering its asset purchases and signaled aggressive tightening ahead. Inflation that month: 8.5%.

May–June 2022: 50 and then 75 basis-point hikes, the first 75-bp move since 1994. Inflation peaked at 9.1% in June. The Fed sent unmistakable signals it would prioritize inflation over employment.

July through December 2022: Three more 75-bp hikes, then a 50-bp hike in December. By year-end, the federal funds rate target was 4.25–4.50%. Inflation had begun easing — CPI fell from 9.1% (June) to 6.5% (December).

2023: Smaller hikes (25 bp) continued through July, reaching 5.25–5.50%. The Fed then paused. Inflation fell from 6.5% (December 2022) to 3.4% (December 2023). Unemployment stayed near 3.7%. GDP grew 2.5% in real terms. This was not how the playbook said it should go.

2024: The Fed held rates steady until September, then cut by 50 bp. Inflation fell to under 3% by mid-year, then drifted back up modestly later. Unemployment crept up to 4.2% by year-end. Real GDP grew 2.8%.

What made the disinflation work without a recession? Several explanations have been offered, none completely satisfactory:

  • Supply normalization: much of the 2021-22 inflation was supply-side (Chapter 15's SRAS shift). As supply chains repaired, energy markets stabilized, and labor markets cooled from their record-tight state, SRAS shifted right, lowering prices without requiring lower demand.
  • Anchored expectations: despite the surge in actual inflation, long-run inflation expectations (measured by market and survey indicators) never moved meaningfully above 2.5%. The Fed's aggressive action reinforced credibility, preventing wage-price spirals.
  • Wealth and consumption resilience: households entered the tightening cycle with unusually strong balance sheets (savings from pandemic stimulus, low debt service costs from years of low rates), buffering them against rate hikes.
  • Labor market repair without unemployment: as discussed in Chapter 14, the Beveridge curve shifted favorably — vacancies fell from 12 million to 8 million without unemployment rising much, suggesting better matching efficiency rather than a demand-driven recession.

Whether these factors will repeat in the next inflation episode is genuinely uncertain. The 2022-2024 disinflation is the canonical case study for monetary policy in our generation, but it should not be taken as a recipe — it depended on conditions that may not recur.

17.8 Rules vs. Discretion

📗 Intermediate Level — Should monetary policy follow a fixed rule?

One of the longest-running debates in monetary economics is whether central banks should follow a fixed rule (e.g., always set rates by the Taylor rule formula) or instead exercise discretion (judge each situation case-by-case). The case for rules: transparency, predictability, accountability, immunity to political pressure, anchored expectations. The case for discretion: ability to respond to novel circumstances, ability to use judgment about supply versus demand shocks, ability to handle non-economic emergencies (financial crises, pandemics) that no rule could anticipate.

Real central banks have settled on something in between: they communicate as if they follow a rule (the Fed's framework statement; the ECB's strategy statement; the published inflation targets), but they preserve substantial discretion in practice. The Fed has never officially committed to a Taylor rule even though its behavior since the 1980s is reasonably well-described by one. Milton Friedman's famous 1968 proposal of a fixed money-supply growth rule was never adopted by any major central bank; the closest implementation was Volcker's 1979-82 monetarist experiment, which was effective at breaking inflation but volatile in its impact on the real economy.

⚠ Why pure rules never quite work

Every fixed rule eventually encounters circumstances its designers didn't anticipate. The classic Taylor rule prescribes a specific federal funds rate based on inflation and output gaps — but what should the rule say when rates hit zero? When there's a financial panic? When inflation is supply-driven? When markets are pricing in a war? The literature has answered each of these with rule modifications — augmented Taylor rules with ZLB caveats, financial stability adjustments, supply-shock buffers — but the proliferation of caveats undermines the original case for a rule, which was simplicity and clarity. Today's mainstream view: rules are useful as benchmarks, but central banks need flexibility to deviate when conditions warrant. The art is in deciding when.

17.9 Interactive: The Taylor Rule Calculator

🎛 Interactive — Taylor Rule

Set current inflation and the output gap; see the rule-prescribed policy rate

What you're seeing: the rule-prescribed real rate (the policy real interest rate the Taylor rule suggests) shown as the blue bar, and the implied nominal rate as the red bar. The gray dashed line marks the zero lower bound. Adjusting φπ below 1 violates the Taylor principle — watch what happens to the real rate when inflation rises in that case.

17.10 Worked Examples

📐 Worked Example 17.1 — Applying the Taylor rule to the 2022 inflation peak
In June 2022, U.S. CPI inflation was 9.1% (PCE measure: about 6.8%). The Fed's inflation target is 2% (PCE basis). The output gap was approximately +1.5% (output slightly above potential). Assume r* = 0.5% (neutral real rate) and use Taylor's original coefficients (φπ = 1.5, φY = 0.5). (a) Compute the rule-prescribed real and nominal policy rate using the PCE measure. (b) Compare to the actual federal funds rate in June 2022, which was 1.50–1.75%. (c) Comment on what this comparison tells you about the Fed's stance in mid-2022.
1
(a) Taylor rule (real): r = r* + φπ(π − π*) + φY(Y − Y*)/Y* = 0.5% + 1.5×(6.8 − 2) + 0.5×1.5 = 0.5% + 7.2% + 0.75% = 8.45% real.
2
Nominal: nominal = real + expected inflation ≈ 8.45% + 6.8% = ~15.2% nominal (assuming expected inflation tracks current inflation, which is a simplification).
3
(b) Actual federal funds rate in June 2022: 1.50–1.75%. Gap between rule-prescribed and actual: ~13–14 percentage points. This is enormous — easily one of the largest deviations from the Taylor rule in modern Fed history.
4
(c) What it tells us: the Fed was running policy extraordinarily loose in mid-2022 relative to what a rule-based approach would have prescribed. Several factors explain this gap: (i) the FAIT framework explicitly authorized running inflation hot for some time; (ii) the Fed believed much of the 2021-22 inflation was supply-driven and would resolve on its own; (iii) the Fed places weight on smoothness — it doesn't want to slam rates higher in single moves of 5-10 percentage points. But the Taylor rule warning was real: by mid-2022, the Fed was very far behind the curve, and the eventual catch-up required 525 basis points of tightening in 18 months.
Lesson: the Taylor rule is a benchmark, not a literal policy prescription. Fed deviations from the rule are revealing — they tell you what the Fed's framework, beliefs about the economic environment, and political constraints are doing. The 2022 gap was the largest in 40 years, and it preceded the largest tightening cycle in 40 years. Rule deviations carry information.
📐 Worked Example 17.2 — Forward guidance and long rates
A central bank announces on January 1 that it will keep its policy rate at 1% for the next three years, regardless of economic conditions. Before the announcement, markets had been expecting the rate to gradually rise to 3% over the next three years. (a) What was the previously-expected average rate over the next three years? (b) What is the new expected average rate? (c) The current 3-year bond yield is approximately the average of expected short rates over three years. By how much should the 3-year yield change immediately after the announcement? (d) Why does this matter for the real economy?
1
(a) Old expected average: if rate rises from 1% to 3% linearly, average ≈ (1% + 3%) / 2 = 2%.
2
(b) New expected average: committed to 1% throughout, so average = 1%.
3
(c) Change in 3-year yield: falls by about 100 basis points (from 2% to 1%), since the 3-year yield reflects the expected average path of short rates.
4
(d) Why this matters: mortgage rates, corporate bond rates, and other long-term borrowing rates are anchored to the 3-, 5-, and 10-year Treasury yields. A 100-basis-point drop in 3-year yields translates almost immediately to lower mortgage rates and lower business borrowing costs. Households refinance and increase consumption; firms invest more; the economy gets stimulated today — even though the actual short rate hasn't moved.
This is the magic of credible forward guidance. The central bank doesn't have to do anything except announce a credible plan, and long-term rates fall today. The August 2011 Fed announcement that it would hold rates low "at least through mid-2013" was precisely this kind of move; 10-year Treasury yields fell about 30 basis points within hours. Communication is policy — but only if it's credible.
🎮 MacroNations Connection — Monetary Policy in the Engine

In MacroNations, you directly set interest_rate_target each round (range 0–20%, default 4.5%, neutral = 4.0%). The engine's monetary transmission is encoded in three equations: (1) GDP impact: each 1pp above neutral subtracts 0.5pp from gdp_growth — applied as a level effect, not a change effect, so sustained tight policy keeps dampening growth every round. (2) Inflation impact: each 1pp above neutral subtracts 0.4pp from inflation. (3) Exchange rate impact: each 1pp rate change moves the currency by 1.5% via UIP. There is no Taylor rule or reaction function — you set the rate, the engine just applies the consequences. The Volcker-style scenario plays out if you set rate well above neutral and hold it: inflation falls steadily, unemployment rises via Okun, fin_health takes a hit, but Stability eventually recovers. The post-disinflation reset (rate back to 4%) restores GDP growth toward potential.

U.S. Federal Funds Rate — Last 25 Years
Source: Federal Reserve Board via FRED (Series: FEDFUNDS, monthly average). The 2008-2015 zero lower bound period appears as the long flat segment at 0.25%. The 2015-2018 gradual lift-off, the 2019 modest cuts, the March 2020 emergency cuts back to zero, and the 2022-2024 aggressive tightening to 5.5% are all visible. The most striking feature: the speed and magnitude of the 2022-2024 cycle, the fastest tightening since 1980-1982.
U.S. Fed Balance Sheet — Last 25 Years
Source: Federal Reserve via FRED (Series: WALCL, total assets, weekly). The transformation of the Fed's balance sheet since 2008 is one of the most consequential changes in central banking history. Pre-2008: roughly $900B, primarily Treasury securities, growing slowly with the economy. 2008-2014: three rounds of QE expand it to $4.5T. 2017-2019: modest QT shrinks it slightly. 2020-2022: explosive expansion to $9T during pandemic QE. 2022 onwards: QT shrinks the balance sheet gradually. The balance sheet is now arguably the Fed's most important policy tool after the federal funds rate itself.

Chapter 17 Quiz

Mandates, FOMC, conventional and unconventional tools, FAIT, the 2022-2024 cycle
Part A — Multiple Choice (Principles)
1. The Federal Reserve's "dual mandate" requires it to pursue:
A Maximum employment and stable exchange rates
B Maximum employment and stable prices
C Stable prices and a balanced federal budget
D Maximum GDP growth and low interest rates
2. What is the Federal Reserve's primary day-to-day tool for adjusting the federal funds rate?
A Changes to reserve requirements
B Changes to the discount rate
C Open market operations
D Direct lending to households
3. Which of the following is an "unconventional" monetary policy tool?
A Changing the federal funds rate target
B Routine open market operations
C Quantitative easing
D Setting the discount rate
4. Central bank independence is generally argued to be desirable because:
A It allows monetary policy to respond faster to economic shocks
B It insulates monetary policy from political pressure that tends toward looser-than-optimal policy
C It eliminates the need for elections
D It ensures the federal budget is balanced
Part B — Multiple Choice (Intermediate)
5. Under Flexible Average Inflation Targeting (FAIT), the Fed committed to:
A Strictly maintaining inflation at exactly 2% at all times
B Letting inflation run above 2% following periods when it had run below 2%, to achieve a 2% average over time
C Targeting nominal GDP rather than inflation
D Following a strict Taylor rule
6. At the August 2025 Jackson Hole symposium, the Fed announced it would:
A Adopt a Taylor rule formally
B Return from FAIT to plain Flexible Inflation Targeting (FIT)
C Adopt yield curve control
D Move to a 3% inflation target
7. The "expectations channel" of monetary policy refers to:
A Changes in consumer expectations of future house prices
B The central bank's influence on inflation and rate expectations through communication and credibility
C Business expectations of future tax policy
D Foreign investor expectations of currency movements
Part C — Numerical Problems
8. Suppose CPI inflation is 4.5% (target 2%) and the output gap is +0.5% (output 0.5% above potential). Assume r* = 1%. (a) Compute the Taylor-rule-prescribed real rate using Taylor's original coefficients (φπ = 1.5, φY = 0.5). (b) Compute the implied nominal rate. (c) Suppose markets expect future inflation of 3%. What is the rule-prescribed nominal rate using that expectation? (d) The actual federal funds rate is 5.25%. Is the Fed running tight or loose policy by Taylor-rule standards?
Model answer: (a) r = 1% + 1.5(4.5 − 2) + 0.5(0.5) = 1% + 3.75% + 0.25% = 5% real. (b) Nominal using current inflation: 5% + 4.5% = 9.5%. (c) Nominal using expected inflation: 5% + 3% = 8%. (d) Actual = 5.25%; rule (using expected inflation) = 8%. Actual is below rule-prescribed by about 275 basis points → Fed is running LOOSER than the Taylor rule prescribes. Whether this is appropriate depends on the Fed's judgment: if inflation is judged temporary, loose policy is defensible; if not, the Fed is behind the curve.
9. A central bank announces on January 1 that it will keep its policy rate at 0.5% for the next two years. Before the announcement, markets were expecting the rate to rise from 0.5% to 4% linearly over those two years. (a) Compute the previously-expected average rate over two years. (b) Compute the new expected average rate. (c) Approximately how much should the 2-year Treasury yield change immediately? (d) If mortgage rates have a 70% pass-through from the 2-year Treasury, how much does the mortgage rate change?
Model answer: (a) Old expected average: (0.5 + 4) / 2 = 2.25%. (b) New expected average: 0.5%. (c) 2-year yield drop: 2.25 − 0.5 = 175 basis points. (d) Mortgage rate change: 0.7 × 175 = 122.5 basis points ≈ 1.25 percentage points lower. This is the power of credible forward guidance: a single announcement, no actual rate change, but the entire term structure repriced, and mortgage rates fall by more than a full percentage point, stimulating housing and consumption immediately.
Part D — Policy Scenario
Scenario: You are advising the FOMC in October 2026. CPI inflation has unexpectedly accelerated to 4.2% (target 2%), partly due to a new energy shock. Output is at potential. Unemployment is 4.1% (at the natural rate). Long-run inflation expectations have risen modestly (from 2.1% to 2.4%) but remain anchored. The federal funds rate is currently 4.25%. The Fed's recent framework (post-2025) is plain Flexible Inflation Targeting. Three options: (1) Hold rates steady, citing the supply-side nature of the shock. (2) Raise rates 25 basis points as a precautionary move. (3) Raise rates 50 basis points and signal more aggressive tightening ahead.
10. Write a 250-word memo to the Chair. Reference: the lessons of the 2022-24 cycle, the Taylor rule, the distinction between demand- and supply-driven inflation, and the Fed's post-2025 framework. Take a clear position.
Model answer (sketch): Inflation has accelerated to 4.2%, well above the 2% target. While the shock has supply-side roots, modest expectations creep (to 2.4%) suggests the Fed cannot ignore it.

Option 1 (hold): rejected. The 2022-24 experience teaches that "transitory" framing of supply-driven inflation is dangerous when expectations have begun to drift. Even though Powell's Fed eventually achieved disinflation without recession in 2023-24, that success depended on aggressive earlier tightening, which started slow but caught up. The lesson: don't fall behind early.

Option 3 (50 bp): too aggressive. Long-run expectations have moved only modestly (0.3 pp). The output gap is zero, not positive — meaning the economy is not overheating. A 50-bp move could signal panic and unsettle markets.

Option 2 (25 bp + signal readiness): recommended. (i) The post-2025 FIT framework treats inflation deviations symmetrically; 4.2% is significantly off-target and demands action. (ii) Taylor-rule benchmark with φπ = 1.5 prescribes a real rate around 4% (nominal ~6.4%), well above the current 4.25% — supporting a tightening bias. (iii) A modest move preserves optionality if the supply shock dissipates; a more aggressive move locks in tightening that may need to be reversed. (iv) Pair the action with strong forward guidance signaling additional tightening if inflation does not return toward target.

Position: 25 bp hike. Communicate that the Fed views the situation seriously, that more tightening is on the table, and that expectations must remain anchored. Reassess at December meeting.
This is a preview — the live textbook auto-grades MCQs and stores progress.

References

Bernanke, B. S. (2022). 21st century monetary policy: The Federal Reserve from the Great Inflation to COVID-19. W. W. Norton.

Blinder, A. S. (2022). A monetary and fiscal history of the United States, 1961–2021. Princeton University Press.

Duncan, R., Martínez-García, E., & Miller, L. (2025). Tempting FAIT: Flexible Average Inflation Targeting and the post-COVID U.S. inflation surge. Dallas Fed Working Paper No. 2511.

Federal Reserve Board. (2020, August 27). Statement on Longer-Run Goals and Monetary Policy Strategy. Federal Reserve System.

Powell, J. H. (2025, August 22). Monetary policy framework review. Remarks at the Jackson Hole Economic Policy Symposium.

Romer, C. D., & Romer, D. H. (2002). The evolution of economic understanding and postwar stabilization policy. Rethinking Stabilization Policy, Federal Reserve Bank of Kansas City, 11–78.

Rogoff, K. (1985). The optimal degree of commitment to an intermediate monetary target. Quarterly Journal of Economics, 100(4), 1169–1189.

Swanson, E. (2024). Advice for the Federal Reserve's review of its monetary policy framework. Brookings Institution.

Taylor, J. B. (1993). Discretion versus policy rules in practice. Carnegie-Rochester Conference Series on Public Policy, 39, 195–214.

Chapter 18 · Part VII: Short-Run Fluctuations

Fiscal Policy

How taxing and spending affect the economy — multipliers, automatic stabilizers, the politics of deficits, and the unprecedented question facing the U.S. in 2026: what does a 99% debt-to-GDP ratio at full employment actually mean?

In October 2025, the United States closed the books on Fiscal Year 2025 with a federal budget deficit of $1.8 trillion — equal to 5.9% of GDP. Federal debt held by the public reached 99.8% of GDP, the highest level in U.S. peacetime history. Interest payments on the national debt topped $1 trillion for the year, making interest the second-largest line in the federal budget, behind only Social Security. The U.S. economy was not in recession. Unemployment was 4.3%, below most estimates of the natural rate. Real GDP was growing at about 2%. This is not a wartime emergency. This is not a financial crisis. This is the new baseline.

This chapter is about fiscal policy — how government spending and taxation affect the economy. Like monetary policy in Chapter 17, fiscal policy is one of the two main tools governments use to stabilize output and prices. Unlike monetary policy, fiscal policy is conducted by Congress and the President, not by an independent central bank, which means it is far more political, far slower, and far less precisely targeted. But fiscal policy is also more powerful in certain circumstances — particularly at the zero lower bound, where monetary policy is constrained, and during deep recessions where the fiscal multiplier is large.

The chapter has two missions. The first is to give you a clean conceptual grasp of how fiscal policy works in the IS-LM framework you learned in Chapter 16 — multipliers, crowding out, automatic stabilizers, balanced-budget effects. The second is to wrestle honestly with the present-day question of fiscal sustainability. Almost every contemporary fiscal-policy debate — from the 2009 ARRA stimulus to the 2020-21 pandemic response to the One Big Beautiful Bill Act of 2025 — centers on whether the U.S. government has been borrowing too much, too little, or about the right amount. The chapter will not give you a political answer to that question. It will give you the analytical tools to think clearly about it.

✦ Sharma's Take

Fiscal policy is taught in textbooks as if it were a clean lever the government can pull whenever stabilization is needed. In practice, it is nothing of the sort. Fiscal policy is slow (months or years between identifying a problem and Congress responding); it is political (every dollar of spending has a constituency that doesn't want it cut, and every tax cut has a constituency that doesn't want it reversed); and it is targeted by interest groups rather than by economic logic. The textbook story of a benevolent technocrat adjusting G and T to optimize output gets the institutional reality almost exactly backwards.

And yet — and this is the central tension of fiscal policy as a field — when fiscal policy works, it works enormously. The 2020-21 fiscal response prevented a depression-grade collapse. The 2009 ARRA, despite endless retrospective disputes, almost certainly prevented unemployment from peaking three or four percentage points higher than it did. Fiscal policy is most powerful when it's most necessary — at the zero lower bound, in deep recessions — and most controversial when it's least helpful — in mature recoveries when the budget can least afford it. The 2021 American Rescue Plan was, by most reasonable analysis, an example of the latter. The challenge for any student of macroeconomics is to develop the judgment to tell these cases apart.

— A.S.

18.1 The Instruments of Fiscal Policy

📘 Principles Level — Two main levers, two main directions

Fiscal policy has two main instruments: government spending (G) and taxes (T). Either can be raised or lowered. The combination defines the fiscal stance — whether policy is expansionary (raising aggregate demand) or contractionary (reducing it). Expansionary fiscal policy means either raising G, cutting T, or both. Contractionary fiscal policy is the reverse.

The two instruments have different economic properties. Government spending changes affect demand directly: every dollar the government spends shows up immediately as a dollar of GDP. Tax changes affect demand indirectly: lower taxes raise household disposable income, but households save some of that income rather than spending it all. The fraction they spend — the marginal propensity to consume (MPC), introduced briefly in Chapter 16 — determines how much of a tax cut actually feeds into aggregate demand.

⚙ Equation Walkthrough — The Simple Multiplier
ΔY = ΔG × 1/(1−MPC)
where 1/(1−MPC) is the spending multiplier
ΔG
A change in government spending (positive = expansion).
MPC
Marginal propensity to consume — the fraction of an additional dollar of disposable income that households spend rather than save. In the U.S., aggregate MPC has been empirically estimated at about 0.5 to 0.7, varying with household income (poorer households have higher MPC since they're closer to subsistence; richer households save more of additional income).
1/(1−MPC)
The textbook "multiplier." With MPC = 0.7, this equals 1/0.3 ≈ 3.33. The idea: $1 of government spending generates $1 of direct demand, plus $0.70 spent by the recipient (who got it as income), plus $0.49 spent by the next recipient, plus $0.34 by the next, and so on — a geometric series summing to about $3.33.

This formula is the single most-taught equation in introductory macroeconomics. It also dramatically overstates real-world multipliers in most situations. The simple multiplier ignores: crowding out (Chapter 16), the response of monetary policy, leakages into imports, and the fact that MPC varies dramatically by household type and economic context. Empirical estimates of actual fiscal multipliers usually fall in the range of 0.5 to 2.5, almost always well below the simple formula's prediction.

⚙ Equation Walkthrough — The Tax Multiplier
ΔY = −ΔT × MPC/(1−MPC)
ΔT
A change in lump-sum taxes.
MPC/(1−MPC)
The tax multiplier. With MPC = 0.7, this is 0.7/0.3 ≈ 2.33. Notice this is smaller than the spending multiplier: a $1 tax cut only generates $0.70 of immediate spending (the rest is saved), so it has a smaller first-round effect than a $1 increase in government spending.

The textbook lesson: dollar-for-dollar, government spending is a more powerful stimulus than tax cuts. This is the textbook case for spending-based stimulus over tax-based stimulus in recessions. Empirical evidence is more mixed: tax cuts that are clearly permanent and targeted at high-MPC households (low-income, financially constrained) can rival spending multipliers, while government spending that's poorly designed or slowly implemented can underperform. Design matters as much as the choice between G and T.

⚠ Common Pitfall — Treating the textbook multiplier as a number you can rely on

The simple multiplier formula 1/(1−MPC) is a useful starting point for thinking about fiscal policy, but it should never be quoted as if it were a literal estimate of what stimulus will deliver. The 2009 American Recovery and Reinvestment Act (ARRA) was sized using a Romer-Bernstein assumption of a multiplier around 1.6. Subsequent retrospective studies have produced estimates ranging from 0.5 (small multiplier, mostly crowding out) to over 2.0 (large multiplier, deep slack absorbed), depending on assumptions and methodology. The honest scientific answer is that we don't know the multiplier to better than a factor of three.

The multiplier varies systematically with macroeconomic conditions. Auerbach and Gorodnichenko (2012) showed that multipliers are roughly twice as large in recessions as in expansions — when resources are slack, fiscal stimulus has room to work; when the economy is at full capacity, it mostly crowds out private activity. Multipliers are also higher at the zero lower bound (no monetary tightening to offset fiscal expansion) than when the central bank is actively responding. Different contexts → different multipliers. Anyone who quotes a single multiplier number with confidence is selling you something.

18.2 Automatic Stabilizers

📘 Principles Level — Why fiscal policy partly runs on autopilot

Not all fiscal policy requires congressional action. A significant portion of the federal budget responds to economic conditions automatically — these are automatic stabilizers. When the economy enters a recession, tax revenues fall (because incomes fall, so income tax receipts fall; and because spending falls, so sales-tax receipts fall) while transfer payments rise (unemployment insurance claims rise, food assistance enrollment rises, Medicaid enrollment rises as people lose employer-sponsored insurance). The federal budget moves toward deficit automatically, providing demand support exactly when it's needed.

The opposite happens in booms: tax revenues rise as incomes and spending grow, transfers fall as unemployment drops, the budget moves toward surplus, and demand is automatically restrained. This counter-cyclical pattern happens with no legislation, no debate, no political decision — it's built into the structure of taxes and spending.

Automatic stabilizers are quantitatively important. Estimates from the IMF and CBO suggest that U.S. automatic stabilizers reduce business-cycle fluctuations by roughly 20-30%. The progressive income tax is the single largest stabilizer; unemployment insurance and means-tested transfers add meaningfully on top. Larger welfare states tend to have larger automatic stabilizers: in Sweden or Denmark, where transfers are larger and tax rates are higher, automatic stabilizers absorb a much greater share of cyclical shocks than in the U.S.

⚙ Discretionary vs. Automatic Fiscal Policy
Discretionary
Active legislative action — new spending bills, tax cuts, infrastructure programs. Slow, political, often miscalibrated. Examples: 2009 ARRA, 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act, 2020-21 pandemic stimulus, 2022 Inflation Reduction Act, 2025 One Big Beautiful Bill Act.
Automatic
Built-in responses of revenue and transfer programs to economic conditions. Fast (immediate), apolitical (no legislation needed), and well-targeted (the income tax falls hardest on those whose income falls hardest; transfers rise for those who actually need them). Most economists prefer larger automatic stabilizers over more aggressive discretionary policy, precisely because of the political dysfunction discretionary policy involves.
✦ Sharma's Take — The case for boring fiscal policy

One underappreciated insight from macroeconomics: the boring parts of fiscal policy — the parts that no politician runs for office on — do most of the actual stabilization work. Unemployment insurance, food assistance, the progressive income tax, Medicaid eligibility expansions during downturns — these don't make headlines. They aren't named after senators. They don't get covered with stimulus-package fanfare. They just quietly inject demand into the economy whenever joblessness rises, and quietly withdraw it whenever joblessness falls.

The interesting econometric debate about fiscal multipliers tends to focus on discretionary stimulus packages, because those are the ones we can identify in the data as a clear policy shock. But the bulk of fiscal stabilization is not really debatable — it's just obvious from the structure of how taxes and transfers move with the cycle. If you wanted to improve U.S. counter-cyclical policy without doing anything controversial, the highest-value reforms would be expansions of automatic stabilizers (e.g., automatic UI extensions when unemployment rises above some threshold, similar to existing extended-benefit triggers but more generous). This is one of the very few macroeconomic reform areas where there's near-universal expert agreement and almost no political traction.

— A.S.

18.3 Crowding Out: The IS-LM Reminder

📘 Principles Level — Why fiscal stimulus has interest-rate side effects

Chapter 16's IS-LM analysis showed that a fiscal expansion shifts the IS curve right, raising both output and the interest rate. The rise in r reduces private investment by the amount I'(r) — that's crowding out. When the central bank is actively setting rates (the modern IS-MP framework), the same logic applies: fiscal expansion pushes up demand for credit; the central bank, if it's responding to inflation pressure, raises rates to lean against the expansion; higher rates dampen private investment.

The size of crowding out depends critically on what the central bank is doing. If the central bank passively holds rates steady (an "accommodating" stance), there's no crowding out — fiscal stimulus reaches its full multiplier. If the central bank actively raises rates to offset fiscal stimulus, crowding out can be near 100% — the rate hike completely cancels the fiscal expansion. Most real cases fall somewhere in between.

18.4 The Zero Lower Bound Exception

📘 Principles Level — Why fiscal policy is uniquely powerful at the ZLB

From Chapter 16: when the policy interest rate is at the zero lower bound, monetary policy can't ease further. The LM curve is effectively flat at r = 0. In this regime, fiscal expansion shifts IS right but the central bank cannot lean against it through rate hikes — and indeed, has every reason not to, since the economy is in a recession needing all available stimulus. Crowding out is essentially zero. The textbook multiplier comes closer to applying in full force.

This insight is the strongest argument in modern macroeconomics for aggressive fiscal policy in deep recessions. The 2008-2009 financial crisis and the 2020 pandemic both produced ZLB conditions; in both cases, the standard policy advice was for large fiscal expansion. This is not a controversial view among professional macroeconomists — even those who are generally skeptical of fiscal stimulus tend to accept its power at the ZLB. The major disagreement is about how much stimulus, not whether.

18.5 Three Case Studies

2009 United States ARRA

The American Recovery and Reinvestment Act — The Test Case for Modern Fiscal Stimulus

The American Recovery and Reinvestment Act, signed by President Obama in February 2009, was an $831 billion package combining tax cuts, transfers to individuals, aid to state governments, and infrastructure spending. About one-third was tax cuts, one-third transfers, and one-third spending on goods and services. The Romer-Bernstein analysis used to justify the bill assumed an output multiplier of about 1.6.

The retrospective evidence on whether ARRA worked is one of the most studied questions in applied macroeconomics. The CBO's 2015 retrospective concluded that ARRA had output multipliers ranging from 0.5 (for tax cuts to high-income households) to 2.5 (for direct purchases and aid to state and local governments). A widely-cited study by Chodorow-Reich et al. (2012) using state-level variation in stimulus exposure found multipliers of about 2.0 for the most stimulus-intensive states. The honest summary: ARRA almost certainly worked — but probably less than its designers initially projected, and with a wide uncertainty band.

The ARRA debate also surfaced a now-standard insight: the multiplier depends on how the stimulus is composed. Aid to state and local governments showed the largest multipliers, because states facing balanced-budget rules were cutting spending sharply, and federal aid offset those cuts dollar-for-dollar without leakage. Direct infrastructure spending also worked well but was constrained by "shovel-ready" requirements that turned out to be a misnomer — most infrastructure projects took years to plan and execute. Tax cuts to higher-income households showed the smallest multipliers because much of the tax cut was saved. Stimulus design matters enormously, and not all stimulus is created equal.

2020–2021 United States Pandemic response

The Pandemic Stimulus — Necessary, Then Excessive

Across three major bills — the CARES Act (March 2020, $2.2 trillion), the December 2020 supplemental ($900 billion), and the American Rescue Plan (March 2021, $1.9 trillion) — the federal government deployed roughly $5 trillion in pandemic-era fiscal stimulus, equal to nearly 25% of GDP across the three packages. The CARES Act was widely seen, even by stimulus skeptics, as appropriately sized to a once-in-a-century pandemic shock that froze the economy nearly overnight. The December 2020 bill arrived as the recovery was already underway. The March 2021 American Rescue Plan landed when unemployment was already at 6% and falling, household savings were unusually high, and supply chains were already strained.

By mid-2021, mainstream macroeconomists — including former Treasury Secretary Larry Summers in a much-discussed Washington Post column from February 2021 — were warning that the combined fiscal stimulus was substantially larger than the output gap and would likely produce inflation. By the end of 2021, CPI inflation had risen to 7%. Whether ARP caused that inflation, accelerated it, or merely coincided with it remains genuinely debated. A Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco study in 2022 estimated that ARP added 3 percentage points to inflation by the end of 2021. Other studies attribute more of the inflation surge to supply-side disruptions and less to fiscal stimulus.

The 2020-21 episode is the cleanest example in recent memory of fiscal policy being too aggressive: not because the early stimulus was wrong (it almost certainly wasn't), but because the late stimulus was sized to fight a worse recession than the one actually unfolding. This is a structural problem with discretionary fiscal policy: by the time legislation passes, the economy has often moved on, and the stimulus is calibrated to conditions that no longer exist.

2025 United States OBBBA

The One Big Beautiful Bill Act — Fiscal Expansion at the Wrong Time

In July 2025, Congress passed the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA), making permanent many of the temporary tax provisions of the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act, expanding several deductions, and tightening eligibility for federal benefits including SNAP and Medicaid. NBER analyses estimate that OBBBA adds approximately 29 percentage points to the debt-to-GDP ratio over 30 years compared to the no-OBBBA baseline. The Congressional Budget Office's preliminary scoring shows OBBBA raising primary deficits by roughly $3-4 trillion over the next decade, on top of the $22 trillion deficit increase already projected.

From a textbook fiscal-policy standpoint, the timing is striking. OBBBA is a major fiscal expansion enacted when (i) unemployment is below the natural rate, (ii) inflation is above the Fed's 2% target, (iii) interest rates are well above zero, and (iv) the debt-to-GDP ratio is approaching 100%. Every condition that makes fiscal expansion more defensible — slack, ZLB, low inflation, low debt — is absent. Every condition that makes fiscal expansion less defensible — full employment, inflation pressure, positive rates, high debt — is present.

None of this is to take a political position on the merits of the underlying policies; that's a question for voters. The point is purely analytical: OBBBA is an example of fiscal expansion driven by political logic rather than macroeconomic logic. It would have been textbook-appropriate in 2009 or 2020 and is textbook-questionable in 2025. The disconnect between when fiscal stimulus is most needed and when it's politically achievable is one of the deepest problems in the field.

18.6 Deficits, Debt, and the Sustainability Question

📗 Intermediate Level — When does borrowing become unsustainable?

The U.S. federal deficit averaged about 3.8% of GDP over the 50 years from 1975 to 2025. It exceeded 5% only six times in that span — during the deepest recession years (2009-2012) and the pandemic years (2020-2021). In every other year, deficits stayed in single digits and the debt-to-GDP ratio remained well below 80%. Then, starting around 2022, something changed: deficits failed to fall back to historical norms even as the economy returned to full employment. FY 2024 deficit: 6.4% of GDP. FY 2025 deficit: 5.9% of GDP. Both peacetime, full-employment years. Both at deficit levels previously associated only with deep crises.

The debt-to-GDP ratio passed 99% in FY 2025. CBO projections show it reaching roughly 120% by 2034 under the OBBBA-included baseline. If OBBBA's temporary provisions are made permanent — which is politically likely — debt reaches 183% of GDP by 2054 according to NBER analyses, and 199% if all temporary provisions become permanent. These are unprecedented levels for a peacetime economy.

⚙ Debt Dynamics — When Does Debt-to-GDP Stabilize?
Δ(D/Y) ≈ (r − g) × (D/Y) − primary surplus / Y
D/Y
Debt-to-GDP ratio.
r − g
Real interest rate minus real GDP growth rate. The single most important number in fiscal sustainability analysis. When r > g, debt-to-GDP grows automatically through compounding interest even with no primary deficit. When r < g, debt-to-GDP shrinks naturally as the economy grows faster than the debt.
Primary surplus
The budget balance EXCLUDING interest payments. This is what's left over from current revenues after paying for current spending. Required primary surplus for stability: (r − g) × (D/Y).

Example: if r = 4%, g = 2%, and D/Y = 100%, then to keep debt-to-GDP flat at 100%, the government needs a primary surplus of (4% − 2%) × 100% = 2% of GDP. The U.S. is currently running a primary deficit of about 4% of GDP. So debt-to-GDP is rising by about 6% per year mechanically: 2% from the r-g gap, plus 4% from the primary deficit. This is what "fiscal unsustainability" actually means: a path that, if unchanged, leads to ever-rising debt-to-GDP without limit.

⚠ Common Pitfall — "The U.S. can never default, so deficits don't matter"

The argument: the U.S. borrows in its own currency, so it can never technically default on dollar-denominated debt — the Treasury can always pay by having the Fed print money. Therefore deficits never constrain anything. This argument confuses the question of legal default with the question of economic cost. The U.S. won't default on its bonds in nominal terms. But the costs of running large deficits indefinitely show up in three places:

  1. Higher interest rates: more debt issuance raises the supply of bonds; absent matching increases in demand, prices fall and yields rise. Higher yields crowd out private investment, raise mortgage rates and business borrowing costs, and constrain future growth.
  2. Higher inflation risk: at some level of debt, markets begin to suspect that future debt servicing will require either monetization (which is inflationary) or sharp fiscal adjustment. Even the perception of this can raise expected inflation and inflation risk premia.
  3. Reduced fiscal space: every dollar going to interest payments is a dollar not available for defense, infrastructure, healthcare, or future stimulus. By FY 2024, the U.S. was spending over $1 trillion per year on interest payments alone — more than defense spending, more than Medicare. This is real money with real opportunity cost.

The U.S. doesn't face an imminent debt crisis. It doesn't even face one within ten years. But the trajectory matters. The question is not "will the U.S. default?" but "what's the cost of crowding out, higher interest rates, and reduced fiscal space, and is that cost worth what we're getting in return for the borrowing?" That's an actual policy question, not a manufactured one.

✦ Sharma's Take — Why fiscal sustainability is hard to discuss honestly

Fiscal sustainability is one of the topics where economists struggle the most to be heard accurately by the public. Two opposing oversimplifications dominate. On one side: "America is bankrupt, the dollar will collapse, hyperinflation is coming." On the other side: "Deficits are fake, money is just numbers in a spreadsheet, the U.S. can borrow forever." Both views are wrong, and both views actively obscure the real question.

The honest analytical framework is the debt-dynamics equation above. A country can sustain very high debt-to-GDP ratios indefinitely if r − g stays favorable (Japan has done so at 250%+ debt-to-GDP for decades, because g has typically exceeded r in Japan). A country can find itself in crisis at modest debt-to-GDP ratios if r − g moves against it sharply (Italy faced crisis at debt-to-GDP around 130% when its r spiked in 2011-12). The U.S. position today is unprecedented: high and rising debt-to-GDP combined with rising r (because the Fed raised rates in 2022-23 and they've stayed elevated) and modest g. That's the combination that makes debt dynamics genuinely difficult, and it's why this generation of policymakers has a problem their predecessors did not face.

The textbook lesson for you: don't take rhetorical shortcuts on either side of this debate. Whether U.S. fiscal policy is "sustainable" is a genuine question, and the answer depends on assumptions about future r, future g, and future political willingness to constrain spending or raise taxes. Reasonable economists can disagree about all three. What's not reasonable is dismissing the question.

— A.S.

18.7 The Cyclically-Adjusted Budget Balance

📗 Intermediate Level — Separating discretionary policy from cyclical effects

One of the trickiest analytical problems in fiscal policy is distinguishing changes in the budget that are caused by policy choices from changes caused by the business cycle. The headline deficit moves with both. To isolate discretionary stance, economists use the cyclically-adjusted budget balance (also called the structural balance or full-employment balance): the budget balance that would prevail if the economy were operating at potential output.

The intuition: in a deep recession, revenues are low and transfers are high purely because the economy is weak, not because Congress is being expansionary. If you want to measure how aggressive the government's fiscal stance actually is, you need to strip out the cyclical part. The CBO and IMF both publish cyclically-adjusted budget balances regularly. This is the right measure for comparing fiscal stances across time or across countries.

By the cyclically-adjusted measure, the U.S. fiscal position is even more striking. The CBO's structural deficit estimate for FY 2025 is approximately 5% of GDP — meaning that even if the economy were perfectly at potential, the deficit would still be 5% of GDP. The structural component is almost entirely discretionary: tax cuts, spending growth, and the rising cost of mandatory programs (Social Security, Medicare, interest). This is fundamentally different from the 2009 deficit, which was largely cyclical (the structural component was much smaller, around 3% of GDP). The U.S. has moved from cyclical deficits to structural deficits, and that's a far harder problem to fix.

18.8 Interactive: The Fiscal Multiplier Calculator

🎛 Interactive — Fiscal Multiplier Under Different Conditions

Show how the same $1 of government spending produces wildly different effects depending on the regime

What you're seeing: the textbook multiplier (the naive 1/(1−MPC) formula) shown in gray, and the realistic multiplier — adjusted for crowding out, ZLB, and slack — shown in blue. The gap between them is the empirical correction the textbook formula misses.

18.9 Worked Examples

📐 Worked Example 18.1 — Computing the Multiplier in Three Different Regimes
A government considers spending $400 billion of fiscal stimulus. Aggregate MPC is 0.6. Compute the effective multiplier and ΔY in three scenarios: (a) Economy at full employment, central bank actively tightening; (b) Economy in mild recession (output gap −3%), central bank holds rates steady; (c) Economy in deep recession (gap −7%), zero lower bound binding.
1
Naive textbook multiplier: 1/(1−0.6) = 2.5. This is the upper bound — what you'd get with no crowding out and perfect transmission.
2
(a) Full employment + Fed tightening: the Fed responds to fiscal expansion by raising rates to neutralize inflation pressure. Empirical evidence suggests the effective multiplier in this regime is approximately 0.5. ΔY ≈ 0.5 × $400B = $200B. Most of the stimulus is crowded out.
3
(b) Mild recession, Fed accommodates: some slack exists; the Fed doesn't lean against the expansion. Empirical multiplier ≈ 1.2. ΔY ≈ 1.2 × $400B = $480B. Stimulus more than pays for itself in dollar terms.
4
(c) Deep recession at ZLB: abundant slack; no monetary tightening possible (LM is flat at zero). The multiplier approaches the textbook ceiling. Empirical multiplier ≈ 1.8 to 2.5. ΔY ≈ 2.0 × $400B = $800B. Stimulus achieves close to its theoretical maximum.
Same $400B of stimulus → $200B, $480B, or $800B of additional output depending on context. The variation is enormous. This is the empirical foundation for the modern view that fiscal stimulus should be aggressive in deep recessions at the ZLB but modest in normal times. The same policy is brilliant in one regime and wasteful in another. Knowing which regime you're in is the central skill of fiscal policy.
📐 Worked Example 18.2 — Debt Dynamics
A country has debt-to-GDP of 100%. Its real interest rate on debt is 3%, real GDP growth is 2%, and its current primary deficit is 3% of GDP. (a) Compute the change in debt-to-GDP this year. (b) What primary surplus would stabilize debt-to-GDP at 100%? (c) If the government wants to reduce debt-to-GDP to 60% over 25 years, what primary surplus is required (approximately)? (d) Comment on the political feasibility of this adjustment.
1
(a) Change this year: Δ(D/Y) ≈ (r − g) × (D/Y) − primary surplus/Y = (3% − 2%) × 100% − (−3%) = 1% + 3% = 4%. Debt-to-GDP rises from 100% to 104% in one year.
2
(b) Primary surplus to stabilize: need (r − g) × (D/Y) = 1% × 100% = 1% of GDP. So a +1% primary surplus stabilizes debt-to-GDP. Going from a 3% deficit to a 1% surplus is a 4 percentage-point swing.
3
(c) Reducing D/Y from 100% to 60% over 25 years: need to reduce D/Y by about 40 percentage points over 25 years = 1.6 percentage points per year. Annual change must be −1.6%. Solving: −1.6% = 1% × (D/Y average) − primary surplus. With average D/Y around 80%, primary surplus needed ≈ 0.8% + 1.6% = 2.4% of GDP. Roughly a 5.4 percentage-point swing from the current 3% deficit position.
4
(d) Political feasibility: a 5+ percentage-point swing in the primary balance is enormous. The largest sustained U.S. fiscal consolidations in the post-WWII era have been about 2-3 percentage points of GDP, achieved gradually over years. Achieving 5+ percentage points would require either large tax increases, large spending cuts, or both, sustained over a generation. Such consolidations are politically extraordinary, requiring rare cross-party consensus that hasn't existed in the U.S. since the late 1990s.
This is what fiscal sustainability arithmetic looks like. Stabilizing debt-to-GDP at current levels requires modest, ongoing adjustment. Actually reducing debt-to-GDP requires far larger and more sustained adjustment. The U.S. is currently doing neither — its debt-to-GDP is rising, and projections show no realistic path back to historical norms. This is the structural fiscal challenge of our generation, and it cannot be solved by growth alone unless r − g shifts dramatically in our favor (which the post-2022 environment suggests is unlikely).
🎮 MacroNations Connection — Fiscal Policy in the Engine

MacroNations gives you direct control over two fiscal levers: gov_spending_pct (range 10–60%, multiplier = 1.4) and tax_rate (range 5–60%, multiplier = −1.1). Note that the engine uses a single multiplier, not a regime-dependent one — there's no ZLB regime switch. If you want larger fiscal effect, you need a larger ΔG. The engine encodes the Laffer curve: tax above 40% has an efficiency drag (−0.008/pp), and below 12% an underfunding drag (−0.015/pp). The Reinhart-Rogoff debt drag activates at debt above 90% of GDP: each 10pp of excess subtracts 0.02pp from gdp_growth — small per round, but compounding. Debt is double-penalized in pillar scoring: Stability's debt_pts (60 pts, threshold 40%) plus Sustainability's sust_debt_pts (80 pts, threshold 30%). The cleanest fiscal strategy is moderate G, moderate T, balanced budget, debt under 60% — preserving fiscal space for crisis response options on crisis rounds.

U.S. Federal Budget Deficit — % of GDP, Last 50 Years
Source: Treasury and CBO via FRED (Series: FYFSGDA188S). Notice the structural shift around 2008: pre-2008 deficits averaged 2-3% of GDP, oscillating with the business cycle. Post-2008 deficits have stayed elevated even outside recession. The 2020-21 pandemic spike (deficits over 15% of GDP) was historic. The current 5-6% range at full employment is unprecedented in peacetime outside of crisis years.
U.S. Federal Debt Held by the Public — % of GDP, Last 50 Years
Source: Treasury and CBO via FRED (Series: GFDEGDQ188S). The debt-to-GDP ratio averaged around 30-40% from 1970-2007. The Great Recession pushed it to about 70%, where it stabilized through the 2010s. The pandemic pushed it past 100% briefly. Since 2023, it has been climbing again toward 100% and beyond, driven by structural primary deficits, higher interest rates, and the OBBBA legislation.

Chapter 18 Quiz

Instruments, multipliers, automatic stabilizers, deficits, debt sustainability
Part A — Multiple Choice (Principles)
1. An expansionary fiscal policy involves:
A Lower G, higher T, both reducing aggregate demand
B Higher G, lower T, both increasing aggregate demand
C Lower G, lower T
D Higher G, higher T
2. If MPC = 0.75, the textbook spending multiplier is:
A 0.75
B 1.33
C 3.0
D 4.0
3. Automatic stabilizers refer to:
A Congress passing emergency stimulus quickly
B Tax and transfer programs that automatically expand the deficit in recessions and contract it in booms
C The Federal Reserve cutting rates automatically
D Currency stabilization through forex intervention
4. The fiscal multiplier is generally LARGEST under which conditions?
A Full employment, central bank actively tightening
B Deep recession at the zero lower bound
C Booming economy with high inflation
D Open economy with floating exchange rate
Part B — Multiple Choice (Intermediate)
5. The cyclically-adjusted budget balance:
A Is the budget balance the government would have if the economy were at potential output
B Is the same as the headline deficit
C Includes only interest payments
D Excludes Social Security and Medicare
6. If r > g (real interest rate exceeds real GDP growth), what does debt-to-GDP do automatically?
A Falls automatically regardless of primary balance
B Stays constant
C Grows automatically through interest compounding even with no primary deficit
D Depends only on the level of debt
7. By FY 2025, U.S. federal debt held by the public is approximately:
A 30% of GDP
B 50% of GDP
C 100% of GDP
D 250% of GDP
Part C — Numerical Problems
8. An economy has MPC = 0.65. The government enacts a $300 billion fiscal package: $200 billion in direct spending and $100 billion in tax cuts. (a) Compute the spending multiplier and tax multiplier. (b) Compute the naive total ΔY assuming both multipliers apply in full. (c) Estimate effective ΔY assuming a deep recession with ZLB (so effective spending multiplier ≈ 1.8 and effective tax multiplier ≈ 1.0). (d) Estimate effective ΔY assuming full employment with Fed tightening (effective multipliers 0.5 and 0.3 respectively). Comment.
Model answer: (a) Spending multiplier = 1/(1−0.65) = 2.86. Tax multiplier = 0.65/0.35 = 1.86. (b) Naive ΔY = $200B×2.86 + $100B×1.86 = $572B + $186B = $758B. (c) ZLB regime: ΔY = $200B×1.8 + $100B×1.0 = $360B + $100B = $460B. (d) Full employment: ΔY = $200B×0.5 + $100B×0.3 = $100B + $30B = $130B. Same $300B package produces $460B of output in a deep recession or just $130B at full employment — a 3.5× difference. Context matters enormously.
9. A country has debt-to-GDP = 95%, real interest rate r = 3.5%, real GDP growth g = 1.5%, and primary deficit = 4% of GDP. (a) Compute the change in debt-to-GDP this year. (b) What primary balance would stabilize debt-to-GDP at 95%? (c) The government wants to stabilize debt within 5 years and lower it to 80% within 25 years. Approximately what primary surplus would be needed during the stabilization phase? (d) If the government instead cut spending by 1% of GDP per year for 5 years, would that be enough? Show why or why not.
Model answer: (a) Δ(D/Y) = (r−g)×(D/Y) − primary surplus = (3.5−1.5)×0.95 − (−4) = 1.9 + 4 = +5.9 percentage points/year. (b) Need primary surplus = (r−g)×(D/Y) = 2%×95% = 1.9% of GDP. So a swing from −4% to +1.9% is required: 5.9 percentage points of fiscal tightening. (c) To lower D/Y by 15 pp over 25 years = 0.6 pp/year. Required primary surplus ≈ 1.9% + 0.6% = ~2.5% of GDP sustained. (d) Cutting spending by 1% per year for 5 years = 5 percentage points of tightening. That's just below the 5.9 pp needed for stabilization. It would slow the rise but not stop it; debt-to-GDP would still rise modestly each year. To genuinely stabilize, would need either deeper cuts, tax increases, or favorable shifts in r-g.
Part D — Policy Scenario
Scenario: You're advising a newly-elected administration in January 2027. The economy is at full employment (unemployment 4.0%, near the natural rate). CPI inflation is 2.7%, modestly above the Fed's 2% target. The federal funds rate is 4.0%. Debt-to-GDP is 105% and rising about 4 percentage points per year. The structural deficit is 5% of GDP. The administration has campaigned on a $2 trillion infrastructure investment package and a $1 trillion tax cut, plus increased defense spending. Three options for your advice: (1) Push forward with the campaign agenda fully. (2) Implement the full agenda but offset with spending cuts elsewhere to make it deficit-neutral. (3) Defer most of the agenda; focus first on reducing the structural deficit through targeted spending reductions.
10. Write a 250-word memo. Reference: the regime-dependent multiplier, the debt dynamics equation, the cyclically-adjusted balance, and the experience of the 2021 ARP. Take a clear position.
Model answer (sketch): The economy is in the WRONG regime for fiscal expansion. With output at potential, inflation above target, rates well above zero, and debt rising, every condition that makes fiscal stimulus effective is absent. The 2021 ARP — a fiscal expansion landing on an already-recovering economy — is the cautionary case study; the result was inflation spike and forced Fed tightening.

Option 1 (full agenda): rejected. At full employment, the multiplier is likely 0.5 or below; the Fed will offset much of the demand impact with rate hikes, undoing most of the stimulus while raising debt. Debt-to-GDP rises faster, interest costs grow, fiscal space erodes. Worst of both worlds.

Option 2 (deficit-neutral): middle-ground. Removes the inflation/Fed-response risk but does nothing for the underlying structural deficit. Defensible if the administration's priorities are valuable enough to justify a reshuffling, but doesn't address the long-run fiscal problem.

Option 3 (deferred, focus on consolidation): recommended on macroeconomic grounds, with caveats. With r-g positive and structural deficits at 5%, the country is on an unsustainable path. Tightening fiscal policy now — even modestly — creates fiscal space for future stimulus when it will be actually needed. Aim for a primary balance improvement of 1-2 percentage points of GDP over four years; combine moderate revenue raisers with targeted spending discipline. Politically this is the hardest option. Macroeconomically it's the right one. The textbook lesson from the 2021 ARP and the 2025 OBBBA is that fiscal expansion at full employment is rarely justified.
This is a preview — the live textbook auto-grades MCQs and stores progress.

References

Auerbach, A. J., & Gorodnichenko, Y. (2012). Measuring the output responses to fiscal policy. American Economic Journal: Economic Policy, 4(2), 1–27.

Blanchard, O., & Leigh, D. (2013). Growth forecast errors and fiscal multipliers. American Economic Review: Papers & Proceedings, 103(3), 117–120.

Chodorow-Reich, G., Feiveson, L., Liscow, Z., & Woolston, W. G. (2012). Does state fiscal relief during recessions increase employment? Evidence from the American Recovery and Reinvestment Act. American Economic Journal: Economic Policy, 4(3), 118–145.

Congressional Budget Office. (2025). The Budget and Economic Outlook: 2025 to 2035. Washington, DC.

Congressional Budget Office. (2025). Monthly Budget Review: Summary for Fiscal Year 2025.

Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget. (2025). 12-month deficit totals $1.7 trillion, debt approaches 100% of GDP.

Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco. (2022). Why is U.S. inflation higher than in other countries? Economic Letter, 2022-07.

Nakamura, E., & Steinsson, J. (2014). Fiscal stimulus in a monetary union: Evidence from U.S. regions. American Economic Review, 104(3), 753–792.

Romer, C., & Bernstein, J. (2009). The job impact of the American Recovery and Reinvestment Plan. Office of the Vice President-Elect.

Summers, L. H. (2021, February 4). The Biden stimulus is admirably ambitious. But it brings some big risks, too. Washington Post.

Chapter 19 · Part VII: Short-Run Fluctuations

The Phillips Curve

The unemployment-inflation trade-off in its short-run, long-run, and expectations-augmented forms — plus an honest treatment of what the 2021-2024 episode taught macroeconomists about the curve's flatness, nonlinearity, and survivability

In December 2024, the U.S. economy presented macroeconomists with a puzzle that should not, according to most introductory textbooks, have been possible. CPI inflation had fallen from a June 2022 peak of 9.1% to 2.4% over thirty months. Unemployment had risen modestly from a cyclical low of 3.4% to about 4.2%. No recession had occurred. Real GDP growth averaged 2.5% throughout the disinflation. The standard Phillips curve framework, which predicts that bringing inflation down by 6+ percentage points requires a substantial recession with unemployment well above the natural rate, had to confront the fact that the U.S. had just executed an "immaculate disinflation" — a disinflation without significant economic cost.

This chapter is about the Phillips curve — the empirical relationship between unemployment and inflation that has been one of the central organizing frameworks of macroeconomics since A.W. Phillips first plotted it in 1958. The chapter covers the original curve, the expectations-augmented modification that ended the simple 1960s-style trade-off interpretation, the long-run vertical Phillips curve at the natural rate, and the more recent nonlinear and "flat" versions that macroeconomists have been developing to explain why the post-2020 data don't fit any of the older forms cleanly. The chapter ends by being honest about what the Phillips curve still teaches us, what it gets wrong, and why every macroeconomic forecaster still uses it despite all its known failures.

✦ Sharma's Take

The Phillips curve is in some ways the most contested empirical relationship in macroeconomics. It's been declared dead by serious economists at least four times in the past fifty years — in the 1970s when stagflation broke the simple version, in the late 1990s when low unemployment failed to produce expected inflation, in the 2010s when the post-GFC "missing deflation" puzzle emerged, and in 2023-24 when the immaculate disinflation seemed to bury the relationship entirely. And yet the Phillips curve keeps coming back, because the underlying economic intuition is sound: when labor markets are tighter than their long-run sustainable level, wage growth accelerates, firms pass costs through to prices, and inflation rises.

What's actually happened over fifty years isn't the death of the Phillips curve. It's the gradual realization that the curve is far more complicated than the simple downward-sloping line of 1958. The curve shifts with expectations. It's nonlinear — much steeper when labor markets are very tight than when they're slack. The slope depends on credibility of the central bank's inflation target. It's been "anchored" by inflation targeting frameworks since the 1990s. And during episodes like 2021-22, supply-side shocks can dominate the demand-side mechanism entirely. The honest summary: the Phillips curve is alive but heavily modified, and any policymaker who treats it as a simple trade-off they can exploit will get burned. The 2021-24 episode is just the latest reminder.

— A.S.

19.1 The Original Phillips Curve

📘 Principles Level — The 1958 relationship

In 1958 William Phillips, a New Zealand economist working at the London School of Economics, published a paper plotting the rate of change of money wages against the unemployment rate in the United Kingdom from 1861 to 1957. He found a strikingly clear negative relationship: when unemployment was high, wage inflation was low or even negative; when unemployment was low, wage inflation was high. The fit across nearly a century of data was tighter than most economic relationships ever achieve.

In 1960 Paul Samuelson and Robert Solow at MIT replicated the analysis for the United States, substituting price inflation for wage inflation. Their version became the textbook Phillips curve: a downward-sloping curve in (unemployment, inflation) space. The economic interpretation was natural — low unemployment means tight labor markets, which means workers can bargain for faster wage growth, which firms pass through to prices. The policy implication seemed equally natural: policymakers can buy lower unemployment at the cost of higher inflation, and vice versa. The Phillips curve looked like a menu of policy options.

⚙ Equation Walkthrough — The Original Phillips Curve
π = α − β × u
π
Inflation rate (originally wage inflation, later generalized to price inflation).
u
Unemployment rate.
α
A positive constant — the inflation rate that would prevail at u = 0%.
β
The slope of the Phillips curve — how much inflation falls for each 1pp rise in unemployment. In Samuelson-Solow's U.S. data of 1960, β was estimated at roughly 0.5 to 1.0.

The Samuelson-Solow policy interpretation: if you want 4% inflation, you have to accept about 3% unemployment; if you want 0% inflation, you have to accept about 7% unemployment. The curve was treated as a stable menu — a structural feature of the economy that policymakers could move along like a demand curve. This interpretation, as it turned out, was wrong. But to understand why, we have to first see what happened in the 1970s.

19.2 The 1970s Breakdown: Stagflation

The simple Phillips curve worked beautifully through the 1960s. U.S. inflation rose from about 1% in 1961 to 4% by 1968 as unemployment fell from 7% to 3.5%. The Federal Reserve, taking the trade-off literally, ran an accommodating monetary policy that pushed unemployment down and accepted moderate inflation. Both rates of change tracked the Samuelson-Solow curve almost perfectly.

Then in the 1970s, something happened that the simple Phillips curve said couldn't happen. Inflation and unemployment both rose, simultaneously, for the better part of a decade. The 1973-75 episode saw U.S. CPI inflation hit 12% while unemployment reached 9%. The 1979-82 episode was worse: inflation peaked at 14.8% in 1980 while unemployment climbed past 10% by 1982. The phenomenon got a name — stagflation, the combination of stagnant growth and inflation. The Phillips curve appeared not just to have shifted but to have disappeared.

1968-1970 Friedman and Phelps Theory before data

The Friedman-Phelps Critique — Predicted Stagflation Before It Happened

In December 1967, in his presidential address to the American Economic Association, Milton Friedman delivered what is now considered one of the most consequential macroeconomic papers of the 20th century. Friedman argued that the Phillips curve was fundamentally misconceived. Workers and firms care about real wages, not nominal wages. If inflation is expected to be 0%, then a nominal wage increase of 3% really is a 3% real raise. But if inflation is expected to be 5%, then the same nominal 3% increase is actually a 2% real cut. Workers will demand higher nominal wages to compensate for expected inflation. The Phillips curve must therefore shift with expectations.

Edmund Phelps reached the same conclusion independently in 1968. Both predicted that if policymakers tried to keep unemployment permanently below its "natural rate" by accepting higher inflation, expectations would adjust upward, the Phillips curve would shift up, and eventually both unemployment and inflation would be higher than before. They predicted stagflation before it happened. When stagflation arrived in the 1970s, Friedman and Phelps were vindicated, and macroeconomics shifted permanently toward expectations-based modeling. Both won the Nobel Prize for this work (Friedman in 1976, Phelps in 2006).

19.3 The Expectations-Augmented Phillips Curve

⚙ Equation Walkthrough — The Expectations-Augmented Phillips Curve
π = πe − β × (u − u*) + supply_shock
πe
Expected inflation — what workers and firms expect inflation to be over the period when wages and prices are being set. The single most important addition to the simple Phillips curve.
u − u*
Unemployment gap — actual unemployment minus the natural rate u*. When unemployment is below the natural rate, inflation rises above expectations; above, inflation falls below expectations.
β
The slope coefficient. Smaller values mean a "flatter" curve — large changes in unemployment produce small changes in inflation. Empirical estimates have fallen from ~1.0 in the 1960s to as low as 0.1-0.2 in some post-2000 estimates.
supply_shock
A residual capturing supply-side disruptions — oil prices, supply chain disruptions, weather events — that move inflation independently of the unemployment gap.

The key insight: there's no permanent trade-off between unemployment and inflation. In the long run, expectations adjust to whatever actual inflation policymakers deliver, and the unemployment rate returns to its natural rate u*. The long-run Phillips curve is vertical at u = u*. Policymakers can only push unemployment below u* in the short run, and they pay for it later with a persistent rise in expected inflation.

⚠ Common Pitfall — Treating the natural rate as a constant

Textbooks often write u* as if it were a constant of nature. It isn't. The natural rate of unemployment depends on labor market institutions, demographics, technology, and search frictions — all of which change over time. The U.S. natural rate is estimated to have peaked near 6.5% in the early 1980s (high union power, demographic bulge of inexperienced young workers, oil price shocks driving structural unemployment) and fallen to as low as 4.0% by 2025 (deunionization, aging workforce, online job matching). What looks like a 5.5% unemployment rate moving above the natural rate in 1985 might look like a 5.5% unemployment rate moving well above the natural rate in 2025.

This matters for policymakers because the "appropriate" unemployment rate at any moment depends on what u* is — and u* can only be estimated, never observed directly. The CBO publishes an estimate, the Fed has its own, and academic estimates vary by 1+ percentage point at any given time. Anyone who quotes a confident estimate of "the" natural rate without uncertainty bands is overstating what we know.

19.4 The Long-Run Phillips Curve is Vertical

📗 Intermediate Level — Why no permanent trade-off exists

The expectations-augmented Phillips curve has a striking long-run property. In long-run equilibrium, expected inflation equals actual inflation: πe = π. Substituting this back into the Phillips equation:

π = π − β × (u − u*)
⟹ 0 = −β × (u − u*)
⟹ u = u*

In long-run equilibrium, unemployment must equal the natural rate, regardless of what inflation is. This is the famous result that the long-run Phillips curve is vertical. The economy can be in long-run equilibrium with 2% inflation and 4% unemployment, or with 10% inflation and 4% unemployment, or with 0% inflation and 4% unemployment — but unemployment in each case is 4%. The only equilibrium that depends on policy is the inflation rate. The unemployment rate is determined by structural factors.

This is the central long-run lesson of post-1970 macroeconomics: monetary policy can choose the long-run inflation rate but not the long-run unemployment rate. There's no permanent trade-off. Attempts to push unemployment below u* succeed temporarily but only by raising expected inflation, which then has to be reversed at the cost of an above-u* recession. The 1979-82 Volcker disinflation was exactly this reverse — bringing inflation back down after the 1960s-70s attempted exploitation of the (illusory) long-run trade-off.

19.5 The Sacrifice Ratio: What Disinflation Costs

⚙ Equation Walkthrough — The Sacrifice Ratio
Sacrifice Ratio = (cumulative excess unemployment) / (reduction in inflation)
cumulative excess unemployment
The sum of unemployment-rate percentage points above the natural rate over the disinflation period. If unemployment is 2pp above u* for 3 years, cumulative excess = 6 percentage point-years.
reduction in inflation
The total percentage-point reduction in inflation achieved over the disinflation episode.

Empirical estimates of the sacrifice ratio: Lawrence Ball's 1994 meta-analysis estimated the U.S. sacrifice ratio at approximately 2.4 percentage point-years of excess unemployment per percentage point of inflation reduction. The Volcker disinflation (15% inflation in 1980 to 4% inflation in 1983, with cumulative unemployment gap of about 20 percentage point-years) produced a sacrifice ratio of about 2.0 — slightly below Ball's average. The 2022-24 disinflation was unusual: 9.1% inflation to 2.4% with cumulative excess unemployment of essentially zero — a sacrifice ratio close to zero, an outcome that no standard model would have predicted.

1979-1983 United States Volcker Disinflation

The Volcker Disinflation — The Textbook Sacrifice Ratio in Action

When Paul Volcker became Federal Reserve Chair in August 1979, U.S. inflation was running at 11.5%. By 1980 it had peaked at 14.8%. Volcker raised the federal funds rate from 11% to a peak of about 20% in late 1980 (with a second peak of 19.1% in June 1981) and held it at extraordinarily high levels for over two years. The result: inflation fell to about 4% by 1983. The cost: unemployment rose from 5.8% in 1979 to 10.8% in November 1982 — the highest unemployment rate since the Great Depression. Cumulative excess unemployment over the 1980-1983 disinflation was approximately 20 percentage point-years.

This was textbook expectations-augmented Phillips curve behavior. To bring inflation down, expected inflation had to be brought down. Bringing expected inflation down required convincing workers and firms that the Fed would tolerate high unemployment indefinitely rather than back off the disinflation.
