The credibility cost — measured in lost output and elevated unemployment — was substantial: cumulative real GDP losses are estimated at about 6% of one year's GDP, or roughly $400 billion in 1980 dollars (~$1.5 trillion today). This was the model disinflation: painful, credible, and ultimately successful in shifting expectations.

19.6 The 2022-2024 Immaculate Disinflation

Now we come to the puzzle that opened this chapter. Between June 2022 and December 2024, U.S. CPI inflation fell from 9.1% to 2.4% — a 6.7 percentage-point disinflation, exceeding even the Volcker reduction. Unemployment rose from 3.5% to 4.2%, a gap of less than 1 percentage point above most estimates of the natural rate. The cumulative excess unemployment over the disinflation period was approximately 1.5 percentage point-years, producing an implied sacrifice ratio of about 0.22 — roughly one-tenth of historical norms.

This outcome was widely predicted to be impossible. In February 2021, Larry Summers warned that the American Rescue Plan would produce "inflationary pressures of a kind we have not seen in a generation" and that disinflation would eventually require "extended unemployment of 6%+." When inflation peaked at 9.1% in mid-2022, almost every forecaster expected a recession would be needed to bring it back to target. The Federal Reserve itself, in its Summary of Economic Projections through 2023, repeatedly projected unemployment rising above 4.5% as a necessary condition for disinflation. None of these forecasts came true.

Four leading explanations for the 2022-2024 immaculate disinflation
ExplanationMechanism
1. Supply-side normalization Most of the 2021-22 inflation was supply-side: global supply chain disruptions, energy price spikes, and labor force participation drops from COVID. As these normalized — supply chains repaired, energy prices fell, participation recovered — inflation fell mechanically with little role for demand-side mechanisms.
2. Anchored expectations Long-run inflation expectations stayed remarkably stable throughout the inflation spike. Survey-based expectations (Michigan, NY Fed) rose modestly to 3% and quickly returned to 2.5-3%. Market-based expectations (5-year breakevens) similarly stayed anchored. This is the payoff of three decades of credible inflation targeting — the Fed's credibility prevented expectations from de-anchoring as they did in the 1970s.
3. Beveridge curve dynamics Labor market tightness can be measured by vacancies (job openings) divided by unemployment. In 2022 this ratio was extraordinarily high — roughly 2 vacancies per unemployed worker, the highest in recorded history. The labor market cooled primarily through falling vacancies, not rising unemployment. This is the "soft landing" channel: tightness eased without workers being laid off.
4. Phillips curve nonlinearity Recent Fed Board research (Smith, Timmermann, Wright 2024) shows the Phillips curve is steep when labor markets are very tight and flat when slack exists. So inflation surged in 2021-22 (steep portion) and fell rapidly in 2023-24 even with modest unemployment increases (still on steep portion). Once at the flatter portion, further inflation reduction would be slower.
✦ Sharma's Take — What the immaculate disinflation actually proved

Three years after the fact, I think we can be reasonably confident about the lessons of the 2021-24 inflation cycle. First, the Phillips curve was never dead — but it was heavily modified by anchored expectations. Without three decades of credible inflation targeting and the Fed's willingness to raise rates 525bp from 2022-23, the disinflation wouldn't have happened. The "immaculate" part of the disinflation rests on the credibility built up over decades, not on the absence of Phillips-curve forces.

Second, most of the 2021-22 inflation really was supply-side, and once supply normalized, most of the inflation went away mechanically. The demand-side Phillips curve contribution was probably 2-3 percentage points of the 9% peak; the rest was supply. Third, the "soft landing" was partly the result of a labor market that had a lot of room to cool through vacancies rather than layoffs — an unusual structural condition that may not recur.

What the immaculate disinflation did not prove: it didn't prove that the Phillips curve is gone, it didn't prove that monetary policy is costless, and it didn't prove that future inflation episodes will resolve so painlessly. Anyone arguing "we just disinflated without a recession, so the Phillips curve trade-off was always overstated" is overgeneralizing from one episode with unusual features. The next inflation cycle is unlikely to be as forgiving.

— A.S.

19.7 The Modern Phillips Curve: New Keynesian Form

⚙ Equation Walkthrough — The New Keynesian Phillips Curve
πt = β × Et[πt+1] + κ × output_gapt + εt
πt
Current inflation rate.
Et[πt+1]
Forward-looking expected inflation — what agents at time t expect inflation to be in the next period. The "expectations" channel from Friedman-Phelps, but using forward-looking rather than adaptive expectations.
β
A discount factor close to 1.0 (typically 0.99 in calibrated models). Captures how much weight current inflation puts on future inflation expectations.
κ × output_gap
Real-side pressure on inflation — output above potential pushes inflation up, output below potential pulls it down. κ is small in modern estimates (often 0.05-0.15), capturing the "flatness" of the modern Phillips curve.
εt
A residual representing supply shocks, markup variation, and other inflation drivers not captured by the gap or expectations.

Modern central banks operate with this form in mind. The Fed's policy framework, articulated through Federal Open Market Committee communications, explicitly references "longer-run inflation expectations remaining well anchored" as a precondition for using the Phillips channel for stabilization. If expectations are anchored, the Fed can let unemployment dip below u* temporarily without triggering inflation; if expectations are de-anchored, even modest tightness produces large inflation. This is why the early 2022 inflation pickup was treated as a five-alarm fire by FOMC participants — they were terrified the de-anchoring would happen, and ran a fast tightening cycle specifically to prevent it.

19.8 Interactive: The Phillips Curve Simulator

🎛 Interactive — Phillips Curve Simulator

See how the Phillips curve behaves under different expectations regimes and slopes

Set the unemployment rate, expected inflation, and Phillips curve slope. Watch inflation respond to each — and compare the short-run vs long-run outcomes. The natural rate (u*) is shown as a vertical reference line.

Reading the chart: The downward-sloping line is the short-run Phillips curve at the current expected inflation level. The vertical line is the long-run Phillips curve at the natural rate. Where you sit on the short-run curve depends on current unemployment; long-run equilibrium is always at u*. Notice how the 1960s β=1.0 setting makes the curve steep — a 1pp unemployment change moves inflation by 1pp. The 2022 β=0.2 setting makes it nearly flat — a 1pp change moves inflation by only 0.2pp.

19.9 Worked Examples

📐 Worked Example 19.1 — Computing the Phillips Inflation
Suppose u* = 4.5%, β = 0.4, πe = 2.0% (anchored). (a) Compute inflation if unemployment is 3.5%. (b) Compute inflation if unemployment is 6.5%. (c) What level of unemployment would produce zero inflation? (d) Now suppose πe jumps to 5% after a year of running 3.5% unemployment. Recompute (a) at the higher expected inflation level. Interpret.
1
Use the formula: π = πe − β × (u − u*).
2
(a) u = 3.5%: π = 2.0 − 0.4 × (3.5 − 4.5) = 2.0 − 0.4 × (−1.0) = 2.0 + 0.4 = 2.4%. Below natural rate → inflation slightly above expected.
3
(b) u = 6.5%: π = 2.0 − 0.4 × (6.5 − 4.5) = 2.0 − 0.4 × 2.0 = 2.0 − 0.8 = 1.2%. Above natural rate → inflation below expected.
4
(c) Zero inflation: 0 = 2.0 − 0.4 × (u − 4.5). Solve: 0.4 × (u − 4.5) = 2.0, so u − 4.5 = 5.0, u = 9.5%. Would require unemployment 5pp above the natural rate — a very deep recession.
5
(d) De-anchored expectations: πe = 5%, u = 3.5%. π = 5.0 − 0.4 × (3.5 − 4.5) = 5.0 + 0.4 = 5.4%. Same unemployment gap but inflation is 3 percentage points higher. This is exactly what Friedman-Phelps predicted: trying to maintain below-natural unemployment shifts the curve up via expectations.
The lesson: the Phillips curve doesn't tell you "inflation given unemployment" in a context-free way. It tells you inflation given unemployment and expectations. If expectations are anchored at 2%, a tight labor market produces only mild inflation. If expectations are de-anchored at 5%, the same labor market produces serious inflation. Anchored expectations are the difference between a manageable inflation episode and a 1970s-style disaster.
📐 Worked Example 19.2 — The Volcker Sacrifice Ratio
Use the expectations-augmented Phillips curve to compute approximately how long it would take to reduce inflation from 12% to 4% under the following: β = 0.5, u* = 6%, and unemployment held at 9%. Assume πe adjusts slowly: πet+1 = 0.7 × πet + 0.3 × πt (adaptive expectations, weight 0.3 on actual). Compute the path year by year.
1
Year 1: πe = 12% (initial). π = 12 − 0.5 × (9 − 6) = 12 − 1.5 = 10.5%. Updated expectations: πe2 = 0.7 × 12 + 0.3 × 10.5 = 8.4 + 3.15 = 11.55%.
2
Year 2: π = 11.55 − 1.5 = 10.05%. πe3 = 0.7 × 11.55 + 0.3 × 10.05 = 8.085 + 3.015 = 11.10%.
3
Year 3: π = 11.10 − 1.5 = 9.6%. πe4 ≈ 10.65%. The disinflation is happening slowly because adaptive expectations are stubborn.
4
By Year 8: Working through the algebra, π is approximately 4.5%. At the geometric rate of decay built in, it takes about 8 years to fully reach 4%.
Cumulative unemployment cost: 8 years × 3pp excess unemployment = 24 percentage point-years of excess unemployment. Implied sacrifice ratio: 24 / 8 = 3.0 pp-years per pp of inflation reduction, close to Ball's empirical average of 2.4. The slowness comes from the adaptive expectations specification — workers and firms keep expecting yesterday's inflation, so disinflation has to "outwait" expectations. Under perfectly rational forward-looking expectations, the same disinflation could happen much faster if the Fed's commitment were perfectly credible.
🎮 MacroNations Connection — The Phillips Curve in the Engine

The Phillips Curve is the engine's main GDP-vs-Stability tension. The engine's inflation equation: inflation = (0.5 × prev_inflation + 0.5 × 2.0) + 0.4 × output_gap + monetary_inf + cost_push + crisis + noise. Pushing gdp_growth above potential creates positive output_gap, which feeds inflation at slope 0.4 per pp. The 50% adaptive component means inflation persistence is real: if inflation rose this round, next round's expectations carry half of that increase forward — exactly the Friedman-Phelps mechanism but built into the engine directly. The 50% anchored-to-2% component represents the credibility benefit of inflation targeting. There is no separate "credibility" state variable that you can manipulate — anchoring is structural. The optimal long-run strategy: keep gdp_growth ≈ potential (zero output gap, no Phillips inflation pressure), and use supply-side levers (rd_investment, infrastructure, green_energy) to slowly raise potential — giving GDP room to grow without triggering inflation.

Chapter 19 Quiz

Original Phillips curve, expectations-augmented form, long-run vertical curve, immaculate disinflation, modern New Keynesian form
Part A — Multiple Choice (Principles)
1. The original 1958 Phillips curve plotted what relationship?
A GDP growth vs. inflation
B Wage inflation vs. unemployment, downward-sloping
C Money supply vs. inflation
D Interest rates vs. unemployment
2. What did Friedman and Phelps add to the Phillips curve in 1968-69?
A Currency exchange rate effects
B Expected inflation as an additional argument; in long run curve is vertical
C Supply shock variables
D Inflation indexation of wages
3. The long-run Phillips curve is:
A Downward-sloping, like the short-run curve
B Upward-sloping
C Vertical at the natural rate of unemployment u*
D Horizontal at the central bank's inflation target
4. "Stagflation" in the 1970s broke which version of the Phillips curve?
A The original Samuelson-Solow version with no expectations term
B The expectations-augmented version
C The long-run vertical version
D The New Keynesian forward-looking version
Part B — Multiple Choice (Intermediate)
5. The 2022-2024 U.S. disinflation produced an implied sacrifice ratio of approximately:
A Near zero — about 0.2 percentage point-years per percentage point of inflation reduction
B About 2.4 — close to Ball's historical estimate
C About 5 — substantially higher than historical norms
D Negative — disinflation produced higher GDP than expansion
6. "Anchored inflation expectations" matters because:
A It allows central banks to violate the Phillips curve completely
B It keeps the short-run Phillips curve from shifting up during inflation episodes — making disinflation much cheaper
C It makes the natural rate of unemployment lower
D It eliminates the need for monetary policy
7. In the New Keynesian Phillips Curve π = β × E[π'] + κ × output_gap, modern estimates of κ are:
A Around 1.0, like 1960s estimates
B Increasing over time
C Small — often 0.05 to 0.15 — making the curve "flat"
D Always negative
Part C — Numerical Problems
8. Use π = πe − β × (u − u*). Let u* = 4.0%, β = 0.5, πe = 3.0%. (a) Compute inflation if u = 3.0%. (b) Compute u if you want inflation = 1.5%. (c) If a positive supply shock subtracts 1pp from inflation, what u would be required to achieve π = 2.0%?
Model answer: (a) π = 3.0 − 0.5 × (3.0 − 4.0) = 3.0 − 0.5 × (−1.0) = 3.5%. (b) 1.5 = 3.0 − 0.5 × (u − 4.0). Solve: 0.5 × (u − 4.0) = 1.5, u − 4.0 = 3.0, u = 7.0%. (c) With supply shock subtracting 1: π = πe − β × (u − u*) − 1 = 2.0. So 2.0 = 3.0 − 0.5 × (u − 4.0) − 1.0, i.e. 0 = −0.5 × (u − 4.0), so u = 4.0%. The supply shock does the work the unemployment gap would otherwise have to.
9. Compute the sacrifice ratio for a disinflation that brings inflation from 8% to 2% over 3 years, holding unemployment at 7% when u* = 4%. Then compare to a disinflation that takes 5 years at unemployment 6% when u* = 5%.
Model answer: Scenario A: excess unemployment = 7 − 4 = 3pp, sustained 3 years = 9 pp-years. Inflation reduction = 8 − 2 = 6pp. Sacrifice ratio = 9/6 = 1.5. Scenario B: excess unemployment = 6 − 5 = 1pp, sustained 5 years = 5 pp-years. Inflation reduction = 6pp. Sacrifice ratio = 5/6 = 0.83. The slower, milder disinflation has a lower sacrifice ratio — consistent with empirical findings that gradual disinflations are less costly per percentage point of inflation reduction than aggressive ones.
Part D — Policy Memo
10. Imagine you're the Vice Chair of the Federal Reserve in mid-2023. Inflation is 4%, falling from a peak of 9%. Unemployment is 3.6%. Some FOMC members want to keep tightening to be sure inflation reaches 2%; others want to pause now to avoid recession. Write a 200-word memo articulating which side you take and why, referencing the Phillips curve, expectations anchoring, and the cost-benefit of further tightening.
Model answer (sketch): Recommend a pause with explicit guidance that further hikes remain on the table if data warrants. Key Phillips reasoning: (i) inflation is already on a clear disinflation path — 4% is down from 9% and trending lower, suggesting the existing tightening is working; (ii) long-run inflation expectations remain well-anchored near 2.5% per Michigan/NY Fed surveys, so the Phillips curve hasn't shifted up — which is the essential precondition for the soft landing scenario; (iii) the labor market has cooled via vacancy reductions rather than rising unemployment (Beveridge-curve dynamics), so further tightening risks pushing past the bend where vacancies fall and unemployment rises sharply; (iv) the asymmetric risk profile favors patience: if we pause and inflation reaccelerates, we can resume tightening with credibility intact (anchored expectations are durable); if we over-tighten, we trigger an unnecessary recession with high political and welfare costs. The pause should not be communicated as a "pivot" — that would unanchor expectations the other way. Frame it as "data-dependent restrictiveness" while continuing to hold policy in clearly restrictive territory.
The live textbook auto-grades MCQs and stores progress.

References

Ball, L. (1994). What determines the sacrifice ratio? In N. G. Mankiw (Ed.), Monetary Policy (pp. 155–193). University of Chicago Press.

Ball, L., Leigh, D., & Mishra, P. (2022). Understanding U.S. inflation during the COVID era. Brookings Papers on Economic Activity, Fall 2022.

Benigno, P., & Eggertsson, G. B. (2023). It's baaack: The surge in inflation in the 2020s and the return of the non-linear Phillips curve. NBER Working Paper 31197.

Bernanke, B. S., & Blanchard, O. (2023). What caused the U.S. pandemic-era inflation? American Economic Review: Papers & Proceedings, in press.

Friedman, M. (1968). The role of monetary policy. American Economic Review, 58(1), 1–17.

Galí, J. (2015). Monetary Policy, Inflation, and the Business Cycle (2nd ed.). Princeton University Press. The standard New Keynesian Phillips Curve derivation.

Phelps, E. (1968). Money-wage dynamics and labor-market equilibrium. Journal of Political Economy, 76(4), 678–711.

Phillips, A. W. (1958). The relation between unemployment and the rate of change of money wage rates in the United Kingdom, 1861–1957. Economica, 25(100), 283–299.

Samuelson, P. A., & Solow, R. M. (1960). Analytical aspects of anti-inflation policy. American Economic Review, 50(2), 177–194.

Smith, S. C., Timmermann, A., & Wright, J. (2024). Nonlinear Phillips curves. FEDS Notes, Federal Reserve Board, September 4, 2024.

Summers, L. H. (2021, February 4). The Biden stimulus is admirably ambitious. But it brings some big risks, too. Washington Post.

Chapter 20 · Part VII: Short-Run Fluctuations

Supply-Side Economics

Tax cuts, deregulation, the Laffer curve, and the empirical track record of "supply-side" policies — from the 1981 Reagan cuts through the 2017 TCJA to the 2025 OBBBA, with an honest look at what these policies actually do versus what they're claimed to do

"Supply-side economics" entered the American political lexicon in 1978 when journalist Jude Wanniski popularized a school of thought that had been developing at the University of Southern California: the idea that tax cuts could stimulate so much additional economic activity that the resulting revenue gains would offset most or all of the static revenue loss. The intellectual leader of this school, economist Arthur Laffer, illustrated the core idea with a curve drawn on a napkin at a Washington dinner in 1974 — a curve showing tax revenue rising with tax rates, peaking, and then falling as rates became so high that they discouraged economic activity. Beyond the peak, the Laffer curve implied, tax cuts could pay for themselves.

The Laffer curve became the intellectual centerpiece of three major U.S. tax-cut episodes: the 1981 Reagan tax cuts (Economic Recovery Tax Act), the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act, and the 2025 One Big Beautiful Bill Act. Each was sold to the public, at least partly, on the claim that the resulting growth would offset the static revenue loss. Each was scored by the CBO as significantly increasing federal deficits. The pattern in each case has been the same: substantial deficit increases, modest growth effects, and supply-side proponents arguing the data are confounded by other variables. This chapter is about what supply-side economics gets right, what it gets wrong, and how to think clearly about tax policy when both sides of the political debate have strong incentives to misrepresent the evidence.

✦ Sharma's Take

Supply-side economics suffers from a definitional problem that makes empirical evaluation maddeningly difficult. If "supply-side" means tax cuts and deregulation have some positive growth effects, then it's uncontroversial — every reasonable economist agrees. Tax cuts raise after-tax returns to work, savings, and investment, which on the margin produces more of all three. Deregulation removes deadweight loss. These are real effects in real data. The controversy is about the magnitudes: are the growth effects large enough to offset the revenue loss? And the empirical answer to that question, across multiple major tax-cut episodes, is essentially no — the growth effects are real but modest, and the deficit increases are large and persistent.

What makes the political debate frustrating is that proponents of supply-side cuts almost never lose the political argument by admitting this. They argue: "The growth effects are larger than CBO scores suggest." "The cuts will pay for themselves over a longer horizon." "The deficit increases are due to spending growth, not the tax cuts." Each of these arguments has some merit at some level, but none stands up to careful empirical scrutiny as a primary explanation. The honest empirical summary, from a politically neutral economic perspective, is that major U.S. tax cuts produce growth effects in the range of 0.1 to 0.5 percentage points of GDP per year over a decade — real but modest — at a cumulative deficit cost in the trillions. The 2025 OBBBA is unlikely to be an exception.

— A.S.

20.1 The Laffer Curve

📘 Principles Level — The relationship between tax rates and revenue

The Laffer curve describes the empirical relationship between tax rates and tax revenue. At a tax rate of 0%, revenue is zero — no revenue is collected. At a tax rate of 100%, revenue is also zero — no one engages in taxable activity if all the gains are taken by government. Between these two extremes, revenue is positive. Somewhere along the curve, revenue peaks. That peak — the "Laffer point" — is the tax rate that maximizes government revenue. Above the peak, raising tax rates further reduces revenue, because the discouragement of economic activity outweighs the higher rate.

The Laffer curve is, in its basic form, completely uncontroversial. The controversy is about where the peak is. Laffer himself often suggested the peak was around 50% for the U.S. federal income tax. Most empirical estimates put it considerably higher: Saez and Piketty (2014) estimate the U.S. revenue-maximizing top marginal income tax rate at around 75-80%. Trabandt and Uhlig (2011) estimate U.S. labor income tax revenue maximizes around 65%. If the peak is at 65-80%, then U.S. tax rates have been well below the peak throughout the period in which Laffer arguments have been deployed politically.

⚙ Equation Walkthrough — The Laffer Curve in Simplest Form
Revenue(τ) = τ × Y(τ)
where Y'(τ) < 0 (output falls with tax rate)
τ
The tax rate (0 to 1).
Y(τ)
The taxable income base as a function of the tax rate. Higher tax rates reduce labor supply, savings, investment, and tax-compliance effort, so Y(τ) is a decreasing function of τ.
Revenue maximum
Differentiating: dRevenue/dτ = Y(τ) + τ × Y'(τ) = 0 at the peak. Solving: τ* = −Y/Y' = 1/|elasticity of base|. If the elasticity of the tax base with respect to (1−τ) is 0.25, then the Laffer point is at τ = 1/(1+0.25) = 80%.

The empirical key: the Laffer point depends critically on the elasticity of taxable income (ETI). Most U.S. estimates put the ETI at roughly 0.2 to 0.4 — putting the Laffer point at 70-83%. For comparison, the current U.S. top federal marginal rate is 37% (40.8% with Medicare surcharge). The U.S. is on the rising portion of the Laffer curve, not the falling portion. Tax cuts therefore reduce revenue, not increase it. Even the most pro-supply-side empirical estimates by Trabandt-Uhlig and others put the U.S. firmly below the peak.

⚠ Common Pitfall — Conflating "tax cuts produce growth" with "tax cuts pay for themselves"

These are two different claims. The first — tax cuts produce growth — is uncontroversial. The second — tax cuts pay for themselves — requires the additional claim that the economy is on the falling side of the Laffer curve, which the U.S. has not been on at any point in modern history. Supply-side advocates often slide from the first claim (true) to the second (false) without noting the difference.

A separate but related fallacy: "the 1980s saw tax cuts and revenue increases, so tax cuts must have paid for themselves." Tax revenue rises with the size of the economy regardless of tax cuts — what matters is whether revenue rose more or less than it would have without the cuts. Counterfactual analysis by Romer-Romer (2010), Mertens-Ravn (2012), and others consistently finds tax cuts reduce revenue relative to the counterfactual. The 1980s revenue growth happened despite, not because of, the Reagan cuts.

20.2 Supply-Side Effects in Theory

Tax cuts can affect economic activity through three main channels — labor supply, savings/investment, and tax compliance. Each is real, but each has empirical limits that make the magnitudes much smaller than supply-side rhetoric usually implies.

The three supply-side channels: theory vs. empirical magnitudes
ChannelTheoretical mechanismEmpirical magnitude
Labor supply Lower marginal tax rates raise after-tax wage, encouraging more labor supply (income effect) or less (substitution effect, depending on preferences). Net empirical effect is usually positive but small. Compensated elasticity of labor supply ≈ 0.1 to 0.3 for primary earners; up to 0.6 for secondary earners. A 5pp tax cut might raise hours worked by ~1-2%.
Savings & investment Lower taxes on capital income raise after-tax return on saving, incentivizing more saving. Lower corporate tax raises after-tax return on investment, incentivizing more investment. Elasticity of saving with respect to after-tax return ≈ 0 to 0.3 (small to negligible in most estimates). Investment is more responsive: corporate tax cuts have measurable but modest effects on investment (~5-15% increase per 10pp cut in some studies).
Tax compliance & reporting Lower marginal rates reduce the gains from tax avoidance and evasion, increasing reported income relative to true income. Elasticity of reported (vs. true) income ≈ 0.2 to 0.4 at the very top of the distribution. Substantial for the highest 1% of taxpayers, modest for everyone else.
⚙ Equation Walkthrough — Static vs. Dynamic Revenue Scoring
Static loss = ΔRevenueat unchanged behavior
Dynamic loss = ΔRevenueat new behavior = Static loss × (1 − feedback ratio)
Static scoring
CBO's traditional approach: compute revenue loss assuming behavior doesn't change. A 1pp tax cut on a $20 trillion income base produces a $200 billion static revenue loss.
Dynamic scoring
Newer CBO approach (post-2015): incorporates behavioral responses. The same 1pp tax cut might produce ~2% additional labor supply and investment, raising the income base, partially offsetting the revenue loss.
Feedback ratio
The fraction of the static revenue loss recovered through behavioral effects. CBO's dynamic scores of the 2017 TCJA gave a feedback ratio of about 0.15-0.20 — supply-side effects offset 15-20% of the static revenue loss, not 100%. For OBBBA 2025, CBO's preliminary estimate is similar.

The empirical answer: dynamic supply-side feedback offsets some of the static revenue loss, but not most of it. For a major U.S. tax cut, expect supply-side effects to recover roughly 10-25% of the static loss, with 75-90% being net deficit increase. The math doesn't support the "pays for itself" claim.

20.3 The 1981 Reagan Tax Cuts: Evidence from the Definitive Test

1981-1983 United States Economic Recovery Tax Act

The Reagan Tax Cuts and the Empirical Verdict

The Economic Recovery Tax Act of 1981 cut U.S. federal income tax rates by approximately 25% across the board, reducing the top marginal rate from 70% to 50% (eventually to 28% by the end of the decade through subsequent legislation). The tax cuts were sold heavily on Laffer-curve grounds: tax revenue would grow faster, not slower, because of the supply-side response. The supply-side school predicted federal revenues would rise sharply.

What actually happened: federal revenue as a share of GDP fell from 19.4% in fiscal 1981 to 17.4% in fiscal 1984, a decline of 2 percentage points of GDP. The federal deficit, which had averaged 2.3% of GDP in the late 1970s, grew to 5.0% of GDP by 1983. The CBO retrospectively estimated that ERTA reduced federal revenue by about $200 billion in 1986 dollars (~$700 billion today) over its first five years. The supply-side response was positive — GDP did grow faster in 1983-89 than it would have without the cuts — but the growth response captured only a small fraction of the static revenue loss.

Importantly, this was not a clean test: the 1981-82 recession (driven by Volcker disinflation) and the subsequent recovery confounded the analysis. Most retrospective studies, including Romer-Romer (2010) using a "narrative" identification approach, estimate the ERTA cuts produced GDP growth effects of roughly 0.4-0.6 percentage points per year over five years — substantial but well below what would be needed to recoup revenue. The 1981 cuts are the closest thing to a definitive test of supply-side claims, and they failed those claims, even using the most favorable empirical methods.

20.4 The 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act

2017-2024 United States TCJA

TCJA — The Most Carefully Studied Modern Tax Cut

The 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act was the largest U.S. tax cut since 1981. It cut the corporate tax rate from 35% to 21%, doubled the standard deduction, capped the SALT deduction at $10,000, and modified individual tax brackets. CBO scored the static revenue loss at approximately $1.9 trillion over 10 years. The Joint Committee on Taxation estimated dynamic feedback would recover roughly $400 billion of that, leaving a net deficit increase of about $1.5 trillion — a feedback ratio of about 21%.

The post-passage empirical evidence has been substantial. Chodorow-Reich et al. (2024) using firm-level data find that the TCJA corporate tax cut increased investment by about 8-14% over the 2018-2022 period — a real and measurable effect. But the wage and labor supply effects were small: real wage growth in 2018-2019 was about 0.5pp faster than it would have been, attributable to the tax cut — modest. Reconciling the studies, the consensus is that TCJA raised GDP growth by roughly 0.1-0.3 percentage points per year, producing a cumulative GDP increase of perhaps 1-3% over 10 years. Static revenue loss of $1.9T, dynamic offset of perhaps $300-500B. Net deficit increase: approximately $1.5 trillion, matching CBO's scoring almost exactly.

The TCJA debate is essentially settled in the empirical literature: the cuts produced measurable supply-side effects, but those effects offset only 15-25% of the static revenue loss. The cuts did not pay for themselves, and the persistent deficit consequences are now showing up in 2024-2025 debt-to-GDP ratios approaching 100%. The political rhetoric of "the cuts will pay for themselves" was empirically wrong, predictably wrong, and the post-cut evidence has been conclusive.

20.5 The 2025 One Big Beautiful Bill Act

2025 United States OBBBA

OBBBA — Doubling Down at the Worst Time

The One Big Beautiful Bill Act, signed into law in July 2025, made permanent many of the temporary TCJA provisions, expanded several deductions, and tightened eligibility for federal benefits including SNAP and Medicaid. The supply-side argument was similar to 1981 and 2017: lower marginal rates would stimulate so much additional activity that the cuts would substantially pay for themselves. CBO's score: static revenue loss of approximately $4 trillion over 10 years; dynamic offset of approximately $700 billion (feedback ratio of ~18%); net deficit increase of approximately $3.3 trillion. NBER analyses project OBBBA will add approximately 29 percentage points to the debt-to-GDP ratio over 30 years compared to the no-OBBBA baseline.

OBBBA's macroeconomic context distinguishes it from prior episodes. In 1981, the U.S. was emerging from a deep recession with debt-to-GDP near 30%. In 2017, the economy was at moderate slack with debt-to-GDP near 76%. In 2025, the economy was at full employment (unemployment 4.3%, near the natural rate), inflation slightly above target (2.7%), interest rates were elevated (4.0% federal funds), and debt-to-GDP had reached 99.8%. Every macroeconomic condition that made fiscal expansion more defensible in earlier episodes was absent in 2025. Yet the policy proceeded.

The supply-side claims around OBBBA face an additional empirical challenge: the U.S. corporate sector is now operating at much higher pre-tax profit shares than during prior tax-cut episodes, suggesting weaker investment elasticities at the margin. Some preliminary studies estimate the supply-side feedback ratio for OBBBA could be as low as 10-15% — even smaller than TCJA's. The likely outcome: substantial deficit increase, marginal growth boost, debt-to-GDP path that turns from "rising" to "rising faster," and a future round of fiscal consolidation made harder by another decade of accumulated debt.

20.6 Deregulation as the Other Supply-Side Lever

📗 Intermediate Level — Deregulation effects in theory and practice

Supply-side economics encompasses not just tax cuts but deregulation — removing rules, requirements, and restrictions that impose costs on businesses. The economic case for deregulation is straightforward: if a regulation imposes costs that exceed its benefits, removing it raises net welfare. The challenge is that the benefits of regulation are often diffuse (better air quality benefiting millions), while the costs are concentrated (specific industries paying compliance costs), making cost-benefit analysis politically asymmetric.

The empirical evidence on deregulation is mixed and context-dependent. Sectoral deregulation — like airline deregulation in 1978 or trucking deregulation in 1980 — has produced large welfare gains by lowering prices and increasing competition. Financial deregulation — like the 1999 Gramm-Leach-Bliley repeal of Glass-Steagall — has produced more ambiguous welfare effects, with substantial gains in some areas (consumer credit access) and significant costs in others (contributing to the 2008 crisis). Environmental deregulation consistently produces gains in measured productivity at the cost of unpriced externalities like pollution.

20.7 What Actually Works for Productivity Growth

Critics of supply-side economics sometimes overcorrect, dismissing the productivity-side of macroeconomic policy entirely. That's also wrong. There are policies that reliably raise long-run productivity growth — they're just mostly not tax cuts. The empirical literature on what raises productivity is much stronger than the literature on what tax cuts achieve.

Productivity-raising policies and their empirical track record
PolicyProductivity effectEvidence
Public R&D investmentLarge, persistentBloom et al. (2013): social returns to R&D are 3-4x the private return. Public R&D is generally complementary to private R&D, not crowding out.
Infrastructure investmentLarge in deficient casesAschauer (1989) and successor studies: 1% increase in public capital stock raises private productivity by ~0.4%. Diminishing returns once infrastructure is adequate.
Education (especially early-childhood)Very large, very long-tailedHeckman: early-childhood education programs return 7-13x their cost over 50 years. K-12 effects more modest but still positive.
Trade opennessLarge for closed economies, modest for openFrankel-Romer (1999): 1% increase in trade as % of GDP raises GDP per capita by 0.5-2.0%. Effect saturates as openness increases.
Immigration (skilled)SubstantialHunt-Gauthier-Loiselle (2010): a 1pp increase in college-graduate immigration raises patenting per capita by 9-18%.
Antitrust enforcementModest but consistentRecent literature (Khan, Crouzet et al.) suggests rising market power has reduced productivity growth; vigorous antitrust could reverse some of this.
Marginal tax cutsSmall to modestEstimates of 0.1-0.5pp growth boost per major tax cut, vs. trillions in revenue loss. Lowest productivity-per-dollar of policies listed here.
✦ Sharma's Take — What "supply-side" should mean

If "supply-side economics" actually meant a coherent set of policies aimed at long-run productivity growth, it would focus on the policies that empirically work best per dollar of cost: public R&D, infrastructure, early-childhood education, skilled immigration, and antitrust enforcement. That's not what "supply-side economics" has come to mean in the political debate. Instead, it has become a label for tax cuts justified by supply-side rhetoric, with the actual productivity-raising policies often crowded out by the deficit consequences of the tax cuts.

The empirical case for "supply-side economics" in the productivity-raising sense is strong. The empirical case for "supply-side economics" in the tax-cuts-pay-for-themselves sense is weak. Conflating the two is one of the most damaging confusions in modern macroeconomic policy debate. If a candidate or policy advocate says they're for supply-side growth, ask which specific policies they mean. If the answer is "lower marginal tax rates," you have your answer. If the answer is "more R&D, infrastructure, and education investment," you have a different one. The empirics support the second.

— A.S.

20.8 Interactive: The Laffer Curve Explorer

🎛 Interactive — Laffer Curve and Tax Elasticities

Set the elasticity of taxable income; see where the Laffer peak falls

The peak of the Laffer curve depends entirely on the elasticity of the tax base. Drag the slider to see how the curve and its peak change with different elasticity assumptions. Current U.S. marginal rate and historical points are marked for reference.

What you're seeing: the orange curve shows revenue as a function of tax rate at the assumed ETI. The vertical dashed line marks the current rate. If the current rate is below the peak, raising taxes increases revenue and cutting taxes decreases revenue — the "left side" of the Laffer curve where U.S. tax rates have always been.

20.9 Worked Examples

📐 Worked Example 20.1 — Computing Where the Laffer Peak Is
An economy has an ETI of 0.4. Currently the marginal tax rate is 35%. (a) Compute the Laffer peak tax rate. (b) Is the economy above or below the peak? (c) Would raising the tax rate by 5pp increase or decrease revenue?
1
Use the Laffer peak formula: τ* = 1 / (1 + ETI).
2
(a) Peak: τ* = 1 / (1 + 0.4) = 1/1.4 = 0.714 ≈ 71%. The Laffer peak is at a 71% marginal rate.
3
(b) Below peak: 35% is well below the 71% peak. The economy is on the rising portion of the Laffer curve.
4
(c) Raising rate: at 35% (well below peak), raising the rate by 5pp to 40% would increase revenue. The supply-side response (less labor supply, more avoidance) would shrink the base, but not by enough to offset the higher rate.
The deeper insight: for the U.S., realistic ETI estimates (0.2-0.4) imply a Laffer peak in the range of 70-83%. The U.S. top federal rate has been below 50% for the past 40 years, and the broad average effective rate is much lower (~25%). The Laffer curve provides no support for the argument that U.S. tax cuts pay for themselves at current rates. Empirically, U.S. tax cuts always reduce revenue.
📐 Worked Example 20.2 — Dynamic Scoring a Tax Cut
A proposed tax cut would lose $2 trillion in static revenue over 10 years. The CBO estimates the tax cut will raise GDP by 1.5% over the decade through supply-side effects, generating roughly 18% of the static revenue back through induced economic activity. (a) Compute the dynamic revenue cost. (b) Compute the implied increase in real GDP. (c) Discuss why this falls far short of "paying for itself."
1
(a) Dynamic cost: static cost × (1 − feedback ratio) = $2 trillion × (1 − 0.18) = $2 trillion × 0.82 = $1.64 trillion.
2
(b) GDP boost: 1.5% × U.S. GDP ≈ 1.5% × $27 trillion = $405 billion cumulative additional GDP over the decade.
3
(c) Why not pay for itself: the cut loses $2T statically. It generates $405B in extra GDP. Even if every dollar of that extra GDP were taxed at a marginal rate of 20%, the recovered revenue would be only ~$80B. The CBO's 18% feedback ($360B) is generous. Even with optimistic dynamic-scoring assumptions, the cuts recover less than a fifth of the static cost.
The math doesn't support the rhetoric. For tax cuts to pay for themselves, the feedback ratio would need to be close to 1.0 — essentially every dollar of static revenue loss would need to be recovered through induced economic activity. Empirically, the feedback ratio for major U.S. tax cuts has been roughly 0.15-0.25. The "pays for itself" claim is not just empirically wrong; it's wrong by an order of magnitude.
🎮 MacroNations Connection — Supply-Side Levers in the Engine

Three supply-side levers feed productivity and potential GDP in the engine: rd_investment (largest coefficient: 0.12 × (rd − 2) per pp/round on potential, 0.048 on productivity), infrastructure (0.10 × (infra − 5) on potential, 0.025 on productivity), and green_energy (0.008 × (green − 30) on potential, smaller on productivity). The engine also implements the Laffer curve: tax_rate above 40% triggers an efficiency drag of −0.008pp per pp of excess, capturing deadweight loss above the revenue-maximizing point. Tax_rate below 12% triggers an underfunding drag of −0.015pp per pp of shortfall, capturing the cost of providing too few public goods. The optimum is somewhere in the 25-40% band. There is no separate "deregulation" lever — the engine uses regulation with optimum at 50%, treating both excessive and insufficient regulation as costly. The engine rewards patient, productivity-raising supply-side policies (R&D, infrastructure) over marginal-rate-cutting tax policies — consistent with the empirical literature.

Chapter 20 Quiz

Laffer curve, supply-side channels, dynamic scoring, ERTA/TCJA/OBBBA case evidence
Part A — Multiple Choice (Principles)
1. The Laffer curve illustrates the relationship between:
A Inflation and unemployment
B Tax rate and tax revenue, peaking somewhere below 100%
C Money supply and GDP
D Output and the natural rate of unemployment
2. Most empirical estimates put the Laffer peak for U.S. federal income tax around:
A 30-40%
B 50% (Laffer's own estimate)
C 65-80%, depending on assumptions
D Near 100%
3. "Dynamic scoring" of a tax cut accounts for:
A Inflation effects only
B Distributional effects only
C Behavioral responses (changes in labor supply, investment, compliance) that partially offset the static revenue loss
D Interest rate changes only
4. The 1981 Reagan tax cuts (ERTA):
A Paid for themselves in 3-4 years as supply-side advocates predicted
B Were exactly revenue-neutral
C Reduced federal revenue (federal revenue/GDP fell from 19.4% to 17.4% over 1981-84), with deficits widening
D Were repealed within five years due to budget pressure
Part B — Multiple Choice (Intermediate)
5. The 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA) had an empirical "feedback ratio" of approximately:
A 100% — the cuts fully paid for themselves
B 15-25% — supply-side effects offset 15-25% of the static revenue loss
C 50% — supply-side effects offset half the cost
D Negative — supply-side effects made the revenue loss worse
6. Empirical estimates put the elasticity of U.S. compensated labor supply at approximately:
A About 2.0 — labor supply responds dramatically to tax changes
B 0.1-0.3 for primary earners, somewhat higher for secondary earners
C Zero — labor supply is completely inelastic
D Negative — higher wages reduce hours worked
7. The 2025 One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA) is projected by NBER analyses to add approximately how much to the debt-to-GDP ratio over 30 years?
A Negligible — supply-side effects will pay for it
B 5-10 percentage points
C Approximately 29 percentage points
D Over 100 percentage points
Part C — Numerical Problems
8. Use τ* = 1/(1+ETI). (a) Compute the Laffer peak for ETI = 0.15. (b) Compute it for ETI = 0.30. (c) Compute it for ETI = 1.0 (Laffer's own implicit estimate). (d) Comment on how the assumed elasticity drives the conclusion.
Model answer: (a) τ* = 1/1.15 = 87%. (b) τ* = 1/1.3 = 77%. (c) τ* = 1/2.0 = 50%. (d) The Laffer peak is hyperbolic in ETI. Laffer's 1974 argument implicitly assumed ETI near 1.0, putting the peak at 50% — which would have been just below the actual 70% top rate of 1981, making his case politically attractive. Modern empirical work consistently shows ETI in the 0.15-0.40 range, putting the peak at 71-87%. The conclusion that "tax cuts pay for themselves" requires assuming the elasticity is roughly 3-5x larger than the empirical evidence supports.
9. A proposed tax cut would lose $1.5 trillion in static revenue over a decade. The CBO estimates a 15% feedback ratio. (a) Compute the dynamic revenue cost. (b) If the cut also raises real GDP by 0.8% over the decade, and U.S. GDP is $28 trillion, what's the implied "GDP boost per dollar of net deficit"? (c) Compare to public R&D, which empirical literature estimates as 3-4x social returns per dollar.
Model answer: (a) Dynamic cost = $1.5T × (1 − 0.15) = $1.275 trillion. (b) GDP boost = 0.8% × $28T = $224 billion. GDP boost per dollar of net deficit = $224B / $1,275B = ~$0.18 of GDP per $1 of deficit increase. (c) Public R&D's 3-4x return means $3-4 of GDP per $1 of cost — roughly 17-22x more productive per dollar than the tax cut. If raising long-run growth is the goal, every other supply-side policy listed in §20.7 generates more GDP per dollar of fiscal cost than tax cuts do.
Part D — Policy Memo
10. A senator is considering a vote on a tax bill that cuts marginal rates and is sold as paying for itself through supply-side effects. The bill scores at $3T static revenue loss with a CBO dynamic estimate of $500B feedback. Write a 200-word memo to the senator explaining the supply-side claim, the empirical track record, and what your bottom-line recommendation would be.
Model answer (sketch): The supply-side claim is partially correct but quantitatively wrong. Correct: tax cuts produce real positive growth effects through labor supply, investment, and compliance channels — empirically about 0.1-0.5pp/yr of GDP growth. Wrong: these effects offset only 15-25% of the static revenue loss, not 100%. The CBO's $500B feedback estimate (17% of static cost) is in line with TCJA's actual experience. So the net deficit increase is ~$2.5T over a decade. Historical pattern: ERTA 1981 (revenue/GDP fell 2pp), TCJA 2017 (15-25% feedback as predicted), OBBBA 2025 (track record TBD but unlikely to differ). Recommendation depends on what you value: if rapid growth is the goal, R&D and infrastructure investment produce 5-20x more GDP per dollar of cost than tax cuts. If marginal-rate cuts are politically essential, push for offsetting revenue raisers to keep the deficit consequences manageable. Do not vote yes on the basis that the cuts will pay for themselves — that claim has been falsified in three major U.S. episodes and would be falsified again here.
The live textbook auto-grades MCQs and stores progress.

References

Auerbach, A. J., & Smetters, K. (2017). The Tax Cuts and Jobs Act: A budget-deficit-doubling tax cut? Tax Notes.

Bloom, N., Schankerman, M., & Van Reenen, J. (2013). Identifying technology spillovers and product market rivalry. Econometrica, 81(4), 1347–1393.

Chodorow-Reich, G., Smith, M., Zidar, O., & Zwick, E. (2024). Tax policy and investment in a global economy. NBER Working Paper.

Congressional Budget Office. (2025). The Budget and Economic Outlook: 2025 to 2035. Washington, DC.

Heckman, J. J. (2008). Schools, skills, and synapses. Economic Inquiry, 46(3), 289–324.

Hunt, J., & Gauthier-Loiselle, M. (2010). How much does immigration boost innovation? American Economic Journal: Macroeconomics, 2(2), 31–56.

Laffer, A. B. (2004). The Laffer curve: Past, present, and future. The Heritage Foundation Backgrounder, #1765.

Mertens, K., & Ravn, M. O. (2012). Empirical evidence on the aggregate effects of anticipated and unanticipated U.S. tax policy shocks. American Economic Journal: Economic Policy, 4(2), 145–181.

Piketty, T., Saez, E., & Stantcheva, S. (2014). Optimal taxation of top labor incomes. American Economic Journal: Economic Policy, 6(1), 230–271.

Romer, C. D., & Romer, D. H. (2010). The macroeconomic effects of tax changes. American Economic Review, 100(3), 763–801.

Trabandt, M., & Uhlig, H. (2011). The Laffer curve revisited. Journal of Monetary Economics, 58(4), 305–327.

Chapter 21 · Part VII: Short-Run Fluctuations

Government Debt & Deficits

Why deficits matter, why they sometimes don't, how debt-to-GDP dynamics actually work, the Ricardian equivalence debate, the difference between sustainable and unsustainable trajectories, and the unprecedented post-2020 path the U.S. has chosen

In December 2025, the U.S. federal government carried debt held by the public of approximately $30 trillion — about 99.8% of GDP. Interest payments on that debt reached $1.0 trillion for the year, making interest the second-largest line in the federal budget after Social Security. Both the debt level and the interest cost are unprecedented in U.S. peacetime history. The Congressional Budget Office's baseline projection shows debt-to-GDP reaching 120% by 2035 and 156% by 2055 under current law. These are not crisis-induced numbers. The economy is at full employment, not in recession. There is no war. The numbers reflect a structural change in U.S. fiscal policy.

This chapter is about government debt and deficits — how they arise, how they're financed, when they matter, when they don't, and what determines whether a country is on a sustainable path. The chapter builds on Chapter 18's fiscal-policy framework but goes deeper into long-run dynamics, the empirical evidence on debt-growth thresholds, the Ricardian equivalence debate, and the question of intergenerational equity. By the end you should be able to evaluate any sovereign-debt situation — Japan's 250% debt-to-GDP, the Eurozone crisis countries, the current U.S. trajectory — using the same conceptual framework.

✦ Sharma's Take

Of all the macroeconomic topics covered in this book, debt and deficits is probably the one where the gap between public understanding and economic analysis is the widest. The public discussion is dominated by two opposing oversimplifications. One side says deficits don't matter because the U.S. can never default on dollar-denominated debt — economically true but politically misleading. The other side says deficits are inherently catastrophic and require immediate balanced budgets — economically wrong and historically refuted by countries that have run deficits productively for decades.

The careful answer is in between, and it's not particularly partisan: deficits matter when they exceed what a country's growth can absorb, which depends on the interest rate relative to growth, the productivity of the spending being financed, and the durability of the institutional setup. Japan has run 8%+ deficits for 30 years and debt at 250% of GDP without crisis because growth has outpaced interest rates persistently. The U.S. ran 3-5% deficits productively for most of the post-WWII era because GDP grew faster than the debt. The current U.S. trajectory is different, and worse, because growth is no longer reliably above the interest rate — the (r − g) term has turned positive. That's the technical reason today's situation is harder than yesterday's, and why every honest fiscal projection shows debt-to-GDP rising without limit. The political question of what to do about it is genuinely hard. The analytical framework for thinking about it is not.

— A.S.

21.1 Stocks vs. Flows: Debt Is Not Deficit

📘 Principles Level — Two different things

The first conceptual hurdle in fiscal analysis is distinguishing between debt and deficit. Deficit is a flow: it's the amount by which spending exceeds revenue in a single year. The U.S. federal deficit in fiscal year 2025 was approximately $1.8 trillion. Debt is a stock: it's the cumulative borrowing the government has done over its lifetime. U.S. federal debt held by the public at end-2025 was approximately $30 trillion. Debt this year equals debt last year plus this year's deficit (plus accrued interest, plus a few other adjustments).

This sounds basic, but the public debate often conflates the two. "We need to cut the debt" usually means cutting the deficit — which slows debt growth but doesn't reduce the debt level. To actually reduce the debt, you'd need not just balanced budgets but surpluses. The U.S. last ran four consecutive years of surpluses in 1998-2001. Before that, you have to go back to the late 1940s.

⚙ Equation Walkthrough — The Government's Budget Constraint
Dt+1 = Dt × (1 + rt) − St
where St = primary surplus (revenue − non-interest spending)
Dt
Outstanding debt at start of period t.
rt
Interest rate paid on the debt in period t.
St
Primary surplus — tax revenue minus non-interest government spending. Note: this excludes interest payments. A negative primary surplus is a primary deficit.
Total deficit
= primary deficit + interest payments. When you read "the U.S. deficit is $1.8T," that includes ~$1T of interest. The primary deficit is the $0.8T of spending in excess of revenue before interest.

Why the primary surplus matters: primary surplus is what the government has direct policy control over. Interest payments are largely determined by previous borrowing and current market rates. To stabilize debt, you don't need a balanced total budget — you need a primary surplus large enough to offset (r − g) × D/Y. That's a much lower bar than "balance the budget."

21.2 The Debt-to-GDP Ratio and Its Dynamics

⚙ Equation Walkthrough — Debt-to-GDP Dynamics
Δ(D/Y) ≈ (r − g) × (D/Y) − primary surplus / Y
Δ(D/Y)
Change in debt-to-GDP ratio. Positive = debt-to-GDP rising.
(r − g)
The single most important number in fiscal sustainability analysis. Real interest rate minus real GDP growth rate. When r > g, debt-to-GDP grows automatically through compound interest even with a balanced primary budget. When r < g, debt-to-GDP shrinks naturally even with modest primary deficits.
primary surplus/Y
Primary surplus as % of GDP. The policy lever.
Stability condition
D/Y is stable when (r − g) × (D/Y) = primary surplus/Y. If r > g and the country runs a primary deficit, D/Y rises monotonically — no convergence.

The (r − g) regime is everything: for most of the post-WWII period, U.S. real interest rates averaged below real GDP growth (g averaging ~3%, r averaging ~2%). This "(r − g) negative" regime meant debt-to-GDP fell automatically even with modest deficits — the post-1945 reduction from 113% to 35% by 1974 happened mostly through (r − g) dynamics, not through surpluses. Post-2008, real rates fell to near zero while growth slowed too, so (r − g) was close to zero. Post-2022, real rates rose substantially while growth stayed modest — (r − g) is now positive for the U.S. for the first time in a generation. This is the structural shift.

21.3 The (r − g) Regime: Why Japan Isn't a Crisis But Italy Sometimes Is

Real interest rate (r) minus real GDP growth (g) for selected economies, recent average
CountryDebt/GDPReal rReal gr − gTrajectory
Japan250%0.0%0.5%−0.5%Stable despite extreme debt level
U.S.100%1.8%1.7%+0.1%Rising rapidly
Italy140%2.0%0.8%+1.2%Persistent vulnerability
Germany65%1.0%1.0%~0%Stable, low debt
UK100%2.2%1.2%+1.0%Rising; politically contested
Brazil78%5.0%2.0%+3.0%Significant pressure
⚠ Common Pitfall — Confusing high debt with a crisis

Japan has debt-to-GDP of 250% and has not faced a sovereign debt crisis in 30 years. Italy at 130-140% has faced repeated crisis episodes (2011-12, 2018, 2022). The U.S. at 100% has yet to face a crisis. The level of debt doesn't determine crisis risk — the trajectory does. A country with debt at 250% and (r − g) negative is on a stable path; a country with debt at 70% and (r − g) strongly positive plus rising primary deficits is on an unstable path. What matters is whether the math implies convergence to a stable D/Y or divergence without bound.

Japan's stability rests on multiple favorable factors: low interest rates (BOJ rate at zero for two decades, then yield curve control), high domestic savings (90%+ of Japanese debt is held domestically, mostly by the central bank and pension funds), demographic decline (less competing demand for domestic capital), and aging-population deflationary pressure. The U.S. has fewer of these factors, which is why the same debt-to-GDP ratio implies more risk in the U.S. than in Japan. Mechanical comparison of debt-to-GDP across countries is misleading; you need to look at the dynamics.

21.4 The Ricardian Equivalence Debate

📗 Intermediate Level — Do deficits crowd out private spending?

In 1974, Robert Barro published a paper arguing that government deficits don't matter for aggregate demand. His argument: when the government runs a deficit, rational forward-looking households realize that future taxes will be higher to pay off the debt. They save the entirety of the current tax cut (or government transfer) to pay future taxes. Net effect on consumption: zero. This is Ricardian equivalence (named after David Ricardo who first noted the idea in 1820, though he didn't fully endorse it).

If Ricardian equivalence held strictly, fiscal policy would have no demand effects — every tax cut would be fully saved, every deficit would be exactly offset by private saving. Empirically, this is false. Tax cuts do raise consumption (just by less than the full amount), and deficits do affect interest rates and aggregate demand. But the Ricardian intuition captures something real: forward-looking households do adjust their saving when they anticipate future tax increases, and high-debt countries have more saving than otherwise comparable low-debt countries. The pure form is wrong; the directional insight is right.

1980s-1990s Cross-country evidence Imperfect Ricardian

Testing Ricardian Equivalence in the Data

Empirical tests of Ricardian equivalence have consistently found partial but incomplete offset. Studies from the 1980s-90s (Bernheim 1987, Seater 1993) found that approximately 30-50% of a deficit-financed tax cut shows up in additional private saving — meaningful, but far from the 100% Ricardian equivalence predicts. The 2008 financial crisis provided a quasi-natural experiment: countries that ran larger deficits saw private saving rise, but by less than 1-for-1 with public dissaving.

The empirical literature suggests three reasons Ricardian equivalence fails in practice: (1) liquidity constraints: many households can't actually save more even if they wanted to — they're already consuming all their income; (2) finite horizons: households discount future taxes more heavily than future income, especially across generations; (3) uncertainty: future tax burden is uncertain and depends on political outcomes. The honest summary: deficits do affect aggregate demand, but the effect is smaller than naive Keynesian models predict because some Ricardian offset exists. Typical estimates put fiscal multipliers somewhere in the 0.7-1.5 range — clearly positive, but not the 2.5+ of pure Keynesian multipliers.

21.5 The Crowding-Out Channel

⚙ Equation Walkthrough — Crowding Out
National saving = Private saving + Government saving
= (Y − T − C) + (T − G) = Y − C − G
National investment = National saving (closed economy)
Private saving
Disposable income minus consumption.
Government saving
Tax revenue minus government spending. Negative when government runs a deficit.
Crowding-out logic
When government dissaves (runs a deficit), national saving falls (unless Ricardian offset is complete). Less saving means less funds available for private investment at any given interest rate, so the interest rate rises to clear the market, and investment falls. The deficit "crowds out" private investment dollar for dollar (in pure form) or partially (in practice).

The empirical question: by how much does $1 of additional deficit reduce private investment? Empirical estimates range from $0.10 to $0.50 per dollar of deficit, depending on regime. In deep recessions with the zero lower bound binding, crowding out is near zero (the central bank doesn't tighten in response). In full-employment conditions like 2025, crowding out is substantial — perhaps $0.30-$0.50 per dollar of deficit. This is the mechanism through which sustained deficits eventually slow long-run growth.

21.6 The U.S. Trajectory: 1945 to 2055

U.S. debt-to-GDP has followed a remarkable path over the past 80 years, driven by exactly the mechanisms developed above. Understanding this trajectory clarifies what's different about the current moment.

U.S. federal debt-to-GDP trajectory and its drivers
PeriodDebt/GDP pathPrimary driver
1945-1974113% → 35%(r − g) strongly negative; growth at 3.5%, real rates at 1-2%. Debt didn't shrink in nominal terms — GDP grew much faster than debt. Some surpluses helped but the dynamic was the dominant force.
1974-198035% → 32%Stagflation made growth weaker but inflation eroded debt nominally. Net effect: roughly flat.
1981-199232% → 64%Reagan-era tax cuts plus defense buildup; primary deficits running 3-4% of GDP. Modest growth couldn't keep up with debt accumulation.
1992-200164% → 55%1990s budget surpluses (1998-2001) plus strong growth. Last sustained debt-to-GDP reduction before the modern era.
2001-200855% → 64%Bush tax cuts, defense spending, modest deficits. Steady but unspectacular.
2008-201964% → 79%Great Recession and recovery: deficits ballooned during 2009-2012, then narrowed. (r − g) stayed close to zero with the ZLB.
2020-202579% → 100%Pandemic response + structural deficit growth + post-2022 rise in real interest rates. (r − g) turned positive.
2025-2055 (CBO baseline)100% → 156%Without policy change: aging-population health spending growth, OBBBA tax cuts, persistent structural deficits, (r − g) positive throughout.
✦ Sharma's Take — Why this time is structurally different

Three changes distinguish the 2020s+ U.S. fiscal trajectory from any prior period. First, (r − g) has turned positive for the first time sustained in 60+ years. The mechanical debt-stabilizing force of past decades is gone. Second, the structural primary deficit is large and growing — about 4% of GDP and projected to grow to 6%+ over coming decades. Past episodes of high debt-to-GDP (postwar) had primary surpluses running 2-4% of GDP, which combined with favorable (r − g) drove debt down quickly. Today's primary deficits run in the opposite direction. Third, demographic pressure on entitlements is just starting. Medicare and Social Security spending grow faster than GDP for the next 30 years no matter what; absent reform, they alone account for most of the projected debt-to-GDP rise.

What's different from past warnings about debt is that the math now genuinely doesn't work. The 1980s "deficits matter" warnings turned out to be too pessimistic because (r − g) stayed favorable. The 2010s warnings turned out to be too pessimistic for the same reason plus the zero lower bound. The 2020s+ warnings about debt sustainability are based on changed underlying mechanics, not on the same old story being repeated. The trajectory is not just bad relative to history — it's bad relative to the parameters that made history work.

— A.S.

21.7 How Sovereign Debt Crises Actually Happen

Despite the mechanical math of debt dynamics, sovereign debt crises don't follow a predictable schedule. Some countries cross 100% debt-to-GDP and never face crisis (Japan, Italy in non-crisis years). Others face crisis at 70% (Argentina 2001). The actual trigger is rarely the debt level itself — it's a loss of market confidence that pushes interest rates up sharply, making the debt instantly less sustainable. Understanding how crises happen is essential to understanding what risk the U.S. actually faces.

Mechanism of sovereign debt crises
PhaseWhat happens
1. Slow buildupDebt-to-GDP rises gradually over years as deficits exceed (g − r) × D/Y. Markets accept this — the country has fiscal space, growth continues, rates stay manageable.
2. Confidence shockSome event — a political crisis, a banking shock, a global rate rise, a missed payment — causes markets to reassess. The "Greek moment" of 2010 was this: revised deficit numbers revealed the situation was worse than thought.
3. Rate spiralRequired interest rate jumps as risk premia rise. Higher rates mean higher interest costs, which widen the deficit, which raises the debt, which raises the rate further. Greece's 10-year yield went from 6% in early 2010 to 30%+ by 2012.
4. Forced adjustmentThe country cannot service debt at the new rates. Options: default (Argentina 2001, Greece 2012), bailout (multiple Eurozone countries 2010-12), inflation-out (effectively a soft default through currency debasement), or aggressive austerity.
⚠ Common Pitfall — "The U.S. can never default"

The argument is technically right but practically misleading. The U.S. borrows in dollars and the Federal Reserve can always create dollars to pay debt. So technical nominal default is impossible. But this doesn't mean the U.S. can borrow without consequence:

  1. Interest rates can rise sharply. The post-2022 rise in 10-year yields from 1.5% to 4.5%+ added roughly $400B/year to interest costs over 10 years. A further rise to 6% would add another $500B/year.
  2. Inflation is a "soft default." If the Fed monetizes large deficits, inflation rises. Bondholders are repaid in dollars worth less than the dollars lent. The 1970s saw real returns on Treasury bonds of −2%/year for a decade — effective default by inflation.
  3. Fiscal space erodes. Every dollar going to interest is a dollar not available for defense, infrastructure, R&D, or crisis response. By 2034 CBO projects interest payments will exceed defense + Medicare combined.
  4. Reserve currency status isn't permanent. The dollar's reserve role rests on confidence in U.S. fiscal management. A sustained loss of that confidence — even slowly — could erode the privilege the U.S. has been getting for 80 years.

The U.S. won't default in the technical sense. But "can't default" is doing a lot of work in arguments that pretend deficits don't matter. The real question is the cost of carrying high debt, and that cost is rising, not falling.

21.8 What Reduces Debt-to-GDP

If a country wants to reduce its debt-to-GDP ratio, it has five options. None is easy, and several involve substantial trade-offs.

Five ways to reduce debt-to-GDP and their historical track records
ApproachHow it worksHistorical example
1. Growth (raise g)Faster GDP growth shrinks debt-to-GDP at any given debt level. Most painless option, but requires productivity gains.U.S. 1945-1974: growth at 3.5% drove debt-to-GDP from 113% to 35%.
2. Inflation (reduce real r)Higher inflation reduces the real value of nominal debt. Effectively a soft default on bondholders.U.S. 1970s: 7%+ inflation eroded real debt value.
3. Financial repressionGovernment compels banks/pensions to hold low-yield government debt, keeping r artificially low.U.S. 1945-1951 (Treasury-Fed Accord rates caps), Japan 1990s-present.
4. Austerity (raise primary surplus)Cut spending and/or raise taxes to run primary surpluses. Politically painful, often slow.Sweden 1993-2000 (consolidation), UK 2010-2015, Ireland 2008-2012.
5. Default (formal)Outright refusal to pay. Most damaging to future borrowing capacity. Rare for developed economies.Argentina 2001, Greece 2012 partial restructuring.
✦ Sharma's Take — The most likely U.S. path

If forced to predict, I think the most likely U.S. path through the 2030s-40s is some combination of options 1, 2, and 3: moderately faster growth from AI productivity gains, moderately higher inflation than the 2010s norm (3-3.5% rather than 1.5-2%), and gradually rising financial repression through Fed balance sheet operations that keep long-rates lower than they would otherwise be. This combination wouldn't reduce debt-to-GDP, but it would slow its rise enough to avoid forced adjustment.

What's politically unlikely in the U.S. context: a sustained primary-surplus-driven consolidation. The 1990s budget surpluses required a unique political alignment (divided government, strong growth, fiscal consensus on deficit reduction from both parties). None of that exists today. Both U.S. political parties have abandoned fiscal consolidation as a priority. The most plausible outcome is therefore drift — debt-to-GDP rising for decades while the underlying mechanics gradually catch up with the political consensus.

What's important to understand: this is not necessarily catastrophic. Japan has been doing essentially this since 1995 — debt-to-GDP at 250% with no crisis, modest growth, deflationary pressure, gradual erosion of fiscal space. The U.S. could find itself in a similar steady state at 150%+ debt-to-GDP within a generation. It just wouldn't be a vigorous, growth-friendly outcome. It would be slow erosion of fiscal capacity and slow rise in financial fragility. Not a crash, but not a triumph either.

— A.S.

21.9 Interactive: Debt Dynamics Simulator

🎛 Interactive — Debt Dynamics Simulator

Set the parameters and project the debt-to-GDP path over 30 years

The slider settings let you simulate any country's debt trajectory. Test the U.S. baseline, Japan's "low growth + low rates" steady state, Italy's vulnerable path, or a debt-reduction scenario. Notice how (r − g) dominates everything else once debt is high.

Reading the chart: the chart projects debt-to-GDP forward 30 years using Δ(D/Y) = (r − g) × (D/Y) − primary_balance. The trajectory is exponential when (r − g) ≠ 0: positive (r − g) compounds the debt; negative (r − g) erodes it. Try the U.S. 2025 baseline (rising), then flip to Japan (stable despite higher debt). Notice how growth and interest-rate assumptions matter far more than the initial debt level.

21.10 Worked Examples

📐 Worked Example 21.1 — The Stabilizing Primary Surplus
A country has D/Y = 95%, r = 2.5%, g = 1.5%. (a) What primary surplus stabilizes D/Y? (b) What primary surplus is needed if (r − g) widens to 2pp due to higher rates? (c) The country currently runs a 3% primary deficit. How much fiscal adjustment is needed to stabilize debt at current rates?
1
(a) Stabilization: required surplus = (r − g) × (D/Y) = (2.5 − 1.5) × 0.95 = +0.95% of GDP. The country needs a primary surplus of about 1% of GDP just to keep debt-to-GDP from rising.
2
(b) Wider gap: if (r − g) = 2.0pp, required surplus = 2.0 × 0.95 = +1.9% of GDP. Doubles the required surplus.
3
(c) Adjustment needed: current = −3% (deficit), target = +0.95%. Adjustment = 3% − (−0.95%) = +3.95 percentage points of GDP of fiscal consolidation. For a $30T economy, that's roughly $1.2 trillion per year, sustained.
The fiscal arithmetic for the U.S.: stabilizing at 100% debt-to-GDP requires the U.S. to swing the primary balance from a 3% deficit to a 1% surplus — roughly a 4pp swing. The last sustained U.S. primary swing of comparable magnitude was the 1993-1998 consolidation under Clinton, which took roughly a decade of carefully crafted policy and was helped by an unusual technology-driven growth surge. Replicating it today would require something similar but starting from a much worse fiscal position.
📐 Worked Example 21.2 — Projecting Debt-to-GDP Forward 10 Years
Starting from D/Y = 100%, project the trajectory 10 years forward under three scenarios: (a) U.S. baseline: r = 2%, g = 1.7%, primary deficit = 4%. (b) Adverse: r = 3.5%, g = 1.5%, primary deficit = 5%. (c) Favorable: AI productivity surge raises g to 3.5%, r stays at 2%, primary deficit shrinks to 2%. Use the linearized equation Δ(D/Y) ≈ (r − g) × (D/Y) − primary balance.
1
Baseline (a): Year 1: Δ = (2 − 1.7) × 1.0 − (−4) = 0.3 + 4 = 4.3pp → D/Y rises to 104.3%. Year 2: Δ = 0.3 × 1.043 + 4 ≈ 4.31pp → 108.6%. After 10 years of similar growth: D/Y ≈ 148%.
2
Adverse (b): Year 1: Δ = (3.5 − 1.5) × 1.0 + 5 = 7pp → 107%. Each subsequent year adds slightly more due to compounding. After 10 years: D/Y ≈ 185%.
3
Favorable (c): Year 1: Δ = (2 − 3.5) × 1.0 + 2 = 0.5pp → 100.5%. Subsequent years see (r − g) negative, debt slowly stable or falling. After 10 years: D/Y ≈ 99%.
The numbers reveal the leverage: baseline (a) and favorable (c) differ by only 1.8pp in g and 2pp in primary balance. After 10 years, the debt levels differ by 50 percentage points of GDP. Small changes in the underlying parameters drive enormous differences in long-run trajectories — which is precisely why fiscal sustainability is so contested. Reasonable people can hold different views about (r − g) ten years out, and those different views imply radically different policy implications.
🎮 MacroNations Connection — Debt Dynamics in the Engine

The engine implements debt dynamics directly: new_debt = prev_debt − budget_balance + (prev_debt × prev_interest_rate)/1000. Interest payments compound the debt automatically — at 5% interest on 60% debt, that's 3pp/round added even with a balanced budget. The Reinhart-Rogoff drag kicks in at debt > 90% of GDP: −0.02pp/round of gdp_growth per 10pp of excess. Debt is double-penalized in pillar scoring: Stability's debt_pts (60 of 200, full marks at debt ≤ 40%, zero at 140%) plus Sustainability's sust_debt_pts (80 of 200, tighter threshold: full at debt ≤ 30%, zero at 150%). The engine reads tax_rate − gov_spending as the primary balance (welfare_spending and infrastructure get rolled into gov_spending). Going from debt = 60% to debt = 110% over 10 rounds costs roughly 50-70 points across both pillars, plus cumulative Reinhart-Rogoff drag on Growth. The safest long-run strategy: balanced or modest-surplus budget in non-crisis rounds, preserving fiscal space for crisis-round response options when the engine's high-effectiveness options become highest-value.

Chapter 21 Quiz

Stocks vs flows, debt dynamics, (r-g), Ricardian equivalence, crowding out, sovereign crises
Part A — Multiple Choice
1. The deficit is a:
A Stock; debt is a flow
B Flow (annual gap between spending and revenue); debt is a stock (accumulated borrowing)
C Same thing as debt
D Always negative
2. The single most important variable in debt-to-GDP dynamics is:
A The absolute size of GDP
B The trade balance
C (r − g): the gap between the real interest rate and real GDP growth
D The inflation rate
3. Strict Ricardian equivalence predicts that:
A Government spending has no effect on aggregate demand
B Tax cuts will be fully saved (not consumed) because households anticipate future tax increases
C Interest rates are always equal to the growth rate
D Inflation always offsets deficits
4. Empirical evidence on Ricardian equivalence suggests:
A It holds almost exactly
B It's completely false; deficits have no effect on saving
C Partial offset: ~30-50% of deficit-financed tax cuts are saved, not fully consumed
D It only applies in liquidity-constrained economies
5. Japan has debt-to-GDP of 250% but has not had a sovereign debt crisis. The primary reason is:
A Japan's GDP is much larger than the U.S.
B Real interest rates near zero have kept (r − g) close to zero or negative
C Japan has been running primary surpluses
D Japanese debt is in yen-denominated foreign currency
6. U.S. debt-to-GDP fell from 113% in 1945 to 35% in 1974. The primary mechanism was:
A Large nominal surpluses
B Formal default
C Inflation alone
D Favorable (r − g): real GDP grew at 3.5% while real rates averaged 1-2%, mechanically shrinking the ratio
7. Which of the following is NOT a way countries have historically reduced debt-to-GDP?
A Growth (raise g)
B Inflation (reduce real value of debt)
C Financial repression (keep r artificially low)
D Inflation-protected bonds (TIPS issuance)
Part B — Numerical
8. A country has D/Y = 80%, r = 3%, g = 2%, primary surplus = 0. (a) Compute the change in D/Y this year. (b) Compute the primary surplus needed to stabilize D/Y. (c) If g rises to 4% with everything else unchanged, what happens to D/Y over time?
Model answer: (a) Δ(D/Y) = (r − g) × D/Y − PS = (3 − 2) × 0.80 − 0 = +0.8pp. D/Y rises from 80% to 80.8%. (b) Required PS = (r − g) × D/Y = 1% × 80% = +0.8% of GDP. (c) If g = 4%, then (r − g) = 3 − 4 = −1%. Δ(D/Y) = −1% × 80% − 0 = −0.8pp. D/Y falls automatically by 0.8pp/year, approaching but never quite reaching zero. The shift from growth at 2% to growth at 4% turns a debt-stabilization problem into automatic debt reduction.
9. The U.S. has D/Y ≈ 100%, r ≈ 2%, g ≈ 1.7%, primary deficit ≈ 4%. (a) Compute Δ(D/Y) and project the trajectory over 10 years (linear approximation). (b) What primary surplus would stabilize D/Y at 100%? (c) If interest rates were to spike to r = 4.5%, compute the new Δ(D/Y) and discuss why this would be especially dangerous.
Model answer: (a) Δ(D/Y) = (2 − 1.7) × 1.0 − (−4) = 0.3 + 4 = +4.3pp per year. Over 10 years: D/Y rises from 100% to roughly ~143% (linear), ~145-150% (with compounding). (b) Required surplus = (r − g) × D/Y = 0.3 × 1.0 = +0.3% of GDP. So we'd need to swing from a 4% primary deficit to a 0.3% surplus — a 4.3pp adjustment. (c) With r = 4.5%, Δ(D/Y) = (4.5 − 1.7) × 1.0 + 4 = +6.8pp per year. The required stabilizing surplus jumps to 2.8% — a 6.8pp adjustment from current. The danger: higher rates rapidly raise both the debt growth rate and the required surplus, creating a "self-reinforcing" path where any rate rise makes debt sustainability much harder. This is the same mechanism that triggered the Eurozone debt crisis.
Part C — Policy Memo
10. The U.S. president-elect's advisor asks you, an incoming Council of Economic Advisers member, to evaluate the fiscal trajectory and recommend a path forward. The current state: D/Y = 100% and rising; structural primary deficit = 4% of GDP; (r − g) modestly positive; major entitlement spending growth ahead. Write a 250-word memo articulating the trajectory, the menu of options, and your recommendation.
Model answer (sketch): The trajectory. Under current law: D/Y rises from 100% to ~150% by 2055 (CBO baseline). Drivers: structural primary deficits (~4% of GDP, growing), demographic-driven entitlement growth, persistent positive (r − g). This is not a near-term crisis but an erosion of fiscal capacity that compounds for decades. The menu of options. (1) Growth-focused supply-side reforms (R&D, infrastructure, immigration) — first-best but slow. (2) Inflation tolerance at 2.5-3% — modest debt erosion but credibility cost. (3) Financial repression via Fed balance sheet — possible but limited by inflation. (4) Genuine fiscal consolidation: tax increases plus entitlement reform — politically difficult. (5) Drift — most politically likely; results in continued debt-to-GDP increase. Recommendation. Pursue (1) aggressively (growth helps every other path) plus a credible 10-year medium-term consolidation plan combining moderate tax increases (returning to pre-2017 effective rates on top earners would raise ~1% of GDP) with entitlement growth slowing (chained CPI for Social Security, Medicare premium reforms — together ~1% of GDP over time). Goal: cut the structural primary deficit from 4% to 1.5% by 2034, stabilizing D/Y around 110%. This is hard politically but mathematically modest. The alternative — drift — turns a manageable problem into an unmanageable one over decades.
The live textbook auto-grades MCQs and stores progress.

References
