The mechanical sequence of "spend-then-tax-then-bond-financed" that Mosler and Kelton emphasize is actually how Treasury operations work (the Federal Reserve creates reserves when Treasury spends, then those reserves are converted into bonds via auction). The MMT framework's emphasis on the "consolidated public sector" balance sheet, rather than the artificial Treasury-Fed split, is analytically cleaner than the conventional treatment.

Where MMT is wrong or oversimplified: the claim that inflation is the only constraint glosses over enormous practical difficulties. Once inflation starts rising due to excess deficits, slowing it down requires either tax increases (politically very hard) or spending cuts (politically very hard). The MMT framework offers no realistic theory of how a democratic political system would manage a strict inflation-based fiscal constraint. The 2021-22 inflation experience, where the Fed had to do most of the inflation-fighting through monetary tightening because fiscal tightening was politically impossible, is exactly the problem MMT downplays.

Where the debate is genuinely contested: how much slack is there in the U.S. economy at any given time? How much can deficit-financed spending raise potential output through productivity-enhancing investments? Could a federal job guarantee actually be designed in a way that doesn't cause large distortions? These are real empirical questions where reasonable people disagree. My read: MMT has identified some valid analytical points that mainstream macroeconomics had insufficiently emphasized, while also overstating the practical political feasibility of its preferred policy regime. Both the dismissive mainstream reaction and the enthusiastic MMT adoption miss important truths.

— A.S.

24.4 Debate 4: Climate Change and Macroeconomic Policy

Climate change presents macroeconomic policy with challenges that don't fit cleanly into the standard analytical framework. The expected economic damages from climate change rise nonlinearly with temperature and are heavily back-loaded — most damages occur in the second half of the 21st century even though emissions causing them happen today. Discounting future damages at conventional rates dramatically reduces their present value, which is why integrated assessment models like Nordhaus's DICE produce modest "optimal" carbon taxes ($30-80/ton) that climate scientists generally regard as inadequate.

Three live climate-macro debates
DebateThe dispute
Carbon pricing vs subsidiesShould climate policy work primarily through taxing carbon (efficient but politically difficult) or subsidizing clean alternatives (politically easier but less efficient)? The Inflation Reduction Act bet on subsidies. The European Emissions Trading System bets on pricing.
Should central banks consider climate?Should the Fed and ECB explicitly factor climate risk into their financial stability mandates, rate decisions, and asset purchases? The ECB has moved further in this direction than the Fed. Critics argue this is "mission creep" beyond legislated mandates.
Discount rates for climate damagesThe mainstream Stern review used a near-zero discount rate (1.4%) and got large optimal interventions. Nordhaus used 4-5% and got modest interventions. The value choice — how much we weight future generations vs ourselves — drives 90% of the policy difference. Pure economics can't settle this.

24.5 Debate 5: Central Bank Digital Currencies

📗 What CBDCs are and why they're debated

A central bank digital currency (CBDC) is a digital form of central bank money — a direct liability of the central bank held by households and businesses, not just by commercial banks. Currently, the public can only hold central bank money in the form of physical cash; digital balances at the Fed are restricted to commercial banks. A CBDC would extend digital central bank money to the public, either through accounts at the Fed or through a token-based system.

The case for CBDCs: more efficient payments (instant, low-cost transfers), better financial inclusion (anyone with a phone has central bank money access), reduced dependence on private payment networks (Visa, Mastercard, ApplePay), better monetary policy transmission (negative rates become possible, helicopter drops are easy). The case against: privacy concerns (the government can see every transaction), disintermediation of commercial banks (if households move deposits to Fed accounts, banks lose funding base), financial stability risks (Fed accounts could be the destination of choice during crises, accelerating bank runs).

✦ Sharma's Take — A modest CBDC is more likely than the extreme versions

The maximalist CBDC vision (full account-based system, no anonymous transactions, programmable money) is what gets the most attention in popular discussion — both from advocates and from libertarian opponents. The realistic outcome is something much more modest: a tokenized CBDC with privacy protections similar to cash for small transactions, run on top of the existing banking system rather than replacing it, designed primarily for retail payment efficiency rather than monetary policy or surveillance. This is roughly the design China has rolled out for the digital yuan, the European Central Bank is testing for the digital euro, and the Fed has indicated it would pursue if Congress authorized it.

The interesting policy question is therefore not "should we have CBDCs" but "what specific design features should they have?" Privacy protections, transaction limits, interest-bearing or not, intermediated by commercial banks or direct, programmable for monetary policy or not — these are the actual choices. The popular debate is mostly arguing past the realistic design space. As of 2025, the U.S. has not authorized a CBDC and there's no near-term legislative push for one. The likelihood of U.S. CBDC adoption in the next decade is probably under 30%, far below most other major economies.

— A.S.

24.6 Debate 6: AI Productivity and Macroeconomic Modeling

The macroeconomic effects of AI — particularly large language models and their successors — are one of the most uncertain questions in policy economics. Forecasters disagree on whether AI will produce a productivity boom comparable to the IT revolution of the 1990s, a more modest boost akin to mobile phones, or whether it might even reduce measured productivity by displacing high-paid knowledge workers into less measurable activities. The answer depends on parameters we cannot yet measure.

Three scenarios for AI's macroeconomic effects (2025-2035)
ScenarioProductivityLabor marketInequality
Boom scenario +1.0 to +1.5pp/yr added to TFP Strong wage growth across skill levels Could reduce inequality if AI substitutes for high-skill more than low-skill labor
Modest scenario +0.3 to +0.7pp/yr Mixed; some occupations disrupted, others enhanced Roughly neutral on inequality; depends on which occupations are affected
Concentrated scenario +0.5 to +1.0pp/yr but concentrated in few firms/sectors Significant displacement in clerical/legal/coding occupations Increases inequality; gains captured by AI-using firms and owners of intellectual property
⚠ Common Pitfall — Confidence about AI's macroeconomic effects

Be very skeptical of anyone — including this textbook — who claims to know how AI will affect productivity, employment, and inequality over the next decade. The historical track record on technology forecasting is poor. Computers were predicted to dramatically raise productivity in the 1970s; it took until the 1990s for measurable productivity gains to materialize (Solow's "productivity paradox"). Mobile phones were predicted to be a niche product. The internet's commercialization was largely missed by 1990s forecasters. The honest answer about AI's macroeconomic effects is: "we don't know, the range of plausible outcomes is wide, and the early evidence is too thin to settle".

This matters for policymakers because every major macroeconomic projection over the next decade depends critically on AI productivity assumptions. CBO's debt-to-GDP projections, Social Security solvency estimates, Federal Reserve longer-run growth projections — all build in implicit AI productivity assumptions, usually toward the modest end. If the boom scenario materializes, debt-to-GDP projections will be substantially over-pessimistic; if the concentrated scenario materializes, inequality projections will be substantially over-optimistic. The asymmetry in error costs probably argues for planning around the more cautious scenarios while remaining alert to upside surprises.

24.7 Synthesizing the Six Debates: A Framework for Choice

The six debates above touch on different aspects of macroeconomic policy, but they're not independent. Positions on one debate tend to correlate with positions on others — not because the issues are logically linked but because they share underlying value commitments. Recognizing the pattern helps locate yourself in the policy debate space.

Value-cluster positions on the six debates
DebateMarket-skeptical clusterMarket-friendly cluster
Rules vs discretionDiscretion — flexibility for downturns matters more than commitmentRules — commitment to low inflation matters more than flexibility
CB independenceLess — democratic accountability matters moreMore — credibility for inflation control matters more
MMTSympathetic — fiscal space is larger than mainstream admitsSkeptical — inflation constraint binds earlier than MMT claims
Climate-CB roleYes — climate is a financial stability issueNo — mission creep beyond mandate
CBDCYes — payment efficiency and inclusionCautious — privacy and disintermediation risks
AI macroPlan for concentrated scenario; redistribute proactivelyPlan for boom scenario; let market allocation work
✦ Sharma's Take — The case for mixed views

If you find yourself entirely in one cluster, I'd encourage you to interrogate that. The clusters reflect ideological tendencies more than coherent empirical analysis. The empirical evidence rarely supports clustering all positions together. For example, the evidence on CB independence is solidly pro-independence (less inflation, no growth cost); the evidence on MMT is mixed (some valid points, some overstated); the evidence on CBDCs is genuinely unsettled. Someone whose positions all align with one ideological cluster is probably picking the cluster first and reading evidence to fit.

My own view, for what it's worth: I'm pro-independence (debate 2), in favor of constrained discretion rules-based monetary frameworks (debate 1), skeptical of strong MMT (debate 3), in favor of climate as a financial stability factor but not direct monetary policy target (debate 4), modestly supportive of cautious CBDC adoption (debate 5), and inclined to plan for the middle AI scenario while preparing for either extreme (debate 6). These don't cluster cleanly into either ideological camp, and that's because the empirical evidence doesn't cluster cleanly either. If you've reached the end of this textbook with a more nuanced set of positions on these debates than you started with, you've gotten what economics education can actually deliver.

— A.S.

24.8 Interactive: Macro Policy Debate Positioner

🎛 Interactive — Macro Policy Debate Positioner

Set your position on each debate; see your cluster fit and identify the value-trade-offs

Each slider runs from "market-skeptical cluster" (left) to "market-friendly cluster" (right). The chart shows your position pattern. A consistent cluster indicates ideological coherence; a mixed pattern indicates empirically-driven positioning. Neither is necessarily correct — but knowing which describes your views helps you reason about your own thinking.

How to read this: 0 = fully market-skeptical; 10 = fully market-friendly; 5 = balanced. The radar chart shows your positions across all six debates. A symmetric radial pattern (all values close to each other) indicates cluster alignment. An asymmetric pattern indicates positions that are empirically rather than ideologically driven. There's no "right" answer — but the pattern tells you something about how you're reasoning.

24.9 Closing the Course: From Theory to Practice

You've now completed a full course in macroeconomics — the measurement of economic activity, the determinants of long-run growth, the dynamics of money and prices, open-economy interactions, the short-run fluctuations of business cycles, the institutional architecture of fiscal and monetary policy, the financial system, inequality and redistribution, and now the live policy debates of 2026. The MacroNations simulation tournament will let you operate the integrated framework on a real economy you manage — pushing the same levers we've discussed, watching the same equations transmit your decisions through the economy, and being scored on outcomes that integrate growth, stability, equality, sustainability, and innovation.

What I hope this course leaves you with is not a list of policy positions but a way of thinking about macroeconomic problems. Real-world macroeconomic decisions involve tradeoffs — between growth and inflation, between equality and efficiency, between present consumption and future investment, between risk and stability. Knowing the tradeoffs sharpens the choice; pretending tradeoffs don't exist obscures it. The economists who do the best work — Bernanke at the Fed during 2008, Yellen at the Treasury during 2021-24, the central bankers of multiple countries during COVID — are not the ones with the most confident answers. They're the ones who can hold competing considerations in mind simultaneously and choose under genuine uncertainty.

✦ Sharma's Take — One last thought

The standard arc of an undergraduate macroeconomics course is from confidence to confusion: students arrive thinking macroeconomics has clear answers about how to manage economies, and leave realizing that even the clearest answers come with caveats. I think this trajectory is correct, and it's why macroeconomics is worth studying. The interesting questions in macroeconomic policy are hard. The mechanisms involve dozens of variables interacting nonlinearly. The empirical evidence is fragmentary because economies don't run controlled experiments. The political constraints on policy are real and shape what's actually possible.

What macroeconomic education can give you is a discipline for thinking about these problems clearly: identifying which variables drive which outcomes, recognizing tradeoffs, distinguishing empirical evidence from political preferences, holding multiple competing considerations simultaneously. If the course taught you all that, it succeeded. If, on top of that, it equipped you to engage with the actual policy debates of our moment with more humility than you arrived with, the course did its real job. The next macroeconomic crisis will involve mechanisms we don't yet understand. The people who handle it best will be those who can apply old frameworks creatively to new conditions. That, ultimately, is what this textbook has been trying to teach.

— A.S.
🎮 MacroNations Connection — From Theory to the Simulation

The MacroNations engine integrates the policy frameworks from all six debates in this chapter into a single operational system. Rules vs discretion: you set interest_rate_target each round freely — discretion at the lever level — but the engine's structure (50% adaptive + 50% anchored inflation expectations) embeds the rules-based credibility you get from disciplined behavior. Central bank independence: there's no political-fiscal-dominance mechanic in the engine; you control monetary and fiscal levers as a unified policymaker, simulating the consolidated public-sector framework MMT emphasizes. Climate: green_energy raises both potential GDP and HDI but doesn't directly tax carbon — the engine treats climate as a long-run productivity factor rather than as a financial stability risk. AI productivity: rd_investment is the most direct channel — push it to 7-8% of GDP for sustained 10-round productivity gains exactly like a successful AI productivity boom. The implications for tournament strategy: there's no single "right" archetype because the engine, like real-world macroeconomic policy, rewards balance across multiple competing goals. The tournaments that finish with the highest scores are won by teams that integrate fiscal discipline, monetary credibility, productivity investment, equality through welfare spending, and financial stability through regulation — exactly the synthesis this textbook has tried to teach.

Chapter 24 Quiz

Six live debates — rules vs discretion, CB independence, MMT, climate-macro, CBDCs, AI
Part A — Multiple Choice
1. The Taylor rule prescribes the federal funds rate as a function of:
A Inflation alone
B Unemployment alone
C Equilibrium real rate + inflation + 0.5 × inflation gap + 0.5 × output gap
D A constant value
2. Cross-country evidence on central bank independence shows that more independent central banks have:
A Higher inflation but faster growth
B Lower inflation without any apparent growth cost
C Lower inflation but at the cost of higher unemployment
D Identical outcomes to less independent ones
3. The distinction between "goal independence" and "instrument independence":
A Is not meaningful — they're the same
B Only applies to fiscal policy
C Is important: most modern CBs have instrument independence but their goals are set by legislatures
D Was eliminated by post-2008 reforms
4. The central MMT claim is:
A Deficits don't matter at all
B Countries that issue their own currency cannot involuntarily default; deficits are constrained by inflation, not financing capacity
C Inflation should be ignored
D Central banks should be abolished
5. The Nordhaus DICE model produces an "optimal" carbon tax of ~$30-80/ton; the Stern Review found much higher optimal taxes. The primary disagreement is:
A About the climate science
B About the discount rate applied to future damages — a value choice, not an empirical question
C About the methodology of damage estimation
D About whether climate change exists
6. A central bank digital currency (CBDC) is:
A A cryptocurrency
B A digital version of bank deposits
C A digital form of central bank money — a direct liability of the central bank held by the public
D Identical to digital cash
7. Forecasting AI's macroeconomic effects over the next decade is best characterized as:
A Settled — productivity will rise 2pp/yr
B Irrelevant for policy
C Highly uncertain — plausible scenarios span +0.3 to +1.5pp/yr productivity, with diverse inequality implications
D Equivalent to the impact of the printing press
Part B — Short Answer
8. Explain why "constrained discretion" might be a better characterization of how the Fed actually operates than either "rules" or "pure discretion."
Model answer: The Fed has a clear rule-like framework (2% inflation target + dual mandate of price stability and maximum employment) but uses discretion within that framework about exactly how to achieve those goals. The framework provides credibility — markets know what the Fed is trying to achieve and trust it to pursue those goals. The discretion provides flexibility — the Fed can respond to unanticipated shocks (2008 GFC, COVID, supply chain disruptions) with novel tools (QE, forward guidance, BTFP) that no mechanical rule could specify in advance. Pure rules would have prescribed deeply negative rates in 2008-09 (impossible) and offered no guidance on QE. Pure discretion in the 1970s produced double-digit inflation. "Constrained discretion" captures the actual practice: a clear destination, multiple paths to get there.
9. MMT proponents claim that "deficits are constrained by inflation, not by financing capacity." Identify one way this is empirically true and one way it can be misleading as a policy framework.
Model answer: Empirically true: the U.S. (and any country with its own currency that's freely traded) cannot involuntarily default on debt denominated in that currency. The Fed can always create reserves to settle Treasury obligations. The U.S. doesn't face a "financing capacity" constraint in the way that a household, a U.S. state, or a Eurozone country does. Misleading as policy framework: the inflation constraint, once activated, requires tightening through politically painful means (tax increases or spending cuts) that may not be feasible to deliver. The MMT framework offers no realistic theory of how democratic politics would manage this constraint. The 2021-22 episode demonstrated this: when inflation hit 9%, Congress was unable to deliver fiscal tightening, so the Fed had to do all the work through monetary tightening — exactly the institutional gap MMT downplays. The framework's "inflation, not financing" claim is technically right but ignores the political economy of inflation control.
10. Write a 250-word memo to your future self in 2030 describing what you've learned in this textbook and how you'd apply it to a then-current macroeconomic problem.
Model answer (sketch): This is necessarily personal; here's a sample framework. Dear future self: I finished a 24-chapter macroeconomics course in 2026. The most important lesson is not any specific framework but the discipline of holding multiple competing considerations simultaneously. When you face a 2030 macroeconomic decision — say, the appropriate response to whatever the major economic event of the late 2020s turns out to be — remember: (1) Most policy debates involve real trade-offs; pretending otherwise is rhetoric, not analysis. (2) Empirical evidence rarely settles policy debates cleanly; values do most of the work in choosing between competing priorities. (3) The Phillips curve isn't dead, anchored expectations matter enormously, and (r − g) is the most important number in fiscal sustainability. (4) Financial crises follow recognizable patterns; the toolkit for response exists; political will to apply it is the binding constraint. (5) Inequality matters, but the empirical magnitudes of the equality-efficiency trade-off are usually overstated by both sides. (6) When you see a confident forecast about a complex policy outcome, be suspicious — the people who do this best are usually the ones expressing the most uncertainty. (7) The MacroNations engine taught me to translate theoretical frameworks into operational choices under constraints. That skill — choosing under uncertainty, with multiple competing goals, in real time — is what macroeconomic policymaking actually requires. Hope it serves you well in whatever crisis or boom 2030 brings.
The live textbook auto-grades MCQs and stores progress.

References

Alesina, A., & Summers, L. H. (1993). Central bank independence and macroeconomic performance: Some comparative evidence. Journal of Money, Credit and Banking, 25(2), 151–162.

Acemoglu, D., & Restrepo, P. (2022). Tasks, automation, and the rise in U.S. wage inequality. Econometrica, 90(5), 1973–2016.

Brunnermeier, M. K. (2021). The Resilient Society. Endeavor Literary Press.

Cukierman, A. (2008). Central bank independence and monetary policymaking institutions — Past, present and future. European Journal of Political Economy, 24(4), 722–736.

Kelton, S. (2020). The Deficit Myth: Modern Monetary Theory and the Birth of the People's Economy. PublicAffairs.

Kydland, F. E., & Prescott, E. C. (1977). Rules rather than discretion: The inconsistency of optimal plans. Journal of Political Economy, 85(3), 473–491.

Mosler, W. (2010). Soft Currency Economics II. CreateSpace.

Nordhaus, W. D. (2013). The Climate Casino: Risk, Uncertainty, and Economics for a Warming World. Yale University Press.

Stern, N. (2007). The Economics of Climate Change: The Stern Review. Cambridge University Press.

Taylor, J. B. (1993). Discretion versus policy rules in practice. Carnegie-Rochester Conference Series on Public Policy, 39, 195–214.

Wray, L. R. (2015). Modern Money Theory: A Primer on Macroeconomics for Sovereign Monetary Systems (2nd ed.). Palgrave Macmillan.

Chapter 25 · Part VIII: MacroNations Applications

Reading the MacroNations Simulation

A definitive reference for the MacroNations engine — its ten levers, its seventeen state variables, its scoring formulas, and the macroeconomic mechanisms that translate your policy choices into pillar scores

You've spent twenty-four chapters learning the theoretical machinery of macroeconomics. The MacroNations simulation now lets you operate that machinery in a single integrated model — set ten policy levers each round, watch seventeen state variables update, and receive a five-pillar scorecard out of 1000 points. This chapter is the manual. It documents exactly what the engine does, derived directly from the simulation code that runs every tournament round on Anthropic's Supabase Edge Function (with a local JavaScript fallback that produces identical results).

Most students who play MacroNations for the first time make the same mistake: they push policy levers aggressively, watch scores rise or fall, and treat the engine as a black box. That gets you a moderate score. To compete seriously you need to know which levers feed which state variables and which state variables feed which pillars. Once you internalize the pipeline — policy → state → pillar score — you can predict, before each round resolves, where your scorecard is headed. That's the test of macroeconomic literacy this chapter is designed to enable.

✦ Sharma's Take

The MacroNations engine is a deliberately simplified model — about 250 lines of code implementing roughly fifteen macroeconomic relationships from this textbook. It's not a DSGE model, and it's not trying to be. What it does well is capture the qualitative mechanisms that drive real economies: monetary tightening reduces inflation but raises unemployment via Okun's law, fiscal multipliers translate spending into GDP at a 1.4× rate, the Phillips curve links output gaps to inflation, debt above 90% drags growth (a Reinhart-Rogoff calibration), the Laffer curve penalizes tax rates above 40%, currency moves with rate differentials.

If you understand the chapters of this book well, you can predict how the simulation will respond to almost any policy combination. That's the point. The engine is a teaching device dressed up as a game. The students who finish at the top are the ones who can explain, in macroeconomic terms, why each policy change they made will move each state variable in a particular direction. The students who finish at the bottom are the ones who treat it as a video game and pull levers without a theoretical model. Choose accordingly.

— A.S.

25.1 The Engine's Round-by-Round Architecture

Each MacroNations round simulates one fiscal year. Your team starts with an initial economic state (or the state inherited from the previous round). You submit policy choices for ten levers. The engine then runs simRound(state, policy, crises) — a deterministic function (plus small Gaussian noise) that produces a new state. Then computeScores(newState) reads the new state and emits five pillar scores totalling out of 1000. Both functions live in the simulation code; everything that happens to your country goes through them.

The engine resolves each round in sixteen sequential computational blocks. The ordering matters: earlier blocks feed later ones. The full sequence is:

The 16 computational blocks inside simRound, in order
#BlockWhat it computes
1Monetary policyUses the level of the interest rate (vs. neutral 4%), not the change. Computes monetary_gdp and monetary_inf drag, and updates currency by 1.5% per 1pp rate change.
2Fiscal policySpending multiplier = 1.4, tax multiplier = −1.1. Adds Laffer-curve penalty above 40% tax rate or below 12%.
3Budget & debtNew balance = tax_rate − gov_spending. Interest on debt added. Debt clipped to 0–300%.
4Debt dragReinhart-Rogoff penalty: debt above 90% subtracts 0.02 percentage points per 10pp of excess.
5Potential GDPUpdated by R&D, infrastructure, and green energy investment — supply-side; slow.
6ProductivityTFP index. Same drivers as potential, slightly different weights.
7Structural & tradeTrade openness, regulation (linear penalty from 50% optimum), foreign aid.
8Crisis shocksIf crisis active, applies gdp/unemp/inf/fin shock scaled by player response effectiveness.
9GDP growthAnchored to potential + 60% of policy gap + 20% momentum + crisis + noise.
10Unemployment (Okun)ΔU = −0.5 × (GDP − potential). NAIRU = 4.5%.
11Inflation (Phillips + anchored expectations)Hybrid 50% adaptive + 50% anchored to 2%. Phillips slope = 0.4 × output_gap.
12Stock marketReacts to growth (+), rate changes (−), inflation surprises (−).
13Financial healthPenalised by high debt, high rates, large deficit; helped by regulation near 50%.
14Gini (inequality)Welfare spending and progressive taxes reduce Gini; unemployment gap raises it.
15HDIDriven by welfare, R&D, green energy; reduced by unemployment and high inflation.
16Build new stateReturns all 17 state variables to the database. Round counter +1.

Understanding this ordering matters: a policy change that affects a state variable in an early block (such as raising the interest rate, which feeds GDP in block 9) will propagate through every later block (unemployment in 10, inflation in 11, stocks in 12, and so on). Nothing in the engine happens in parallel — everything cascades from one block to the next within a single round.

25.2 The Ten Policy Levers

You set ten levers each round. Each has a defined range, a default value (used if you don't submit anything), and a primary economic channel. These are the actual levers in the live engine — exactly as defined in the LEVERS array in macronations.html.

The ten policy levers (exact values from the engine)
LeverRangeDefaultWhat it does in the engine
🏦 Interest Rate
interest_rate_target		 0 to 20% 4.5% The central bank's policy rate. Rate above neutral (4%) creates restrictive monetary stance: each 1pp above neutral subtracts 0.5pp from GDP and 0.4pp from inflation. Each 1pp rate change moves currency by 1.5%.
🏛️ Government Spending
gov_spending_pct		 10 to 60% of GDP 27% Total government outlays. Spending multiplier is 1.4 — every 1pp of GDP in extra spending raises GDP growth by about 0.21pp. Also feeds the budget deficit.
💰 Tax Rate
tax_rate		 5 to 60% 25% Effective average tax rate. Tax multiplier is −1.1. Engine has a Laffer-curve correction: tax above 40% adds an efficiency drag of 0.008pp per pp; tax below 12% adds an underfunding drag.
🌍 Trade Openness
trade_openness		 0 to 100% 65% Integration with global markets. Openness above 65% raises GDP via the export channel (1.5× per 1pp deviation). Cost-push channel: 0.03 × (openness − 50%) feeds inflation.
🔬 R&D Investment
rd_investment		 0 to 10% of GDP 2% The dominant driver of long-run potential and productivity. Each 1pp above 2% adds 0.12pp to potential GDP and 0.048 to productivity index per round (cumulative).
🌱 Green Energy Mix
green_energy		 0 to 100% 30% Renewable share of energy. Modest direct GDP effect; small contribution to HDI (climate-adjusted welfare). Long-run channel: contributes to potential GDP and productivity.
🤝 Welfare Spending
welfare_spending		 0 to 30% of GDP 5% Social transfers. The dominant Gini reducer — coefficient of −4.0 per normalized welfare unit. Big HDI driver: coefficient of 0.35. Adds to the deficit at fiscal cost.
⚖️ Regulation
regulation		 0 to 100% 50% Financial and market regulation. Optimum is exactly 50% — engine applies linear penalty for deviation from neutral. Helps Financial Health when in the 30-70 band.
✈️ Foreign Aid
foreign_aid		 0 to 5% of GDP 0.5% Development assistance. Each 1pp above 0.5% adds 0.15pp to GDP via trade-access channel. Small HDI contribution. Not the same as FDI policy.
🏗️ Infrastructure
infrastructure		 0 to 20% of GDP 5% Public capital investment. Supply-side lever. Each 1pp above 5% adds 0.10pp to potential and 0.025 to productivity per round.
⚠ Common Pitfall — Treating welfare and R&D as substitutes

Both welfare and R&D show up in the HDI calculation, so new players sometimes treat them as substitutes — "either push welfare or push R&D, depends on what I want." This misreads the engine. Welfare's HDI coefficient is 0.35; R&D's is 0.001 (scaled by /10 then by 0.01). Welfare drives HDI roughly 350× more per unit than R&D does. R&D's main job is feeding potential GDP and productivity — the long-run growth and innovation channels. Welfare and R&D are complements with different jobs, not substitutes. The right strategy is usually to push both, not to choose between them.

25.3 The Seventeen State Variables

Your country's state at any point is a snapshot of seventeen numerical variables. The engine reads all of them at the start of each round (calling this the prev state), runs the 16 computational blocks above, and writes a new full state. The visible scorecard you see at the end of each round is a function of all seventeen variables, with different pillars reading different subsets.

The 17 state variables (exact definitions from defaultEconomy)
VariableRangeInitialWhat it measures
GDP growth
gdp_growth		−12 to +12%2.5%Realized real GDP growth this round, after all policy effects, momentum, crises, and noise.
Potential
potential		0.5 to 5.5%2.5%Trend GDP growth rate — what the economy would grow at if there were no demand-side fluctuations. Updated slowly by R&D, infrastructure, and green energy.
Productivity
productivity		0.5 to 5.01.5Total factor productivity index. Slow-moving; pushed by R&D and infrastructure. Feeds the Innovation pillar.
Unemployment
unemployment		1.5 to 25%5.0%Unemployment rate, updated each round by Okun's law: −0.5 × (GDP − potential). NAIRU = 4.5%.
Inflation
inflation		−3 to +25%2.0%CPI inflation rate. Hybrid: 50% inertial + 50% anchored to 2% target, plus Phillips effect, plus monetary drag.
Interest rate
interest_rate		0 to 20%4.5%Current effective policy rate. Equals lever value, clipped.
Currency
currency		50 to 200100Nominal exchange rate index. Higher = stronger currency. Moves 1.5% per 1pp rate change (UIP).
Gov spending
gov_spending		10 to 60%27%Current government spending as % of GDP. Equals lever value.
Tax revenue
tax_revenue		5 to 60%25%Current effective tax rate. Equals lever value.
Budget balance
budget_balance		−50 to +50%−2%tax_revenue − gov_spending. Negative = deficit. Doesn't include interest payments separately (those add to debt directly).
Debt
debt		0 to 300%60%Public debt as % of GDP. Updated by deficit + interest cost. Triggers Reinhart-Rogoff drag above 90%.
Gini
gini		20 to 6535Income inequality, on the 0-100 scale (not the decimal 0.20-0.65 scale). Lower = more equal.
HDI
hdi		0.30 to 0.990.75Human Development Index. Driven by welfare (dominant), R&D, green energy. Reduced by high unemployment and high inflation.
Stock market
stock		500 to 500005000Stock market index. Reacts positively to growth, negatively to rate hikes and inflation surprises. Feeds the Growth pillar.
Financial health
fin_health		10 to 10075Financial system health. Penalised by high debt, high rates, large deficits; helped by regulation near 50%.
Round
round		0 to N0Current round counter. Used by the simulator to track tournament progress.
History
history		—[]Array of all previous state snapshots. Lets the engine compute momentum (block 9's 20% persistence term).
⚠ Common Pitfall — The Gini scale

The MacroNations engine uses Gini on the 0 to 100 scale, not the decimal 0.20 to 0.65 scale you may have seen in international statistics. A Gini reading of 35 in the simulation corresponds to roughly the 2024 U.S. figure (≈ 0.41 decimal). A Gini of 27 corresponds to the Nordic countries. A Gini of 50 corresponds to high-inequality emerging markets like South Africa or Brazil. This is purely a scaling convention — economically equivalent to the decimal scale. But if you mentally translate "0.35" when the engine shows "35," you'll mis-calibrate every welfare and tax decision.

25.4 The Five Pillar Scoring Formulas

After each round, computeScores(newState) reads the seventeen state variables and produces five pillar scores. Each pillar maxes out at 200 points. The total is the sum (max 1000 per round). The formulas below are the exact ones from the engine code — not simplifications.

⚙ Pillar 1: GDP Performance (max 200 points)
gdp_score = growth_pts + gap_pts + stock_pts
= 120 × clamp((gdp_growth + 2)/6, 0, 1)
+ 50 × clamp(1 − |gdp_growth − potential|/4, 0, 1)
+ 30 × clamp((stock − 2000)/8000, 0, 1)
growth_pts
120-point component for raw growth. Zero at −2% growth; full marks at +4% growth or better. Linear in between.
gap_pts
50-point component for staying near potential. Best score at exactly trend; falls linearly with deviation. Penalizes both over-heating and deep recessions.
stock_pts
30-point component for investor confidence. Stock index above 10000 maxes this out; near baseline 5000 yields about 11 points.

Key insight: the Growth pillar is not just about high GDP — it's about high GDP near potential, with a healthy stock market. A country at +6% growth but with stocks at 3000 and a wide output gap will score worse than a country at +3% growth with stocks at 12000 and growth exactly at potential. Stable growth beats hot-then-cold growth even when average GDP is the same.

⚙ Pillar 2: Stability (max 200 points)
stability_score = inf_pts + debt_pts + bal_pts + fin_pts
= 70 × (1 − |inflation − 2|/8)
+ 60 × (1 − max(0, debt − 40)/100)
+ 40 × (1 − |budget_balance|/10)
+ 30 × (fin_health/100)
inf_pts
70 points for inflation control. Maxed at inflation = 2%; zero at inflation = 10% or inflation = −6%. Symmetric: deflation is penalised as much as overshoot.
debt_pts
60 points for debt control. Full marks at debt ≤ 40% of GDP; zero at 140%. Linear in between.
bal_pts
40 points for fiscal balance. Full marks at a balanced budget. Surplus penalized symmetrically with deficit.
fin_pts
30 points for financial system health. Reads fin_health/100 directly.

Key insight: Stability is the most multifaceted pillar — four sub-components. Inflation dominates (70 of 200) but the others matter substantially. A country with 2% inflation, 90% debt, a 5% deficit, and weak financial health will score around 70+12+20+22 ≈ 124 — below half despite hitting the inflation target. You need all four sub-components working, not just price stability.

⚙ Pillar 3: Equality (max 200 points)
equality_score = gini_pts + unemp_pts + hdi_pts
= 130 × (1 − (gini − 20)/45)
+ 40 × (1 − max(0, unemployment − 3)/12)
+ 30 × ((hdi − 0.3)/0.7)
gini_pts
130 points for income equality. Full marks at Gini = 20 (extraordinarily equal); zero at Gini = 65 (highly unequal). The dominant component.
unemp_pts
40 points for low unemployment. Full marks at u ≤ 3%; zero at u = 15%.
hdi_pts
30 points for human development. Reads HDI on its 0.30 to 1.00 scale.

Key insight: The Gini component dominates Equality scoring (130 of 200). And the welfare lever has the largest coefficient on Gini (−4.0 per normalized unit). If you want a high Equality score, the cheapest path is welfare spending — far more direct than taxation or growth.

⚙ Pillar 4: Sustainability (max 200 points)
sust_score = sust_debt_pts + sust_hdi_pts + sust_pot_pts
= 80 × (1 − max(0, debt − 30)/120)
+ 70 × ((hdi − 0.3)/0.7)
+ 50 × ((potential − 0.5)/4.5)
sust_debt_pts
80 points for debt sustainability. Full marks at debt ≤ 30% of GDP; zero at 150%. Lower threshold than Stability's debt component.
sust_hdi_pts
70 points for human development sustainability. HDI also appears in Equality and Innovation — it's triple-counted across pillars.
sust_pot_pts
50 points for productive capacity. Driven by accumulated R&D, infrastructure, and green energy investment.

Key insight: Debt is penalised in two pillars — Stability and Sustainability — but with different thresholds. Stability starts penalising at 40%; Sustainability at 30%. The engine treats high debt as a multi-dimensional problem, not a single line item. Hitting 100% debt loses you roughly 36 points across both pillars combined.

⚙ Pillar 5: Innovation (max 200 points)
innovation_score = prod_pts + inn_hdi + inn_fin
= 100 × ((productivity − 0.5)/4.5)
+ 60 × ((hdi − 0.3)/0.7)
+ 40 × (fin_health/100)
prod_pts
100 points for productivity. The TFP index drives this. Productivity is slow-moving — patient R&D investment is the only reliable path.
inn_hdi
60 points for HDI. Third pillar where HDI appears.
inn_fin
40 points for financial system health. Same metric as in Stability, scored separately.

Key insight: Innovation is half about productivity (100 of 200), which only R&D and infrastructure can build, and they build it slowly. A team that doesn't invest in R&D from round 1 will have a permanently low Innovation pillar — it's not recoverable in late game. The other 100 points split between HDI (welfare-driven) and Financial Health (regulation-driven) — both are recoverable but less important than the slow-moving productivity.

25.5 The Hidden Connections: Where Variables Show Up Multiple Times

The engine's scoring system has several variables that feed multiple pillars. Understanding these cross-pillar dependencies is essential for reading the scorecard. Push HDI up via welfare spending, and you gain points in Equality, Sustainability, and Innovation simultaneously. Let debt rise, and you lose points in Stability and Sustainability simultaneously. These dependencies are what make the simulation feel rich — small policy changes can move multiple pillar scores at once.

Cross-pillar dependencies in the scoring formulas
State variableAppears inTotal points at risk
HDIEquality (30) + Sustainability (70) + Innovation (60)160 of 1000 points
DebtStability (60, threshold 40%) + Sustainability (80, threshold 30%)140 of 1000 points
Financial HealthStability (30) + Innovation (40)70 of 1000 points
UnemploymentEquality (40), plus indirect: HDI penalty + Gini penalty40+ points
InflationStability (70), plus indirect: HDI penalty if >4%, stock market drag70+ points
ProductivityInnovation (100). Indirectly feeds Growth via potential.100 points
Potential GDPSustainability (50). Indirectly via Growth gap_pts.50+ points
✦ Sharma's Take — The HDI lever

If I had to pick the single most under-appreciated scoring variable in the MacroNations engine, it would be HDI. It appears in three different pillars, totaling 160 of the 1000 available points. The dominant driver of HDI is welfare spending (coefficient 0.35, the largest in the engine). So a single decision — to spend more on welfare — can move 160 points worth of pillar scoring simultaneously, plus another 130 points worth of Gini-driven Equality scoring, plus inequality reduction across all pillars. Welfare spending is the highest-leverage lever in the engine, scoring-wise.

The catch: welfare adds to the deficit, which feeds debt, which is penalised in two pillars. So you can't simply maximize welfare without consequences. But teams that ignore welfare entirely — usually because they're chasing a "low-tax growth-maximizing" strategy — leave 150-200 points on the table per round across multiple pillars. The math says welfare deserves more attention than it usually gets in tournament play. This isn't an ideological claim; it's a reading of the scoring formulas.

— A.S.

25.6 The Engine's Macroeconomic Equations

Inside simRound, four equations do most of the macroeconomic work — translating policy levers into the state variables that ultimately feed scoring. Knowing these four equations gives you a strong intuition for what every policy change will do.

⚙ Equation 1: GDP Growth (block 9)
new_gdp = new_potential
+ 0.60 × (monetary_gdp + fiscal_gdp + structural_gdp + debt_drag)
+ 0.20 × (prev_gdp − prev_potential)
+ crisis_gdp_shock + noise

GDP is anchored to potential and pushed away by policy choices, with 60% pass-through and 20% momentum from the prior period. Persistent policy stance keeps GDP above or below potential as long as you hold it — this is what makes monetary tightening feel slow but cumulative. The 60% pass-through means that even an aggressive policy move only delivers most of its effect over 2-3 rounds.

⚙ Equation 2: Unemployment via Okun's Law (block 10)
new_unemployment = prev_unemployment + (−0.5 × output_gap) + crisis_shock + noise
where output_gap = new_gdp − new_potential, NAIRU = 4.5%

This is pure Okun's Law. Every 1pp of GDP above potential pushes unemployment down by 0.5pp. Above-potential growth held for two rounds drops unemployment by 1pp; held for four rounds, by 2pp. You don't directly control unemployment — it's a derived state. Manage GDP relative to potential, and unemployment follows.

⚙ Equation 3: Inflation (Phillips Curve, block 11)
new_inflation = (0.5 × prev_inflation + 0.5 × 2.0) + 0.4 × output_gap
+ monetary_inf + cost_push + crisis_shock + noise

Three components: inertial (50% adaptive on previous inflation, 50% anchored at the 2% target), Phillips curve (0.4 × output gap — engine uses GDP gap, not unemployment gap), monetary drag (each 1pp rate above 4% neutral subtracts 0.4pp from inflation). The 50/50 anchor is what prevents inflation from spiraling — and from collapsing to zero. It's an explicit modeling choice to give players a stable target to defend.

⚙ Equation 4: Debt Dynamics (blocks 3-4)
new_debt = prev_debt − (tax_rate − gov_spending) + (prev_debt × prev_interest_rate / 1000)
debt_drag = −0.02 × (new_debt − 90)/10 if debt > 90%

Debt grows by the deficit (spending minus revenue) plus interest on existing debt. The Reinhart-Rogoff calibration kicks in at 90% — every 10pp of debt above that threshold subtracts 0.02pp from GDP growth permanently until debt comes back down. This is small per round but compounds: 150% debt held for 5 rounds eats roughly 0.6pp of cumulative GDP growth. Letting debt creep past 90% is a slow trap.

25.7 Interactive: Pillar Score Estimator

🎛 Interactive — Estimate Pillar Scores from State

Set your country's state variables and see the engine's exact pillar scoring

This widget runs the engine's exact computeScores formulas — not approximations. The numbers you see are what you'd get in the tournament for the same state values.

What you're seeing: the exact pillar scores the live engine's computeScores function would produce. Drag the sliders to see how each state variable contributes. Notice how HDI, debt, and unemployment each affect multiple pillars at once.

25.8 Diagnostic Patterns: Reading the Scorecard

After each round, your scorecard tells a story. Different score patterns indicate different underlying problems. Here's how to read them.

Common score patterns and what they indicate
PatternLikely cause
Growth high, Stability fallingOverheating. GDP growing above potential pushes Phillips curve up, inflation rising, debt-pts component slipping. Action: raise rates by 50-100bp.
Growth high, Sustainability lowHot growth funded by deficits. Debt climbing past 30% (Sustainability threshold) and 40% (Stability threshold). Action: tighten fiscal stance, raise tax_rate by 2-3pp or cut gov_spending.
Equality stuck despite low GiniHigh unemployment dragging the unemp_pts component. Gini might be fine but unemployment above 6% costs ~20pts. Action: ease monetary or fiscal policy to reduce unemployment.
Innovation flat for 3+ roundsProductivity is slow-moving. R&D and infrastructure investments take 3-5 rounds to compound through potential and productivity. Action: patience — keep R&D high; don't switch course.
All five pillars dropping togetherCrisis active. Banking, pandemic, trade war, or debt crisis hit this round. Action: check which response option you selected — its effectiveness determines how much of the shock you absorbed.
Stability dropping while inflation looks fineThe inf_pts component is only 70 of 200. The 60 debt_pts + 40 bal_pts + 30 fin_pts components account for 130. Likely debt or deficit is the issue. Action: check budget_balance — if >5pp deficit, tighten fiscal.
Currency strengthening unexpectedlyYou raised rates (each 1pp adds 1.5% to currency). Strong currency hurts net exports via the cost_push channel. Action: balance rate strategy against trade openness.
✦ Sharma's Take — Read direction, not level

The single most important diagnostic skill is reading the direction of change, not the level. The engine has 20% momentum on the GDP gap, slow-moving potential and productivity, and Reinhart-Rogoff debt drag that compounds. A country with a Stability score of 130 rising for three rounds is in much better shape than a country with 160 falling for three. The first is on a trajectory toward a higher equilibrium; the second toward a lower one.

Top players in pilot tournaments tolerated temporarily lower scores while making structural adjustments (raising R&D, switching to greener energy, reducing debt) because they knew the trajectory was good. Lower-scoring teams panicked when individual scores fell and reversed course, which usually made things worse: reversals fragmented the policy path and wasted the engine's compounding effects on slow-moving levers. Direction over level. Always.

— A.S.

25.9 The Crisis System

The engine includes four distinct crises that hit on designated crisis rounds. Each is a defined shock package — exact GDP, unemployment, inflation, and financial-health shocks. When a crisis fires, you choose from three response options; each has a specific effectiveness percentage that scales how much of the shock you absorb. This is fundamentally different from the qualitative "shocks" I described in pre-engine drafts of this chapter — every crisis is fully specified.

The four crises (exact shock values from the engine's CRISES object)
CrisisGDPUnempInfFinResponse options (effectiveness)
Banking Crisis −2.5pp +1.8pp −0.5pp −15 Government Bailout (70%), Liquidity Injection (50%), Let Banks Fail (25%)
Global Pandemic −4.0pp +3.5pp +1.5pp −10 Strict Lockdown + Relief (65%), Targeted Restrictions (55%), Keep Economy Open (35%)
Trade War −1.5pp +0.8pp +1.2pp −5 Seek Bilateral Deal (60%), Diversify Trade Partners (50%), Impose Counter-Tariffs (30%)
Sovereign Debt Crisis −3.0pp +2.0pp +2.0pp −20 IMF Bailout (60%), Selective Default (40%), Monetary Financing (35%)

Crisis rounds are pre-scheduled by the tournament organizer (default: rounds 3, 6, and 9). Each crisis round, the engine picks one crisis type and applies it to every team in the tournament simultaneously. This is important: in any given tournament, every team faces the same crisis on the same round, so the spread of outcomes is driven entirely by which response option each team chose and what state their country was in when the crisis hit.

⚠ Common Pitfall — Failing to respond to crisis

If you don't submit a crisis response, the engine applies the full shock with 0% effectiveness — meaning you absorb 100% of the GDP/unemployment/inflation/financial-health hit. A pandemic with no response: −4pp GDP, +3.5pp unemployment, +1.5pp inflation, −10 financial health. A debt crisis with no response: −3pp GDP, +2pp unemployment, +2pp inflation, −20 financial health. These are devastating. Always submit a crisis response. The worst response option (25-35% effectiveness depending on crisis) is still vastly better than no response.

25.10 Worked Examples: Predicting Pillar Scores

📐 Worked Example 25.1 — A Welfare State Build
You start round 1 with the default economy (GDP 2.5%, inflation 2%, debt 60%, Gini 35, HDI 0.75). You raise welfare_spending from 5 to 12% of GDP and tax_rate from 25 to 32%. All other levers held at defaults. Predict the round-2 pillar scores.
1
Fiscal impulse on GDP. Welfare is not a separate GDP channel — it appears in gov_spending if you raise gov_spending_pct. Since we only raised welfare (which adds to deficit) and tax_rate, GDP impulse is just from the tax change: fiscal_tax_gdp = −1.1 × (32 − 25) × 0.12 = −0.92pp.
2
Round-2 GDP. new_gdp ≈ potential + 0.60 × (−0.92) ≈ 2.5 − 0.55 ≈ 1.95% (plus small noise).
3
Gini. welfare_norm = 12/30 = 0.40. gini_change = −4.0 × 0.40 + tax_equality = −1.6 + (−0.5 × 7/35) = −1.6 − 0.10 = −1.7. New Gini = 35 − 1.7 = 33.3.
4
HDI. hdi_change = 0.35 × 0.40 = 0.14 (plus small R&D and other contributions). HDI rises sharply from 0.75 toward 0.89.
5
Pillar scores (using exact formulas). Equality: gini_pts = 130 × (1 − 13.3/45) = 92, unemp_pts ≈ 32, hdi_pts = 30 × 0.84 = 25 → ~149 (up from 122 at baseline). Sustainability: hdi up adds 0.84 × 70 ≈ 59 to its component → ~158. Innovation: hdi adds to its 60-pt slot → ~141.
Net change vs baseline: Equality +27, Sustainability +25, Innovation +15. Growth slightly down (lower gdp from tax drag). Round 2 total: roughly +60 to +70 points just from one welfare expansion plus tax increase. This is the largest single-move score swing the engine permits, and it illustrates why welfare is the highest-leverage scoring lever — three pillars improve simultaneously through HDI, plus a fourth (Equality) directly via Gini.
📐 Worked Example 25.2 — Recovering from a Pandemic
Round 3 hits and a pandemic crisis fires. Your country was at GDP 2.8%, inflation 2.2%, unemployment 5.0%, debt 65%, fin_health 80. You choose "Strict Lockdown + Relief" (65% effectiveness). Compute the post-crisis state for the GDP, unemployment, inflation, and financial health variables.
1
Effectiveness multiplier. 65% effective means the absorbed shock is reduced by 65%; the remaining 35% hits. So eff_factor = (1 − 65/100) = 0.35.
2
Apply scaled shocks.
GDP shock = −4.0 × 0.35 = −1.4pp
Unemp shock = +3.5 × 0.35 = +1.225pp
Inflation shock = +1.5 × 0.35 = +0.525pp
Fin shock = −10 × 0.35 = −3.5 (on the 10-100 scale)
3
Round-3 state (approximate, ignoring noise).
GDP: 2.8 + policy_effects − 1.4 ≈ +0.5 to +1.5% (mild recession)
Unemployment: 5.0 + Okun_change + 1.225 ≈ 6.5-7.0%
Inflation: ~2.5% + 0.525 ≈ 3.0%
Financial Health: 80 − 3.5 = 76.5
4
Compare to no-response counterfactual. With no response, the same crisis would have produced GDP shock of −4.0 (full hit), pushing GDP toward −1.5%, unemployment toward 8.5%, inflation toward 4.0%, fin_health to 70. Choosing the 65%-effectiveness response saved roughly 2.5pp of GDP, 2.3pp of unemployment, 1pp of inflation, and 7 points of financial health — a substantial difference.
Score impact: the response choice converts what could be a 150+ point score loss into roughly a 70-90 point loss. Across a 10-round tournament with three crisis rounds, the cumulative value of consistently choosing the highest-effectiveness response option is roughly 200-300 points. This is the single largest source of score variation between teams in tournaments — not policy strategy, but crisis response quality.

25.11 Interactive: Engine Pulse Monitor

🎛 Interactive — Engine Pulse Monitor

Set your current state and policy changes; see the engine's exact next-round response

This widget implements the engine's exact equations from simRound blocks 1-11 (monetary, fiscal, structural, GDP, Okun, Phillips). Use it to test your intuition before submitting in a real round.

What you're seeing: the engine's exact computational chain from policy changes through monetary/fiscal/structural impulses, GDP update with 60% pass-through and 20% momentum, Okun's law for unemployment, and the Phillips curve for inflation. Compare these predictions to your in-tournament results.

References

Akerlof, G. A., & Yellen, J. L. (1985). A near-rational model of the business cycle, with wage and price inertia. Quarterly Journal of Economics, 100, 823–838. The microfoundations behind the engine's 50% adaptive / 50% anchored inflation expectations rule.

Auerbach, A. J., & Gorodnichenko, Y. (2012). Measuring the output responses to fiscal policy. American Economic Journal: Economic Policy, 4(2), 1–27. Empirical basis for the 1.4 spending multiplier (regime-averaged) used by the engine.

Christiano, L., Eichenbaum, M., & Evans, C. (2005). Nominal rigidities and the dynamic effects of a shock to monetary policy. Journal of Political Economy, 113(1), 1–45. Source for the 60% pass-through and 20% momentum coefficients in the engine's GDP equation.

Mankiw, N. G. (2024). Macroeconomics (12th ed.). Worth Publishers. The textbook source for Okun's law, the Phillips curve, and the IS-LM framework that underpin the engine.

Phillips, A. W. (1958). The relation between unemployment and the rate of change of money wage rates in the United Kingdom, 1861–1957. Economica, 25, 283–299.

Reinhart, C. M., & Rogoff, K. S. (2010). Growth in a time of debt. American Economic Review: Papers & Proceedings, 100(2), 573–578. The 90% debt threshold and 0.02pp/10pp drag coefficient in the engine come from this paper's empirical estimates.

Romer, C. D., & Romer, D. H. (2004). A new measure of monetary shocks: Derivation and implications. American Economic Review, 94(4), 1055–1084.

Smets, F., & Wouters, R. (2007). Shocks and frictions in US business cycles: A Bayesian DSGE approach. American Economic Review, 97(3), 586–606. The full DSGE machinery the MacroNations engine simplifies.

Chapter 25 Quiz

Engine architecture, levers, state, scoring formulas, crises
Part A — Multiple Choice
1. How many levers does the MacroNations engine actually expose?
A 8 (interest rate, gov spending, tax, trade, education, R&D, green energy, labor flexibility)
B 12 (the above plus 4 others)
C 10 (interest_rate, gov_spending, tax_rate, trade_openness, rd_investment, green_energy, welfare_spending, regulation, foreign_aid, infrastructure)
D 5
2. The engine's fiscal spending multiplier is:
A 0.5 — flat regardless of regime
B 1.4 — fixed (not regime-dependent in this engine)
C Varies from 0.5 to 2.5 based on output gap
D 1.0 in normal times, 1.8 at the ZLB
3. Which state variable appears as a scoring component in THREE different pillars?
A GDP growth
B Inflation
C HDI (in Equality, Sustainability, and Innovation)
D Unemployment
4. The engine's Phillips curve uses which form of "gap"?
A Unemployment gap (NAIRU − u)
B Output gap (GDP − potential), with slope 0.4
C Credibility-weighted expectations gap
D Wage gap
5. The Reinhart-Rogoff debt drag in the engine activates when debt-to-GDP exceeds:
A 60%
B 75%
C 90%
D 100%
6. The engine's neutral interest rate (against which monetary stance is measured) is:
A 2.5%
B 4.0%
C 5.0%
D The Taylor rule prescribed rate
7. Crisis rounds in the engine default to which rounds?
A Every round, randomly
B Only rounds 8-10
C Rounds 3, 6, 9 (customizable by the tournament organizer)
D Round 5 only
Part B — Short Answer
8. Explain in 2-3 sentences why welfare_spending is the highest-leverage lever for total pillar scoring, even though it's not the largest dollar spend in most countries' budgets.
Model answer: Welfare spending feeds HDI directly with the engine's largest coefficient (0.35) and reduces Gini directly with the largest negative coefficient (−4.0 per normalized welfare unit). HDI then appears in three of the five pillars (Equality, Sustainability, Innovation), and Gini drives the dominant 130-point component of Equality. So a single welfare-spending increase can move 4 of 5 pillars simultaneously — roughly 300+ points of scoring exposure.
9. Your country has Gini = 30 (low/equal), unemployment = 8% (high), HDI = 0.75 (moderate). Compute the Equality pillar score using the engine's exact formula.
Model answer: gini_pts = 130 × (1 − (30−20)/45) = 130 × 0.778 = 101.1. unemp_pts = 40 × (1 − max(0, 8−3)/12) = 40 × (1 − 5/12) = 40 × 0.583 = 23.3. hdi_pts = 30 × ((0.75−0.3)/0.7) = 30 × 0.643 = 19.3. Total Equality = round(101.1 + 23.3 + 19.3) ≈ 144.
10. A pandemic crisis fires (GDP shock −4.0pp, unemp shock +3.5pp, inflation shock +1.5pp, fin shock −10). You choose "Targeted Restrictions" (55% effectiveness). Compute the residual shock that hits your country.
Model answer: Effectiveness factor = 1 − 0.55 = 0.45. Residual shocks: GDP −4.0 × 0.45 = −1.8pp, Unemp +3.5 × 0.45 = +1.575pp, Inflation +1.5 × 0.45 = +0.675pp, Fin −10 × 0.45 = −4.5. Compared to no response (full shock), the player saved roughly 2.2pp GDP, 1.9pp unemployment, 0.8pp inflation, and 5.5 points of financial health.
This is a preview — the live textbook auto-grades MCQs and stores progress.
Chapter 26 · Part VIII: MacroNations Applications · Final Chapter

Policy Strategy Guide

How to think about MacroNations as a 10-round strategic exercise — early-game positioning, mid-game execution, late-game optimization, and the contrarian moves that separate top scores from average ones

If you've made it this far in the textbook — through twenty-five chapters of theory and one chapter on how to read the simulation — you have the analytical tools to play MacroNations seriously. This final chapter is about something different. It's about strategy: how to use those analytical tools to actually win a 10-round tournament. Strategy in MacroNations is not the same thing as economics. Economics tells you what each policy does. Strategy tells you which policies to use when, and how to think about the tournament as a coherent multi-round game rather than ten disconnected rounds.

The strategic structure of a 10-round MacroNations tournament has three distinct phases: early game (rounds 1-3), mid-game (rounds 4-7), and late game (rounds 8-10). Each phase has different objectives, different risks, and different optimal moves. Most teams play all ten rounds with the same playbook, which is why most teams score moderately. The teams that finish at the top of the leaderboard understand the phases and adapt their strategy as the tournament progresses.

This chapter walks through each phase, presents four well-tested country archetypes you can adopt or adapt, and ends with a set of contrarian moves that show deeper economic thinking. By the end of it, you should have not just the economic knowledge to play MacroNations well, but the strategic framework to play it competitively.

✦ Sharma's Take

I've watched students play MacroNations across multiple semesters now, and I've seen one pattern repeat itself relentlessly. The teams that win are not the ones with the best economic intuition; they're the ones with the best strategic discipline. There are plenty of teams that understand the economics perfectly well but make impulsive, inconsistent decisions round-to-round, chasing whichever pillar fell last round and ignoring everything else. They never finish in the top quartile.

Top-finishing teams almost always share three characteristics. First, they pick a coherent national strategy in round 1 and stick to it. Second, they read the scorecard for direction of change, not level. Third, they think one or two rounds ahead — they don't just respond to what happened last round, they anticipate what will happen next round given current trends. If you internalize those three habits, you'll outperform 80% of teams without doing anything else fancy. This chapter is largely an exercise in making those habits explicit.

— A.S.

26.1 The Three Phases of a 10-Round Tournament

Phase 1 · Rounds 1–3 · Early Game

Establishing Your Country

The first three rounds are about positioning. Your country starts with a set of initial conditions, but you have wide latitude to shape its long-term trajectory. The decisions you make here matter disproportionately because they compound over the remaining seven rounds.

Objectives:

  • Choose a national strategy (you can pick one of the archetypes in §26.3 or design your own)
  • Establish credibility with predictable monetary policy
  • Make initial investments in slow-moving levers (R&D, infrastructure, green energy) — they take 3-5 rounds to show full effect, so starting late means missing the payoff
  • Avoid debt traps — get your fiscal position to a sustainable starting place

Common mistakes: pushing levers too aggressively in round 1 (over-correcting from initial conditions); ignoring the slow-moving levers because they don't pay off immediately; making large monetary policy changes that damage credibility before it's even established.

Phase 2 · Rounds 4–7 · Mid-Game

Compounding and Adjustment

By round 4, your early-game investments are starting to pay off. R&D and infrastructure are pushing potential GDP and Innovation scores upward. Your monetary credibility is established. You should now see consistent score improvement from round to round if your strategy is working. This is also the phase when shocks become more frequent, testing whether your country can absorb surprises.

Objectives:

  • Maintain consistency on your established strategy — don't second-guess
  • Make tactical adjustments to specific pillar weaknesses identified in rounds 1-3
  • Build buffers (low debt, anchored expectations, strong current account) ahead of late-game shocks
  • Watch for new opportunities — sometimes a productivity surprise lets you push growth without inflation cost

Common mistakes: getting bored with consistency and making changes for the sake of change; misreading random round-to-round volatility as a signal of strategy failure and reversing course; underinvesting in shock resilience because nothing has gone wrong yet.

Phase 3 · Rounds 8–10 · Late Game

Endgame Optimization

The final three rounds are where most tournaments are decided. Your country has whatever long-term trajectory you've built; the question is whether you can extract maximum points from your remaining rounds without breaking the structure. Shocks are most frequent here, so resilience matters most.

Objectives:

  • Convert your accumulated stocks (TFP, infrastructure, green energy, anchored expectations) into pillar scores by making the right marginal adjustments
  • If you have fiscal space, deploy it for politically-valuable spending that the engine rewards (targeted social spending raises Equality at low Stability cost when inflation is well-anchored)
  • Hedge against late-game shocks — keep some monetary policy room and fiscal space available
  • Don't try desperate catch-up moves if you're behind — they almost always backfire

Common mistakes: "Hail Mary" moves to catch up to leading teams — these usually involve aggressive fiscal expansion that triggers Stability collapse; running out of credibility space because monetary policy has been too volatile; getting caught with no buffer when a late shock hits.

26.2 The Universal Principles

Regardless of which national strategy you choose, four principles apply to every tournament:

Four universal principles for MacroNations strategy
PrincipleWhy it matters
Credibility compounds Stable, predictable monetary policy raises the credibility index, which anchors inflation expectations, which lets you maintain low inflation at lower interest rates. Erratic policy destroys credibility, which forces you to use higher rates to achieve the same anti-inflation effect, which hurts Growth. Credibility is the cheapest pillar to build and the most expensive to rebuild after losing.
Slow-moving levers reward early commitment R&D, infrastructure, green energy, and welfare spending all take 3-5 rounds to show full effects. Starting from low values means losing 3-5 rounds of compounding. Get the slow levers right in round 1, and don't change them much afterward.
Fiscal space is optionality Keeping debt below 80% of GDP gives you the ability to deploy fiscal stimulus when shocks hit — which is precisely when fiscal multipliers are highest. Countries with debt above 100% have effectively lost that option. The cost of fiscal space is small now; the value is high later.
Don't fight the engine's logic The engine implements standard macroeconomic relationships. If you try to run high G and low taxes and low interest rates simultaneously at full employment, you will get high inflation, high debt, and eventual policy whiplash. The engine doesn't permit free lunches. Trying to "outsmart" it by ignoring the trade-offs you've learned about will fail in predictable ways.

26.3 Four Tested National Archetypes

You can design your own national strategy, but if you want a tested starting point, here are four archetypes that have performed well in pilot tournaments. Each is a coherent combination of lever choices that pushes the country toward a particular pillar profile. Try the interactive below to see them.

🎛 Interactive — Four Tested National Archetypes

Click a strategy to see its lever profile, expected pillar emphasis, and Sharma's commentary

Click an archetype above to see its strategy

Each archetype is a coherent combination of policy levers that reflects a real-world country model and produces a recognizable pillar profile. None is "best" — they reflect different national priorities.

26.4 Five Contrarian Moves That Separate Top Scores

The archetypes above are well-tested strategies. They will get you into the top half of the leaderboard if executed consistently. But the very top scores often come from teams that make one or two contrarian moves — small departures from the obvious play that exploit something subtle about the engine's structure or the macroeconomic theory. Here are five contrarian moves I've seen produce outsized scores.

⚡ Contrarian Move #1

Tighten monetary policy before inflation appears

Most teams wait for inflation to rise above 3% before raising rates. By that point, expectations have already started to de-anchor, and you need a much bigger hike to bring inflation back down. Top teams raise rates preemptively — when the output gap closes and unemployment falls below 4.5%, they hike by 50-75 bp even if inflation is still at target. The result: inflation never gets going, credibility stays high, and the Stability score holds steady through the entire tournament. This is the central bank version of "an ounce of prevention is worth a pound of cure" — and the engine rewards it richly.

⚡ Contrarian Move #2

Pair R&D with infrastructure — not at the expense of welfare

The obvious play for Innovation is to push R&D to its 10% max immediately. But the engine's productivity equation also weights infrastructure (0.10 per pp above 5%) and green energy (0.008 per pp above 30%), plus HDI feeds 60 of Innovation's 200 points and 70 of Sustainability's. Top Innovation scores in pilots came from teams running R&D at 5-7% combined with infrastructure at 10-12% and welfare at 12-15%. The infrastructure builds long-run productivity capacity that compounds with R&D; the welfare keeps HDI rising to fill the 130-point HDI scoring across three pillars. Naive max-R&D-only teams underperformed this combination by 40-60 points per round on Innovation alone.

⚡ Contrarian Move #3

Use trade openness as a productivity multiplier

Trade openness is often treated as a balance-of-payments question — does it raise or lower net exports? But its biggest engine effect is actually on the Innovation pillar, because openness increases exposure to foreign technologies and forces domestic firms to compete with the global frontier. Pushing trade openness above 70 often produces Innovation gains that exceed the gains from raising R&D itself. Combine this with high infrastructure spending and you get compounding productivity effects. This is the empirical insight from the cross-country growth literature — open economies systematically outperform closed ones at the productivity frontier.

⚡ Contrarian Move #4

Tolerate one bad round of Equality early to fix it later

The Equality pillar responds to a mix of factors — Gini coefficient, unemployment, transfer programs. If your country starts with a Gini around 0.45 and you try to fix it by raising taxes aggressively in round 1, you'll damage Growth and trigger a cascade of secondary problems. The better play is to let Equality lag in rounds 1-3 while you build up infrastructure (which raises productivity) and welfare spending (which raises HDI and reduces Gini). By round 4-5, the human-capital channel starts compressing the income distribution, and your Equality score rises naturally without the Growth penalty of aggressive redistribution. The macro insight: long-run inequality is mostly a function of skill distribution, not transfer policy.

⚡ Contrarian Move #5

Build a "fiscal war chest" by round 6 even if it costs current Growth

Most teams keep deficits high throughout the tournament because deficits boost current Growth via the multiplier. But late-game shocks are when fiscal multipliers are highest (the engine, like the real world, applies a deep-recession multiplier of ~1.8 when output gap goes negative). Teams that keep their debt-to-GDP below 70% by mid-game have the ability to deploy a large fiscal expansion exactly when it's worth most — converting low-multiplier "expensive" stimulus into high-multiplier "cheap" stimulus through better timing. This is the single biggest source of variation between top-quartile and middle-quartile teams in our pilots: the discipline to save fiscal space for when it matters most.

✦ Sharma's Take — The meta-strategy

The five contrarian moves above are not arbitrary. Each one represents a place where the engine implements a piece of standard macroeconomic theory more faithfully than common intuition assumes. Preemptive tightening exploits the time-inconsistency literature (Ch 17). Infrastructure-paired R&D exploits the public-capital growth literature (Ch 9). Trade openness exploits the cross-country productivity literature (Ch 13). Tolerating early inequality exploits the skill-biased technical change literature. Building fiscal space exploits the regime-dependent multiplier literature (Ch 18). What separates top players from average players is not knowing more economics; it's trusting the economics they already know.

If you're a student reading this for the first time before your first tournament, here's my one piece of meta-advice: treat MacroNations as an exercise in applied macroeconomics, not as a game. The engine rewards the same things real economies reward: credibility, consistency, long-term thinking, attention to fundamentals, and trade-off awareness. If you find yourself making decisions that contradict what you'd recommend if you were actually advising a finance minister, stop and reconsider. The students who finish first are almost always the ones who internalized this advice. The students who finish last are almost always the ones who treated the simulation as a video game. Choose accordingly.

— A.S.

26.5 What to Avoid: The Top Six Strategic Mistakes

The most common — and most costly — strategic errors
MistakeWhy it costs you
1. Lever-pulling chaosChanging 5+ levers per round without a coherent strategy. Destroys credibility, makes the simulation harder to read, and produces wildly variable scores that compound poorly.
2. Late panic movesTrying to catch up to leading teams in rounds 9-10 by making aggressive moves. Almost always triggers Stability collapse via inflation or debt-sustainability penalties.
3. Ignoring slow-moving leversR&D, infrastructure, green energy all need 3-5 rounds to pay off. Starting them late in the tournament wastes most of their potential value.
4. Pro-cyclical fiscal policyRunning deficits in booms and surpluses in busts (the opposite of what theory recommends). Often happens because teams react to current Growth without thinking about the cyclical context.
5. Maximum-everything attemptsTrying to push all five pillars at once. Produces a mediocre score on each instead of an excellent score on three or four.
6. Round-by-round myopiaOptimizing each round in isolation, without thinking about how this round's choices constrain or enable next round's options. The opposite of the meta-strategy described in §26.4.

26.6 Closing the Course: From Simulation to Real Policy

You've now finished twenty-six chapters of macroeconomics. You've learned the theory — supply and demand, GDP and inflation, growth and unemployment, the AD-AS framework, IS-LM, monetary and fiscal policy, the Phillips curve, supply-side economics, government debt, financial crises, inequality, contemporary policy debates. You've learned how to read the MacroNations simulation, which encodes most of that theory into a single integrated system. And you've now learned how to play the simulation strategically, exploiting the engine's deeper macroeconomic logic to produce strong scores.

What I hope you take from this is something larger than tournament performance. Macroeconomic policy in the real world is, fundamentally, the same kind of exercise as MacroNations. Real finance ministers face the same trade-offs you've faced. They balance Growth against Stability, equality against efficiency, sustainability against immediate political pressure. They deal with shocks they didn't anticipate, with policy tools that have lags and uncertain multipliers, with limited credibility and short electoral horizons. The toolkit you've used to think about MacroNations is the same toolkit they use. The intuitions you've developed by playing rounds in a simulated economy will sharpen your reading of real economic news for the rest of your life.

That's the deeper purpose of this textbook and the simulation it accompanies. Macroeconomic literacy is a citizenship skill in any modern democracy. Citizens who understand how policy works — who can read a Fed press conference and understand what Powell is actually saying, who can read a CBO report and see what's actually being projected, who can listen to political claims about the deficit and recognize which ones make sense — produce better policy outcomes than citizens who don't. The course you've taken is, in a small way, an effort to produce more such citizens. Whatever your career, I hope what you've learned here makes you a more effective participant in the public discussions about the economy that will shape the next several decades of your life.

✦ Sharma's Take — A final word

I've been teaching macroeconomics for about two decades now, and I've been collecting student feedback the whole time. The single most common piece of feedback I get from former students, years later, is some version of: "I can't believe how often what I learned in your macro class shows up in the news." This always pleases me but never surprises me, because macroeconomics is genuinely one of the most policy-relevant subjects in any university curriculum. Tax policy is macroeconomics. Inflation is macroeconomics. Trade wars are macroeconomics. Recession debates, Fed decisions, debt-ceiling fights, immigration economics, climate policy — almost every major economic story you'll see in your life is a macroeconomics story.

I built this textbook and the MacroNations simulation because I wanted to teach macroeconomics in a way that students would actually retain. Lecture-based macro is a notorious "in one ear, out the other" subject. You hear a definition, you memorize a formula, you take the test, and three years later you couldn't tell me what the Phillips curve was if your life depended on it. By making you actually run a country through the simulation, I'm hoping the concepts will be glued to specific experiences in your memory — you'll remember the round you raised interest rates and inflation didn't fall, the round a financial crisis cratered your Growth score, the round you finally got Innovation up to 175 after five rounds of patient R&D investment. Those experiences make abstract concepts concrete.

So my last piece of advice: play the tournament hard, but reflect on it harder. Each round is a small experiment in applied macroeconomics. Each pillar score is feedback from a simplified version of the real economy. The more you treat each round as data to learn from, the more macroeconomic intuition you'll build — and the more useful this course will be to you for the rest of your life. Good luck.
